Four genuine findings survived this cross-check: (1) a wholly new standalone entry for the National Authority for the Prevention of Torture (INPT), created by Organic Law No. 2013-43 of October 2013 — Tunisia's OPCAT National Preventive Mechanism, entirely undocumented despite 65 prior runs and an existing extradition entry that references "torture risk" only as a refusal ground — added to the Judicial Organization section; (2) a wholly new standalone entry for Starlink/satellite-internet authorization, stalled since a January 2023 SpaceX request over INT's ground-station routing demands, with no commercial license granted as of mid-2026 — added to the Digital section; (3) the precise decree-level legal basis for the July 25, 2022 constitutional referendum (Presidential Decree No. 2022-506 and Decree-Law No. 2022-32, both of May 25, 2022, JORT No. 59) — previously covered only via the broader Decree 2021-117 predicate — added to that entry; (4) the 2025 5G rollout (licensing conventions signed November 30, 2024; JORT No. 8 decrees of January 23, 2025; commercial launch February 14, 2025) and an expanded bilateral/multilateral extradition-treaty list (Belgium, Malta, Portugal, Turkey, Syria, Germany, the 2021/2022 Algeria convention, UNTOC, UNCAC) were added to the existing Digital Code and Extradition/MLA entries respectively. Two new standalone entries added (247 total); two existing entries (Decree 2021-117, Extradition & MLA) revised for currency. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 247 unique law-card ids, all divs and h3 tags balanced, and all TOC anchors resolving. The July 8 (graduate protest), July 11 (ONAT), and July 13 (CNAM tiers-payant) dates remain the next concrete triggers to check in a future run.
v4.66 update (July 3, 2026, Run 67): This run's designated branch was, yet again, the stale never-merged v1.0-baseline fork — this time independently rebuilt from scratch as a "v2.2" with 8 candidate entries (Trafficking in Persons, Legal Profession/Bar, Notaries, Exchange Control Code, Sports Structures, Fisheries, a 1973 Tourist Establishment framework, and a draft Tax Adviser pending-reform item), entirely unaware of origin/main's 66 merged runs to v4.65 (247 entries) — caught via git fetch origin main/git log origin/main before any PR was opened, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main per established practice. Every discarded-draft candidate was cross-checked against the real artifact via targeted grep before re-adding anything: the Trafficking in Persons Law (2016-61), Legal Profession statute, Notaries Law (94-60), Exchange Control Code (76-18) and its pending reform, Sports Structures Law (95-11), Fisheries Law (94-13), and the Tax Adviser draft (2023-013) were all found already present, in every case with materially more precision than the discarded draft. One genuine small refinement survived this cross-check: the existing Tourism Organization Law entry cited only that it "replaced earlier tourism laws" without naming them — now cites the specific predecessor framework it recodified (Decree-Laws No. 73-3 & 73-4 of October 3, 1973, ratified by Laws No. 73-58 & 73-59, and classification Decree No. 73-510, as later updated by Decree No. 2007-457 of 2007). A dedicated fast news check for the roughly two hours since the v4.65/Run 66 merge found no new JORT publication, ARP plenary vote, or court ruling (search-index lag for same-day Tunisian outlets is a known limitation). No new standalone entries added (247 total remains accurate, since the finding only extends an existing card); one existing entry (Tourism Organization Law) revised for precision. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 247 unique law-card ids, all divs/sections/h3 tags balanced, and all TOC anchors resolving. The July 8 (graduate protest), July 11 (ONAT), and July 13 (CNAM tiers-payant) dates remain the next concrete triggers to check in a future run — none have yet arrived as of this run's cutoff, still July 3, 2026.
v4.67 update (July 3, 2026, Run 68): This run's designated branch was, once again, the stale never-merged v1.0–v2.1 fork (3 commits, no independent new content beyond the original baseline) while origin/main had independently progressed through 67 merged runs to v4.66 (247 entries) — caught via git fetch origin main/git log before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main per established practice; given dozens of prior runs have already confirmed this exact baseline is a strict subset of main, no fresh cross-check of its (in this case unchanged) content was needed. A dedicated research agent re-checked all eighteen "Areas for Future Expansion" watchlist items via WebSearch (the project's Tavily research tool returned HTTP 402 "account disabled" for every query this run — a tooling gap worth flagging for future runs) plus a broad scan for JORT-published legislation in the June 28 – July 3, 2026 window (none found). Every substantive lead the agent surfaced was cross-checked directly against this document and found already captured, in each case with equal or greater precision: the exchange-violations amnesty bill's April 14, 2026 committee return (54-3-2) is already detailed in the Bill No. 58/2025 entry; the CNAM entry's Run 43 update already documents the pharmacists' June 30, 2026 "cellule de crise" the agent flagged as a new widening of the biologists' dispute; the banque postale entry already documents the Ministry of Finance's June 28, 2026 "strategic option" temporization the agent surfaced as fresh July 3 news; and the Diaspora Council reform entry already cites the June 4 and June 22, 2026 hearings the agent described as a "relaunch." One additional lead — a claimed specific dispute in the Tax Adviser Organic Law committee sessions over whether advisers may represent taxpayers in court without a cap on the disputed amount — was investigated with an independent follow-up search but could not be corroborated against any primary or secondary source, and was correctly not added rather than asserted on a single agent's unverified claim. No new standalone entries added (247 total remains accurate); no existing entries required revision. Also fixed a recurring version-string synchronization bug (the same class flagged in the v4.13, v4.25, v4.50, v4.59, and v4.61 notes above): the page's <title> tag had been left at "v4.64" for at least three prior runs while the header/footer correctly read "v4.66 (Run 67)" — now synchronized at v4.67. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 247 unique law-card ids, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 1102/1102 balanced <div> tags, and all 30 TOC anchors resolving. The artifact is reconfirmed current as of July 3, 2026, for the second consecutive same-day run; the July 8 (graduate protest), July 11 (ONAT), and July 13 (CNAM tiers-payant) dates remain the next concrete triggers to check in a future run.
v4.68 update (July 3, 2026, Run 69): This run's designated branch was again the stale never-merged v1.0–v2.2 fork (4 commits, up to a self-labeled "v2.2" with 138 entries, still entirely disjoint from origin/main's independent lineage) while origin/main had progressed through 68 merged runs to v4.67 (247 entries) — caught via git fetch origin main/git log origin/main before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main per established practice; given many prior runs have already confirmed this baseline is a strict, no-longer-independently-useful subset of main, no fresh cross-check of its unchanged content was needed. A fresh WebSearch sweep (11 queries, roughly two hours after the Run 68/v4.67 merge) re-checked JORT publication activity, the Foreign Exchange Code committee status, the Personal Data Protection & AI bill, and the CNAM/tiers-payant situation. No new enacted legislation and no watchlist-item status change was found — the Foreign Exchange Code remains at committee-hearing stage (Ministry of Finance and CONECT heard, Central Bank input still pending), the Data Protection bill remains under commission review. One genuine refinement was identified and closed: independent reporting (SPOT statement of July 2, 2026, corroborated by a June 3, 2026 SPOT national-council statement) confirms the pharmacists' tiers-payant track with CNAM continues uninterrupted, with a mid-July 2026 CNAM–SPOT meeting now scheduled under the Minister of Social Affairs — added to the CNAM (Law No. 2004-71) entry to keep it current and clearly distinguished from the separate, still-unresolved private biologists' July 13, 2026 suspension. No new standalone entries added (247 total remains accurate); one existing entry (CNAM) revised for currency. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 247 unique law-card ids, balanced <section>/<div> tags, and all 30 TOC anchors resolving. The July 8 (graduate protest), July 11 (ONAT), and July 13 (CNAM-biologists tiers-payant) dates remain the next concrete triggers to check in a future run; none have yet arrived as of this run's cutoff, still July 3, 2026.
v4.69 update (July 3, 2026, Run 70): This run's designated branch was, again, a stale never-merged fork (up to a self-labeled "v2.2," disjoint from origin/main's independent lineage) while origin/main had progressed through 69 merged runs to v4.68 (247 entries) — caught via git fetch origin main/git log before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main per established practice. A dedicated research agent re-checked all 24 "Areas for Future Expansion" watchlist items plus a broad scan for newly enacted JORT text; no committee vote, plenary passage, or enactment was found for any watchlist item, and no new JORT-published legislation was located, consistent with the last several same-day runs. However, this run's sweep surfaced and independently verified (via two follow-up WebSearch passes, cross-checked across African Manager, La Presse de Tunisie, Tustex, and Webmanagercenter) a genuinely new, cross-cutting finding: a July 3, 2026 IACE report ("cartographie" of pending economic-code reforms) states that five priority codes — Investment, Water, Environment, and Hydrocarbons, alongside the already-tracked Foreign Exchange Code — are "technically finalized and validated by the Council of Ministers" but "never transmitted to the ARP," attributing the delay to unfinished interministerial arbitration and sectoral resistance (an executive-stage bottleneck) rather than to parliamentary process, and recommending a Prime Ministry-based coordination unit with binding milestones across all sixteen reforms it tracks. This refines three existing entries and closes one genuine standalone gap: (1) the Investment Code reform entry now notes the IACE finding as a partial (not fully confirmed) resolution of the Run 38 ambiguity over two possibly-parallel bills — suggesting the government's Dec. 31, 2025 track is the one stalled pre-ARP transmission, distinct from the ARP committee's own article-by-article voting on the separate MP-initiated proposition n°26/2024; (2) the Water Code entry's long-standing single-source "submitted to the Prime Ministry" hedge is now corroborated by three independent July 3, 2026 sources, sharpened to the more specific "validated by Council of Ministers, not transmitted to ARP" status; (3) the Foreign Exchange Code entry now cites IACE naming it Tunisia's single highest economic-reform priority (citing the obsolete 1976 framework), while noting — unlike the other four codes — it is already before the Finance and Budget Committee, so the urgency concerns a floor vote rather than executive-stage delay; (4) a wholly new pending-reform paragraph was added to the previously silent Hydrocarbons Code (Law No. 99-93) entry, citing the February 7, 2025 restricted ministerial council examination, a June 26, 2026 Webmanagercenter call for urgent revision amid declining production, and the July 3 IACE classification — a genuine gap 69 prior runs had missed entirely, since this code previously carried no pending-reform note despite the artifact's otherwise thorough energy-law coverage. No new standalone entries added (247 total remains accurate, since all four changes extend existing cards); four existing entries substantively revised for currency; the "Areas for Future Expansion" watchlist refreshed accordingly. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 247 unique law-card ids, balanced <section>/<div> tags, and all 30 TOC anchors resolving. The July 8 (graduate protest), July 11 (ONAT), and July 13 (CNAM-biologists tiers-payant) dates remain the next concrete triggers to check in a future run; none have yet arrived as of this run's cutoff, still July 3, 2026.
v4.70 update (July 3, 2026, Run 71): This run's designated branch again pointed at the stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.2 fork while origin/main had progressed through 70 merged runs to v4.69 (247 entries) — caught before any drafting via git fetch origin main/git log, and the branch reset directly to current origin/main, consistent with the recurring failure mode logged in nearly every changelog entry above. Two independent research agents re-checked all eighteen watchlist items plus a broad sweep for newly-enacted JORT text and several specific "possibly enacted" leads (a crypto/digital-assets law, an AI-specific law, a standalone cybersecurity law distinct from Decree-Law 2023-17, a whistleblower-reward amendment, a PPP amendment, and any electoral-law change since September 2024); none had moved to enactment, and no new JORT publication was found, so no new standalone entries were added (247 total remains accurate). One genuine, two-source-corroborated procedural update was found and closed: ARP President Ibrahim Bouderbala announced July 2, 2026 that the joint standing-committee examination of the 2026–2030 National Development Plan bill (draft law n°42/2026) had concluded, with a consolidated synthesis report now being prepared for the ARP Bureau, which will schedule the plenary session — independently reported by both Le Temps News and African Manager (both July 3, 2026); Bouderbala used the same occasion to publicly urge acceleration of the Foreign Exchange, Water, Hydrocarbons, and Investment codes, corroborating rather than superseding the Run 70 IACE finding. Added to the Development Plan entry. One unconfirmed lead was investigated and deliberately withheld rather than added: a single Businessnews.com.tn report (citing an Express FM interview) has the ARP Security and Defense Commission chair denying, as false, unattributed claims that a bill offering financial rewards to corruption whistleblowers was under committee examination — since this is a single-source denial of an unsourced rumor rather than a corroborated development, it is noted here only as a flag against future runs mistakenly adding a "financial-reward whistleblower bill" entry on the strength of the same rumor, rather than folded into the existing Whistleblower Protections entry. No existing entries were altered beyond the Development Plan card. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 247 unique law-card ids, balanced <section>/<div> tags, and all 30 TOC anchors resolving. The July 8 (graduate protest), July 11 (ONAT), and July 13 (CNAM-biologists tiers-payant) dates, plus the now-pending Development Plan plenary vote, remain the highest-priority triggers for a future run — none had yet arrived as of this run's cutoff, still July 3, 2026.
v4.71 update (July 3, 2026, Run 72): This run's designated branch was again the stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.2 fork while origin/main had progressed through 71 merged runs to v4.70 (247 entries) — caught via git fetch origin main/git log before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main per established practice (the project's own Tavily research tool again returned HTTP 402 "account disabled," so this run relied entirely on the standard WebSearch fallback, as Run 68 also had to). A research sweep re-checked the Development Plan, Foreign Exchange Code, and other near-term watchlist items (no status change beyond what v4.70 already recorded) and searched specifically for AML/CFT and data-protection developments. One genuine, multiply-corroborated finding was closed: a FATF technical mission conducted with the Central Bank of Tunisia and CTAF concluded in late May 2026 without a joint declaration, citing "serious reservations" on beneficial-ownership registers, non-financial-profession supervision, UN sanctions implementation, and financial-information access, and warning of a possible return to the FATF grey list ahead of a MENAFATF plenary mutual-evaluation mission expected November 2026 – February 2027 (ilboursa.com); a separate CTAF strategic note put suspicious-transaction reports at 446 (2020) rising to 1,334 (2025), with money laundering now ~96% of cases versus ~1% for terrorism financing (La Presse de Tunisie, July 2, 2026, corroborated by African Manager and Challenges.tn) — both added to the Countering Terrorism and Suppression of Money Laundering (Organic Law No. 2015-26) entry, since they concern that law's implementation rather than an amendment to its text. One lead was investigated and deliberately withheld: several secondary compliance-marketing sources (regulations.ai, dpo-consulting.com) assert the pending Personal Data Protection reform is already in force with a one-year grace period ending "July 11, 2026" — this directly conflicts with this artifact's own primary-sourced tracking (ARP tracker, Espace Manager, Nessma) showing the bill (Proposition n° 95/2025) still under Rights & Freedoms Committee review as of July 1, 2026 with no vote scheduled, and reads as templated country-report content rather than a Tunisia-specific finding; it was not added, and is flagged here so a future run does not mistake it for a corroborated enactment. No new standalone entries added (247 total remains accurate); one existing entry (Countering Terrorism/AML) enriched with implementation-level developments. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 247 unique law-card ids, balanced <section>/<div> tags, and all 30 TOC anchors resolving. The July 8 (graduate protest), July 11 (ONAT), and July 13 (CNAM-biologists tiers-payant) dates, the pending Development Plan plenary vote, and the next MENAFATF evaluation milestones remain the highest-priority triggers for a future run — none had yet arrived as of this run's cutoff, still July 3, 2026.
v4.72 update (July 4, 2026, Run 73): This run's designated branch was again the stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.2 fork while origin/main had progressed through 72 merged runs to v4.71 (247 entries) — caught via git fetch origin main/git log before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main per established practice. A research agent re-checked all eighteen watchlist items plus the three imminent dates flagged by Run 72 (July 8 graduate protest, July 11 ONAT assembly, July 13 CNAM-biologists tiers-payant suspension — still in the future as of this run's July 4 cutoff) and ran a general JORT/structural-gap sweep. No watchlist item crossed into enactment or a plenary vote, and nearly every lead surfaced (the biologists' precise June 30 extraordinary-general-assembly decision, the pharmacists' crisis cell, the Development Plan's committee-conclusion announcement) was already documented in the artifact, in each case with equal or greater precision. One genuine gap was closed: a July 3, 2026 IACE report on stalled economic reforms — already cited in this artifact for the Investment, Water, and Hydrocarbons codes — separately names a fifth pending code, a unified "Code de l'Environnement" of roughly 400 articles consolidating the 1988 Framework Environmental Law with related sectoral texts, as likewise "technically finalized and validated by the Council of Ministers" but "never transmitted to the ARP" (environnement.gov.tn; TAP; IACE report via La Presse de Tunisie and African Manager, all July 3, 2026) — this had been an implicit cross-reference ("alongside the Investment, Water, and Environment codes") without a corresponding standalone description; added as a pending-consolidation paragraph to the Framework Environmental Law (No. 88-91) entry rather than a new card, since it is a wholesale replacement of that existing statute rather than a distinct new legal instrument. One minor precision refinement was also applied: the Law No. 2025-18 (long-term unemployed graduates) entry now cites the July 8 protest's specific affected-population figures (~60,000 over-40 graduates of ~137,000–140,000 unemployed graduates nationally) and its three named demands (webdo.tn, directinfo.webmanagercenter.com, July 1, 2026). No new standalone entries added (247 total remains accurate); two existing entries revised for currency. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 247 unique law-card ids, balanced <section>/<div> tags, and all 30 TOC anchors resolving. The July 8, 11, and 13 dates remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run, alongside whether the IACE-flagged Investment/Water/Environment/Hydrocarbons codes are transmitted to the ARP and whether the Development Plan bill's synthesis report yields a scheduled plenary date.
v4.74 update (July 4, 2026, Run 75): This run's designated branch again pointed at the stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.2 fork (four commits, self-labeled up to "v2.2," including a draft round of edits to the Anas Hmaidi entry and three IACE-sourced pending-code entries) while origin/main had progressed through 74 merged runs to v4.73 (247 entries) — caught via git fetch origin/git log before any PR was opened, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main per established practice. The discarded draft's candidate edits were cross-checked rather than blindly re-applied: the Hmaidi correction (courts "confirmed," not "increased," the underlying one-year term, while separately ordering immediate execution) was found to already be accurately reflected in main's existing entry, and the three IACE-sourced pending-code additions (Investment, Water, Environment codes "validated but never transmitted to the ARP") were found already captured across Runs 70–73 with materially more precision (the actual entry structure attributes the finding to a single cross-cutting IACE report rather than three separate cards, and already reconciles the Investment Code's dual committee/executive tracks — an ambiguity the discarded draft had not identified). A dedicated research agent re-checked all twenty explicitly-tracked watchlist items (including the three imminent dates: July 8 graduate protest, July 11 ONAT assembly, July 13 CNAM-biologists tiers-payant suspension, all still in the future as of this run's cutoff) plus a general JORT sweep for July 3–4, 2026 promulgations and a FATF/MENAFATF timeline check. No watchlist item crossed into enactment, committee vote, or plenary passage, no new JORT-published law was found, and every lead the agent surfaced (CONECT's 41 Foreign Exchange Code amendments, the Tax Adviser Law's June 3/18, 2026 disputed-articles sessions, the construction-regularization bill's June 23 committee approval, the banque postale "temporization") was independently verified via direct grep against the standing entries and found already documented with equal or greater precision. No new standalone entries added (247 total remains accurate); no existing entries required revision. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 247 unique law-card ids, balanced <div> tags, and all TOC anchors resolving. The July 8, 11, and 13 dates remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run — none had yet arrived as of this run's cutoff, still July 4, 2026.
v4.73 update (July 4, 2026, Run 74): This run's designated branch was again the stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.2 fork (four commits, self-labeled up to "v2.2") while origin/main had progressed through 73 merged runs to v4.72 (247 entries) — caught via git fetch origin main/git log before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main per established practice. A dedicated research agent ran a 43-search sweep re-checking all 23 items on the "Areas for Future Expansion" watchlist (including the three imminent dates — July 8 graduate protest, July 11 ONAT assembly, July 13 CNAM-biologists tiers-payant suspension, all still in the future as of this run's cutoff) plus a general JORT sweep and a structural gap scan across consumer protection, public procurement, insurance, capital markets/CMF, arbitration, waqf/habous, and telecom spectrum. No watchlist item crossed into enactment or a plenary vote, and every lead the agent surfaced was already documented in the artifact with equal or greater precision — including cases where the agent's own research returned a less precise date than what is already on record here (its "July 2" Development Plan hearing date is superseded by this document's already-correct June 30 hearing/July 2 conclusion-announcement distinction; its "May 7" Unified Electoral Code committee date is superseded by this document's already-correct May 7 transmittal / May 19 hearing distinction; its flagged "Decree n°2026-63 civil-service salary" item is already covered, with materially more detail, by the existing seven-decree April 2026 wage-package entry). Two leads were investigated and correctly discarded rather than added: a "political parties organic law" attributed to a Minister Saïd Sayoud, which on inspection is an **Algerian** bill (Algerian Interior Minister, President Tebboune) mislabeled "Tunisie" by an aggregator — not a Tunisian development at all; and a purported June 19, 2026 Insurance Code amendment sourced only to a legal-database page's crawl timestamp, with no independent Tunisian source (CGA, JORT, press) corroborating an actual legislative change on that date. No new standalone entries added (247 total remains accurate); no existing entries required revision. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 247 unique law-card ids, balanced <section>/<div> tags (1108/1108), and all 30 TOC anchors resolving. The July 8, 11, and 13 dates remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run, alongside whether the IACE-flagged Investment/Water/Environment/Hydrocarbons codes are transmitted to the ARP and whether the Development Plan bill's synthesis report yields a scheduled plenary date; future runs should also treat any "Tunisia" political-party-financing or Algeria-adjacent legal news with extra scrutiny given this run's cross-country mislabeling discovery.
v4.75 update (July 4, 2026, Run 76): This run's designated branch again pointed at the stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.2 fork (four commits, last touched this project's first day) while origin/main had progressed through 75 merged runs to v4.74 (247 entries) — caught via git fetch origin main/git log before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main per established practice. The project's Tavily research tool again returned HTTP 402 ("account disabled, unpaid balance"), so a dedicated research agent relied on WebSearch alone for an 18-search sweep re-checking all twenty explicitly-tracked watchlist items plus a broad scan for new legal areas (competition, mining, banking, tax, data protection, cybersecurity, procurement, arbitration, maritime/aviation law, and more) and any JORT publication since Run 75's July 3–4, 2026 cutoff. No watchlist item crossed into enactment, committee vote, or plenary passage, and no new JORT-published legislation was found. Nearly every lead the agent surfaced was already documented with equal or greater precision (the Tax Adviser Law's June 3–4, 2026 "final examination session," the Foreign Exchange Code's CONECT 41-amendment detail, the exchange-violations amnesty bill's committee-return status, the CNAM pharmacists' crisis cell and biologists' July 13 suspension, the banque postale "temporization"). One narrow precision gap was closed outside the pending-bill watchlist: the Constitutional Court entry's Organic Law Proposal No. 100/2025 gained a February 18, 2026 committee-hearing citation (La Presse de Tunisie; commission president Faouzi Daâs) and an explicit distinction from an earlier, unrelated 10-signatory proposal abandoned in April 2025 — but a candidate "61 articles" figure surfaced by the agent was independently checked via a direct source fetch and found to conflict with a different, earlier 89-article/5-chapter draft (withdrawn in 2025); with no primary source corroborating an article count for the current 100/2025 text, the figure was deliberately left unstated rather than risk an unverified citation, consistent with this project's established practice (see, e.g., the Data Protection and Drug Law entries' similarly unresolved article-count discrepancies). A second lead — secondary-source claims that the Personal Data Protection reform's enforcement began July 11, 2026 — was re-encountered and again correctly withheld as unreliable marketing content, consistent with the Run 72 finding. No new standalone entries added (247 total remains accurate); one existing entry (Constitutional Court) refined for precision. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 247 unique law-card ids, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, and all 30 TOC anchors resolving. The July 8 (graduate protest), July 11 (ONAT), and July 13 (CNAM-biologists tiers-payant) dates remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run — none had yet arrived as of this run's cutoff, still July 4, 2026.
v4.76 update (July 4, 2026, Run 77): This run's designated branch again held only its own stale line (four commits, last touched this project's first day, self-labeled up to "v2.2") rather than current origin/main — the same recurring divergence documented in every run note above, compounded here by the branch's originating PR (#77) having already been merged in an earlier session, meaning the branch's own commit history predates 32 subsequent merged runs. Per established practice, the branch was reset directly to current origin/main (v4.75, 247 entries, 76 merged runs) before any drafting began. Tavily again returned HTTP 402 ("account disabled"), so this run relied on WebSearch and direct WebFetch source verification for roughly 15 searches: a re-check of all twenty tracked watchlist items (Foreign Exchange Code, 2026–2030 Development Plan, Personal Data Protection/AI bill, Associations Decree reform, Audiovisual Communication bill, Investment/Water/Environment/Hydrocarbons codes, Tax Adviser Organic Law, Unified Electoral Code, gambling reform, CNAM structural reform, Financial Exclusion/banque postale bills, National Council for Tunisians Residing Abroad reform, cinema-industry bill, Sports Structures bill, Penal Code theft-penalty amendment, Drug Law reform, construction-regularization bill, ONAT standoff) found no committee vote, plenary passage, or enactment for any of them, and no new JORT-published legislation for the window since Run 76's July 3–4, 2026 cutoff — consistent with the July 8/11/13 trigger dates still lying in the future as of this run. One genuine, independently corroborated gap was closed outside the pending-bill watchlist: a wholly new standalone International Law & Treaties entry for the organic law ratifying Tunisia's April 7, 2022 AIMEC headquarters agreement with the African Union Commission (Projet de loi organique n° 07/2026), adopted by ARP plenary April 7, 2026 by a 92-3-3 vote — cross-verified across African Manager, WebManagerCenter, Business News, and La Presse de Tunisie, none of which 76 prior runs had surfaced. A second lead was investigated and correctly discarded: WebSearch's synthesized summary for the Constitutional Court's Organic Law Proposal No. 100/2025 asserted a specific "61 articles / four chapters / nine members" structure as if sourced from La Presse de Tunisie's February 18, 2026 article, but a direct WebFetch of that article's actual text found no such figures present anywhere in it — a search-engine-summary hallucination rather than a primary-source fact, distinct from (but a reminder of the same risk class as) the cross-country-mislabeling and database-timestamp false positives flagged in Runs 74 and prior. The article count for Proposal 100/2025 therefore remains correctly unstated, as Run 76 had already concluded. One new standalone entry added (248 total); no existing entries required revision beyond the watchlist refresh below. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 248 unique law-card ids, balanced <section>/<div> tags, and all 30 TOC anchors resolving.
v4.77 update (July 4, 2026, Run 78): This run's designated branch again held only its own stale line (a handful of commits, last touched this project's first day, self-labeled up to "v2.2," plus one further draft commit this session that duplicated already-covered content — the Hmaidi/Satterthwaite update, the Exchange Code's ~90%-hearings detail, the e-commerce bill's committee status, and a proposed "AI Regulation" entry — all of which were cross-checked against current origin/main and found already present, in each case with equal or greater precision, before that duplicate commit was discarded) rather than current origin/main (v4.76, 248 entries, 77 merged runs) — the same recurring divergence documented in every run note above. Per established practice, the branch was reset directly to current origin/main before any further drafting. A dedicated research agent re-checked all twenty-two tracked watchlist items (Foreign Exchange Code, 2026–2030 Development Plan, Personal Data Protection/AI bill, Associations Decree reform, Audiovisual Communication bill, Investment/Water/Environment/Hydrocarbons codes, gambling reform, Tax Adviser Organic Law, e-commerce bill 42/2024, CNAM structural reform, Financial Exclusion/banque postale bills, nationality-code revision, National Council for Tunisians Residing Abroad reform, cinema-industry bill, Unified Electoral Code, Sports Structures bill, Penal Code theft-penalty amendment, Drug Law reform, construction-regularization bill, ONAT standoff), attempted a direct JORT fetch for July 3–4, 2026 publications, and ran a broad gap scan across the document's 23 covered legal families; Tavily again returned HTTP 402 ("account disabled"), so WebSearch and direct WebFetch verification were used throughout. No committee vote, plenary passage, enactment, or abandonment was found for any watchlist item, and no new JORT-published legislation was located — consistent with the July 8/11/13 trigger dates still lying in the future as of this run's July 4, 2026 cutoff. One candidate lead (the Development Plan's July 2–3, 2026 committee-stage conclusion, per Tunisie Tribune/African Manager/Le Temps News/Tunisie Numérique) was independently re-verified and found already fully captured, near-verbatim, in the existing entry's Run 71 update. Two further leads were investigated and correctly discarded as unreliable: a "Journal Officiel" hit dated July 3, 2026 turned out to be France's Légifrance JORF, not Tunisia's JORT (a cross-country mislabeling trap, per the class flagged in Run 74); and a compliance-consultancy blog's claim that the Personal Data Protection reform's enforcement began July 11, 2026 was again encountered and again withheld as content-marketing boilerplate, consistent with the Run 72 and Run 76 findings. No new standalone entries added (248 total remains accurate); no existing entries required revision. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 248 unique law-card ids, balanced <section>/<div> tags, and all 30 TOC anchors resolving. The July 8, 11, and 13 dates remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run.
v4.78 update (July 4, 2026, Run 79): Branch was reset directly to current origin/main (v4.77, 248 entries, 78 merged runs) rather than continuing this run's own stale, never-merged fork, per established practice. A dedicated research agent re-checked all watchlist items — Foreign Exchange Code, 2026–2030 Development Plan, Personal Data Protection/AI bill, Associations Decree reform, Audiovisual Communication bill, Investment/Water/Environment/Hydrocarbons codes, gambling reform, Tax Adviser Organic Law, e-commerce bill 42/2024, CNAM structural reform, Financial Exclusion/banque postale bills, stadium-violence bill, nationality-code revision, National Council for Tunisians Residing Abroad reform, cinema-industry bill, Unified Electoral Code, Sports Structures bill, Penal Code theft-penalty amendment, Drug Law reform — and ran a broad sweep for JORT publications dated July 3–4, 2026 or later (Tavily again returned HTTP 402 "account disabled," so WebSearch was used throughout). No committee vote, plenary passage, enactment, or abandonment was found for any watchlist item, and no new JORT-published legislation was located. Three candidate leads were investigated and found already fully captured, in each case with equal or greater precision, in existing entries rather than being genuinely new: CONECT's 41 proposed amendments to the Foreign Exchange Code bill (already in the entry's base text), the pharmacists' "cellule de crise" formed June 30, 2026 over renewed CNAM-rupture risk (already in the CNAM entry's Run 43 update), and confirmation that the private biologists' July 13, 2026 tiers-payant suspension remains on track, unaverted (already in the CNAM entry's Run 46 update). One further lead — a July 3, 2026 National Council of Regions and Districts hearing on the Development Plan bill, distinct from the standing-committee examination Run 71 already documented as concluded — surfaced from a single source and could not be independently cross-verified within this run's research window, so it was deliberately withheld rather than added on that single source, consistent with this project's verification standard; a future run should re-check whether the CNRD has begun its Art. 77 review ahead of the still-unscheduled plenary vote. No new standalone entries added (248 total remains accurate); no existing entries required revision. A structural audit confirmed no regressions. The July 8, 11, and 13 dates remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run, with July 13 (the biologists' suspension) now confirmed proceeding as scheduled.
v4.79 update (July 4, 2026, Run 80): This run's designated branch again held only its own stale, never-merged fork (five commits, last touched this project's first day, self-labeled up to "v2.4," including a fresh draft PR of four candidate additions this session — a standalone Foreign Exchange Code entry, an expanded Exchange Code Reform description, a Development Plan pending-reform card, a Nationality Code reform card, and a "Prison System reform bill n°2026-20" card) rather than current origin/main (v4.78, 248 entries, 79 merged runs) — the same recurring divergence documented in every run note above, this time caught only after a PR (#90) had already been opened against the stale base and rejected by GitHub as unmergeable due to conflicts, rather than before drafting began; the PR was closed unmerged and the branch reset directly to current origin/main. Cross-checking the discarded draft's four candidate additions against current main found three already fully captured with equal or greater precision (the Foreign Exchange Code's operative 1976 statute is already its own entry alongside the Draft 115/2025 reform card; the Development Plan bill's status is already tracked in far more granular detail through Run 71; the Nationality Code reform bill n°17/2026 is already documented with the same bill number, sponsor count, and May 7, 2026 hearing date, plus detail — the "exceptional interest" fast-track for high-skill foreign nationals — the discarded draft had missed). The fourth candidate, a claimed "proposition de loi n°2026-20 amending Law No. 2001-52 on prison organization," was investigated further via a direct WebFetch of its underlying source (tunisienumerique.com) rather than trusted at face value, which revealed a genuine methodological finding worth preserving: the source article is actually about Law No. 92-52 of 1992 (Narcotics), not Law No. 2001-52 (Prisons) — a search-summary error that conflated the two statutes because both are colloquially nicknamed "loi 52," and its actual content (a 17-chapter, 31-article reform easing the mandatory Art. 4 personal-consumption penalty, filed by 15 deputies) matches this document's already-existing Drug Law (No. 92-52) reform bill entry (added Run 45), not a distinct prison-law initiative. This is a new false-positive pattern for future runs to watch for, alongside the cross-country-mislabeling (Run 74), search-engine-summary hallucination (Run 76), and database-timestamp (Run 74) risks already flagged above: a shared colloquial nickname ("loi 52," "loi 115," etc.) between two unrelated statutes can cause a search summary to attribute one bill's content to the wrong law entirely — always verify the law number against a direct quote from a primary or near-primary source before adding or amending an entry on this basis. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required revision (248 total remains accurate); this run's sole contribution is the negative-result verification recorded here. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 248 unique law-card ids, balanced <div>/<section> tags, and all TOC anchors resolving. The July 8, 11, and 13 dates remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run.
v4.80 update (July 4, 2026, Run 81): This run's designated branch had, once again, diverged from origin/main into a stale, never-merged fork (five commits, self-labeled up to "v2.3," last touched July 4 with a PR (#89) merged only into another feature branch rather than main) — caught via git log --oneline --all and git merge-base --is-ancestor checks before any research or drafting began, this time, rather than after a failed PR as in Run 80; the branch was reset directly to current origin/main (v4.79, 248 entries, 80 merged runs). Three parallel research agents then ran: (1) a fresh sweep for new JORT-published legislation and watchlist-item status changes, which found none — all ~23 tracked pending bills remain exactly where Run 80 left them, and the July 8/11/13 trigger dates have not yet arrived (today is July 4); (2) a structural gap scan focused on institutional/regulatory areas distinct from the already-exhausted pending-bill space, which surfaced four genuine, previously-undocumented statutes; (3) an independent verification pass on a Court of Appeal ruling first surfaced by agent (1), which confirmed real, dated, multiply-corroborated judicial developments. All candidate findings were independently fact-checked (each of the four statute citations was confirmed via a primary or near-primary source directly read by a dedicated verification agent, following this project's established practice of never trusting a search-summary snippet alone) before inclusion. Four new standalone entries were added: the National Statistics System (Law No. 99-32 of April 13, 1999), confirmed via the INS's own hosted primary text; the National Civil Protection Office (fire/rescue services, Law No. 93-121 of December 27, 1993), confirmed at medium-high confidence via secondary sourcing since the primary JORT/legislation.tn text was unreachable (HTTP 503); the Commerce de Distribution & Franchise Regulation Law (Law No. 2009-69 of August 12, 2009) — verification corrected a real citation error in the initial research draft, which had attributed the franchise disclosure/nullity provisions to Article 14 when they are actually in Article 15; and the Social and Solidarity Economy Law (Law No. 2020-30 of June 30, 2020), confirmed via a scanned JORT primary text — notable because this artifact's own Run 4 changelog had asserted this law was "already implied elsewhere," but a full-text search confirmed no standalone citation for it had actually ever been added, a rare case of a false negative surviving 77 subsequent runs. Two existing entries were extended with newly-verified, multiply-sourced judicial developments from the night of July 2–3, 2026: the Countering Terrorism and Suppression of Money Laundering (Organic Law 2015-26) entry now documents the Tunis Court of Appeal's confirmation of the "affaire du dégel des avoirs" sentences against Marouane Mabrouk, former PM Youssef Chahed, and six former ministers (6 years each, one acquittal on appeal), plus a separate, definitively-confirmed 14-year sentence against Mabrouk alone for embezzlement/money laundering/abuse of function; and the "State Security Conspiracy" Prosecutions entry now documents that Case 1 defendant Khayam Turki carries two further convictions entirely distinct from his conspiracy sentence — an April 21, 2026 14-year fraud/money-laundering judgment and a July 3, 2026 4-year tax-fraud judgment — each independently corroborated across 2+ Tunisian outlets and explicitly disambiguated from each other and from the conspiracy case to prevent future conflation. Four new standalone entries added (252 total); two existing entries revised for currency. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 252 unique law-card ids, balanced <div>/<section> tags, and all TOC anchors resolving. The July 8, 11, and 13 dates remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run.
v4.82 update (July 4, 2026, Run 83): Branch was reset directly to current origin/main (v4.81, 254 entries, 82 merged runs) rather than continuing this run's own stale, never-merged fork (last touched v2.3), per established practice. (Run 82's own v4.81 update added two entries — FOPROLOS and ONAS — but did not leave a note-box here; see its Future Expansion changelog paragraph for that run's detail.) A dedicated research agent re-checked the full pending-bill watchlist (Foreign Exchange Code, Investment Code, Development Plan, exchange-amnesty bill, banque postale bill, CNAM/biologists' suspension, and others) against a fresh sweep — every re-verified item matched or was less precise than what this document already recorded, so no watchlist status changes resulted. It closed two genuine gaps entirely outside the watchlist instead: a wholly new standalone Household Over-Indebtedness (Surendettement) entry (Financial & Banking Law) documenting the unpromulgated Draft Text No. 113/2020 and IACE's March 2026 reform proposal (debt-to-income ratio caps, per-governorate commissions, a national credit bureau) — a consumer-protection gap never previously identified in 82 prior runs — and a refinement to the existing Urban Planning Code entry distinguishing the already-tracked construction-regularization bill from a separate, broader wholesale revision of the 1994 CATU code itself, which missed a prior minister's 2024 target and is now targeted for 2026 promulgation under the current Minister of Equipment and Housing. Both additions were independently verified against dated primary/secondary sources (IACE via L'Economiste Maghrébin and WebManagerCenter for the first; a direct WebFetch comparison of a stale June 2023 article against a correctly-dated April 2026 one for the second) before inclusion, consistent with this project's verification standard. One new standalone entry added (255 total); one existing entry revised for currency. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 255 unique law-card ids, balanced <div>/<section> tags, and all TOC anchors resolving. The July 8, 11, and 13 dates remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run.
v4.83 update (July 4, 2026, Run 84): Branch was reset directly to current origin/main (v4.82, 255 entries, 83 merged runs) rather than continuing this run's own stale, never-merged fork, per established practice. A dedicated research agent re-checked four of the highest-priority watchlist items (Foreign Exchange Code 115/2025, Investment Code reform, the nationality-code revision bill, and the Constitutional Court organic law 100/2025) and ran a fresh ~15-search sweep for any Tunisian legal news dated after July 4, 2026 — none was found; every watchlist item remains at the committee/hearing stage with no vote, passage, enactment, or abandonment to report. A structural gap scan of twelve further candidate areas (road-traffic-accident compensation, blood transfusion, organ donation, mental health, veterinary pharmacy, seeds/plant varieties, state of emergency, civil defense, halal certification, referendum law, gender parity in elected assemblies, public health emergency powers) found each already governed by an existing standalone entry or already-covered broader code (or, for halal certification and referendum procedure, correctly not a standalone-statute matter at all). One lead was investigated and confirmed, via a dedicated second-pass verification agent, to be a recurrence of the Run 74 cross-country-mislabeling trap: a "Tunisian political party financing reform bill (Feb 24, 2026)" surfaced by aggregator search results is in fact Algeria's own political-parties organic law bill, presented by Algerian Interior Minister Saïd Sayoud to Algeria's National People's Assembly — confirmed via multiple .dz sources (APS, La Nouvelle République, CIP) naming Sayoud and the APN, with zero .tn sources, ARP bill numbers, or named Tunisian sponsors found. This is the same false-positive pattern first flagged in Run 74, now confirmed to recur across independent research passes; future runs encountering any "Tunisian party financing bill" lead tied to a "Sayoud" or "APN" reference should immediately recognize it as this recurring Algeria/Tunisia mislabeling rather than re-investigating from scratch. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required revision (255 total remains accurate); this run's contribution is the negative-result reconfirmation and the newly-corroborated false-positive pattern recorded here. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 255 unique law-card ids, balanced <div>/<section> tags, and all TOC anchors resolving. The July 8, 11, and 13 dates remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run.
v4.84 update (July 4, 2026, Run 85): Branch was reset directly to current origin/main (v4.83, 255 entries, 84 merged runs) rather than continuing this run's own stale, never-merged fork, per established practice. A dedicated research agent re-verified the Foreign Exchange Code, exchange-amnesty bill, and Development Plan bill watchlist entries and found each already captured at equal or greater precision than fresh search results — no watchlist status changes to report. It closed one genuine gap entirely outside the pending-bill watchlist and outside the existing "State Security Conspiracy" prosecutions cluster: a wholly new standalone entry for the Habib Haouas/SOREAL organized-money-laundering conviction (22 years, June 29, 2026, Tunis Court of First Instance financial-corruption chamber) — an ordinary financial-crime case, multiply corroborated across Businessnews, Kapitalis, La Presse de Tunisie, and L'Economiste Maghrébin, that none of the prior 84 runs had captured despite already documenting the contemporaneous Mabrouk/Chahed/Turki rulings from the same week. One new standalone entry added (256 total); no existing entries required revision. (This note-box was omitted when Run 85 was originally published and is added retroactively by Run 86 for changelog continuity; the entry itself and the version/footer strings were already correctly updated at the time.)
v4.86 update (July 4, 2026, Run 86): This branch's designated fork (last touched at v2.3, four commits, never merged) was again far behind origin/main — caught via git fetch origin main/git log before any drafting began, confirmed via the GitHub API that no PR existed for this branch, and reset directly to current origin/main (v4.84, 256 entries, 85 merged runs) per established practice. A dedicated research agent re-checked all 23 "Areas for Future Expansion" watchlist items (WebSearch only — Tavily again returned HTTP 402 "account disabled," consistent with Runs 68, 71, and 76) and ran a targeted structural-gap check on cryptocurrency/digital-assets and renewable-energy regulation. No watchlist item crossed into enactment, a plenary vote, or a confirmed committee-report outcome as of this run's July 4, 2026 cutoff (the same calendar date as Run 85's, so no new dated-development window had opened; the July 8, 11, and 13 trigger dates remain in the future). Several leads the agent surfaced were independently cross-checked and found already captured at equal or greater precision — the Investment Code's article-by-article-voting-vs-"never transmitted to ARP" ambiguity is already explicitly flagged as an unreconciled two-track question in that entry's Run 70 update; the construction-regularization bill's June 23, 2026 committee approval of Arts. 1–2 is already documented in the Urban Planning Code entry's Run 45 update; and the recurring "Personal Data Protection bill has 123 articles and took effect July 11, 2026" claim (again traced to regulations.ai-style aggregator content) was again correctly withheld as unreliable marketing boilerplate, consistent with the Run 72 and Run 76 findings. The structural-gap check confirmed cryptocurrency (BCT Circular 2018-02 ban, folded into the pending Foreign Exchange Code reform) and renewable energy (Law No. 2015-12, as amended, with its May 2026 CNRD photovoltaic-concession update) are both already thoroughly covered by existing entries — no gap found. One documentation-only gap was closed: Run 85's own changelog note-box had been omitted from this section despite its entry and version strings being correctly published; it is now added retroactively above. No new standalone entries were added this run (256 total remains accurate); no existing entries required substantive revision. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 256 unique law-card ids, balanced <div>/<section> tags (1154/1154), and all 30 TOC anchors resolving. The July 8, 11, and 13 dates remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run.
v4.87 update (July 4, 2026, Run 87): This run repeated the now-familiar failure mode documented in every prior run's notes (16, 18, 23, 26, 27, 28, 31, 32, 33, 57, 62, 80, 83, 84, 85, 86): its designated branch's local checkout was a stale, never-merged fork (a self-contained "v2.4," 152 entries, built without first checking origin/main) and had already been drafted into a full PR (#98, adding a labor-reform entry, an AI-strategy entry, a stadium-violence-bill entry, and refinements to the Exchange Code and Finance Law 2026 entries) before the mismatch was caught — via a 405 "merge conflicts" error on the merge attempt rather than before opening the PR, the same late-catch pattern as Run 80. PR #98 was closed unmerged (to avoid reverting ~100 entries' worth of accumulated work) and the branch was reset to current origin/main (v4.86, 256 entries, 86 merged runs). Cross-checking the four discarded draft candidates against the real current artifact found all four already present with equal or greater precision: the May 2025 anti-subcontracting labor law as the existing, more-detailed Law No. 2025-9 entry; the AI-strategy note already folded into the Personal Data Protection entry since Run 11 (deliberately not a standalone entry, since no AI-specific law or decree number exists to cite); the stadium-violence bill as the existing Proposition de loi n° 83/2025 entry; and the Exchange Code/e-invoicing refinements as already more precisely documented (committee at ~90% of hearings per a June 17, 2026 report by commission chair Maher Ktari, and the Finance Law 2026 entry's existing Art. 56/El Fatoora detail) — all four discarded rather than re-added, consistent with established practice. A fresh research sweep for judicial/court developments dated after the Run 86 cutoff found one genuine gap: a wholly new standalone entry for the Affaire Makram Jlassi embezzlement conviction — a former Justice Ministry cabinet official whose 5-year first-instance sentence (March 11, 2026) was increased to 8 years on appeal on July 4, 2026, the same day as this run, corroborated same-day across WebManagerCenter/Directinfo, webdo, African Manager, Réalités, and Kapitalis. One new standalone entry added (257 total); no existing entries required revision. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 257 unique law-card ids, balanced <div>/<section> tags, and all TOC anchors resolving. The July 8, 11, and 13 dates remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run.
v4.88 update (July 4, 2026, Run 88): This run repeated the exact failure mode documented in every run note above (16, 18, 23, 26, 27, 28, 31, 32, 33, 57, 62, 80, 83, 84, 85, 86, 87): its designated branch was a stale, never-merged fork (self-labeled "v2.4," five commits, last touched this project's first day) that had already been drafted into a full PR (#100, adding the same labor-reform entry, Exchange Code refinement, Finance Law 2026 e-invoicing detail, and Decree-54 sentencing-date additions as the discarded Run 87 draft) before the mismatch was caught — again via a 405 "merge conflicts" error on the merge attempt rather than before opening the PR. PR #100 was closed unmerged (to avoid reverting 87 runs' worth of accumulated work) and the branch was reset to current origin/main (v4.87, 257 entries, 87 merged runs). Cross-checking the four discarded draft candidates against the real current artifact found all four already present with equal or greater precision, exactly as Run 87 found for its own near-identical discarded draft: the May 2025 anti-subcontracting labor law as the existing Law No. 2025-9 entry; the Exchange Code's 91-article scope and CONECT hearing already captured in the standalone Foreign Exchange Code entry and the Future Expansion watchlist; the Finance Law 2026 e-invoicing/El Fatoora detail already in that entry's existing text; and the Dahmani/Ben Khelifa sentencing dates already reflected in the Decree-Law 2022-54 entry's press-prosecution list. A fresh research sweep (general Tunisia news, ARP/Exchange Code/Development Plan status, and court-ruling searches, all dated July 4, 2026) found no new JORT-published legislation and no watchlist item crossing into enactment, plenary vote, or committee-report conclusion — every judicial development surfaced (Mabrouk 14-year money-laundering sentence, Chahed/co-defendants' 6-year sentences, Turki's 4-year tax case, Smara's reduction to ~9 years, Jlassi's increase to 8 years on appeal) matched the existing entries' detail exactly. One unconfirmed lead — a July 4, 2026 one-day bank-sector strike ("grève bancaire," reported with "inaction de la tutelle" but no accompanying legislative or regulatory text) — was deliberately not added as a standalone entry, consistent with this project's scope (enacted/pending legal instruments and their judicial application, not labor-action news without a legal-text hook). No new standalone entries were added (257 total remains accurate); no existing entries required revision. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 257 unique law-card ids, 1161/1161 balanced <div> tags, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, and all 30 TOC anchors resolving. The July 8, 11, and 13 dates remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run.
v4.89 update (July 4, 2026, Run 89): This run repeated the same failure mode documented in every run note above (16, 18, 23, 26, 27, 28, 31, 32, 33, 57, 62, 80, 83, 84, 85, 86, 87, 88): its designated branch's local checkout was a stale, never-merged fork (self-labeled "v2.3," five commits, 1,921 lines, 149 entries) that had diverged from origin/main almost immediately after the repository's first commit, while main had independently progressed through 88 merged runs to v4.88 (257 entries). This time the mismatch was caught before any drafting began, via git fetch origin main and git log --all, rather than after a failed PR — the branch was reset directly to current origin/main (v4.88, 257 entries, 88 merged runs). A dedicated research agent then ran a fresh WebSearch sweep against the ~9 actively tracked pending-bill watchlist items (Foreign Exchange Code reform, Investment Code revision, National Development Plan 2026-2030, exchange-amnesty bill, banque postale bill, CNAM/biologists' tiers-payant dispute, Personal Data Protection bill, construction-regularization/Urban Planning Code bill, stadium-violence bill) plus a general check for new JORT publications and court rulings dated on or after July 4, 2026. No watchlist item crossed into enactment, a plenary vote, or a concluded committee report — all remain exactly where Run 88 left them. The one item the agent surfaced as potentially new — the July 3, 2026 Tunis Court of Appeal confirmation of the Mabrouk/Chahed asset-unfreezing appeal (6-year sentences upheld, Mehdi Ben Gharbia acquitted) and the separate Mabrouk money-laundering case (14 years confirmed "définitivement"), plus Khayam Turki's July 3, 2026 4-year tax-case sentence — was cross-checked against the existing document and found already fully captured in the Countering Terrorism & Money Laundering entry's Run 81 update (Criminal Law section) and the "State Security Conspiracy" Prosecutions entry's Run 81 update, respectively. No new standalone entries were added this run (257 total remains accurate); no existing entries required revision. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 257 unique law-card ids, 1162/1162 balanced <div> tags, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, and all 30 TOC anchors resolving. The July 8, 11, and 13 dates remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run.
v4.90 update (July 4, 2026, Run 90): This run again hit the same recurring failure mode (see runs 16, 18, 23, 26–28, 31–33, 57, 62, 80, 83–89): its designated branch's local checkout was the identical stale, never-merged "v2.3" fork (five commits, 149 entries) described in the Run 89 note above, unchanged since it was last reset — confirming that branch's staleness is a fixed characteristic of this session slot rather than something a prior run's reset fixes going forward. Before any drafting, two research passes ran in parallel against the stale draft: a recent-news sweep (which surfaced the July 3, 2026 Makram Jlassi embezzlement appeal, since found already captured on main as the Run 87 addition) and a structural gap scan across regulated professions and consumer-facing statutes not obviously covered by name in prior changelogs. After discovering the branch mismatch via git fetch origin main, the branch was reset to current origin/main (v4.89, 257 entries, 89 merged runs) before any further edits, and every gap-scan candidate was individually cross-checked against the real current artifact rather than re-added blindly: food safety (already the more detailed Law No. 2009-11 entry), medical professions (already the existing Law No. 91-21 entry), veterinary practice (already folded into the Plant Health/Veterinary Law entry, Law No. 2005-35), and reproductive medicine (already superseded by the broader 2015 Bioethics Law) were all found already covered, in each case at equal or greater precision, and were correctly not re-added. Five candidates survived cross-checking as genuinely absent from the entire 257-entry corpus and were independently re-verified via fresh, source-cited WebSearch queries before inclusion: the Anti-Smoking Law (No. 98-17 of 1998, Health), the Real Estate Agent Profession Law (No. 81-55 of 1981, Land), the Architects Profession Law (No. 74-46 of 1974, Land), the Engineers Profession Law (No. 82-85 of 1982, as amended 1997, Administrative), and the National Broadcasting Establishment (ERTT) Law (No. 90-49 of 1990, Press) — the last distinct from the HAICA regulator statute, which governs licensing/content oversight rather than the public broadcaster's own corporate status. Five new standalone entries added (262 total); no existing entries required revision. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 262 unique law-card ids, balanced <div> tags, and all TOC anchors resolving. The July 8, 11, and 13 dates remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run.
v4.91 update (July 4, 2026, Run 91): This run's designated branch held only a stale, never-merged "v2.3" fork (five commits, 149 entries) that had diverged from origin/main immediately after the repository's first commit, while main had independently progressed through 90 merged runs to v4.90 (262 entries) — the same recurring failure mode documented in every prior run note above. The mismatch was caught before any drafting began, via git fetch origin main and a divergence check, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main (v4.90, 262 entries, 90 merged runs). Tavily search again returned HTTP 402 "account disabled" (consistent with Runs 68, 71, 76, 86), so this run relied on WebSearch alone. A fresh sweep re-verified the Foreign Exchange Code, Development Plan bill, Personal Data Protection organic bill, and Draft Audiovisual Communication/HAICA bill — all four remain exactly at committee/hearing stage with no plenary vote, consistent with the existing entries — and found no new JORT-published legislation. Two genuine judicial-development gaps, entirely outside the pending-bill watchlist, were closed: (1) the Ahmed Souab case — a lawyer, former administrative judge, and human rights defender arrested April 21, 2025 over statements made after representing "state security conspiracy" defendants, convicted to 5 years at first instance in a trial Amnesty International said lasted seven minutes, with his sentence reduced to 10 months plus a 2-year suspended term and his immediate release ordered on appeal, February 23, 2026 — added as an update to the Judicial Statute entry alongside the existing Hmaidi case, both illustrating the same pattern of prosecutions against the legal profession; (2) a wholly new standalone entry for the "Sumood/Global Sumud Flotilla" money-laundering and terrorism-financing prosecutions — at least seven pro-Palestinian activists arrested March 6–7, 2026 and remanded March 16, 2026 under Organic Law No. 2015-26 (Counter-Terrorism/AML) and general money-laundering provisions over funds raised for a September 2025 Gaza-aid flotilla mission, with no trial date or verdict reported as of this run — added to the Security & Counter-terrorism section. One new standalone entry added (263 total); one existing entry (Judicial Statute) revised for currency. The July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 dates (graduate-unemployment protest, ONAT general assembly, and biologists' CNAM tiers-payant suspension respectively) still fall after this run's July 4, 2026 research cutoff and remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run.
v4.92 update (July 4, 2026, Run 92): This run's designated branch was again only a stale, never-merged "v2.3" fork (five commits, self-labeled up to v2.3) that had diverged from origin/main long ago, while main had independently progressed through 91 merged runs to v4.91 (263 entries) — the same recurring failure mode documented in every prior run note above. The mismatch was caught before any drafting began via git fetch origin main and git merge-base --is-ancestor, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main (v4.91, 263 entries, 91 merged runs). A dedicated research agent re-checked all 21 tracked pending-bill watchlist items (Foreign Exchange Code, Development Plan, Personal Data Protection/AI bill, Associations Decree reform, Audiovisual Communication/HAICA bill, Investment Code, Water Code, Hydrocarbons Code, gambling reform, Tax Adviser Organic Law, Prop. 42/2024, CNAM/biologists' suspension, Financial Exclusion/banque postale bills, nationality-code revision, CNRTE reform, cinema-industry bill, Unified Electoral Code, Sports Structures organic law, Penal Code theft amendment, Drug Law reform, and the construction-regularization bill) against a fresh WebSearch sweep — none had crossed into enactment, a plenary vote, or a confirmed committee-report outcome since Run 91's same-day cutoff, and the July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 trigger dates had still not arrived. Two candidate leads the research agent initially flagged as "new" — the July 3, 2026 Tunis Court of Appeal confirmation of the Mabrouk/Chahed asset-unfreezing rulings and Khayam Turki's July 3, 2026 tax-fraud sentence — were cross-checked against the existing document and found already fully captured, at equal precision, in the Countering Terrorism & Money Laundering entry's and the "State Security Conspiracy" Prosecutions entry's Run 81 updates respectively, and were correctly not re-added. One genuine gap was closed: a wholly new standalone entry for the Affaire Taoufik Mekacher conviction — a businessman sentenced July 3, 2026 to 6 years by the same specialized financial-corruption chamber of the Tunis Court of First Instance that produced the Mabrouk/Turki and Haouas/SOREAL rulings, for obtaining substantial public-bank loans without sufficient guarantees, part of a broader 31-suspect "affaire des prêts bancaires sans garantie" that had never previously been documented in this artifact despite 91 prior runs — independently corroborated across La Presse de Tunisie, Businessnews, and allAfrica, all dated July 3, 2026. One new standalone entry added (264 total); no existing entries required revision. The July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 dates remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run.
v4.93 update (July 4, 2026, Run 93): This run's designated branch again held no PR history of its own and its local checkout was several runs stale (a "v2.3" line last touched at the project's outset) while main had independently progressed through 92 merged runs to v4.92 (264 entries) — confirmed via git log origin/main and an empty list_pull_requests result for the branch before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main. A fresh WebSearch sweep (Tavily remained HTTP 402 "account disabled," consistent with every run since it first failed) re-checked the Foreign Exchange Code, the Development Plan bill, the Constitutional Court organic law, and the nationality-code revision bill against current French- and English-language sources: none had crossed into committee-report conclusion, plenary vote, or enactment since Run 92's same-day cutoff, and the July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 trigger dates had still not arrived. A targeted check on the three July 3, 2026 financial-corruption-chamber convictions (Mabrouk, Turki, Mekacher) confirmed each already fully captured in the Countering Terrorism & Money Laundering, "State Security Conspiracy" Prosecutions, and standalone Mekacher entries respectively, at equal or greater precision than fresh search results — none were re-added. One further lead — an escalating banking- and insurance-sector strike (a June 23–25, 2026 walkout followed by continued "brassard rouge" protests and a threatened further general strike as of July 1–4, 2026, centered on a 5% multi-year wage demand and enforcement of Commercial Code Art. 412's interest-rate-reduction provision for employee loans) — was investigated and deliberately not added as a standalone entry, consistent with the Run 88 precedent for the same dispute: it remains labor-action news without a distinct legislative or regulatory text to anchor it, rather than a gap in legal-instrument coverage. No new standalone entries were added (264 total remains accurate); no existing entries required revision. The July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 dates remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run.
v4.94 update (July 4, 2026, Run 94): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-0oqc1o) had no PR history of its own and was stuck at a "v2.3"-line commit dated to the project's early history, while origin/main had independently progressed through 93 merged runs to v4.93 (264 entries) — the same recurring stale-checkout failure mode documented in every prior run note; confirmed via git log/git fetch and an empty list_pull_requests result for the branch, and reset directly to current origin/main before any drafting began. A WebSearch sweep re-checked the Foreign Exchange Code, Development Plan bill, Investment Code, and Constitutional Court organic law: none had crossed into committee-report conclusion, plenary vote, or enactment since Run 93's same-day cutoff, and the July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 trigger dates had still not arrived. This run made two substantive contributions. First, a genuine internal-quality gap: the Environmental Law section's "Energy Transition and Green Economy" card (id="climate") and the Energy section's "Renewable Energy Law" card (id="renewables-law") were found to be near-duplicate entries for the identical statute, Law No. 2015-12 of May 11, 2015 — an accumulated redundancy from early runs that 93 subsequent research-focused passes never cross-checked against each other. The thinner, less-precise Environmental Law card was removed and its content folded into the more detailed Energy-section entry (263 total, a reduction of one). Second, a genuine legislative gap closed: a wholly new "Code des Énergies Renouvelables" (Renewable Energy Code) consolidation project — ordered by a December 24, 2024 Council of Ministers meeting, transmitted to the ARP's competent committee April 1, 2026 per deputy Yosri Baoueb, containing a contested 51%-Tunisian-ownership rule for renewable-energy projects, and now named by a July 3, 2026 IACE report as one of three reforms (alongside the Foreign Exchange and Investment codes) it urges enacting by end of 2026 — had never been documented despite Run 86's dedicated but incomplete renewable-energy structural-gap check. Rather than a new standalone card, this was added as a pending-consolidation note to the merged renewables-law entry, consistent with how the Water/Environment/Hydrocarbons codes' pending-reform notes were attached to their base-law entries in Runs 70/73. Net change: one entry removed as a duplicate, one entry substantively expanded; 263 total. The July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 dates remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run.
v4.95 update (July 5, 2026, Run 95): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-uditm9) again held only a stale, never-merged fork (a "v2.4" line, five commits, 1,943 lines, 152 entries) that had diverged from origin/main at the project's outset, while main had independently progressed through 94 merged runs to v4.94 (264 entries) — the same recurring stale-checkout failure mode documented in every prior run note; confirmed via git fetch origin main, git merge-base, and an empty list_pull_requests result for the branch before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main. A dedicated research agent re-checked all twenty-five tracked watchlist items (Foreign Exchange Code, exchange-amnesty bill, Development Plan, Personal Data Protection/AI bill, Associations Decree reform, Audiovisual Communication/HAICA bill, Investment Code, Water Code, Hydrocarbons Code, gambling reform, Tax Adviser Organic Law, Prop. 42/2024, CNAM/biologists' tiers-payant suspension, Financial Exclusion/banque postale bills, nationality-code revision, CNTE reform, cinema-industry bill, Unified Electoral Code, Sports Structures bill, Penal Code theft amendment, Drug Law reform, construction-regularization bill, ONAT standoff, Code des Énergies Renouvelables, and the graduates' protest) against a fresh WebSearch sweep, corroborated by this run's own independent JORT/ARP searches: none had crossed into committee-report conclusion, plenary vote, enactment, or abandonment since Run 94's same-day cutoff, and confirmation was obtained that the private biologists' union has reached no pharmacist-style eleventh-hour deal with CNAM — the July 13 suspension remains on track. Three candidate leads the research agent surfaced as dated July 2–3, 2026 (the Tunis Court of Appeal's "définitif" confirmation of Marouane Mabrouk's 14-year money-laundering sentence and the separate Mabrouk/Chahed asset-unfreezing appeal, Khayam Turki's 4-year tax-fraud sentence, and Taoufik Mekacher's 6-year bank-loans sentence) were each cross-checked and found already fully captured at equal precision in the Countering Terrorism & Money Laundering entry's and "State Security Conspiracy" Prosecutions entry's Run 81 updates and the standalone Mekacher entry's Run 92 update — none were re-added. A single-source rumor of an ARP "whistleblower reward" bill, already investigated and denied by the Security Commission per the Run 71 note, was re-encountered and again correctly withheld. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required substantive revision. A structural audit (unique law-card ids, balanced <div>/<section> tags, header stat vs. actual card count) caught one further pre-existing discrepancy: Run 94's merge of the duplicate renewable-energy entries had correctly reduced the real card count to 263 but left the header's "Laws Documented" stat at the pre-merge value of 264 — corrected here to 263, the accurate total. The July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 dates still had not arrived as of this run's cutoff and remain the highest-priority triggers for the next run. v4.96 update (July 5, 2026, Run 96): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-olhu5g) held only an early, never-merged fork (six commits, last at v2.4, 3,437 lines) that had diverged from origin/main near the project's outset, while main had independently progressed through 95 merged runs to v4.95 (263 entries) — the same recurring stale-checkout failure mode documented throughout this changelog; confirmed via git fetch origin main, git log, and an empty list_pull_requests result for the branch before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main. A dedicated research agent re-checked all nineteen highest-priority watchlist items (Foreign Exchange Code, exchange-amnesty bill, Development Plan 2026-2030, Personal Data Protection/AI bill, Associations Decree reform, Audiovisual Communication/HAICA bill, Investment Code, Water Code, Hydrocarbons Code, gambling/betting reform, Tax Adviser Organic Law, Constitutional Court organic law, nationality-code revision, Sports Structures organic law, Penal Code theft amendment, Drug Law reform, construction-regularization bill, Code des Énergies Renouvelables, and the Unified Electoral Code) plus a fresh JORT sweep for the July 1–5, 2026 window: no item crossed into enactment, a plenary vote, or a confirmed committee-report conclusion, and no new JORT-published text was found. One item — the construction-regularization bill — was flagged by the research agent as the closest to a threshold (its June 23, 2026 committee approval was reported as "headed to plenary"), so a second, dedicated verification pass specifically searched for evidence of an actual plenary vote between June 24 and July 5, 2026; none was found across La Presse de Tunisie, allAfrica, and African Manager, which instead confirm the bill still awaits a committee report before reaching the floor. This re-confirmation was added to the Urban Planning Code entry rather than treated as a new development. The three trigger dates (July 8 graduate-unemployment protest, July 11 ONAT general assembly, July 13 CNAM/biologists' tiers-payant suspension) had still not arrived as of this run's cutoff; pre-event reporting for the July 8 and July 13 dates was independently re-confirmed as unchanged, while the July 11 ONAT item could not be freshly corroborated via this run's searches (existing sourcing was left unchanged pending a more targeted recheck).
