v4.151 update (July 7, 2026, Run 151): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-z24qqq) again held only a stale, never-merged baseline (topping out at v2.6, 162 entries, PRs #77–#158) while origin/main had independently progressed through 150 merged runs to v4.150 (274 entries) — confirmed via git log/git merge-base --is-ancestor against origin/main before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly to origin/main (git checkout -B) per established practice. A dedicated research agent ran a fresh WebSearch sweep (the project's Tavily tool was not attempted this run given its documented HTTP 402 "account disabled" status since Run 68) prioritizing the same-day watchlist: it found no reported outcome of the July 6, 2026 Foreign Exchange Code committee session (still unreported, a twenty-seventh consecutive trigger-date pass), no new JORT-published law or decree for July 2026, and re-confirmed the Development Plan bill's joint-committee-review stage, the Investment Code's unresolved article-by-article-voting-vs.-"validated but never transmitted" discrepancy (flagged since Run 70, still unreconciled), and the CNAM/biologists' (July 13) and ONAT (July 11) trigger dates as still on track but not yet arrived. Two candidate leads were independently cross-checked against the live artifact and found already fully captured rather than genuine gaps: the July 8, 2026 graduate-recruitment protest demanding implementing decrees for Law No. 2025-18 (see the existing Labor Law entry, added Run 64), and Judge Anas Hmaidi's July 1, 2026 appellate sentence confirmation with immediate execution (see the existing State Security Conspiracy Prosecutions entry, Run 42 update). No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required correction — this run confirms the artifact remains current as of July 7, 2026 (274 total). The July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 trigger dates remain the highest-priority items for the next run to check the moment each passes.
v4.152 update (July 7, 2026, Run 152): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-drnhub) again held only a stale, never-merged v2.0–v2.6-era fork (eight commits, 162 entries, PRs #77–#158) while origin/main had independently progressed through 151 merged runs to v4.151 (274 entries) — confirmed via git fetch origin main and git log --oneline origin/main before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main (git checkout -B) per established practice. Tavily search again returned HTTP 402 "account disabled" on the first call attempted, consistent with every run since roughly Run 68/131; this run relied on WebSearch/WebFetch instead. A structural audit at the start of this run found no regressions to fix: 274 unique law-card ids, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 1287/1287 balanced <div> tags, 274/274 balanced <h3> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) already synchronized at v4.151/Run 151. A same-day watchlist re-check (roughly one hour after Run 151's own cutoff) found no movement on any tracked item: the Foreign Exchange Code (Draft Law 115/2025) committee outcome remains unreported a twenty-eighth consecutive trigger-date pass, with Ktari's June 17–23, 2026 ~90%-hearings-complete/BCT-non-response status still the last hard data point (independently re-confirmed via fresh French-language searches and a direct WebFetch of a June 11, 2026 Tunisie Numérique article on the committee's process, which predates and is superseded by the artifact's existing June 17/23 citations); no JORT publication dated July 7, 2026 was found; and the July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 trigger dates had each still not arrived as of this run's cutoff. A fresh sweep of recent Tunisian court rulings, run specifically to catch judicial developments outside the pending-bill watchlist (the category that has most reliably yielded genuine gaps in recent runs), surfaced one new item: the Tunis Court of Appeal's July 6, 2026 confirmation of a 55-year sentence against jihadist leader Bilel Chaouachi (alias Abou Yahya Zakaria, Ansar al-Charia/Daech leadership, tied to the November 24, 2015 presidential-security-bus bombing) — independently corroborated across Kapitalis (both its July 6, 2026 report and a December 11, 2025 first-instance report), Directinfo/WebManagerCenter, and webdo before being added as a new standalone entry in the Security & Counter-terrorism section, distinct from the state-security-conspiracy, Secret Apparatus, and Instalingo cases already documented under the same Anti-Terrorism Law. One new standalone entry added (275 total, up from 274); no existing entry required revision. A structural audit after editing confirmed no regressions: 275 unique law-card ids, balanced <section>/<div>/<h3> tags, and all three version markers synchronized at v4.152/Run 152. The July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 dates remain the highest-priority items for the next run to check the moment each passes.
v4.153 update (July 7, 2026, Run 153): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-s1lh1h) again held only a stale, never-merged v2.0–v2.6-era baseline (162 entries, PRs #77–#158) while origin/main had independently progressed through 152 merged runs to v4.152 (275 entries) — confirmed via git fetch origin main and git log origin/main before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main per established practice. A fresh watchlist sweep found no movement: the Foreign Exchange Code (Draft Law 115/2025) committee outcome remains unreported a twenty-ninth consecutive trigger-date pass (last hard data point still Ktari's June 17–23, 2026 ~90%-hearings-complete/BCT-non-response status), a direct jort.tn check found no July 2026 law/decree publications, and the July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 trigger dates had each still not arrived as of this run's cutoff. A businessnews.com.tn fact-check re-debunking the "new Road Traffic Code in force July 1, 2026" rumor was re-confirmed already fully captured (Run 136/139/145 updates) and not re-added. This run's genuine contribution came from a fresh sweep of Tunisian court rulings outside the pending-bill watchlist: a July 7, 2026 Human Rights Watch report (independently corroborated across Businessnews, La Presse de Tunisie, Kapitalis, webdo, and LTDH statements, all dated June 23–26, 2026) detailed two clusters of rights-defender sentencing previously captured only in outline. (1) The State Security Conspiracy Prosecutions entry's existing note on Sihem Bensedrine's June 26, 2026 25-year sentence was enriched with the case's actual charges (fraud/forgery/unfair-advantage tied to an IVD arbitral reconciliation with businessman Slim Chiboub, not a state-security offense) and its ~1.776 billion-dinar joint fine, and gained a wholly new co-defendant addition: lawyer and former IVD official Khaled Krichi, arrested June 3 and sentenced the same day (June 26, 2026) to a cumulative 10 years on the same docket — a previously undocumented prosecution despite 152 prior runs' coverage of this cluster. (2) The Racial Discrimination Law entry's existing Saadia Mosbah note was sharpened from a vague "June 2026" to the precise June 23, 2026 appeal date and fine, and gained the same ruling's disposition of five co-defendant Mnemty members (Ghofrane Binous, Zied Rouin, Fares Gueblaoui, and two others — suspended terms and fines) plus three acquittals, none previously documented. A candidate lead from the same search sweep — a "July 8, 2026" mass Ennahda/opposition sentencing (21 of 24 defendants, 12–35 years) — was traced via direct source-date verification to the already-documented July 8, 2025 Case 2 first-instance verdict (a year-dropped search-summary artifact, not a new 2026 event); it is flagged here as a new false-positive trap for future runs, parallel to the Algeria/Sayoud and Morocco/Law-08-12 country-mislabeling traps already on record. No new standalone entries were added (275 total remains accurate, since this run enriches two existing cards rather than extending the count); a structural audit confirmed no regressions: 275 unique law-card ids, balanced <section>/<div>/<h3> tags, and all three version markers synchronized at v4.153/Run 153. The July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 dates remain the highest-priority items for the next run to check the moment each passes.
v4.154 update (July 7, 2026, Run 154): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-515u4e) again reproduced the recurring stale-checkout failure mode documented throughout this changelog: it initially held eight commits topping out at the same v2.0–v2.6-era baseline (162 entries, PRs #77–#158) and a first drafting pass was mistakenly started against that stale fork (adding a biometric-national-ID pending-reform entry and a v2.7 changelog note) before git fetch origin main and git log --oneline origin/main revealed origin/main had independently progressed through 153 merged runs to v4.153 (275 entries); the stale draft was discarded and the branch reset directly to current origin/main (git checkout -B) per established practice before any of that work was carried forward. A dedicated research agent then ran a fresh, narrowly-targeted sweep (rather than repeating the full same-day pending-bill re-check for the twenty-ninth-plus time): it searched specifically for a post-July-6, 2026 outcome of the Foreign Exchange Code (Draft Law 115/2025) committee session — none found; the only July 6, 2026 hit was a businessnews.com.tn piece quoting deputy Issam Chouchane urging faster passage of the exchange, investment, and water codes during Development Plan debate, not a reported committee vote — so the outcome remains unreported a thirtieth consecutive trigger-date pass, with Ktari's June 17–23, 2026 ~90%-hearings-complete/BCT-non-response status still the last hard data point. A direct jort.tn check again found no substantive July 2026 law/decree text, only routine "Annonces Légales" issue numbers 077–079. A broad sweep for new July 2026 JORT publications, ARP votes, or décrets-lois surfaced nothing beyond items already documented. A targeted re-check of whether the July 7, 2026 Human Rights Watch report added in Run 153 was itself superseded by anything newer found no distinct later ruling — the same HRW piece (Bensedrine/Mosbah/Krichi) remains the most recent court-ruling development. The July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 trigger dates had each still not arrived as of this run's cutoff. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required correction — this run confirms the artifact remains current as of July 7, 2026 (275 total). A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 275 unique law-card ids, balanced <section>/<div>/<h3> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.154/Run 154. The July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 dates remain the highest-priority items for the next run to check the moment each passes.
v4.155 update (July 7, 2026, Run 155): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-3ni50p) again held only a stale, never-merged v2.0–v2.6-era baseline (eight commits, 162 entries, PRs #77–#158) with no PR ever opened for it, while origin/main had independently progressed through 154 merged runs to v4.154 (275 entries) — confirmed via git fetch origin main, git log --oneline origin/main, and an empty list_pull_requests result for the branch before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main (git checkout -B) per established practice. A dedicated research agent ran a fresh sweep (Tavily still HTTP 402 "account disabled," per every run since roughly Run 68; WebSearch/WebFetch used instead) targeting court rulings and legislative action from the last 24–48 hours rather than re-running the full same-day pending-bill re-check for the thirty-first-plus time. The Foreign Exchange Code (Draft Law 115/2025) committee outcome remains unreported a thirty-first consecutive trigger-date pass, with Ktari's June 17–23, 2026 ~90%-hearings-complete/BCT-non-response status still the last hard data point; no July 2026 JORT law/decree publication was found beyond routine "Annonces Légales" notices; and the July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 trigger dates had each still not arrived as of this run's cutoff. Two genuine developments were found and independently corroborated via direct WebFetch of primary sources before being added. (1) The State Security Conspiracy Prosecutions entry's Run 60 note on the then-pending Ajmi Lourimi/Mossaâb Gharbi terrorism-chamber case was updated to reflect its verdict: the Tunis Court of First Instance's specialized terrorism chamber sentenced both to 3 years' imprisonment on July 4, 2026 (Lourimi for failing to report a terrorism-related offense, Gharbi for harboring/concealing a person linked to terrorism offenses), corroborated across Businessnews, webdo.tn, Mosaïque FM, Réalités, and Kapitalis — an existing open item resolved rather than a new standalone entry. (2) A wholly new standalone entry, Affaire "Créateurs de Contenu", was added to the Security & Counter-terrorism section: the Tunis Court of First Instance's criminal chamber sentenced 18 social-media content creators to 1–27 years' imprisonment on July 6, 2026 for TikTok/Instagram-linked offenses (sexually explicit content, human trafficking, money laundering, drug-trafficking/promotion), prosecuted jointly under the Anti-Trafficking Law (Organic Law 2015-61), Decree-Law No. 2022-54 (ICT offenses), and the Anti-Terrorism/Money-Laundering Law (Organic Law 2015-26) — traced to an October 27, 2025 Justice Ministry announcement and previously undocumented across 154 prior runs (webdo.tn, July 7, 2026). Several other leads were checked and confirmed already fully captured, not genuine gaps: the Mabrouk/Chahed/Ben Gharbia asset-unfreezing appeal, the Sahbi Smara appeal reduction, and the Rached Khiari case (whose underlying court event predates this run by nearly three months, with only a delayed CFJ advocacy statement being new). One new standalone entry added (276 total, up from 275); one existing entry updated. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 276 unique law-card ids, balanced <section>/<div>/<h3> tags, and all three version markers synchronized at v4.155/Run 155. The July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 dates remain the highest-priority items for the next run to check the moment each passes.
v4.156 update (July 7, 2026, Run 156): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-14ipv5) again held only a stale, never-merged v2.0–v2.6-era baseline (eight commits, 162 entries, PRs #77–#158) with no PR ever opened for it, while origin/main had independently progressed through 155 merged runs to v4.155 (276 entries) — confirmed via git fetch origin main, git log --oneline origin/main, and git merge-base --is-ancestor before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main (git checkout -B) per established practice. A fresh WebSearch sweep (Tavily again returned HTTP 402 "account disabled") re-checked the standing watchlist rather than assuming it unchanged: the Foreign Exchange Code (Draft Law 115/2025) committee outcome remains unreported a thirty-second consecutive trigger-date pass, with Ktari's June 17–23, 2026 ~90%-hearings-complete/BCT-non-response status still the last hard data point (independently re-confirmed via a fresh search for any post-July-6 committee session report — none found); a direct check for July 2026 JORT publications again surfaced nothing beyond routine "Annonces Légales" notices; and the July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 trigger dates had each still not arrived as of this run's same-day cutoff. This run's genuine contribution closed the open sourcing question Runs 119, 128, 131, 145, and 147 had each flagged but left unresolved: a June 30, 2026 Réalités Magazine article ("Avocats: Dans l'attente d'un dialogue"), independently fetched and read directly, corroborates the July 11, 2026 ONAT general assembly and its "evaluate the dispute and adopt consequent measures" strategy-review framing already documented in the Judicial Statute entry since Runs 43/46 — added there as a Run 156 update citing this fresh, independently-verified source. Two candidate leads were traced and found already fully captured rather than genuine gaps: a "ARP approves bill for exceptional recruitment of long-term unemployed university graduates" headline (the already-documented Law No. 2025-18 of December 22, 2025, adopted by the ARP December 16, 2025 — a stale-date trap, not a new 2026 vote) and the July 1, 2026 "zero paper" administrative-digitalization milestone (already captured in the Electronic Procedures entry's Run 111 update). No new standalone entries were added; one existing entry (Judicial Statute) was enriched (276 total remains accurate). The July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 dates remain the highest-priority items for the next run to check the moment each passes.
v4.157 update (July 7, 2026, Run 157): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-oavoy3) again held only a stale, never-merged v2.0–v2.6-era baseline (eight commits, 162 entries, PRs #77–#158) — the same recurring failure mode documented throughout this changelog. Unusually, a full draft (11 entries: five new anti-corruption/reconciliation-cluster cards plus associations-decree/pending-law updates) was written and a PR (#175) was opened against that stale base before git push/merge revealed the discrepancy: origin/main had independently progressed through 156 merged runs to v4.156 (276 entries), and the merge attempt returned a conflict. PR #175 was closed unmerged with an explanatory comment and the branch reset directly to current origin/main (git checkout -B) before restarting, consistent with how Runs 28, 53, 100, and others handled the same mistake. Rather than re-adding the discarded draft's content blindly, each of its five candidate items was individually cross-checked against the real current artifact: all five (Decree-Law 2011-13/confiscation, Organic Law 2017-62/administrative reconciliation, Decree-Law 2022-13/criminal reconciliation with its June 2026 Ali Abbès-commission relaunch, Decree-Law 2022-14/anti-speculation, and Decree-Law 2022-15/sociétés communautaires as amended by Decree-Law 2025-3) were confirmed already present under the Criminal Reconciliation, Reporting of Corruption, and Investment & Business Environment sections, in every case with materially greater precision than the discarded draft (e.g. the existing Decree-Law 2022-13 entry already cites Law No. 2024-3 and Presidential Decree No. 2026-109, which the draft lacked). The associations-decree "2026 NGO crackdown" and "pending foreign-funding bill" updates were likewise confirmed already captured — with a named committee chair (Thabet El Abed), specific session dates, and the Fatma Mseddi correspondence — in the existing Associations Decree entry and its companion pending-bill card. One genuine, independently-verified gap survived this cross-check: the Confiscation Law entry had cited "Law No. 2011-29 of April 18, 2011" since the artifact's earliest versions, a citation with no confirming primary source across the official legislation-securite.tn database, ILO NATLEX, or independent Tunisian reporting. Direct verification against legislation-securite.tn identified the actual instrument as Decree-Law No. 2011-13 of March 14, 2011 (amended by Decree-Law No. 2011-47), with the asset-management body created by a distinct Decree-Law No. 2011-68 of July 14, 2011 (previously mischaracterized in this entry as "the Tunisian Privatisation Authority" and as an amendment) and the CNIICF investigation commission traced to a separate, earlier Decree-Law No. 2011-7 of February 18, 2011 (previously conflated with the confiscation decree-law itself) — the entry has been corrected and cross-referenced accordingly. No new standalone entries were added (276 total remains accurate, since this is a citation correction rather than a new legal instrument); one existing entry (Confiscation Law) corrected. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 276 unique law-card ids, balanced <section>/<div> tags, and all internal anchor links resolving. The July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 dates remain the highest-priority items for the next run to check the moment each passes.
v4.159 update (July 7, 2026, Run 159): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-lc07m6) again reproduced the recurring stale-checkout failure mode documented throughout this changelog: its own head held only a stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.6-era baseline (seven commits, no PR of its own — confirmed via an empty search_pull_requests result) while origin/main had independently progressed through 158 merged runs to v4.158 (276 entries); confirmed via git fetch origin main and git log --oneline origin/main before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main (git checkout -B) per established practice. A dedicated research agent re-checked the standing watchlist (Foreign Exchange Code, Development Plan, Personal Data Protection/AI bill, Audiovisual bill, Investment/Water/Hydrocarbons/Environment codes, nationality-code revision, Electoral Code, the July 8/11/13 trigger dates) and found no status changes beyond what Run 158 already captured — the Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported, and the Development Plan's July 3, 2026 committee-close (Bouderbala) was already fully captured by the Run 71 entry update. Three candidate court-ruling leads (Lourimi/Gharbi's July 3 conviction, Chaouachi's July 6 appellate confirmation, and the July 7 "Créateurs de Contenu" TikTok/Instagram verdict) were independently verified already documented at equal or greater precision by existing entries (Runs 152 and 155). A proposed "elderly/pensioners' welfare bill" lead traced to the already-documented Proposition de loi n° 30/2026 entry (Run 115), not a new bill. This run's genuine contribution was two wholly new standalone entries closing gaps missed by all 158 prior runs: a Private Security, Guarding & Valuables-Transport Activities Law (Law No. 2002-81 of August 3, 2002, as amended by Law No. 2008-14 of 2008 — Security & Counter-terrorism section) and a Telemedicine framework (Presidential Decree No. 2022-318 of April 8, 2022, supplemented by June 13, 2024 ministerial orders — Health & Pharmaceutical Law section). A candidate French decree (2024-164) surfaced during telemedicine research was verified via direct source fetch to be French, not Tunisian, and correctly excluded — a new instance of the project's recurring cross-country-mislabeling trap pattern. Two new standalone entries added (278 total, up from 276); no existing entries required revision. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 278 unique law-card ids, balanced <section>/<div>/<h3> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.159/Run 159. The July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 dates remain the highest-priority items for the next run to check the moment each passes.
v4.158 update (July 7, 2026, Run 158): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-sah7r4) again reproduced the recurring stale-checkout failure mode documented throughout this changelog: it held only a stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.6-era baseline (seven commits, its sole PR — #158 — merged only into the unrelated claude/nice-edison-ga2s6f fork, never into main) while origin/main had independently progressed through 157 merged runs to v4.157 (276 entries) — confirmed via git fetch origin main, git log --oneline origin/main, and an empty search_pull_requests result for this branch's own head before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main (git checkout -B) per established practice. A fresh WebSearch sweep (Tavily again returned HTTP 402 "account disabled," consistent with every run since roughly Run 68) re-checked the standing watchlist: the Foreign Exchange Code (Draft Law 115/2025) committee outcome remains unreported — Ktari's June 17–23, 2026 ~90%-hearings-complete/BCT-non-response status is still the last hard data point, and no post-July-6, 2026 committee session outcome could be found by this run either (Run 157 did not re-check this specific item, so this run extends the no-movement streak Run 156 last confirmed at its thirty-second consecutive pass); a direct search for July 2026 JORT publications again surfaced nothing beyond routine "Annonces Légales" notices; and the Bensedrine/Mosbah/Krichi rulings, the CNRP relaunch, and the biologists'/ONAT triggers were each independently re-verified already fully captured at equal or greater precision. This run's genuine contribution was a narrow implementation-level gap outside the pending-bill watchlist: the Maritime Ports Code entry gained a Run 158 addition documenting the Skhira commercial port's roughly 18-year-old OMMP/TRAPSA concession-expiry impasse and the Transport Ministry's July 6, 2026 announcement of urgent regularization measures (convention finalization, boundary delimitation, a public-domain-delimitation implementing text, and 2040 ports-master-plan integration) — independently corroborated across Businessnews, Tunisie Tribune, Le Temps News, African Manager, Managers.tn, and La Presse de Tunisie, and previously undocumented across 157 prior runs despite the code's own standalone entry existing since early in this project. No new standalone entries were added (276 total remains accurate, since this enriches an existing card); one existing entry (Maritime Ports Code) updated. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 276 unique law-card ids, balanced <section>/<div>/<h3> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.158/Run 158. The July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 dates remain the highest-priority items for the next run to check the moment each passes.
v4.160 update (July 7, 2026, Run 160): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-ia8woz) again held only a stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.6-era baseline (topping out at v2.6/162 entries, PR #158 merged only into the unrelated claude/nice-edison-ga2s6f fork) while origin/main had independently progressed through 159 merged runs to v4.159 (278 entries) — confirmed via git fetch origin main and git log --oneline origin/main before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main (git checkout -B) per established practice. Tavily again returned HTTP 402 "account disabled" (consistent with every run since roughly Run 68); a WebSearch sweep re-checked the standing watchlist and found no movement: the Foreign Exchange Code (Draft Law 115/2025) committee outcome remains unreported, and the July 8 (graduate-recruitment protest), July 11 (ONAT general assembly), and July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant suspension) trigger dates had each still not arrived as of this run's same-day cutoff. Three fresh-search leads were each traced to material already fully captured at equal or greater precision: the July 6, 2026 Skhira port regularization measures (Run 158's Maritime Ports Code update), the July 1, 2026 RNE "zero paper" digitalization milestone (the Electronic Procedures entry's Run 111 update), and the July 6–7, 2026 "Créateurs de Contenu" TikTok/Instagram sentencing (Run 155's entry). Following up on Run 159's suggested next-vein leads (private detective/investigation licensing, funeral-services regulation, taxi-meter/fare regulation): funeral/burial services surfaced no citable dedicated Tunisian statute (civil-status/burial provisions are administrative, not a standalone code) and taxi tariffs are set by periodic Interior/Transport Ministry pricing decrees rather than a distinct law, so neither rises to a new standalone entry; but the private-detective question resolved into a genuine, previously-undocumented enrichment rather than a dead end — Article 15 of the existing Private Security Law (No. 2002-81, added Run 159) was fetched directly from legislation-securite.tn and found to be the actual legal basis (together with Art. 16) for Tunisia's blanket prohibition on private-investigation activity, a specific point the Run 159 entry had not yet captured despite documenting the statute itself. No new standalone entries were added (278 total remains accurate, since this enriches an existing card); one existing entry (Private Security Law) enriched. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 278 unique law-card ids, balanced <section>/<div>/<h3> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.160/Run 160. The July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 dates remain the highest-priority items for the next run to check the moment each passes.
v4.161 update (July 7, 2026, Run 161): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-uqdlpk) again held only a stale, never-merged v2.0–v2.8-era baseline (nine commits, no PR of its own — confirmed via an empty search_pull_requests result) while origin/main had independently progressed through 160 merged runs to v4.160 (278 entries) — confirmed via git fetch origin main and git log --oneline origin/main before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main (git checkout -B) per established practice. Two independent research agents re-checked the full standing watchlist: the Foreign Exchange Code (Draft Law 115/2025) committee outcome remains unreported (Ktari's ~90%-hearings-complete, BCT-non-responsive status of mid-to-late June 2026 is still the last hard data point; fresh same-day searches for "code des changes commission finances juillet 2026" surfaced nothing newer); the July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 trigger dates had each still not arrived as of this run's same-day cutoff; the 2026–2030 Development Plan bill's post-July-2 synthesis report is being drafted for Bureau submission but no plenary date has been scheduled; and the Personal Data Protection/AI bill, Audiovisual bill, Investment/Water/Hydrocarbons/Environment codes, Nationality Code revision, and Electoral Code all showed only advocacy-level or non-committal movement, not status changes. The July 6–7, 2026 "Créateurs de Contenu" TikTok/Instagram mass sentencing resurfaced in a fresh sweep and was independently re-confirmed already fully captured at equal or greater precision by Run 155's entry (same 18 defendants, same 1–27-year range, same three statutes cited). A Constitutional Court organic-law false-positive trap was identified and resolved: a April 23, 2025 report describing five of ten sponsoring deputies withdrawing signatures from a Constitutional Court bill was verified via direct source fetch to concern a distinct, earlier proposal that predates Proposal No. 100/2025 (filed July 15, 2025) by three months — Proposal 100/2025 itself remains pending in committee, unaffected. A thirteen-topic "everyday regulated activity" gap-scan (money changers, customs brokers, actuaries, credit-rating agencies, driving schools, crèches, elderly care homes, pawnshops, sworn translators, beekeeping, halal certification, street vending, public weighbridges) found twelve already covered by existing entries or decree-level rather than statute-level (credit-rating agencies confirmed as a genuine legislative void — no Tunisian law exists — rather than a citable gap), and one candidate (elderly care homes, traced to "Law No. 94-114") was cross-checked against this artifact's existing Elderly Persons — Rights and Protection entry (Law No. 1994-2 of January 3, 1994) and confirmed already comprehensively covered, not a distinct gap. This run's genuine contribution was a citation correction, found via a targeted spot-check rather than a coverage gap: the Judicial Officers (Huissiers de Justice) entry had cited a non-existent "Law No. 66-49 of July 2, 1966" since early in this project; independent verification (WebSearch plus a direct fetch of the legislation-securite.tn primary-source text) found no such 1966 law and instead confirmed the real succession — a 1958 governing text, repealed and replaced by Law No. 95-29 of March 13, 1995, itself repealed and replaced by the currently operative Organic Law No. 2018-9 of January 30, 2018 — and the entry was rewritten accordingly with real article numbers (appointment/exam exemption, service-of-process duties, registers, fees, discipline) drawn from the primary text. This is the same "real but superseded/fabricated citation" error class first identified in Runs 134, 140, and 146; future runs should continue the rolling early-run citation spot-check. No new standalone entries were added (278 total remains accurate); one existing entry (Judicial Officers/Huissiers de Justice) corrected. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 278 unique law-card ids, balanced <section>/<div>/<h3> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.161/Run 161. The July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 dates remain the highest-priority items for the next run to check the moment each passes.
v4.162 update (July 8, 2026, Run 162): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-x662y7) again held only a stale, never-merged v2.1–v2.8-era baseline (eight commits, no PR of its own — confirmed via an empty list_pull_requests result) while origin/main had independently progressed through 161 merged runs to v4.161 (278 entries) — confirmed via git fetch origin main and git log --oneline origin/main before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main (git checkout -B) per established practice. Tavily again returned HTTP 402 "account disabled" (consistent with every run since roughly Run 68); two parallel WebSearch-based research agents covered (1) the July 8, 2026 trigger date itself and the full standing pending-bill watchlist, and (2) a fresh ten-topic economic/regulatory gap-scan. On (1): July 8, 2026 — the graduate-recruitment protest date documented in the Public-Sector Recruitment of Long-Term Unemployed Graduates entry — has now arrived, but no same-day report was found confirming the protest occurred or that implementing decrees were published, consistent with the same-day indexing-lag pattern first noted for the July 6 Foreign Exchange Code committee session in Run 126; the entry's existing "implementing decrees remain unpublished" status stands unchanged and unresolved. The Foreign Exchange Code (Draft Law 115/2025) committee outcome remains unreported; the Development Plan bill's June 30/July 1, 2026 ministerial hearings and headline figures (40,748 proposals, 34,715 retained, TND ~102 billion, 21,100 projects) were independently re-confirmed already fully captured by the entry's existing text; and the Investment, Water, and Hydrocarbons code reforms, Personal Data Protection/AI bill, and Constitutional Court proposal (No. 100/2025) all showed no status change. The July 11 (ONAT) and July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant) trigger dates had not yet arrived as of this run's cutoff. On (2): ten candidate economic/regulatory areas were checked (Environmental Code, Water Code, Data Protection Law, Competition Law/Conseil de la Concurrence, Telecommunications Code, mandatory e-invoicing, Investment Code, renewable-energy self-production, Hydrocarbons Code, Constitutional Court organic law) — all ten were independently confirmed already covered by existing entries at equal or greater precision (e.g., the Finance Law 2026 entry's Art. 56 already documents the e-invoicing regime in more technical detail — TEIF/XML format, TunTrust/ANCE signature, penalty tiers — than this run's fresh research surfaced), or correctly remain undocumented as still-unenacted drafts (the Environmental Code remains a ~400-article draft never transmitted to the ARP, not positive law). A viral claim that a "new Code de la Route" entered into force July 1, 2026 was investigated and found to be a debunked false report (businessnews.com.tn, July 6, 2026) — the 1999 Road Traffic Code remains unamended and in force; correctly excluded, not added. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entries required revision (278 total remains accurate). A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 278 unique law-card ids, balanced <section>/<div>/<h3> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.162/Run 162. The July 8 trigger date remains open pending same-day confirmation; July 11 and July 13 remain the next-nearest triggers for the next run to check.
v4.163 update (July 8, 2026, Run 163): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-ppeoo6) again held only a stale, never-merged v2.0–v2.8-era baseline (nine commits, PRs #77–#158 merged only into an unrelated fork) while origin/main had independently progressed through 162 merged runs to v4.162 (278 entries) — confirmed via git fetch origin main and git log --oneline origin/main before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main (git checkout -B) per established practice. A dedicated research agent re-checked the standing watchlist: the Foreign Exchange Code (Draft Law 115/2025) committee outcome remains unreported; the July 8, 2026 graduate-recruitment protest and Law No. 2025-18 implementing-decree publication status showed no confirmable same-day outcome (consistent with the same-day indexing-lag pattern noted since Run 126); and the July 11 (ONAT) and July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant) trigger dates had not yet arrived as of this run's cutoff. Two candidate "new development" leads from a fresh sweep — the plastic-bag reduction bill and the "Créateurs de Contenu" TikTok/Instagram sentencing — were independently re-verified as already fully captured at equal or greater precision by the existing Run 109/132 and Run 155 entries respectively, and were not re-added. A second research pass, targeting specific "everyday regulated activity" areas not yet individually verified by name (as opposed to assumed covered by a broader existing entry), found ten of twelve already covered by dedicated statutes correctly cited elsewhere (whistleblower protection/INLUCC — Organic Law 2017-10, already documented with its pending 42-article reform bill; national digital ID — Organic Law 2024-22; national archives — Law 88-95, already a standalone entry; data centers/cloud — confirmed decree/circular-level only, no dedicated statute, not a gap), and closed two genuine gaps that had survived prior gap-scans: (1) a wholly new standalone Sales Techniques and Commercial Advertising Law (No. 98-40 of June 2, 1998) entry (Commercial Law section) — distinct from, and more detailed than, the Consumer Protection Law's general "misleading advertising" language, verified via jurisitetunisie.com and cross-corroborated by the JORT n°44 filename on pist.tn; and (2) a wholly new standalone Railway Law entry (Transport section) covering SNCFT's founding and governing statutes (Law No. 69-31 of 1969, Law No. 98-74 of 1998, Law No. 98-90 of 1998) — a gap that had specifically survived the Run 136 and Run 140 rail-transport gap-scans, both of which appear to have assumed the general Land Transport Law (No. 2004-33) covers railways without verifying that its text is road-transport-only; verified via SNCFT's own official documents page and Tunisia's legislation.tn portal, though the underlying JORT issue numbers for the three railway laws could not be independently confirmed this run and remain a flagged open item. Two new standalone entries added (280 total, up from 278); no existing entries required revision. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 280 unique law-card ids, balanced <section>/<div>/<h3> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.163/Run 163. Future runs should prioritize (1) confirming the JORT issue numbers for the three railway laws, (2) same-day confirmation of the July 8 decree-publication status the moment fresher reporting appears, and (3) the July 11 and July 13 trigger dates.
v4.164 update (July 8, 2026, Run 164): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-3ronck) again held only a stale, never-merged v2.0–v2.9-era baseline (ten commits, no PR of its own — confirmed via an empty list_pull_requests result) while origin/main had independently progressed through 163 merged runs to v4.163 (280 entries) — confirmed via git fetch origin main and git log --oneline origin/main before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main (git checkout -B) per established practice; the discarded draft's sole candidate addition, the Agricultural Debt Regularization Law (Law No. 2026-5), was independently cross-checked and confirmed already present verbatim under the Financial & Banking section, added back in Run 30. A dedicated research pass re-checked the standing watchlist: the July 8, 2026 graduate-recruitment protest and Law No. 2025-18 implementing-decree publication status again showed no confirmable same-day outcome (a fifteenth-plus instance of the same-day indexing-lag pattern first noted at Run 126); the Foreign Exchange Code (Draft Law 115/2025) committee outcome remains unreported, with the most recent hard data point (a June 17–18, 2026 report that the committee would choose, at its "next Monday" session, between an article-by-article vote without Central Bank input or a further delay) predating and not superseding the artifact's existing June 23, 2026 sourcing, so the entry was left unchanged; and the July 11 (ONAT) and July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant) trigger dates had still not arrived as of this run's cutoff. A "driving-school licensing: decree or statute?" gap-scan — flagged as a next-vein suggestion by Run 163 — resolved into a small enrichment rather than a standalone gap: driving-instructor certification and auto-école approval are confirmed decree-level matters under the existing Road Traffic Code entry, specifically Décret gouvernemental n° 2021-510 of June 18, 2021 (JORT No. 2021-059, driving-license categories and ATTT-approved training curricula) plus a September 1, 2025 procedural tightening requiring a signed candidate–school training contract — added as a dated note to that entry. A parallel check of Run 163's other two suggested leads (cinema/audiovisual production incentives, port-worker/docker labor status) found both already adequately covered or, for dockers, resolved no citable dedicated statute beyond the existing OMMP/Maritime Ports Code framework, and neither was added. No new standalone entries were added (280 total remains accurate); one existing entry (Road Traffic Code) enriched. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 280 unique law-card ids, balanced <section>/<div> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.164/Run 164. Future runs should continue prioritizing same-day confirmation of the July 8 decree-publication status, then July 11 and July 13, and consider a fresh narrow-statute gap-scan (e.g., consumer-credit-specific rules distinct from the general Banking Law, actuary/insurance-broker licensing, or halal-certification authority) given the near-exhaustion of prior broad thematic sweeps.

Following up on Run 164's suggested next-vein leads (consumer-credit rules, actuary/insurance-broker licensing, halal-certification authority): all three were independently confirmed already resolved by prior runs rather than open gaps — consumer credit and over-indebtedness are already a standalone entry distinct from the general Banking Law (Consumer Credit & Over-Indebtedness), insurance brokers and actuaries are already governed by the existing Insurance Code entry's intermediary-licensing provisions, and halal certification was already confirmed by the Run 84 gap-scan to be an INNORPI/Mufti-administered administrative process with no dedicated Tunisian statute, not a citable legislative gap. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entries required revision (280 total remains accurate). v4.168 update (July 8, 2026, Run 168): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-7u3c48) again held only a stale, never-merged v2.0–v2.10-era fork (eleven commits, its own internal changelog topping out at "170"/"171" entries, no PR ever opened for it per an empty list_pull_requests result) while origin/main had independently progressed through 167 merged runs to v4.167 (280 entries) — confirmed via git fetch origin main, git log --oneline origin/main, and git merge-base --is-ancestor before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main (git checkout -B) per established practice. Before resetting, this run's own independent research (conducted against the stale fork, unaware of origin/main's true state) had identified Abir Moussi's absence as an apparent gap — cross-checking that finding against the real current artifact after the reset showed it was not a gap at all: Moussi's cases are already covered within the consolidated "State Security Conspiracy" Prosecutions entry, which 167 runs of iteration have built into a thorough, cross-referenced record. However, that entry's own Moussi sentence — "a Tunis Court of Appeal ruling reported around March 2026" — was a vague placeholder unresolved since Run 40, so this run's research was redirected into resolving it rather than discarded outright. The placeholder is now replaced with the specific verified detail: the "bureau d'ordre" case (12-year first-instance sentence, December 2025, reduced to 10 years on appeal March 13, 2026 — 9 years for "aggression aimed at disrupting the functioning of the state, incitement to violence, and public disorder" plus two 6-month counts; PDL co-defendant Meriem Sassi received 1 year) and the separate ISIE/Decree-Law 2022-54 case (2-year sentence, first handed down August 5, 2024, reduced to 16 months on appeal, annulled and remanded by the Court of Cassation, then re-confirmed at 2 years by a newly-constituted Tunis Court of Appeal panel on December 30, 2025), cross-checked across Amnesty International, Kapitalis, and Business News. A fresh sweep of two further leads was also cross-checked against this changelog's own prior findings before use: a purported March 27, 2026 ONAT/Bar-Association hearing on the Constitutional Court bill (Proposal 100/2025), surfaced again by search, was not added — Runs 62 and 122 had already independently tested this exact claim and found it uncorroborated, and this run's own attempt to verify it via direct source fetch also failed (403 error, no independent confirmation), so it remains a twice-(now three-times-)tested, uncorroborated lead rather than a status change. A driving-school/auto-école licensing lead and a dockworker/port-labor-statute lead were both checked and found not to be standalone Tunisian legislative gaps — the former falls under the existing 1999 Road Traffic Code and ATTT administrative regulation, the latter appears to have no dedicated Tunisian statute distinct from general Labor Code and Maritime Ports Code provisions. No new standalone entries were added (280 total remains accurate, since this run's genuine contribution enriches an existing card); one existing entry (State Security Conspiracy Prosecutions) revised for currency. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 280 unique law-card ids, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.168/Run 168. The July 11 and 13, 2026 trigger dates remain the highest-priority items for the next run to check the moment each passes; future runs encountering the "March 27, 2026 ONAT Constitutional Court hearing" claim should treat it as a closed, thrice-uncorroborated lead rather than re-investigating again.
v4.169 update (July 8, 2026, Run 169): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-dt1zr5) again held only a stale, never-merged v2.0–v2.10-era fork (twelve commits, its own changelog topping out at "170" entries) while origin/main had independently progressed through 168 merged runs to v4.168 (280 entries) — confirmed via git fetch origin main and git log --oneline origin/main before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main per established practice. Tavily returned HTTP 402 ("account disabled") again this run, so research relied on WebSearch plus direct WebFetch verification of primary articles. The Foreign Exchange Code (Draft Law 115/2025) committee outcome remains unreported since the June 17–23, 2026 ~90%-hearings-complete/BCT-non-response status; the July 11 (ONAT) and July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant) trigger dates had still not arrived as of this run's cutoff. Two candidate leads were investigated and confirmed already fully captured rather than gaps: a search-engine synthesis suggesting a "July 6, 2026" vote on the Local/Regional/District Councils organic law was traced, via direct WebFetch of legislation-securite.tn, to the real (and already-documented, since Run 13) Organic Law No. 2025-4 of March 12, 2025 — the "July 6, 2026" date was a search-synthesis artifact, not a genuine event, and was correctly discarded; and the July 6–7, 2026 "Affaire Créateurs de Contenu" TikTok/Instagram mass-conviction (18 defendants, 1–27 years) was independently re-confirmed already added at Run 155. This run's genuine contribution is a substantial enrichment, closed after cross-verifying four independent sources (africanews.com, entreprises-magazine.com via direct WebFetch, plus corroborating webdo.tn, Managers.tn, and WebManagerCenter search hits) of the Renewable Energy Law entry's existing May 13, 2026 CNRD note: the underlying five project-specific concession-ratification bills (Laws n°01/2026–05/2026, covering the El Khobna/El Khabna, Mezzouna, El Ksar, Segdoud, and Menzel Habib photovoltaic plants already named in that entry) were in fact first adopted by the ARP on April 28, 2026, each by a separately recorded plenary vote (ranging from 70–33–8 to 75–33–9 for/against/abstain), before the CNRD's already-documented May 13, 2026 concurrence — a bicameral sequence the artifact had only told half of. Also added: the same-day political controversy independently corroborated across all four sources — President Kais Saied dismissed Industry, Mines & Energy Minister Fatma Thabet Chiboub on April 28, 2026 (Equipment Minister Salah Zouari installed as interim, no permanent successor confirmed as of this run) amid UGTT and opposition-deputy (Bilel El Mechri, "energy colonisation") criticism of the concessions' terms — 20-year foreign-operator concessions (renewable once for 10 years) with a five-year corporate-tax exemption, all output sold exclusively to STEG. This is a genuine addition entirely missed by 168 prior runs, since none had previously connected the CNRD-only citation to its ARP origin or to the ministerial dismissal that same news cycle centered on. No new standalone entries were added (280 total remains accurate, since this enriches an existing card); one existing entry (Renewable Energy Law) substantially revised for currency. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 280 unique law-card ids, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.169/Run 169. The July 11 and 13, 2026 trigger dates remain the highest-priority items for the next run to check the moment each passes.
v4.170 update (July 8, 2026, Run 170): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-46m0de) again held only a stale, never-merged v2.0–v2.10-era fork (thirteen commits, its own changelog topping out at "170" entries, no PR ever opened per an empty list_pull_requests result) while origin/main had independently progressed through 169 merged runs to v4.169 (280 entries) — confirmed via git fetch origin main and git log --oneline origin/main before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main per established practice. A fresh WebSearch/WebFetch sweep re-checked the standing watchlist: the Foreign Exchange Code (Draft Law 115/2025) committee outcome remains unreported; the July 8, 2026 graduate-recruitment protest was independently re-confirmed as already fully captured (Run 167's update); and the July 11 (ONAT) and July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant) trigger dates had still not arrived as of this run's cutoff. This run's genuine contribution resolves a loose thread the changelog had carried unconfirmed since Run 145: which body's "general assembly" the July 11 trigger actually refers to. Direct verification (avocat.org.tn) confirms "ONAT" is the Ordre National des Avocats de Tunisie (National Bar Association) — not the architects' or chartered-accountants' orders, both of which Run 145 had also considered and could not corroborate. The Lawyers Statute entry, which had never documented any 2026 developments despite the artifact's otherwise thorough Judicial Organization coverage, is enriched with the underlying dispute: ONAT's January 23, 2026 written reform demands to the Justice Ministry (prosecutions targeting lawyers, the still-provisional Supreme Judicial Council, and criminal-chamber composition), the disputed May 1, 2026 exceptional general assembly (challenged by the Tunis Court of Appeal's prosecutor general, with a June 10, 2026 legality hearing), the May 13, 2026 Council vote for regional strikes and a boycott of improperly-composed criminal chambers, and the June 18, 2026 national general strike at the Palace of Justice — cross-verified across directinfo.webmanagercenter.com (May 2 and May 19, 2026) and African Manager. The July 11 assembly was expected to set conditions for resuming dialogue if the dispute remained unresolved; this run's cutoff preceded confirmed reporting on its outcome. A parallel check of five further "everyday regulated activity" candidates (midwifery, customs brokers, driving-school licensing, hunting, and the Development Plan bill's plenary-scheduling status) found each already governed at ministerial-order/existing-entry level or, for the Development Plan, still awaiting its committee synthesis report with no plenary date set — none rose to a new standalone gap. No new standalone entries were added (280 total remains accurate, since this run's genuine contribution enriches an existing card); one existing entry (Lawyers Statute) substantially enriched. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 280 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 1329/1329 balanced <div> tags, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 280/280 balanced <h3> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.170/Run 170. The July 11 ONAT assembly outcome and the July 13 CNAM/biologists tiers-payant suspension remain the highest-priority items for the next run to check the moment each passes.

⚖️ Constitutional Law

Constitution of 2022Constitution of the Republic of Tunisia

Adopted: July 25, 2022 (referendum) | Published: August 18, 2022 | In force: August 2022
The current governing constitution, promulgated by Presidential Decree No. 2022-691 of August 17, 2022. Establishes a presidential system with a bicameral legislature (National Council of Regions and Districts + Assembly of People's Representatives). Supersedes the 1959 and 2014 constitutions as well as Decree-Law No. 2011-14 of March 23, 2011. Article 5 references Islam and "the aims of Pure Islam." Comprises 142 articles organized into 10 chapters.
Key provisions
  • Art. 1: Tunisia is a free, independent, sovereign state; Islam is its religion, Arabic its language, the republic its form of government
  • Art. 2: Tunisia is a civil state based on citizenship, the will of the people, and the supremacy of law
  • Art. 5: The state shall work to achieve the objectives of Pure Islam
  • Art. 29: Intellectual property is guaranteed
  • Art. 40: Freedom to form political parties, unions, and associations is guaranteed
  • Art. 42: Freedom of peaceful assembly and demonstration is guaranteed
  • Art. 86–90: President elected for 5-year term, limit of two terms
  • Repeals: 1959 Constitution, Decree-Law No. 2011-14, 2014 Constitution
Constitutional

Constitution of 2014 [Superseded]Constitution of the Republic of Tunisia

Adopted: January 26, 2014 | In force: February 2014 | Superseded: August 2022
Post-Arab Spring constitution adopted by the National Constituent Assembly with 200 votes for, 12 against, 4 abstentions. Comprised 149 articles across 10 chapters. Established a semi-presidential system. Article 74 restricted the presidency to Muslims. Now superseded by the 2022 Constitution.
Key provisions (historical)
  • Art. 1: Tunisia is a free, independent, sovereign state; Islam its religion, Arabic its language
  • Art. 2: Tunisia is a civil state based on citizenship, the will of the people, and the primacy of law
  • Art. 20: Equality before the law guaranteed without discrimination
  • Art. 46: Enshrined women's rights and gender parity in elected bodies
  • Art. 74: Presidential candidates must be Muslim, Tunisian nationals, born of Tunisian parents
  • Art. 88: Mechanism for presidential impeachment
  • Art. 115: Prime minister removal required majority of assembly members
ConstitutionalSuperseded

Constitution of 1959 [Superseded]Constitution of the Tunisian Republic

Promulgated: June 1, 1959 | In force: June 1, 1959 | Superseded: August 2022
Tunisia's first post-independence constitution. Modeled on the French system, vested strong executive power in a president. Arabic declared national language. Presidents elected for 5-year terms. Amended multiple times — notably in 2002 (removed presidential term limits, opened candidacy to persons over 75). In force for 63 years until replaced by the 2022 Constitution.
Key provisions (historical)
  • Art. 1: Tunisia is a free, independent, sovereign state; Islam its religion, Arabic its language, the republic its form
  • Art. 39: Presidential term set at 5 years
  • Art. 40: President elected by universal direct suffrage
  • 2002 amendment: removed two-term limit, raised age ceiling to 75
ConstitutionalSuperseded

Decree-Law No. 2011-14 of March 23, 2011Provisional Organization of Public Authorities

Enacted: March 23, 2011 | In force: March 2011 | Superseded: August 2022
Established the transitional constitutional framework following the 2011 revolution. Organized interim executive and legislative powers pending the drafting and adoption of a permanent constitution. Superseded by the 2022 Constitution.
ConstitutionalSuperseded

Presidential Decree No. 117 of September 22, 2021Exceptional Measures

Article 80 invoked: July 25, 2021 | Decree 2021-117: September 22, 2021 | ARP formally dissolved: March 30, 2022
On July 25, 2021 (Republic Day), President Kais Saied invoked Article 80 of the 2014 Constitution, dismissing PM Hichem Mechichi's government, freezing (not yet formally dissolving) the Assembly of the Representatives of the People (ARP), and lifting MPs' immunity — despite Article 80 itself requiring Parliament to remain in continuous session while invoked. Presidential Decree No. 2021-117 of September 22, 2021 then formalized and radically extended these powers: it suspended the 2014 Constitution except its preamble and Chapters 1–2 (general provisions; rights and freedoms), transferred all legislative power to the president (exercised via presidential decrees with the Council of Ministers), declared such decrees non-appealable (Art. 7), suspended the interim constitutional-review body (Art. 21), and tasked the president with drafting a new constitution for referendum (Art. 22). Run 44 correction: the ARP was not dissolved by this decree — it remained merely frozen until March 30, 2022, when Saied announced its formal dissolution hours after roughly 120 MPs defied the freeze in an online plenary session voting to repeal the exceptional measures. This 2021–2022 sequence is the direct legal predicate for the July 2022 constitutional referendum, the 2022 Constitution, and the judiciary/electoral decree-laws elsewhere in this reference. Update (Run 66): the July 25, 2022 referendum's precise legal basis, previously undocumented at the decree level, consists of two texts published together in JORT No. 59 of May 25, 2022, both invoking this decree's authority: Presidential Decree No. 2022-506, convoking voters for the referendum and setting the ballot question and voting hours, and Decree-Law No. 2022-32, enacting derogations from Art. 113 of the standing Electoral Law (Organic Law No. 2014-16) to permit late publication of the draft constitution (by June 30, 2022). Tunisia's referendum mechanism otherwise rests on Organic Law No. 2014-16's general election/referendum provisions (successor to Arts. 162–165 of the 1969 Electoral Code); the Venice Commission criticized the reliance on ad hoc derogating decrees rather than rules "established well in advance." The 2022 Constitution subsequently created a broader standing presidential referendum power (Art. 97) and allows referendum to substitute for a two-thirds parliamentary majority in constitutional revision (Arts. 136–138).
ConstitutionalSuperseded

2022 Constitution, Articles 116–120Constitutional Court

Established by: 2022 Constitution | Replaces: defunct Constitutional Council
The 2022 Constitution establishes a Constitutional Court replacing the defunct Constitutional Council. Composed of 9 members: 3 appointed by president, 3 elected by parliament, 3 elected by Supreme Judicial Council. 9-year non-renewable terms, staggered. Reviews constitutionality of laws before promulgation and settles conflicts of competence. Under the 2022 Constitution, the Constitutional Court cannot rule on the validity of the president's exceptional measures (Art. 96). Status as of June 2026: still not established — neither this court nor its 2014-Constitution predecessor has ever become operational; legal scholars and the Venice Commission describe Tunisia as having effectively gone without a functioning constitutional review body since 2011. The ARP's General Legislation Committee resumed hearings in February 2026 on Organic Law Proposal No. 100/2025, a private member's bill (filed July 15, 2025 by 21 deputies) to actually create the Court under Chapter VI of the 2022 Constitution, activating Articles 125–132; distinct from an earlier, unrelated 10-signatory proposal abandoned in April 2025 after five signatories withdrew. Update (Run 76): a February 18, 2026 committee hearing had the sponsors present the bill, with commission president Faouzi Daâs stating consultations would continue with other parties including executive officials; as of this run the proposal remained under committee review with no vote scheduled, and no primary source could corroborate a specific article count for the current 100/2025 text (a distinct, earlier 89-article/5-chapter draft was withdrawn in 2025) — that figure is deliberately left unstated pending confirmation. Update (Run 187): the General Legislation Committee relaunched the file on March 27, 2026 with a hearing of the Ordre National des Avocats de Tunisie (ONAT, the national Bar Association); the bâtonnier reaffirmed the profession's support for creating the Court but linked it to completing the wider constitutional architecture, in particular the still-unformed Conseil Supérieur de la Magistrature (Supreme Judicial Council), and the ONAT delegation specifically objected that the bill's proposed composition — drawn exclusively from sitting magistrates — risks isolating the Court from academic and other outside legal expertise; deputies said they remained open to further participatory consultation toward a "balanced text." No vote date has been set (businessnews.com.tn, March 27, 2026; corroborated by a second independent search pass returning matching detail, though no fully distinct second outlet's reporting of this specific hearing could be located this run). (Run 274): see also the Human Rights Instance entry below, a second Chapter VI constitutional body created by law but never operationalized.
Constitutional

Organic Law No. 2018-51 of October 29, 2018Human Rights Instance (Instance des Droits de l'Homme)

Adopted by ARP: October 16, 2018 (144–0–0, unanimous) | Promulgated: October 29, 2018 | Constitutional basis: 2022 Constitution, Arts. 126 & 128 (successor to 2014 Constitution, Arts. 125 & 128)
A 62-article, 5-chapter organic law establishing the composition, powers, and procedures of the Instance des Droits de l'Homme (IDH), one of the constitutional independent bodies mandated by Chapter VI of the 2014 Constitution and carried forward by the 2022 Constitution. Under Art. 128, the IDH must be consulted on all draft legislation touching human rights and fundamental freedoms and may conduct investigations and inquiries into alleged violations, referring findings to the competent authorities or courts; Art. 126 obliges other state institutions to facilitate its work. The IDH is to comprise 9 members elected by the ARP by two-thirds majority for a single non-renewable 6-year term — required by law to include an administrative-court judge, a judicial-court judge, a lawyer, a physician, and five representatives of human-rights organizations — and enjoys administrative and financial autonomy, reporting annually to the ARP. Status as of July 2026: despite unanimous adoption of the enabling law nearly eight years ago, the IDH has never been operationalized — the ARP has never convened the two-thirds-majority vote needed to elect its 9 members, leaving Tunisia without a functioning constitutional human-rights body since the 2014 Constitution first mandated one. This mirrors the non-implementation of the Constitutional Court above and compounds the accountability gap left by INLUCC's loss of constitutional status in 2022 — together leaving three of the four bodies envisioned by Chapter VI of the 2014/2022 constitutional architecture (Constitutional Court, IDH, and a permanent anti-corruption authority) either unformed or unentrenched, with only the Independent High Authority for Elections (ISIE) actually functioning, and even ISIE's own post-2022 independence has been separately criticized above.
ConstitutionalAdministrative

Local Council Elections of December 24, 2023Seating of the National Council of Regions and Districts (CNRD)

First-round vote: December 24, 2023 | Second round: January 2024 | Implements: 2022 Constitution bicameral structure
Tunisia's first elections held entirely under the 2022 Constitution's "bottom-up" local governance model. Voters elected 2,155 members across 279 local councils for 5-year terms via a majoritarian two-round system (turnout: 11.7% round one, 12.53% round two — record lows). Each local council designates a member by lot to a regional (governorate) council; the 24 regional councils each elect a representative to one of 5 district councils; the district and regional councils together indirectly elect the 77-member National Council of Regions and Districts (CNRD), the upper chamber of parliament envisioned by the 2022 Constitution. The CNRD held its inaugural session in 2023, completing the bicameral legislature alongside the Assembly of People's Representatives. Critics, including IFES and the Carnegie Endowment, describe the multi-tiered indirect system as insulating the upper chamber from direct accountability.
ConstitutionalAdministrative

Décret n° 2025-165 of March 20, 2025Appointment of the Head of Government

Enacted: March 20, 2025 | Published: JORT No. 2025-033
Presidential decree appointing Sarra Zaafrani Zenzri as Cheffe du Gouvernement (Head of Government), replacing Kamel Madouri — the fourth such change since President Kais Saied assumed exceptional powers in July 2021. Zaafrani Zenzri, previously Minister of Equipment and Housing, is only the second woman to head a Tunisian government (after Najla Bouden, 2021–2023). No official reason was given by the Presidency; press coverage (L'Orient-Le Jour, Africanews) attributed the change to Saied's public dissatisfaction with government performance.
ConstitutionalAdministrative

Decree-Law No. 2022-22 of March 21, 2022ISIE Reorganization

Enacted: March 21, 2022 | In force: March 2022
Amended Organic Law No. 2012-23 governing the Independent High Authority for Elections (ISIE). Reduced ISIE membership from 9 to 7 members; altered appointment procedure giving the president greater influence. Criticized by Venice Commission for undermining electoral independence. Issued under presidential decree-law authority (Decree 117). Update (Run 40): the seven members appointed under this decree-law by Presidential Decree No. 2022-459 of May 9, 2022 serve non-renewable 4-year terms; those terms expired May 9, 2026, and as of mid-2026 no successor members had been appointed and no formal renewal mechanism had been triggered, per CRLDHT legal commentary — a single-source finding, flagged here pending stronger corroboration in a future run, describing the electoral authority's legal status as unresolved.
ConstitutionalAdministrative

Decree-Law No. 2022-55 of September 15, 2022Electoral Reform — Individual Candidacies System

Enacted: September 15, 2022 | In force: September 2022 | Applied in: December 2022 / January 2023 elections
Replaced the proportional closed-list system for legislative elections with a single-member constituency majority system under which candidates run as individuals, not on party lists. Abolished the gender parity requirement for electoral lists. Widely seen as structurally weakening political parties and reducing women's parliamentary representation. Applied in the December 17, 2022 (first round) and January 29, 2023 (runoffs) elections for the Assembly of People's Representatives, producing historically low turnout (~8.8%).
ConstitutionalAdministrative

📜 Civil Law & Law of Obligations

Code of Obligations and Contracts (COC) — 1906Majallat Al-Iltizamat wa-l-Uqud

Promulgated: December 15, 1906 (under French Protectorate) | In force: January 1, 1907
The foundational civil code governing contracts, torts, and obligations. Derived from the French civil law tradition, adapted for Tunisian context. Covers general theory of obligations, specific contracts (sale, lease, agency, loan, deposit, surety), and civil liability. Has been substantially amended multiple times since independence. Remains in force as the core private law instrument.
Key provisions
  • Art. 1–106: General theory of obligations (formation, validity, effects, extinction)
  • Art. 13: Requirements for valid contract — capacity, consent, object, cause
  • Art. 62–80: Nullity of contracts (absolute vs. relative)
  • Art. 82–96: Effects of obligations; performance and non-performance
  • Art. 100: Damages for non-performance; principle of full reparation
  • Art. 222–262: Contract of sale (conditions, effects, warranty)
  • Art. 728–733: Civil liability for delict and quasi-delict
  • Art. 786–828: Mandate and agency
CivilAmended

Code of Civil and Commercial Procedure — 1959Majallat Al-Murafa'at Al-Madaniyya wa-l-Tijariyya

Enacted: 1959 | Multiple amendments
Governs civil and commercial litigation procedures including jurisdiction, service of process, evidence, trials, judgments, and appeals. Regulates enforcement of judgments and provisional measures. Organizes appeals to Courts of First Instance, Courts of Appeal, and the Court of Cassation.
Key provisions
  • Rules on jurisdiction: territorial and subject-matter
  • Provisional and conservatory measures
  • Rules of evidence and burden of proof
  • Default judgments and opposition procedures
  • Appeals and cassation procedures
  • Enforcement of domestic and foreign judgments
CivilCommercialAmended

Law of August 1, 1957Civil Status (Registry of Births, Deaths, Marriages)

Enacted: August 1, 1957 | In force: 1957 | Amended: multiple times
Establishes the civil registration system for Tunisian nationals including registration of births, deaths, marriages, divorces, and changes of nationality. Mandates registration within specified time limits. Organizes the function of civil status officers (officiers d'état civil).
CivilAdministrative

Nationality Code — Law No. 63-6 of February 28, 1963

Enacted: February 28, 1963 | In force: 1963 | Amended: 1975, 2002, 2010
Cross-referenced from the Nationality, Immigration & Asylum section (see the fuller entry there for full provisions, the 2010 maternal-transmission amendment, and the pending 2026 naturalization-reform bill). Defines Tunisian nationality by birth, descent, naturalization, and reacquisition; governs loss, renunciation, and deprivation of nationality.
CivilNationality

Law No. 98-97 of November 27, 1998Private International Law

Enacted: November 27, 1998 | In force: 1998 | Supplements: COC Arts. 52–67
Governs conflict-of-laws rules in Tunisia. The 1998 law comprehensively codified private international law rules on jurisdiction, applicable law, and recognition of foreign judgments. Applies French-tradition choice-of-law methodology. Islamic law applies to personal status of Muslim Tunisians.
Civil

Code of Civil Procedure Title XI (1959, as amended)Enforcement of Judgments

Enacted: 1959 | Amended: multiple times
Governs compulsory enforcement of court judgments and notarial instruments. Establishes the role of huissiers de justice (bailiffs) in enforcement. Provides for seizure of movable and immovable property, garnishment of wages, and forced sale. Amended to streamline enforcement timelines and protect minimum wage from seizure. (Added Run 246): A new proposition de loi organique relative à l'exécution des jugements (19 articles) was freshly submitted to the ARP's General Legislation Commission around July 9–10, 2026, carried by ten deputies led by Fatma Mseddi, targeting the chronic non-enforcement of judicial and administrative rulings against public officials and administrations. It would replace the existing derisory penalty for obstructing enforcement (reportedly as low as two weeks' imprisonment and a 4-dinar fine) with prison terms of up to three years and substantially higher fines, aggravated when a public official is responsible, plus a new obligation for administrations to file annual transparency reports on outstanding unenforced rulings. Independently corroborated across La Presse de Tunisie/allAfrica (July 10–11, 2026) and Tuniscope's Arabic edition; no bill number, committee hearing date, or vote has yet been reported — still at the freshly-filed/committee-referral stage. Distinct from deputy Mseddi's separate Ennahdha-designation bill (see Administrative Law section).
CivilAdministrativePending

👪 Personal Status Law

The Code of Personal Status (CPS) is Tunisia's most internationally distinctive statute — it abolishes polygamy and replaced unilateral repudiation (talaq) with court-supervised divorce. It has been continuously in force since 1957.

Code of Personal Status (CPS) — Beylical Decree of August 13, 1956Majallat Al-Ahwal Al-Shakhsiyya

Enacted: August 13, 1956 (beylical decree under Lamine Bey) | In force: January 1, 1957 | Amended: 1959, 1966, 1981, 1993, 2017
Tunisia's landmark personal status code, principally drafted by President Habib Bourguiba. Abolished polygamy (criminal offense under Art. 18), replaced unilateral male repudiation (talaq) with court-supervised divorce, set minimum marriage age. The most progressive family law in the Arab world. Covers marriage, divorce, filiation, adoption (recognition of kafala), inheritance, guardianship, and wills. Never suspended or repealed despite political pressures (2011–2013). The 2022 Constitution has not altered its operation.
Key provisions
  • Art. 3: Marriage requires consent of both spouses; minimum age 18 (with exceptions)
  • Art. 5: Marriage must be registered and conducted before a civil status officer
  • Art. 18: Polygamy prohibited and criminalized — up to 1 year imprisonment + fine for marrying while already married
  • Art. 29–37: Divorce only by court order; grounds include mutual consent, harm, abandonment
  • Art. 31: Either spouse may initiate divorce; court-ordered alimony mandatory
  • Art. 46–67: Filiation and parentage; paternity presumption
  • Art. 68–78: Legal guardianship (tutelle) of minors
  • Art. 85–199: Inheritance rules (miras) — blends civil and Islamic principles
  • Art. 154–165: Guardianship over persons under disability
  • 1993 amendment: mother gains equal custody rights; divorced mothers may become guardians
  • 2017 amendment: allowed Muslim women to marry non-Muslim men (circular, not codified)
Personal StatusCivilAmended

Child Protection Code — Law No. 95-92 of November 9, 1995Majallat Himayat Al-Tifl

Enacted: November 9, 1995 | In force: 1995 | Amended: 2002, 2008
Comprehensive statute protecting minors from abuse, neglect, exploitation, and trafficking. Establishes the Children's Judge (juge des enfants) with jurisdiction over child protection cases. Covers child welfare, delinquency, protective placement, and guardianship. Implements Tunisia's obligations under the UN Convention on the Rights of the Child (ratified 1992).
Key provisions
  • Creates specialized Children's Judges and children's courts
  • Art. 24–30: Protective measures for children in danger
  • Art. 70–80: Juvenile delinquency proceedings; preference for rehabilitation over punishment
  • Art. 101: Prohibition of child labor below age 16 in principle
  • Art. 105–110: International child abduction; return mechanisms
Personal StatusCivil

Law No. 58-27 of March 4, 1958Kafala (Legal Sponsorship of Abandoned Children)

Enacted: March 4, 1958 | In force: 1958 | Amended: 1994
Regulates the kafala system — the legal guardianship of abandoned children (Tunisia does not recognize formal adoption in the Islamic sense). Allows Tunisian families to take in abandoned children as legal dependents. Creates rights and obligations equivalent to adoption without severing the child's original lineage.
Personal Status

Law No. 2017-58 of August 11, 2017Elimination of Violence Against Women

Enacted: August 11, 2017 | In force: February 2018
Landmark statute criminalizing all forms of violence against women — physical, psychological, sexual, and economic. Abolished the provision allowing rapists to escape prosecution by marrying their victim. Created specialized police units and prosecution procedures for gender-based violence. Established protective orders and victim shelters. Considered one of the most progressive anti-GBV laws in the Arab world.
Key provisions
  • Removed Art. 227 bis of the Penal Code (marriage escaping rape prosecution)
  • Defines and criminalizes economic, psychological, and sexual violence
  • Establishes specialized prosecution units for violence against women
  • Provides emergency protective orders for victims
  • Mandatory reporting obligations for health professionals
  • National Observatory on Violence Against Women created
Personal StatusCriminal

Presidential Commission Report (2018)Inheritance Equality Debate

Report issued: August 2018 | Proposal not enacted
In August 2018, President Essebsi proposed legislation granting equal inheritance rights to men and women (departing from Quranic inheritance shares retained in CPS). The proposal, while debated in the national dialogue, was not enacted into law before his death in 2019. The CPS inheritance provisions (Arts. 85–199) remain in force, blending Islamic and civil principles.
Personal Status

⚔️ Criminal Law & Procedure

Penal Code — Law of July 9, 1913 (as amended)Majallat Al-Qanun Al-Jaza'i

Originally enacted: July 9, 1913 (French Protectorate) | Revised: 1964, 1968, 1987, 1993, 1999, 2004, 2010, 2017
Tunisia's criminal code. Enacted under the French Protectorate in 1913, substantially revised after independence. Classifies offenses into crimes (felonies), délits (misdemeanors), and contraventions (infractions). Organized in two books: general provisions and specific offenses.
