Notable provisions include criminalization of same-sex relations (Art. 230), blasphemy provisions, and offenses against state security. Amended significantly in 2017 following the Violence Against Women Law.
Key provisions
  • Art. 1–59: General principles — criminal liability, complicity, recidivism, penalties
  • Art. 52: Capital punishment available for certain crimes (murder, rape causing death, terrorism)
  • Art. 72–85: Offenses against state security (treason, espionage)
  • Art. 86: Attacks against the head of state or government
  • Art. 121: Prohibited associations
  • Art. 214: Abortion prohibited except to save mother's life
  • Art. 218: Homicide and serious assault
  • Art. 226: Offenses against public morals
  • Art. 226 bis: Defamation of public officials via new communication technologies
  • Art. 227: Rape — minimum 6 years imprisonment
  • Art. 230: Consensual same-sex relations criminalized — up to 3 years imprisonment
  • Art. 231–245: Theft, fraud, embezzlement
  • Art. 305: Receiving stolen goods
CriminalAmended

Code of Criminal Procedure — Law No. 68-23 of July 24, 1968

Enacted: July 24, 1968 | Published: JORT No. 32 (August 2–6, 1968) | Amended: Law No. 87-70 (Nov. 26, 1987), Law No. 93-114 (Nov. 22, 1993)
Governs all stages of criminal proceedings in Tunisia. Organized in five books covering: public action and preliminary investigation, trial court jurisdictions, extraordinary remedies (appeals, cassation), special procedures, and execution of sentences. Article 1 establishes that any infraction gives rise to public action aimed at applying penalties.
Key provisions
  • Art. 1: Any infraction triggers public action ("Toute infraction donne ouverture à une action publique")
  • Art. 13–14 (as amended by Law No. 87-70): Limits on garde à vue detention
  • Art. 47: Preliminary judicial investigation mandatory for crimes (felonies)
  • Art. 57: Rights of suspects during investigation
  • Art. 85–87 (as amended by Law No. 93-114): Preventive detention — initial 6-month maximum, extendable
  • Art. 106–107: Extended detention provisions
  • Book II: Jurisdiction of cantonal courts, courts of first instance, courts of appeal
  • Book III: Opposition, appeal, and cassation remedies
  • Book IV: Military courts, terrorism courts, special jurisdictions
  • Book V: Execution of criminal sentences
CriminalAmended

Organic Law No. 2015-26 of August 7, 2015Countering Terrorism and Suppression of Money Laundering (as amended)

Enacted: August 7, 2015 | In force: August 2015 | Amended: Organic Law No. 2019-9 of January 23, 2019
Date/title correction: Previously listed as "Law No. 2015-26 of July 7, 2015" — primary sources (legislation-securite.tn, UNODC, OHCHR, ecolex) confirm the enactment date is August 7, 2015, and the official title covers both terrorism and money laundering in a single statute (this is also the same underlying law separately, and incorrectly, cited under a fictitious "Law No. 2019-23" on the former Anti-Money Laundering entry below — now corrected/merged). Enacted following the Bardo Museum and Sousse beach attacks (2015), replacing the 2003 anti-terrorism law. Defines terrorism broadly, criminalizes financing, propaganda, recruitment, and travel for terrorist purposes; establishes specialized anti-terrorism courts and the Financial Intelligence Unit (CTAF — Commission Tunisienne des Analyses Financières, created by Art. 118). Organic Law No. 2019-9 (Jan. 23, 2019) strengthened beneficial-ownership/PEP definitions, judicial interception rules, targeted financial sanctions, and NPO foreign-funding oversight — reforms credited with Tunisia's October 2019 removal from the FATF grey list. Criticized by human rights groups for overly broad definitions and extended pre-charge detention. Update (Run 72): a FATF technical mission conducted jointly with the Central Bank of Tunisia and CTAF visited Tunis and concluded, in late May 2026, without a joint declaration — a diplomatically rare outcome — after formulating "serious reservations" on beneficial-ownership-register effectiveness, supervision of non-financial professions (lawyers, notaries, accountants), delays implementing UN targeted sanctions, and limited access to financial information; the mission chief warned that absent corrective action, Tunisia risks reappearing on the FATF grey list at the next evaluation, with a MENAFATF plenary mutual-evaluation mission now expected between November 2026 and February 2027 (ilboursa.com, May/June 2026). Separately, a CTAF strategic note ("Terrorism Financing in Tunisia: Lessons Learned from Cases Submitted Between 2020 and 2025," covering 69 prosecutor referrals) reported suspicious-transaction reports nearly tripling from 446 (2020) to 1,334 (2025), with money laundering now accounting for at least 96% of cases versus roughly 1% for terrorism financing in the most recent three years — a marked compositional shift since the law's 2015 enactment (La Presse de Tunisie, July 2, 2026; corroborated by African Manager and Challenges.tn the same week). Both findings bear on this law's implementation rather than its text, which remains unamended since 2019-9. Update (Run 81): two independently-corroborated money-laundering/corruption appeal rulings, both from the Tunis Court of Appeal's specialized financial-corruption chamber, landed the night of July 2–3, 2026: (1) the "affaire du dégel des avoirs" (asset-unfreezing case), concerning a January 2018 restricted ministerial council decision that unfroze businessman Marouane Mabrouk's EU-frozen assets, ended with 6-year sentences (plus reported 800 million TND fines/restitution each) upheld on appeal against Mabrouk, former Prime Minister Youssef Chahed, and six former ministers (Mabrouk Korchid, Mohamed Ridha Chalghoum, Mohamed Anouar Maarouf, Khemaies Jhinaoui, Adel Grar, Lotfi Ben Sassi) for exploiting public office for an unjustified advantage — with former minister/deputy Mehdi Ben Gharbia's conviction overturned (non-lieu) on appeal, the only change from the March 3, 2026 first-instance judgment (Businessnews, Webdo, Gnet News, La Presse de Tunisie, all July 3, 2026); (2) a separate case naming Mabrouk alone saw his 14-year first-instance sentence (6 years embezzlement of public funds, 5 years money laundering, 3 years abuse of function within a public-capital entity, plus fines and a 100 million TND confiscation) confirmed "définitivement" on appeal (La Presse de Tunisie, Webdo, Tunisie Numérique, Kapitalis, all July 3, 2026). These are two distinct dockets, not additive sentences, and both concern implementation/enforcement of this law's money-laundering provisions rather than a text amendment. Update (Run 242): a first-ever repatriation operation of Tunisian women and children held in Syrian camps and prisons was reported underway July 10, 2026 — a development bearing on this law's "prohibition on travel to conflict zones for terrorist purposes" provision (see Key Provisions below) and the broader foreign-fighter-returnee question this statute was partly designed to address. According to Mostafa Abdelkebir, president of the Tunisian Observatory of Human Rights (OTDH), the group being processed comprises more than 200 children and over 60 women, whose fathers/husbands were affiliated with Daesh/ISIS (Businessnews.com.tn, Kapitalis, both July 10, 2026). This claim is flagged as single-advocacy-sourced and not yet officially confirmed — as of this run, no Tunisian government ministry, the Presidency, or an official communiqué had confirmed the operation, its scale, or a legal basis/procedure (e.g., any deradicalization, prosecution, or child-welfare framework to be applied on return); OTDH's estimates are explicitly self-described as unverified by the authorities. No dedicated Tunisian statute or decree governing repatriation of conflict-zone returnees could be identified — the topic instead touches this law's travel-related provisions, the Nationality Code (jus sanguinis transmission to children born abroad), and the Child Protection Code (Law No. 95-92, see Personal Status section) for any minors returned. Watch for official confirmation and any implementing legal framework in a future run.
Key provisions
  • Broad definition of "terrorist act" including threats to public order
  • Criminalization of glorification of terrorism and incitement
  • Criminalizes financing of terrorism and related financial flows; establishes AML/CFT obligations on banks, DNFBPs, notaries, and exchange bureaus
  • Prohibition on travel to conflict zones for terrorist purposes
  • Specialized Terrorism Chamber at the Court of First Instance of Tunis
  • Extended detention periods for terrorism suspects
  • Asset freezing without prior judicial authorization
CriminalSecurityFinancialAmended

Organic Law No. 2017-10 of March 7, 2017Declaration of Assets and Conflict of Interest

Enacted: March 7, 2017 | In force: 2017
Requires elected officials, senior public servants, and government appointees to declare assets, income, and interests. Creates the National Anti-Corruption Authority (INLUCC — Instance Nationale de Lutte contre la Corruption). Whistleblower protections included. Status as of June 2026: INLUCC's funding and operations were curtailed following the July 2021 presidential power consolidation, and unlike under the 2014 Constitution, the 2022 Constitution contains no provision establishing a constitutionally protected anti-corruption body — INLUCC has continued under a presidentially appointed interim head (since October 2022) with no permanent statutory replacement enacted, leaving Tunisia without an independent constitutionally entrenched anti-corruption authority. (Run 274): compare the Human Rights Instance entry above — a constitutionally-entrenched body whose enabling law was adopted but whose members have never been elected, the inverse failure mode from INLUCC's loss of constitutional status.
CriminalAdministrative

Anti-Money Laundering and Financing of TerrorismSee Organic Law No. 2015-26 (as amended)

Citation corrected — see below
Correction: This entry previously cited a standalone "Law No. 2019-23 of March 6, 2019" as Tunisia's AML/CFT statute — no such law could be verified against any primary source (JORT, legislation-securite.tn, FATF documentation), and it appears to have been a citation error, possibly conflating the amending law's year (2019) with the base law's number (26). Tunisia's actual, sole AML/CFT legal instrument is Organic Law No. 2015-26 of August 7, 2015 ("relative à la lutte contre le terrorisme et à la répression du blanchiment d'argent"), as amended by Organic Law No. 2019-9 of January 23, 2019 — FATF itself identifies it as "Organic Law Number 26 for the year 2015 relating to Countering Terrorism and Stopping Money Laundering." See the full entry under Criminal Law above for provisions, KYC/beneficial-ownership obligations, and the Financial Intelligence Unit (CTAF). This card is retained as a cross-reference so a reader searching "money laundering" or "AML" still finds the correct law.
CriminalFinancialAmended

Law No. 92-52 of May 18, 1992Narcotics and Psychotropic Substances

Enacted: May 18, 1992 | In force: 1992 | Amended: 1999, 2017
Governs narcotics and psychotropic substances including cultivation, production, trafficking, possession, and use. Imposes severe penalties — mandatory minimum 1 year for consumption (Art. 4), up to life imprisonment for trafficking. Article 4 on personal drug use attracted international criticism; the 2017 amendment introduced some discretion for judges in consumption cases. Pending reform (Run 45): a 17-article amendment bill, filed by roughly 15 deputies and transmitted to committee in April 2025, would combine expanded investigative tools against trafficking networks with a shift toward a therapeutic/medical approach to simple consumption; the Tunisian Society for Addiction Studies (STADD) presented its position at a May 22, 2026 committee hearing chaired by Faouzi Daas. Still in committee as of mid-2026; not yet enacted.
Key provisions
  • Art. 4: Personal consumption of narcotics — 1 to 5 years imprisonment + fine (mandatory)
  • Art. 5: Trafficking — 6 years to life imprisonment
  • Art. 12: Confiscation of proceeds and instruments of offenses
  • 2017 amendment: judges given discretion on sentencing for first-time consumption offenses
Criminal

Decree No. 2022-54 of September 13, 2022Combating Information Technology Offenses

Enacted: September 13, 2022 | In force: September 2022
Criminalizes "false information" spread online that "harms third parties, public order, or national defense." Criticized by press freedom organizations for its vague language and potential to criminalize journalism and free speech. Passed by presidential decree without parliamentary scrutiny.
CriminalDigitalPress

Law No. 2017-62 of July 24, 2017Administrative Reconciliation Law

Enacted: July 24, 2017 | In force: 2017
Controversial statute granting amnesty and legal impunity to civil servants who participated in corruption under the Ben Ali regime (1987–2011) in exchange for voluntary financial restitution to the state. Allowed implicated officials to resume positions in the public administration. Strongly opposed by civil society and transitional justice advocates as undermining accountability.
CriminalAdministrative

Decree-Law No. 2022-13 of March 20, 2022Criminal Reconciliation (Saied-Era)

Enacted: March 20, 2022 | Published: JORT No. 30, March 21, 2022 | Amended: Law No. 2024-3 of January 18, 2024
Distinct from the 2017 Administrative Reconciliation Law above: creates the National Commission for Criminal Reconciliation (CNRP), allowing businesspeople and officials accused of corruption, embezzlement, money laundering, or tax/customs/exchange-control violations to end prosecution by repaying misappropriated funds (plus 10%/year interest) or financing local/regional development projects. Targeted an initial list of roughly 460 businesspeople accused of misappropriating TND 10–13.5 billion. Amended by Law No. 2024-3 (passed by the ARP, 126 votes for), attaching the CNRP to the Presidency, adding two vice-presidents, and setting a 4-month maximum review period for applications. Update (Run 46): President Saied relaunched the process by swearing in a fully reconstituted CNRP before him at Carthage Palace on June 27, 2026, chaired by magistrate Ali Abbès; the appointment was formalized through Presidential Decree No. 2026-109 of June 8, 2026 (naming the chair) and Decree No. 131/2026, published in JORT on June 26, 2026, completing the commission's membership. At the swearing-in, Saied reaffirmed his framing of criminal reconciliation as a mechanism for recovering misappropriated public funds rather than a general amnesty.
CriminalCommercial

Decree-Law No. 2022-14 of March 20, 2022Anti-Speculation Decree-Law

Enacted: March 20, 2022 | Published: JORT No. 30, March 21, 2022
Criminalizes hoarding or concealing goods to create artificial scarcity or manipulate prices, and criminalizes spreading "false news" that causes consumers to avoid buying goods or disrupts supply chains. Standard penalty up to 10 years' imprisonment plus a TND 100,000 fine; aggravated penalties of 10–30 years for hoarding subsidized staples (e.g., flour). Amnesty International warned in March 2022 that the broad "false news" provision risks chilling public debate on food shortages.
CriminalCommercial

Law No. 2025-14 of July 28, 2025Penal Code Amendment — Abuse of Function

Enacted: July 28, 2025 | Published: JORT No. 96, July 29, 2025
Amends Articles 96 and 98 of the Penal Code governing "abuse of function" by public officials. Reduces the maximum prison sentence under Article 96 from 10 years to 6 years and converts the offense from strict liability to an intentional one, requiring proof the official deliberately sought an undue advantage to the detriment of the administration. Originated from 2023 parliamentary proposals; framed as reducing the "chilling effect" of Article 96 on civil servants' investment and administrative decisions.
CriminalAmended

Draft Amendment to Penal Code Arts. 261, 262 & 264 (pending)Harsher Theft & Aggravated-Robbery Penalties

Under General Legislation Committee review, hearings confirmed April 7 and May 16, 2026 | Committee requested resubmission: June 19, 2026 | Status: not yet enacted as of July 10, 2026
A pending bill that would repeal and replace Penal Code Articles 261, 262, and 264 (theft, aggravated theft, and embezzlement), sharply raising penalties: new Art. 261 would impose 15–20 years' imprisonment for aggravated theft committed under the circumstances numbered 1, 2, or 5 of Article 260 (e.g., theft with breaking-and-entering, by an armed person, or at night by two or more persons); new Art. 262 would impose 12–15 years for the circumstances numbered 3 or 4 of Article 260; new Art. 264 would set 5 years' imprisonment plus a 2,000 TND fine for lesser theft and embezzlement offenses. The draft excludes the mitigating-circumstance provisions currently available under the existing articles, a feature the Syndicat des Magistrats Tunisiens has publicly criticized as disproportionate and likely to remove judicial discretion in minor cases. Distinct from Law No. 2025-14 (July 28, 2025, above), which amended unrelated Penal Code Articles 96 and 98 on public officials' abuse of function. Update (Run 208): per La Presse de Tunisie (June 19, 2026, "Vols avec violence : vers une révision du Code pénal"), the General Legislation Committee (chaired by MP Faouzi Daâs), after hearings with Justice Ministry representatives, the judges' union, and criminal-law academics, found that the proposed amendments had substantially altered the original text and requested that its initiators file a new bill proposal rather than continue amending this one — effectively restarting the legislative process. Not yet enacted as of July 10, 2026.
CriminalPending

Decree-Law No. 2011-13 of March 14, 2011Confiscation of Assets (Ben Ali/Trabelsi Era)

Enacted: March 14, 2011 | Amended: Decree-Law No. 2011-47 of May 31, 2011 | Asset-management commission created by: Decree-Law No. 2011-68 of July 14, 2011
Citation correction (Run 157): this entry had cited "Law No. 2011-29 of April 18, 2011" since the artifact's earliest versions; no primary source (legislation-securite.tn's official database, ILO NATLEX, or independent Tunisian reporting) confirms a law of that number and date concerns confiscation, and it is now replaced with the verified citation. The actual instrument is Decree-Law No. 2011-13 of March 14, 2011, confiscating movable and immovable property and rights acquired after November 7, 1987 by Zine el-Abidine Ben Ali, Leila Trabelsi, and 112 named relatives/associates — roughly 550 properties, 48 boats/yachts, ~400 companies, and 367 bank accounts, valued near USD 13 billion. It was amended by Decree-Law No. 2011-47 of May 31, 2011, and a separate Decree-Law No. 2011-68 of July 14, 2011 created the National Commission for the Management of Confiscated/Recovered Assets and Funds to administer the seized estate (previously mischaracterized in this entry as "the Tunisian Privatisation Authority," and as an amendment rather than a distinct instrument). The National Commission for the Investigation of Corruption and Embezzlement (CNIICF) — also previously conflated with this decree-law — was in fact created separately and earlier, by Decree-Law No. 2011-7 of February 18, 2011, and was later superseded by INLUCC under the framework Decree-Law No. 2011-120 of November 14, 2011 (see the Reporting of Corruption entry). The Tunis Administrative Tribunal later found Decree-Law No. 2011-13 legally defective for never having been ratified by an elected assembly (neither the 2011 Constituent Assembly nor the 2014 ARP), a status that remains legally contested even as the confiscated estate continues to be administered in practice and feeds the asset pool addressed by the Criminal Reconciliation mechanism below.
CriminalAdministrative

Law No. 2016-61 of August 3, 2016Anti-Human Trafficking Law

Enacted: August 3, 2016 | In force: 2016
Comprehensive statute criminalizing all forms of human trafficking including labor exploitation, sexual exploitation, and organ trafficking. Defines aggravated offenses. Establishes the National Authority to Combat Trafficking in Persons (INLCTP). Provides victim protection, temporary residence permits for foreign trafficking victims, and rehabilitation assistance.
Criminal

Child Protection Code, Book III (Law No. 95-92 as amended)Juvenile Justice

Enacted: 1995 | Amended: 2002, 2008
Sets out a rehabilitative approach to juvenile delinquency: minors under 13 cannot be criminally prosecuted; those 13–17 may face protective measures or suspended sentences. Specialized Children's Courts presided over by a judge for children (juge des enfants). Diversion and rehabilitation preferred over incarceration. Placement in certified institutions possible.
CriminalPersonal Status

Organic Law No. 2017-10 of March 7, 2017 (Chapter V)Whistleblower Protections — Denunciation of Corruption

Enacted: March 7, 2017 | In force: March 2017 | Amendment bill pending since 2025
Chapter V of the Organic Law on the Good Governance and the Fight against Corruption establishes the first comprehensive whistleblower protection regime in Tunisia. Grants legal immunity and confidentiality to persons who report in good faith acts of corruption, embezzlement, or misuse of public funds to the national anti-corruption authority (INLUCC). Prohibits retaliation, demotion, or dismissal against public or private sector employees who report. Provides for witness protection measures in serious cases. Pending reform: a 42-article draft amendment, submitted by 15 MPs and under review by the ARP's Administration, Governance and Anti-Corruption Committee as of 2026, would more precisely define reporting procedures and protection timelines and create a new specialized anti-corruption body to centralize complaints and coordinate protective measures — filling the institutional gap left by INLUCC's effective sidelining since 2021 (see the Declaration of Assets entry). Not yet enacted.
Key provisions
  • Art. 44: Definition of protected disclosure in good faith
  • Art. 45: Prohibition of reprisals; reinstatement remedy
  • Art. 46: Confidentiality of reporter's identity
  • Art. 47: Criminal immunity for reporter (no prosecution for breach of professional secrecy)
  • Art. 48: INLUCC's duty to notify reporter of follow-up within 90 days
CriminalAdministrative

Law No. 2001-52 of May 14, 2001 (as amended)Penitentiary and Reform Law

Enacted: May 14, 2001 | Amended: 2016 (Decree-Law on probation)
Governs the organization of prisons, the treatment of detainees and convicted persons, and the conditions of detention in Tunisia. Establishes the rights of prisoners (medical care, education, family visits) and the duties of prison staff. A 2016 reform introduced electronic monitoring bracelets as an alternative measure for non-violent offenders, and expanded early-release (parole) conditions to ease prison overcrowding.
Key provisions
  • Art. 1–10: Organization of the prison service under the Ministry of Justice
  • Art. 11–30: Classification of detainees (pre-trial vs. sentenced)
  • Art. 31–50: Rights of prisoners — healthcare, education, family contact
  • Art. 51–70: Work and vocational training for sentenced prisoners
  • Art. 71–85: Disciplinary regime and sanctions within prisons
  • 2016 addition: Electronic monitoring bracelet (bracelet électronique) as alternative sentence
CriminalAdministrative

Decree-Law No. 74-20 of October 24, 1974 (reform bill pending, 2026)Games of Chance & Gambling

Baseline enacted: October 24, 1974 (ratified by Law No. 74-96 of December 11, 1974) | Reform bill filed: January 19, 2026 | Referred to committee: January 29, 2026 | Not yet enacted
Tunisia's baseline gambling statute, Decree-Law No. 74-20 of October 24, 1974 (on itinerant installations, parlor games, and lotteries), bans unlicensed games of pure chance and lotteries under decades-old, minor penalties; a companion Decree-Law No. 74-21 of the same date separately authorizes licensed tourist casinos. A group of 23 Assembly of People's Representatives deputies filed a proposition de loi on January 19, 2026, referred to the Commission de la Législation Générale on January 29, 2026, to broaden the definition of "jeux de hasard" to explicitly cover online and digital gambling, criminalize unlicensed online betting, and require internet service providers and payment processors to block access to, and freeze flows toward, unlicensed offshore gambling platforms. A separate, government-drafted bill on sports-betting oversight — developed by the Ministry of Youth and Sports (Minister Sadok Mourali) to align with international anti-money-laundering standards — has been sent to the Head of Government's office but as of mid-2026 remains awaiting Council of Ministers review, not yet before Parliament. Neither reform has been enacted as of July 2026; the 1974 framework remains in force. Note: some press reports cite specific penalty figures for the pending bill (prison terms, fine amounts); these could not be independently corroborated against a primary text and are omitted here.
CriminalDigital

Organic Law No. 2018-50 of October 23, 2018Elimination of All Forms of Racial Discrimination

Adopted by the ARP: October 9, 2018 | Promulgated: October 23, 2018 | In force: 2018
Landmark statute making Tunisia the first country in the Middle East and North Africa to criminalize racial discrimination. Article 2 defines racial discrimination broadly as any distinction, exclusion, restriction, or preference based on race, color, descent, or national or ethnic origin that impairs the equal enjoyment of rights and freedoms, aligning domestic law with Tunisia's obligations under the International Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of Racial Discrimination (ratified 1967). Criminalizes discriminatory acts, incitement to racial hatred, and public glorification of racial discrimination, with enhanced penalties for organized or repeated offenses; legal entities that promote racial discrimination face fines. Article 11 establishes a National Commission against Racial Discrimination, attached to the ministry responsible for human rights, tasked with data collection, monitoring, and policy proposals. Implementation note (2024–2026): civil society and international observers (EuroMed Rights, MERIP, Arab Reform Initiative) describe the law's practical enforcement as limited — few prosecutions have been brought under it directly — while Saadia Mosbah, president of the prominent anti-racism organization Mnemty, was instead prosecuted on unrelated money-laundering and "illicit enrichment" charges (May 2024 arrest; 8-year first-instance sentence upheld by the Tunis Court of Appeal's criminal chamber on June 23, 2026, with a combined fine over 100,000 dinars), a case Amnesty International and other rights groups characterize as the weaponization of ordinary criminal law against a leading advocate for the rights this statute was meant to protect. (Run 153 update): the same June 23, 2026 appeal ruling also disposed of five co-defendant Mnemty members on related illicit-enrichment/money-laundering/accounting charges: Ghofrane Binous (3 years, in absentia), Zied Rouin and Fares Gueblaoui (2-year suspended terms, with fines of 34,113.5 and 23,665 dinars respectively), two further members (2-year and 1-year suspended terms), plus a five-year stripping of voting and candidacy rights for all five — while three other co-defendants were acquitted (Human Rights Watch, July 7, 2026, corroborated by Businessnews and Kapitalis).
CriminalConstitutionalSocial

🏪 Commercial Law

Commercial Code — Law No. 59-129 of October 5, 1959Majallat Al-Tijara

Enacted: October 5, 1959 | In force: January 1, 1960 | Amended: multiple times (through 2024)
Core statute governing commercial activities, traders, and commercial acts in Tunisia. Defines the status of traders (commerçants), regulates commercial registers, commercial agencies, and trade names. Covers bills of exchange, promissory notes, cheques, and commercial contracts. Has been substantially amended since 1960 to modernize commercial law, most recently by Law No. 2024-41 (see separate entry) which overhauled the cheque provisions (Arts. 410 et seq.).
Key provisions
  • Art. 1–15: Definition of commercial acts and traders
  • Art. 16–30: Commercial register obligations
  • Art. 57–90: Agency and commercial representation
  • Art. 192–260: Bills of exchange (lettres de change)
  • Art. 261–290: Promissory notes (billets à ordre)
  • Art. 291–325, 410 et seq.: Cheques — form, endorsement, payment, protest (cheque-specific rules substantially rewritten by Law No. 2024-41, effective February 2, 2025)
  • Art. 400+: Commercial leases (fonds de commerce)
CommercialAmended

National Business Register Law — Law No. 2018-52 of October 29, 2018Registre National des Entreprises (RNE) (Run 54 addition; corrected Run 140)

Enacted: October 29, 2018 | Predecessor: Law No. 95-44 of May 2, 1995 (repealed)
Creates the Registre National des Entreprises (RNE), Tunisia's unified national business register, replacing the older Registre du Commerce. Correction (Run 140): 139 prior runs cited this entry's governing statute as Law No. 95-44 of May 2, 1995 ("Registre du Commerce," JORT No. 37, May 9, 1995, as amended by Law No. 2010-15 of April 14, 2010) — that citation is historically accurate but describes a law no longer in force. Independent verification (Article 64 of Law No. 2018-52 itself) confirms Law No. 95-44 was expressly and entirely repealed by Law No. 2018-52 of October 29, 2018, which created the RNE as the current single register that all traders and commercial companies must join, consolidating filings for incorporation, capital changes, management changes, and dissolution under one national database (superseding the fragmented central/local commercial-register offices of the 1995 regime) and organized under the Ministry of Industry and Trade. Law No. 95-44 is retained here only as historical background, not as the operative citation. Update (Run 104): the RNE announced on July 2, 2026 the "definitive and irreversible" end of paper-based filings — all registrations, capital/management changes, and dissolutions must now be completed online using the Mobile ID ("e-Houwiya," an administrative digital-identity service, not a standalone statute) or a DigiGo electronic certificate. RNE director general Adel Chouari described it as a transitional phase, with limited physical-document verification still possible temporarily. No new law or decree number accompanies this operational change — it implements Law No. 2018-52's existing filing obligations entirely online rather than amending them.
CommercialAdministrative

Law No. 2024-41 of August 2, 2024Reform of Bad-Cheque Rules (amending Commercial Code Arts. 410 et seq.)

Published in JORT No. 94: August 2, 2024 | Entered into force: February 2, 2025
Major reform of the bounced-cheque ("chèque sans provision") regime, widely described as a turning point given Tunisia's historically high rate of check-related criminal prosecutions. Shifts the system toward bank-side solvency screening, a centralized verification platform, and partial decriminalization for low-value cheques.
Key provisions
  • Banks must assess a customer's solvency before issuing a chequebook and set a global ceiling and validity period (max. 6 months) per chequebook based on financial capacity
  • Maximum face value capped at 30,000 TND per cheque leaf
  • Decriminalizes issuing a bad cheque for amounts ≤ 5,000 TND — recourse becomes a civil/payment-order matter unless the beneficiary initiates proceedings
  • Criminal liability retained for cheques exceeding the 5,000 TND threshold, fraud, or bad faith
  • Establishes the Central Electronic Cheque Platform (PECC), operated by the Central Bank of Tunisia, allowing real-time verification and reservation of available funds by beneficiaries
Update (Run 40): Central Bank of Tunisia payment-systems data published June 7, 2026 shows the reform's real-world effect: cheque volume fell from 12.25 million (2024) to 4.07 million (2025) — cited by the Justice Minister as roughly a two-thirds drop — with businesses substituting bills of exchange (lettres de change), which rose 35.9% in volume (1.2 million units) and 23.5% in value (TND 13.9 billion) in Q1 2026 alone, alongside strong growth in mobile and e-payments. (Run 53): Human Rights Watch's June 10, 2024 report "No Way Out: Debt Imprisonment in Tunisia" — published before this reform took effect — documented 496 officially confirmed (and up to ~7,200 business-association-estimated) people imprisoned for unpaid cheques, framing pre-reform practice as a disguised debt-imprisonment regime; see the new Debt-Recovery Companies entry below for the wider debt-collection framework this reform sits alongside.
CommercialAmended

Companies Code — Law No. 2000-93 of November 3, 2000Majallat Al-Sharikaat

Enacted: November 3, 2000 | In force: 2001 | Amended: 2002, 2005, 2009, 2016, 2019
Comprehensive statute regulating all forms of companies in Tunisia. Governs formation, management, operation, auditing, transformation, merger, and dissolution of companies. Covers: SARL (private limited company), SA (joint-stock company), SNC (general partnership), SCS (limited partnership), and cooperative societies.
Key provisions
  • Art. 1–50: General provisions applicable to all companies
  • Art. 51–128: Joint-stock companies (Sociétés Anonymes — SA): board of directors, general meetings, auditors
  • Art. 129–165: Private limited companies (SARL): capital minimum, management
  • Art. 166–200: General partnerships (SNC)
  • Art. 201–230: Limited partnerships (SCS, SCA)
  • Mandatory: external statutory auditor (commissaire aux comptes) for SA
  • Minority shareholder protection provisions
  • 2019 amendment: introduced single-member SARL (SUARL)
CommercialAmended

Collective Proceedings Code — Law No. 1995-34 of April 17, 1995Insolvency and Business Recovery

Enacted: April 17, 1995 | In force: 1995 | Amended: 1999, 2003, 2016
Governs insolvency, bankruptcy, judicial reorganization (redressement judiciaire), and liquidation of insolvent businesses. Establishes procedures for appointing judicial administrators and liquidators, protecting creditors, and rehabilitating viable enterprises. Amended in 2016 to expand preventive settlement mechanisms.
Key provisions
  • Preventive settlement (règlement amiable) for pre-insolvency situations
  • Judicial recovery (redressement judiciaire): 6-month observation period
  • Liquidation (faillite): asset realization and creditor distribution
  • Ranking of creditors: secured, preferred, unsecured
  • Art. 36: Suspension of creditor actions during reorganization
CommercialAmended

Consumer Protection Law — Law No. 92-117 of December 7, 1992

Enacted: December 7, 1992 | In force: 1993 | Amended: 2009, 2015
Protects consumers against defective goods, unfair commercial practices, misleading advertising, and price manipulation. Establishes the right to information, right to safety, and right to redress. Regulates consumer contracts including distance contracts and credit. Creates the National Council for Consumer Protection.
CommercialCivil

Law No. 98-40 of June 2, 1998Sales Techniques and Commercial Advertising

Enacted: June 2, 1998 (JORT No. 44, June 2, 1998) | In force: 1998
Tunisia's dedicated statute governing sales promotion techniques and commercial advertising, distinct from — and more detailed than — the general "misleading advertising" language in the Consumer Protection Law (No. 92-117) above. Chapter 1 defines the regulated actors (merchant, consumer, advertiser, product). Chapter 2 governs price-reduction sales (soldes/liquidations): a genuine reduction must be measured against the lowest price charged in the prior three months, restricts promotional sales during the 40 days preceding the two statutory seasonal-sales periods, and requires prior declaration to the Ministry of Commerce. Chapter 3 regulates off-premises (door-to-door) and distance selling. Chapter 4 requires advertisers to be able to substantiate the truthfulness of any factual claim and prohibits misleading advertising. Chapter 5 sets fines of 500–10,000 dinars for violations. (Added Run 163) A wholly new standalone entry, previously undocumented across 162 prior runs despite the Consumer Protection Law's overlapping general language; verified via jurisitetunisie.com's full-text database, cross-confirmed by the JORT n°44 filename on pist.tn (Tunisia's official scientific/technical information portal, which mirrors JORT issues). No amendment to this 1998 law was found in this run's research; comparative-advertising and pyramid-scheme provisions, if any, were not located within this specific text and may fall under Competition Law (No. 2015-36) instead.
CommercialCivil

Draft Law No. 113/2020 (adopted by Council of Ministers, never promulgated)Consumer Credit & Household Over-indebtedness

Adopted by Council of Ministers: December 2020 | Status: Never promulgated; revived debate 2026
Tunisia has no dedicated statute defining household over-indebtedness or regulating consumer-credit affordability; a bill (referenced in secondary sources as "Loi n° 113/2020") addressing both was approved by the Council of Ministers in December 2020 but was never submitted to, or adopted by, parliament, leaving a persistent legal vacuum — no over-indebtedness observatory and no statutory debt-to-income caps. Correction: credit-bureau infrastructure specifically is no longer entirely absent — see the separate Credit Reporting (Credit Bureau) Decree-Law entry below, a narrower licensing framework that does not itself address affordability caps or over-indebtedness. Household debt reached an estimated TND 32.2 billion in 2024 (91% bank-issued, 9% microfinance), with a household debt-service burden reported to have risen from 107% to 171% of disposable income between 2014 and 2024. In March 2026 the Institut Arabe des Chefs d'Entreprises (IACE) published a policy note ("Le surendettement des ménages : une urgence nationale") renewing calls to promulgate the dormant bill and proposing debt-to-income caps (40% for consumer credit, 50% for mortgage credit) plus governorate-level over-indebtedness commissions offering a 7-year repayment plan, partial debt write-off with reintegration commitments, or full write-off paired with a 3-year credit ban. As of mid-2026 no such law had been enacted. Note: the "113/2020" bill number is drawn from secondary press/policy sources, not independently verified against a primary JORT or ARP record.
CommercialFinancialSocial

Decree-Law No. 2022-2 of January 4, 2022Credit Reporting (Credit Bureau) Licensing

Enacted: January 4, 2022 | Published: JORT N°2, January 5, 2022 | BCT implementing circular: No. 2022-09 of October 25, 2022
Creates a licensing ("agrément") regime, under Central Bank of Tunisia (BCT) supervision, for private "sociétés d'information sur le crédit" (credit-reporting companies/credit bureaus) — a narrower measure than, and distinct from, the still-unenacted household over-indebtedness bill above; it does not itself impose affordability caps or debt-to-income limits. Article 7 licensing criteria cover shareholder quality, technical/IT capacity, management fitness, governance, and personal-data-protection compliance (cross-referencing Law No. 2004-63). One firm, MITIGAN CIB, was initially licensed, but the BCT withdrew its agrément on March 17, 2026 for failing to launch operations within the required timeframe — leaving Tunisia with no operating licensed credit bureau as of mid-2026 despite the legal framework being in force since 2022.
FinancialDigital

Law No. 98-4 of February 2, 1998Debt-Recovery Companies

Enacted: February 2, 1998 | Amended: Law No. 2001-91 of August 7, 2001; Law No. 2003-42 of June 9, 2003
Regulates "sociétés de recouvrement de créances" (third-party debt-collection companies), setting minimum capital, licensing conditions, and permissible collection practices; Law No. 2001-91 and Law No. 2003-42 subsequently amended aspects of the regime. Actual debt-collection litigation runs through ordinary civil process under the Code of Obligations and Contracts and Code of Civil and Commercial Procedure. Separately, Tunisia's Commercial Code criminalizes issuing a bad cheque (see Law No. 2024-41 above), which Human Rights Watch's June 10, 2024 report "No Way Out: Debt Imprisonment in Tunisia" documented as a de facto debt-imprisonment regime — citing official figures of 496 people imprisoned for unpaid cheques and business-association estimates as high as ~7,200, and arguing the practice is inconsistent with ICCPR Article 11's prohibition on imprisonment for inability to fulfill a contractual obligation.
FinancialCivil

Competition and Prices Law — Law No. 2015-36 of September 15, 2015

Enacted: September 15, 2015 | In force: 2015 | Replaced: Law No. 91-64
Governs competition policy, prohibits anti-competitive agreements, abuse of dominant position, and anti-competitive mergers. Establishes the Competition Council (Conseil de la Concurrence) as independent regulatory authority. Regulates prices for essential goods and services. Pending reform: the Competition Council's president announced in April 2025 a planned "radical revision" of the law to align it with COMESA competition standards; as of mid-2026 this remained in a preparatory/consultative phase with no bill text yet before the ARP. Enforcement (added Run 141): the Conseil de la Concurrence issued three separate decisions dated December 31, 2025 (reported January 7–8, 2026) fining companies a combined TND 4.2 million under this Law's Article 5 anti-competitive-practices provisions: TND 1.2 million against a pharmaceutical-sector company, TND 2.5 million combined against companies in the smartphone market, and TND 0.5 million combined against packaging-sector companies — a distinct, dated enforcement action under the currently-enacted law, separate from the still-pending COMESA-alignment revision above.
Commercial

Electronic Commerce Law — Law No. 2000-83 of August 9, 2000

Enacted: August 9, 2000 | In force: 2000 | Amended: 2004
Cross-referenced from the Digital Law & ICT section (see the fuller entry there). First statute to legally recognize electronic contracts and digital signatures in Tunisia; governs online commercial transactions, electronic payment, and online offers.
CommercialDigital

Law No. 94-89 of July 26, 1994Financial Leasing Law

Enacted: July 26, 1994 | In force: 1994
Regulates financial leasing (crédit-bail / ijar) of movable and immovable assets. Defines the leasing contract, rights and obligations of lessor and lessee, and end-of-term purchase options. Financial leasing companies must be licensed by the BCT. Key instrument for SME financing in Tunisia.
CommercialFinancial

Law No. 88-92 of August 2, 1988, as amended by Law No. 95-87 of October 30, 1995 (Run 146 correction)SICAR Law

Base law enacted: August 2, 1988 | SICAR-specific amendment: October 30, 1995 | Further amendment: 1992
Run 146 correction: prior runs cited only "Law No. 95-87 of October 30, 1995" as if it were the foundational statute; independent verification against jurisitetunisie.com and the CMF's own official reference PDF confirms the actual framework-creating law is Law No. 88-92 of August 2, 1988 "sur les sociétés d'investissement," which repealed Law No. 59-29 and created Tunisia's investment-company regime (SICAV — variable-capital, and SICAF — fixed-capital), laying the initial groundwork for venture capital. Law No. 95-87 (following an intermediate 1992 amendment) is the amendment that specifically developed and refined the SICAR (Société d'Investissement à Capital Risque) sub-category for venture-capital financing of SMEs at various growth stages — a real but distinct legislative act from the 1988 base law, not a standalone "SICAR Law" in its own right. SICARs invest in the capital of SMEs and startups and benefit from significant tax advantages: contributions to SICARs are deductible from taxable income, and SICARs are exempt from capital-gains tax on their principal-activity dispositions where at least 80% of resources are invested within the second year following each capital release. Regulated by the CMF (Financial Market Council).
CommercialFinancial

Law No. 96-80 of October 8, 1996 (amending COC)Commercial Agents Law

Enacted: October 8, 1996 | In force: 1996
Regulates commercial agency agreements (contrats de représentation commerciale). Defines the rights and obligations of commercial agents (independent representatives). Provides mandatory compensation to agents on termination (indemnité de clientèle). Applied in cross-border distribution and agency arrangements.
Commercial

Law No. 2020-37 of August 6, 2020 (Run 103 correction)Crowdfunding Law

Adopted by ARP: July 21, 2020 (127–0–0) | Enacted/published: August 6, 2020 | Implementing decrees: Nos. 2022-765 & 2022-766 of October 19, 2022
Run 103 correction: prior runs cited this statute as "Decree-Law No. 2020-170 of November 10, 2020" — no such decree-law could be independently verified in any source searched (CMF, WebManagerCenter, ilBoursa), and it appears to have been a fabricated citation. The verified statute, adopted by the Assembly of the Representatives of the People on July 21, 2020 and published in JORT on August 6, 2020, is Law No. 2020-37, establishing Tunisia's legal framework for crowdfunding (financement participatif) across three models — loan-based, securities/equity investment (limited to SARLs and SAs), and donation-based — through platforms that must be Tunisian-registered joint-stock companies, with projects restricted to those carried out in Tunisia. Regulatory oversight is split among the CMF (securities/equity crowdfunding), the BCT (loan-based crowdfunding), and the Microfinance Control Authority. Implementing decrees were delayed roughly two years, finally issued as Decree No. 2022-765 (crowdfunding in securities investment) and Decree No. 2022-766 (crowdfunding lending), both of October 19, 2022. Enables startups under the Startup Act to raise equity via registered platforms.
CommercialDigitalFinancial

Law No. 2008-66 of October 20, 2008Factoring (Affacturage)

Enacted: October 20, 2008 | In force: January 2009
Establishes the legal framework for factoring operations (affacturage) in Tunisia. Authorizes banks and specialized financial institutions licensed by the Banque Centrale de Tunisie to provide factoring services — the purchase of trade receivables from commercial enterprises at a discount. Regulates the assignment of receivables to the factor, notification obligations to debtors, and the recourse/non-recourse models. Facilitates access to working capital for Tunisian exporters and domestic suppliers.
Key provisions
  • Art. 1–5: Definition of factoring; licensed operators
  • Art. 6–12: Assignment of receivables — form and notification to debtors
  • Art. 13–18: Rights of the factor against debtor and seller
  • Art. 19–22: Recourse factoring vs. non-recourse factoring
  • Art. 23–27: BCT regulatory oversight; reporting obligations
CommercialFinancial

Law No. 2009-69 of August 12, 2009Commerce de Distribution & Franchise Regulation (Run 81 addition)

Enacted: August 12, 2009 | Published: JORT N°65, August 14, 2009 | Implementing decree: Décret n° 2010-1501 of June 21, 2010
A wholly new standalone entry, confirmed via direct reading of the primary text hosted by jurisitetunisie.com and a Ministry of Commerce document. Tunisia's general framework statute for the retail/distribution sector, regulating large-surface retail authorizations, commercial-zoning requirements, and — the aspect most relevant to cross-border business — franchise contracts. Article 14 defines the franchise contract; Article 15 (not Article 14, a common misattribution in secondary sources) imposes a mandatory pre-contractual disclosure document at least 20 days before signature, with nullity as the sanction for non-compliance. No statutory anti-competitive-clause restriction was found within Articles 14–17 of the base law itself; that restriction, where reported, appears to stem from the implementing decree or a separate competition-law order rather than the 2009-69 text directly, and is flagged here as unconfirmed at the statutory level.
Commercial

👷 Labor Law

Labor Code — Law No. 66-27 of April 30, 1966Majallat Al-Shughl

Enacted: April 30, 1966 | In force: May 1966 | Amended: 1994, 1996, 2006, 2007, 2011, 2017
The primary statute governing employment relationships in Tunisia. Covers individual employment contracts, conditions of work, wages, termination, workplace safety, collective bargaining, and trade unions. Applies to the private sector; public servants are governed by the General Statute of Civil Servants. Implements ILO core labor conventions (Tunisia has ratified 62 ILO conventions).
Key provisions
  • Art. 6: Employment contract — types (fixed-term, indefinite-term, project-based)
  • Art. 14–22: Trial period (période d'essai)
  • Art. 23–35: Obligations of employer and employee
  • Art. 40: Minimum wage (SMIG/SMAG) — set by decree
  • Art. 73–83: Annual paid leave — minimum 1 day per month (18 days/year)
  • Art. 90–100: Work hours — 48 hours/week maximum (40 hours in industry)
  • Art. 120: Dismissal — requires just cause; procedural requirements for economic dismissal
  • Art. 123: Severance pay for unfair dismissal
  • Art. 152: Trade union rights; freedom of association
  • Art. 155: Right to strike (with pre-notice requirements)
  • Art. 193–200: Labor inspectorate powers
  • Art. 230–240: Occupational health and safety requirements
LaborAmended

General Statute of Civil Servants — Law No. 83-112 of December 12, 1983

Enacted: December 12, 1983 | In force: 1984 | Amended: 2007, 2011, 2017
Governs the employment conditions, rights, and obligations of Tunisian civil servants (fonctionnaires). Covers recruitment, grades, promotions, disciplinary procedures, leave, retirement, and termination. Complemented by specific statutes for particular sectors (teachers, judges, military, etc.).
LaborAdministrative

Social Security Law — Law No. 60-30 of December 14, 1960

Enacted: December 14, 1960 | In force: 1961 | Amended: extensively
Establishes the compulsory social security system. Organizes the National Social Security Fund (CNSS for private sector) and the National Retirement and Social Welfare Fund (CNRPS for public sector). Covers retirement pensions, invalidity, survivors' benefits, occupational accidents, and maternity allowances.
LaborSocial

Occupational Accidents and Diseases Law — Law No. 94-28 of February 21, 1994

Enacted: February 21, 1994 | In force: 1994
Defines occupational accidents and diseases, establishes mandatory employer liability, and sets compensation levels for temporary or permanent disability and death. Requires employers to report occupational accidents within 2 days. Organizes compulsory insurance through CNSS.
Labor

Law No. 2025-9 of May 21, 2025Employment Reform Law

Enacted: May 21, 2025 | Published: JORT May 23, 2025
Landmark reform overhauling Tunisia's employment framework. Three pillars: (1) Indefinite employment contracts (CDI) are the statutory default; CDDs (fixed-term contracts) permitted only in three circumstances: temporary workload increase, replacement of absent worker, and seasonal work; any non-compliant CDD is void and automatically converted to CDI with full seniority. (2) Prohibition of labor subcontracting — workers supplied via subcontracting arrangements are deemed permanent employees of the beneficiary company; employers have 3 months to regularize. (3) Criminal penalty of TND 10,000 fine for engaging prohibited subcontracting. Contested by employer federations; supported by UGTT.
Labor

Law No. 2025-18 of December 22, 2025Public-Sector Recruitment of Long-Term Unemployed Graduates

Adopted by ARP: December 16, 2025 (92 for, 4 abstentions, 1 against) | Published: JORT No. 2025-153, December 23, 2025 | In force: December 23, 2025
An 8-article member's bill (sponsored by the "Pour que le peuple triomphe" parliamentary bloc) creating an exceptional mechanism for public-sector and civil-service recruitment of higher-education graduates in long-term unemployment, applied alongside the General Statute of Civil Servants above. Priority is given to candidates over 40 and to those whose diploma is 10+ years old, limited to one member per family, via a dedicated digital application platform. Excludes candidates who previously benefited from a regularization program, who had continuous social-security affiliation, who held a tax ID in the prior year, or who hold loans exceeding TND 40,000. Recruitment is staged in waves over up to 3 years; Finance Law 2026 (Law No. 2025-17) Art. 57 allocates budget for a first tranche of roughly 3,000 graduates. Status as of July 2026: implementing decrees remain unpublished; affected graduates staged protests (Siliana, reported July 1, 2026; a further protest planned for Tunis on July 8, 2026) demanding their publication. Update (Run 73): protest organizers put the affected pool at roughly 60,000 over-40 graduates out of some 137,000–140,000 unemployed higher-education graduates nationally, and specify three demands for the July 8 protest — publication of the implementing decrees, the promised digital application platform's launch, and a public implementation timetable (webdo.tn, directinfo.webmanagercenter.com, July 1, 2026). Update (Run 167): the July 8, 2026 protest occurred as planned — dozens of graduates demonstrated near Place de la Kasbah in Tunis renewing the same three demands — with the implementing decrees and digital platform still unpublished as of that date (webdo.tn, July 8, 2026). Update (Run 201): escalating beyond street protest, 52 ARP deputies sent a formal petition to the Head of Government on July 9, 2026 — over seven months after JORT publication — demanding the same three items: publication of the implementing decrees, launch of the digital application platform, and a public recruitment timetable (La Presse, July 9, 2026).
LaborAdministrative

Decrees No. 2026-63 through 2026-69 of April 30, 2026Public & Private Sector Wage Increase Package 2026–2028

Published: JORT No. 44, April 30, 2026 | Implements: Finance Law 2026 (Law No. 2025-17), Art. 15 | Effective: retroactive to January 1, 2026 | Covers: 2026–2028
A seven-decree package implementing Finance Law 2026 Art. 15 and Labor Code Art. 40, staging wage and pension increases across the entire public and private sectors over three years (2026–2028). Update (Run 38): previously this entry documented only the SMIG/SMAG/collective-agreement decrees (66–68); the package's other four decrees, covering the civil service, public enterprises, and the judiciary, are now added. Decree No. 2026-63 sets the raise program for agents of the state, local authorities, and administrative public establishments (fonctionnaires). Decree No. 2026-64 covers employees of public enterprises and establishments governed by special statutes or establishment-level collective agreements, staging monthly increases of TND 120 (cadres), TND 100–105 (supervisory/maîtrise), and TND 90 (execution-grade) across 2026–2028. Decree No. 2026-65 raises the "prime de magistrature" by TND 120/year (2026–2028) for judges of the judicial order, the Administrative Court, and the Court of Accounts, also extended to their retirement pensions. Decree No. 2026-66 sets the guaranteed agricultural minimum wage (SMAG) for workers 18 and over at TND 21.336/day in 2026, rising to TND 22.400 (2027) and TND 23.520 (2028), plus a technical-skill premium for specialized agricultural workers. Decree No. 2026-67 sets the guaranteed inter-professional minimum wage (SMIG) for non-agricultural sectors at TND 554.736/month (48-hour regime) or TND 470.251/month (40-hour regime) in 2026, rising in stages to TND 611.520 / TND 517.571 by 2028. Decree No. 2026-68 mandates an annual 5% increase in base salaries plus transport and attendance allowances for non-agricultural sectors covered by sectoral collective bargaining agreements, with monthly increases of roughly TND 36–46 for line workers, ~TND 53 for supervisory staff, and ~TND 69 for management-grade employees in 2026. Decree No. 2026-69 sets smaller, separately staged increases (roughly TND 30–39/month execution-grade, TND 44 maîtrise, TND 58–64 cadres in 2026, rising through 2028) for non-agricultural Labor Code employees covered by neither an enterprise-specific statute nor a sectoral collective agreement. A further schedule of monthly increases (TND 90–120, staged by category A1/A2/A3/B and phased through 2027–2028) applies to specified "sensitive" public-service corps, including health professionals (physicians, dentists, pharmacists, veterinarians), oversight/control bodies, scientific researchers, university academic staff, social-service personnel, and labor inspectors. Across all seven decrees, the increases extend to retirement pensions, are non-cumulative with certain pre-existing allowances, are excluded from year-end/performance-bonus calculations, and remain subject to standard social-security contributions; non-compliance with the SMIG/SMAG minimums is subject to the penalties under Art. 3 of the Labor Code Law of April 30, 1966.
LaborAdministrativeJudicial

Circular No. 11 of the Head of Government, August 11, 2025End of Trade-Union Secondment

Issued: August 11, 2025 (effective same day) | Reported: August 14, 2025
A circular from the Head of Government (then Sarra Zaafrani Zenzri) to all ministers and secretaries of state cancelling, effective August 11, 2025, all standing authorizations for "détachement syndical" — the practice of seconding civil servants to work full-time for trade-union structures while remaining on the public payroll. Ministries were instructed to compile lists of seconded agents, recall them to their original posts, and pursue "appropriate administrative and judicial procedures" against those who refuse. UGTT deputy secretary-general Sami Tahri called the measure "a political gesture" — arguing union secondment had "practically disappeared since 2022" — intended to "incite a war against union rights" rather than address a live administrative problem; some press subsequently referred to the instrument as "Decree No. 20," though the underlying text is a head-of-government circular, not a numbered decree. Confirmed via two independent French-language reports (webdo.tn and WebManagerCenter, both dated August 14, 2025) that agree on the August 11 effective date. Contemporaneous with, but legally distinct from, the Finance Law 2026 Art. 15 wage-setting-by-decree mechanism above, which separately bypassed UGTT's traditional role in tripartite wage negotiation — together read by UGTT and independent commentators as a broader narrowing of the union's institutional role in 2025–2026.
Labor2025

Law No. 2024-44 of August 12, 2024Maternity and Paternity Leave Reform

Adopted by ARP: July 31, 2024 (111-1-4) | Published: JORT No. 2024-099, August 12, 2024
Restructures maternity and paternity leave across the public and private sectors. Expands paternity leave from the prior baseline of 1 day (private sector, Labor Code) / 2 days (civil servants) to 7 days at full pay — extended to 10 days for multiple births or a newborn with disability, prematurity, or a congenital condition requiring care, plus 3 additional days in the event of stillbirth; must be taken within 30 days of the birth. Restructures maternity leave into a prenatal component (up to 15 days in the final month, on medical order) and a postnatal component extended from 30 to 90 days (120 days for complicated or multiple births).
LaborSocialPersonal Status

Law No. 93-10 of February 17, 1993 (as amended)Vocational Training Law

Enacted: February 17, 1993 | In force: 1993 | Amended: multiple times
Framework law governing vocational and professional training in Tunisia. Creates the National Agency for Vocational Training (ATFP — Agence Tunisienne de la Formation Professionnelle). Establishes the national apprenticeship system, certification standards, and quality assurance for vocational qualifications. Employers must contribute 1% of payroll to the Vocational Training Fund (TFP).
LaborEducation

Digital Platform ("Gig-Economy") Workers — Regulatory Vacuum (Run 99 addition)

UGTT study launched: June 2025 | Findings presented: June 29, 2026
A genuine legislative gap, previously undocumented despite 98 prior research runs: Tunisia has no law, decree, or Labor Code amendment specifically classifying or regulating digital-platform ("gig-economy") workers — the general Labor Code (No. 66-27, above) applies loosely at best, since most platform workers are treated as independent contractors rather than employees. A UGTT research-department study, launched June 2025 and presented June 29, 2026, found roughly 60,000 workers across four categories (commercial order delivery, pharmacy/medicine delivery, home care/caregiving, and motorcycle passenger transport) operating in what it called the "parallel economy," entirely outside any legal framework: 98% lack any social coverage, 60% of delivery workers are denied all insurance, and 75% suffer irregular income. UGTT's recommendations: integrate platform workers into the CNSS social-security regime, mandate clear written contracts defining the parties' relationship, create professional/union representation mechanisms, provide occupational health and safety training, and establish a national database of platform workers. The union also invoked newly-adopted ILO Convention No. 193 on decent work in the platform economy (see the International Law & Treaties section's companion entry) as a reference framework. No Tunisian bill or draft text addressing platform work has been filed with the ARP as of this run; corroborated across Business News, L'Économiste Maghrébin, WebManagerCenter, La Presse de Tunisie, Gnet News, and allAfrica, all dated June 29–30, 2026. Update (Run 378): the government moved from documenting the gap to publicly proposing to close it: Minister of Social Affairs Issam Lahmar, opening a July 14, 2026 workshop on new forms of employment and decent work, called for adapting several Labor Code provisions to establish a balanced framework covering digital-platform work, and for deepened tripartite consultation toward an inclusive national approach (La Presse de Tunisie, July 14, 2026). UGTT responded on July 17, 2026 by demanding formal, mandatory inclusion of its social-protection and informal-sector department in the technical committees that will draft the resulting sectoral bills, specifically naming delivery workers' (livreurs) and ride-hailing drivers' (chauffeurs) legal status as the priority to address within the Labor Code revision (La Presse de Tunisie and allAfrica, both July 17, 2026). Still no bill text has been filed with the ARP on gig/delivery-platform work specifically — see the separate, already-filed freelance/digital-economy self-employment bill below, which addresses a related but distinct category of independent digital workers. Update (Run 438): Minister Lahmar advanced from the July 14 workshop's general call to a concrete two-bill framing: opening the 8th Arab Conference on Pensions and Social Insurance in Tunis (July 20–22, 2026), he announced that two draft laws are nearing finalization as part of a broader social-security/protection-system overhaul — one dedicated to regulating private-sector telework, the other specifically to the digital "platform economy," intended to give new forms of employment a legal framework, support the underlying economic activity, and extend social protection to the workers concerned (Business News and La Presse de Tunisie, both July 20, 2026; corroborated by allAfrica same-day). Still pre-filing — neither bill had been transmitted to the ARP as of this update.
LaborSocialGap

Freelance Work & Digital-Economy Self-Employment Bill (Proposition de loi)

Filed at the ARP: June 9, 2026 | Status: pending committee examination, not yet enacted
A member's bill (proposition de loi) filed with the Assembly of the Representatives of the People on June 9, 2026, creating for the first time a dedicated legal framework for independent ("freelance") workers operating in Tunisia's digital economy, distinct from the gig/delivery-platform gap tracked in the companion entry above. Structured in six substantive chapters plus a seventh on transitional provisions. Chapter I sets general provisions and defines the bill's core concepts: independent/freelance work, the legal status of the freelance worker, electronic-payment platforms, and dedicated bank accounts for freelance-related transactions. A further chapter sets out state obligations to support independent workers, including facilitating access to the global digital-services market and to electronic payment for cross-border freelance income (Business News, June 16, 2026). The transitional-provisions chapter establishes a phase-in period of not less than five years, during which caps on certain electronic-payment and funds-transfer operations, together with security and financial-verification (anti-money-laundering/KYC-style) procedures, apply on a graduated basis to manage the sector's controlled integration into the formal economy — addressing long-standing complaints from Tunisian freelancers about restricted access to international payment platforms (PayPal, Payoneer, and similar services) and the foreign-currency repatriation rules under the Foreign Exchange Code (see that entry). As of this update, the bill remains at the pre-committee stage at the ARP; no committee referral, hearing schedule, or vote has been reported (Business News, June 16, 2026; Directinfo/WebManagerCenter, June 16, 2026).
LaborCommercial2026Pending

🏛️ Administrative Law

Administrative Court Law — Law No. 72-40 of June 1, 1972

Enacted: June 1, 1972 | In force: 1972 | Amended: multiple times
Establishes the Administrative Court (Tribunal Administratif) as the supreme administrative jurisdiction. Has jurisdiction over disputes between citizens and public bodies, annulment of illegal administrative acts, and administrative contracts. Also serves as the Supreme Audit Institution reviewing public accounts. Organized into chambers; appeals go to Administrative Court plenary.
Administrative

Local Government Code — Law No. 2018-29 of May 9, 2018Majallat Al-Jama'aat Al-Mahaliyya

Enacted: May 9, 2018 | In force: May 2018 | Implements: 2014 Constitution Chapter VII
Comprehensive decentralization legislation replacing the previous municipalities law. Organizes three levels of local government: municipalities (balladiyyat), regions (wilayat), and districts (aqalim). Establishes directly-elected municipal councils with legislative powers in local matters. Introduces participatory budgeting and citizens' right to petition. Implemented after 2018 municipal elections. Superseded in part: Organic Law No. 2025-4 of March 12, 2025 (JORT No. 30, March 13, 2025; adopted by the ARP February 27, 2025, 109–2–4) repealed this code's region and district provisions (and Organic Law No. 89-11 of February 4, 1989 on regional councils), replacing the elected-region model with local, regional, and district councils organized by decree, and transferring former regional councils' assets and holdings to the State for governors to administer. The 2018 code's municipal-level provisions remain in force; local council elections continue to be organized under Decree-Law No. 2023-10 of March 8, 2023 (see the CNRD entry in Constitutional Law).
Key provisions
  • Three levels: municipality, region (governorate), district
  • Directly elected councils at all three levels
  • Fiscal autonomy — own revenue sources and transfers from central budget
  • Participatory democracy mechanisms (citizen petitions, public consultation)
  • Gender parity requirement for municipal council lists
  • Youth quota (minimum 1/3 of each list under 35)
Administrative

Public Procurement Decree — Decree No. 2014-1039 of March 13, 2014

Enacted: March 13, 2014 | In force: 2014 | Amended: 2022
Governs all public procurement by state entities, public establishments, and local authorities. Establishes competitive bidding requirements, transparency obligations, evaluation criteria, and remedies for tenderers. Creates the National Commission for Public Procurement Investigations (HAICOP). Sets thresholds for direct award, restricted tendering, and open tendering. Pending reform (added Run 122): Head of Government Sarra Zaâfrani Zenzri presided over a Council of Ministers session on November 22, 2025 devoted to overhauling the public-procurement system, described in a January 3, 2026 report as "one of the most important legislative reforms currently under examination" for its expected effect on investment and competition; as of this run the reform remains at the policy-objective stage, with no decree or bill number yet filed to amend or replace Decree No. 2014-1039.
Administrative

Access to Information Decree — Decree-Law No. 2011-41 of May 26, 2011

Enacted: May 26, 2011 | In force: 2011 | Strengthened by 2016 Organic Law
Grants citizens the right to access information held by public bodies. Establishes a 20-day response deadline for requests. Replaced in part by the more comprehensive Organic Law No. 2016-22 of March 24, 2016, which created an independent Access to Information Commission (IVD predecessor).
AdministrativePress

Law No. 2016-46 of July 11, 2016Electronic Procedures in Administrative Transactions

Enacted: July 11, 2016 | In force: 2017
Authorizes and regulates e-government services. Allows administrative procedures to be conducted electronically. Establishes standards for electronic authentication, digital archives, and online payment to the state. Part of Tunisia's e-government reform strategy. (Run 111) A "zero paper" administrative-digitalization measure took effect July 1, 2026, extending electronic authentication and paperless processing across further categories of state administrative transactions under this law's framework.
AdministrativeDigital

Law No. 2021-35 of July 21, 2021Administrative Procedure Law

Enacted: July 21, 2021 | In force: 2021 | Partially implemented
Codifies and standardizes administrative procedures, notification requirements, and citizens' rights when dealing with public bodies. Establishes the "silence means approval" principle for certain administrative decisions (within 30 days). Strengthens citizens' right to be heard before adverse administrative decisions. Partially implemented pending regulatory decrees.
Administrative

Law No. 2017-58 addendum / Circular No. 39 of 2017Civil Service Reform

Enacted: 2017
Introduces performance management and results-based evaluation for civil servants. Creates management by objectives (MBO) framework for public entities. Reorganizes career tracks and introduces merit-based promotion criteria. Part of broader public administration reform supported by World Bank.
AdministrativeLabor

Law No. 99-32 of April 13, 1999National Statistics System (Run 81 addition)

Enacted: April 13, 1999 | Published: JORT | In force: 1999
A wholly new standalone entry — despite 80 prior research runs, no dedicated entry existed for the statute organizing Tunisia's official statistics. Confirmed via direct reading of the law's full 29-article primary text (hosted by the National Institute of Statistics, ins.tn) cross-checked against legislation-securite.tn. Establishes the "Système National de la Statistique" (Art. 12), comprising the National Statistics Council (CNS), the National Institute of Statistics (INS) as the system's central executive body (Art. 18–19), other public statistical bodies, and statistical-training institutions. Article 3 guarantees the system's structures scientific/methodological independence and impartiality in their statistical work, and Article 5 imposes a 60-year confidentiality rule on individual survey data collected under the law.
Administrative

Law No. 82-85 of December 2, 1982Engineers Profession Organization

Enacted: December 2, 1982, ratifying Decree-Law No. 82-12 of October 21, 1982 | Amended: Law No. 97-41 of June 9, 1997
Creates the Ordre des Ingénieurs Tunisiens (OIT) and restricts legal use of the professional title "ingénieur" — and provision of engineering services under that title — to persons registered on the Order's roll; an engineering diploma alone does not confer the right to use the title. OIT's missions include upholding professional honor and ethics and advising on national engineering-workforce planning. A reform petition to modernize OIT governance was reported circulating among engineers as of late 2025, with no bill filed with the ARP as of this run. (Run 90 addition)
Administrative

Administrative Mediator — Law No. 93-51 of May 3, 1993Médiateur Administratif (Run 179 addition)

Institution created: Decree No. 92-2143 of December 10, 1992 | Legal framework: Law No. 93-51 of May 3, 1993 | Amended: Law No. 2000-16 of February 7, 2000; Law No. 2002-21 of February 14, 2002 | Attributions/organization decree: Decree No. 96-1126 of June 15, 1996
A wholly new standalone entry — despite 178 prior research runs across this section, no dedicated entry existed for Tunisia's ombudsman-equivalent institution. The Médiateur Administratif ("Administrative Mediator") was created by Decree No. 92-2143 of December 10, 1992 and given its operating legal framework by Law No. 93-51 of May 3, 1993, later extended by Law No. 2000-16 of February 7, 2000 and Law No. 2002-21 of February 14, 2002; Decree No. 96-1126 of June 15, 1996 (superseding the 1992 decree's own provisions) fixes the institution's attributions and its administrative/financial organization. Appointed by decree for a renewable five-year term, the Mediator examines individual and corporate complaints against state services, local authorities, public establishments, and public enterprises, and — per the law's own independence guarantee — "receives no instructions from any public authority" while examining a request. The Mediator directs the institution and administers its own budget, which attaches to the general state budget but falls under the Presidency's direct authority; regional mediation offices were added following the 2000 amendment to extend the service nationwide. Recommendations issued are non-binding, distinct from the judicially enforceable rulings of the Administrative Court (Law No. 72-40, above). Tunisia's Administrative Mediator is a member of the Association des Ombudsmans et Médiateurs de la Francophonie (AOMF). Confirmed via legislation-securite.tn's primary-text reproductions of Law No. 93-51 and Decree No. 96-1126, and the AOMF's institutional profile of Tunisia.
Administrative

Proposition de Loi Organique (unnumbered, ~10 deputies)Administrative Transparency & Anti-Lobbying Bill (Run 284 addition)

Reported: July 10, 2026 | Status: recently submitted to ARP Commission de la Législation Générale | Not yet numbered | Not yet enacted
A deputy-initiated organic bill of 19 articles, carried by a group reported as around ten deputies, aimed at curbing "powerful lobbies" and administrative non-compliance/evasion by public officials. Per a July 10, 2026 La Presse editorial ("Quand la justice retrouve ses clés") and a July 11, 2026 allAfrica republication, the text introduces prison sentences of up to three years and increased, "finally dissuasive" fines, and imposes a transparency obligation on administrations via mandatory annual reports, framed by the source as a first step toward institutional accountability. Caveat: neither article named the bill's official title, an assigned proposition/projet de loi number, or the sponsoring deputies, and a direct fetch of the La Presse source (its primary framing is a broader opinion piece on judicial enforcement) likewise did not surface these details — this entry is added on the strength of the specific, dated (July 10–11, 2026), multi-outlet-corroborated factual claims (19 articles, ~10 deputies, 3-year maximum prison term, annual-report transparency mechanism) while the bill's formal identifiers remain unconfirmed and are a priority to pin down in a subsequent run. Distinct from the unrelated, already-documented Public Procurement reform (still at the policy-objective stage, no bill filed) and the Tax Adviser organic bill (proposition n° 2023-013) above.
AdministrativePending

💰 Tax & Fiscal Law

Income Tax Code — Law No. 89-114 of December 30, 1989Code de l'Impôt sur le Revenu des Personnes Physiques et l'Impôt sur les Sociétés

Enacted: December 30, 1989 | In force: January 1, 1990 | Amended: annually in Finance Laws
Governs personal income tax (IRPP) and corporate income tax (IS) in Tunisia. Personal income tax is progressive with rates from 0% to 35%. Corporate income tax standard rate is 15%, with higher rates for banks and telecoms (35%) and reduced rates for SMEs and certain sectors. Amended annually through the Finance Law. Digital fiscal cash-register mandate (added Run 280): a Ministerial Order of October 14, 2025, implementing CIRPP/IS Art. 59 ter, phases in mandatory digital cash registers ("caisses enregistreuses numériques") for on-site food/beverage-consumption businesses (restaurants, cafés, tea rooms, fast food), defined as any establishment selling prepared food or drink for consumption on the premises. Phase 1 (November 1, 2025): classified tourist restaurants and 2nd/3rd-category cafés. Phase 2 (July 1, 2026): extended to all other legal entities (companies) in the sector. Phase 3 (July 1, 2027): extends to real-regime individual taxpayers filing monthly returns. Phase 4 (July 1, 2028): extends to remaining individual operators. Distinct from the Finance Law 2026 Art. 56 e-invoicing mandate (see below), which governs invoice issuance rather than point-of-sale transaction recording; confirmed via La Presse, Tunisie Numérique, Le Temps News, and L'Economiste Maghrébin, cross-checked against each other.
Key rates (as of 2024)
  • Personal income tax: 0% up to 5,000 TND, 26% bracket, 35% top rate
  • Corporate income tax: 15% standard rate
  • Banks, financial institutions, telecoms: 35%
  • SMEs: reduced rate of 10% (conditions apply)
  • Withholding tax on dividends: 10%
  • Capital gains tax: included in income tax
TaxAmended

VAT Code — Law No. 88-61 of June 2, 1988Code de la Taxe sur la Valeur Ajoutée

Enacted: June 2, 1988 | In force: January 1, 1989 | Amended: annually
Establishes value-added tax in Tunisia. Standard rate 19%; reduced rates of 13% (certain services) and 7% (basic goods, agricultural products). Exports are zero-rated. Registration required for businesses with turnover exceeding 100,000 TND/year (services) or 200,000 TND (goods).
TaxAmended

Tax Procedures Code — Law No. 2000-82 of August 9, 2000Code des Droits et Procédures Fiscaux

Enacted: August 9, 2000 | In force: 2001 | Amended: annually
Unifies and governs all tax administration procedures including filing obligations, tax audits, assessment, payment, penalties, and dispute resolution. Establishes taxpayers' rights during tax audits. Provides for administrative appeals before tax committees and judicial appeals before administrative courts.
Tax

Customs Code — Law No. 2008-77 of December 22, 2008
