Political party financing regulated: prohibits foreign funding, limits campaign spending. Update (Run 201): a wholly separate legislative track — a 6-article proposition de loi formally filed July 9, 2026 by deputy Fatma Mseddi — would designate Ennahdha as the Muslim Brotherhood's "political and organizational continuation" in Tunisia and classify it (along with any linked association or structure) as a terrorist organization, distinct from this decree-law's own party-dissolution mechanism; Mseddi had raised the idea informally since 2023, and this filing now formally opens the ARP's ordinary legislative examination process. No committee hearing date has yet been set (L'Économiste Maghrébin, Business News, both July 9, 2026).
AdministrativeConstitutional

Organic Law No. 2014-16 of May 26, 2014 (as amended)Electoral Law

Enacted: May 26, 2014 | In force: 2014 | Amended: 2019, 2022, September 27, 2024
Governs elections for the President of the Republic, the Assembly of People's Representatives, and the Assembly of Regions and Districts. Establishes the Independent High Authority for Elections (ISIE) as the permanent electoral commission. Sets rules on candidacy, campaigning, vote counting, and dispute resolution. The 2022 amendments (Decree-Law 2022-22) modified ISIE membership and reduced its independence according to critics. September 27, 2024 amendment: adopted by the ARP just nine days before the October 6, 2024 presidential election, it transferred jurisdiction over electoral disputes — including candidate-eligibility appeals previously heard by the Administrative Court — to the ordinary Court of Appeal of Tunis, with a 48-hour deadline to file contested-result challenges. The change followed Administrative Court rulings reinstating several candidates ISIE had excluded, and was criticized by Human Rights Watch and other observers as weakening judicial checks on election integrity at a sensitive moment.
ConstitutionalAdministrative

Draft Law No. 027/2023 and Government Counter-Draft (2024)Proposed NGO Reform (Pending)

Parliamentary draft introduced: 2023 | Government draft reported: July 2024 | New organic law proposal: April 2026 | Status: Not enacted as of July 2026
Several competing proposals to replace Decree-Law No. 2011-88 on associations. The most advanced, presented to the ARP in April 2026, is a comprehensive organic law proposal of 45 articles across 8 chapters governing the creation, management, financing, oversight, and sanctioning of associations. Its Article 26 would ban all foreign funding of associations without prior written authorization from the Prime Minister's office (Présidence du Gouvernement), with a 2-month response deadline and implicit approval if the deadline lapses unanswered. The ARP held a dedicated study day on the bill in April 2026, and its Rights and Freedoms Committee was tasked with finalizing its report by June 24, 2026. Strongly opposed by Tunisian civil society, Article 19, ICNL, and Amnesty International as reversing the 2011 notification-based system. As of July 2026, no final law adopted; the 2011 Decree-Law remains formally in force, though see the entry below on its administrative circumvention in practice.
AdministrativeConstitutional

Proposition de loi organique (May 2026)Unified Electoral Code Proposal

Presented: May 10, 2026 (initial proposal); May 19, 2026 (expanded coverage) | Status: Under committee review, not enacted
A group of 29 Assembly of People's Representatives deputies presented an organic law proposal to consolidate Tunisia's electoral and referendum rules — currently scattered across the base 2014 Electoral Law and its many subsequent amending organic laws and decree-laws (including the 2022 ISIE reorganization and the September 27, 2024 dispute-jurisdiction amendment already documented above) — into a single unified electoral code. Transmitted to the ARP's Committee on Internal Rules, Electoral Laws, and Parliamentary Functions on May 7, 2026. As drafted, the code runs to 261 articles across three parts: (1) rules for direct elections (presidential, legislative) and referendums; (2) rules for regional/district council elections and the Conseil National des Régions et des Districts; and (3) transitional and final provisions. The proposal would centralize rules governing presidential, legislative, municipal, local, and regional elections plus referendums in one text, revise the distribution of electoral constituencies, address vacant-seat procedures in overseas electoral districts, and clarify the relationship between candidates and the Court of Accounts on campaign-finance disputes. A May 19, 2026 committee session opened debate on two contested provisions carried over from earlier electoral-law drafts: a proposed 5,000-dinar candidacy deposit (caution) and a minimum education-level requirement for candidates, both described in Tunisian press coverage as a "sensitive debate" among deputies. Distinct from, and broader in scope than, the narrower September 2024 amendment; as of July 2026 it remains under committee examination with no vote scheduled.
ConstitutionalAdministrative

Administrative Suspensions & Judicial Dissolution Proceedings Against Civil Society OrganizationsLTDH, ASF Tunisia, ATFD, FTDES, Al Khatt/Inkyfada & Mnemty (2025–2026)

ATFD & FTDES measures: 2025 | Al Khatt & Mnemty suspensions: October 2025 | LTDH suspension: notified April 24, 2026, for one month | Avocats Sans Frontières Tunisia suspension: notified May 5, 2026 | Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution case opened: May 11, 2026, postponed to June 1 then July 6, 2026 | LTDH court appeal: postponed June 10, 2026, to July 15, 2026, then postponed a third time to July 29, 2026
Rather than amending Decree-Law 2011-88 itself, authorities have increasingly relied on administrative and fiscal powers to suspend prominent civil society organizations' activities. In April 2026, the Ligue Tunisienne pour la Défense des Droits de l'Homme (LTDH) — founded 1976, one of the oldest human rights organizations in Africa and the Arab world, and a member of the National Dialogue Quartet awarded the 2015 Nobel Peace Prize — was formally notified of a one-month suspension of its activities, officially justified on grounds of regularizing its fiscal and administrative situation. LTDH filed a court appeal against the suspension; the competent chamber of the Tunis Court of First Instance held a hearing on June 10, 2026 but postponed examination of the appeal to July 15, 2026, leaving the matter unresolved as of this update. (Run 299 update): Kapitalis and Business News (both June 10–11, 2026) report that approximately 180 lawyers have formally constituted themselves as part of LTDH's defense team for the appeal — an unusually large collective mobilization by the bar reflecting the case's profile — with the hearing still confirmed for tomorrow, July 15, 2026, and no further postponement or early ruling reported by any outlet checked as of this update. Update (Run 313): as of this run's cutoff — the same calendar day as the scheduled hearing — no ruling or outcome has been reported by any outlet checked (Business News, Réalités, Kapitalis, Gnet), consistent with the possibility that same-day coverage has simply not yet been published or indexed. One relevant procedural note not previously flagged: Tunisian courts' annual judicial recess ("vacations judiciaires") conventionally begins July 15, which may affect whether and how quickly this appeal is actually resolved on the scheduled date; this is offered as plausible context rather than a confirmed explanation for the reporting gap, since no source directly linked the recess to this specific case. Update (Run 315): a further same-day sweep (French, English, and Arabic-language search) still found no ruling reported by any outlet, including ONAT's and LTDH's own channels — the reporting gap now spans the entirety of the hearing day itself, consistent with the judicial-recess timing noted at Run 313 rather than any indication the hearing was cancelled or further postponed. Update (Run 342): the July 15, 2026 hearing did not, in fact, produce a ruling — African Manager and Gnet (both July 15, 2026) report the Tunis Court of First Instance's correctional chamber adjourned the appeal a third time, to July 29, 2026, to allow time for a response to a report from the prosecutor general for state litigation ("rapport du procureur général auprès du contentieux de l'État"). This resolves the ambiguity Runs 313 and 315 had flagged around the judicial-recess timing: the case was formally postponed rather than silently unreported. Days later, in May 2026, Avocats Sans Frontières Tunisia (ASF Tunisia) was notified of a comparable suspension. The Tunisian Association of Democratic Women (ATFD) and the Tunisian Forum for Economic and Social Rights (FTDES) faced comparable measures during 2025, part of a wave reported by rights groups as exceeding 20 NGO suspensions since July 2025. Decree-Law 2011-88, Art. 26 requires dissolution to occur only by judicial order; rights groups (FIDH, Committee for Justice, SNJT) characterize these administrative suspensions as circumventing that judicial-order requirement and warn they set a precedent for sidelining the 2011 framework without formally repealing it. Update (Run 39): Amnesty International (May 2026) and Human Rights Watch (June 9, 2026) reports independently put the wider tally at roughly 25 associations affected by suspension or comparable administrative obstruction since mid-2025, and name additional organizations beyond the four detailed above, including Aswat Nissa, the World Organisation Against Torture's Tunisia programme (OMCT-Tunisia), I Watch (the Tunisian chapter of Transparency International), and the investigative outlet Nawaat. Both organizations frame the pattern as a coordinated use of fiscal-regularization and administrative-authorization pretexts against rights, anti-corruption, and media-adjacent groups specifically, rather than an across-the-board enforcement sweep. This remains executive/judicial practice under the existing Decree-Law 2011-88, distinct from the still-pending organic-law reform of that decree tracked in the entry above. Dissolution escalation (added Run 173): two of the suspended associations have since progressed from suspension to actual judicial dissolution proceedings — a materially more severe stage of the same Decree-Law 2011-88, Art. 26 mechanism. Al Khatt, founded December 2013 and the publisher of the investigative outlet Inkyfada since 2014 (an ICIJ partner), was notified of a 30-day suspension in October 2025 over disputed foreign-funding declaration compliance; its court challenge to that suspension failed, after which the Presidency of the Government (La Kasbah) filed a formal request for Al Khatt's judicial dissolution, announced by the association April 21–23, 2026. The Tunis Court of First Instance opened examination of the dissolution request on May 11, 2026 — jointly with an identical dissolution request against Mnemty, the anti-racism association whose president Saadia Mosbah was separately convicted on unrelated money-laundering/illicit-enrichment charges (see the Racial Discrimination Law entry) and which had itself been administratively suspended in October 2025 — with the session postponed first to June 1 and then to July 6, 2026 "to allow completion of case-file documents." No outcome has been confirmed from any source as of this run's cutoff. Update (Run 315): a dedicated search for post-July 6 coverage again found nothing beyond the same pre-hearing postponement reporting already captured — the dissolution outcome remains unreported, now nine days on. RSF, CPJ, the SNJT, and ICIJ all condemned the Al Khatt proceeding as the culmination of a multi-year pressure campaign (repeated multi-month freezes of Al Khatt's bank accounts, a January 2026 financial-crimes-unit summons) rather than a response to any single new complaint, and warned that dissolving a media outlet's publishing association achieves the same silencing effect as a direct press-law prosecution while bypassing press-specific legal safeguards entirely — see also the Decree-Law 2022-54 entry's note on CPJ's broader May 2026 "Beyond Decree 54" findings. Update (Run 186): the Intersection association for rights and freedoms' civil-society monitoring report (published via Kapitalis, July 8, 2026, and the same source underlying the Abdallah Saïd case added at Run 184) puts a first aggregate figure on the pattern's scale over its full May 2024–May 2026 monitoring window: 88 documented cases affecting civil-society organizations and activists, comprising 47 restriction/prosecution situations against associations and organizations and 41 separate judicial proceedings against individual activists and organizational leaders, of which 8 involved pre-trial detention (34 defendants prosecuted but released, 7 first-instance convictions recorded), plus 22 separate one-month administrative suspension orders against associations. The report frames the pattern as resting on four recurring mechanisms: criminalization via counterterrorism and anti-money-laundering statutes, rapid-succession administrative suspension orders, financial obstruction through frozen bank accounts, and official stigmatization branding targeted organizations as "traitors" or "foreign agents" — and documents concrete service losses from the suspensions already detailed above, including the ATFD closure eliminating four support centers for abuse victims and the loss of psychological services for refugee and migrant children. Update (Run 477): a further individual civil-society detention, previously absent from this artifact, was identified and added as a standalone entry below: the Saloua Ghrissa case (executive director of the Association for the Promotion of the Right to Difference, held over fifteen months in pretrial detention on foreign-funding allegations before her March 19, 2026 provisional release), corroborated across Amnesty International, Human Rights Watch, Business News, Nawaat, and Kapitalis.
AdministrativeConstitutional

Law No. 69-4 of January 24, 1969Public Meetings, Processions & Demonstrations

Enacted: January 24, 1969 | In force: 1969 | Replaced: colonial-era decrees of 1905 and 1936
Regulates public meetings, processions, parades, demonstrations, and gatherings (réunions publiques, cortèges, défilés, manifestations et attroupements) — the base statute governing freedom of assembly in public spaces, distinct from and predating Decree-Law No. 2011-88 above (which governs the formation of associations, not the conduct of gatherings). Requires prior declaration to governorate or delegation authorities, or to the National Security Directorate in Tunis, typically 3 to 15 days before the event; authorities may restrict or ban gatherings on public-order grounds. Frequently cited in UN Special Rapporteur communications and civil-society reporting on freedom of assembly in Tunisia. No amendment or replacement bill was identified as of July 2026.
AdministrativeSecurityConstitutional

🛡️ Security & Counter-terrorism

Organic Law No. 2015-26 of August 7, 2015Countering Terrorism and Suppression of Money Laundering (as amended)

Enacted: August 7, 2015 (corrected from a previously listed July 7, 2015) | Amended: Organic Law No. 2019-9 of January 23, 2019
Cross-referenced from the Criminal Law section (see the fuller entry there for provisions and the 2019-9 amendment). Comprehensive counter-terrorism statute enacted following the Bardo Museum and Sousse beach attacks (2015). Replaced the 2003 anti-terrorism law. Defines terrorism broadly, criminalizes financing, propaganda, recruitment, and travel for terrorist purposes. Establishes specialized anti-terrorism courts and the Financial Intelligence Unit (CTAF). Criticized by human rights groups for overly broad definitions and extended pre-charge detention.
Key provisions
  • Broad definition of "terrorist act" including threats to public order
  • Criminalization of glorification of terrorism and incitement
  • Criminalizes financing of terrorism and related financial flows
  • Prohibition on travel to conflict zones for terrorist purposes
  • Specialized Terrorism Chamber at the Court of First Instance of Tunis
  • Extended detention periods for terrorism suspects
  • Asset freezing without prior judicial authorization
SecurityCriminal

Law No. 2019-73 of December 31, 2019Internal Security Forces Statute

Enacted: December 31, 2019 | In force: 2020
Governs the organization, status, and conduct of Tunisia's internal security forces (National Police and National Guard). Defines missions, hierarchy, disciplinary procedures, and accountability mechanisms. Establishes an independent oversight body for police conduct complaints.
SecurityAdministrative

Law No. 2013-50 of December 19, 2013Compensation for Internal Security Force Work Accidents & Occupational Diseases (Run 120 addition)

Enacted: December 19, 2013 | Published: JORT No. 101, December 20, 2013 | Pending amendment: Proposition de loi n° 42/2025 (referred to committee May 15, 2025)
A wholly new standalone entry — despite extensive existing coverage of the Internal Security Forces Statute (Law No. 2019-73, above), this distinct compensation statute had never been documented. Establishes a special regime (régime particulier de réparation) compensating internal security force (police and National Guard) agents, cadets at their basic-training academies (per implementing Decree No. 2021-365 of May 26, 2021), or their surviving dependents, for bodily harm from workplace accidents and occupational diseases. Defines a workplace accident as any incident occurring "by reason of or on the occasion of" service duties and recognizes a schedule of presumed occupational illnesses. A dedicated medical commission establishes the causal link, sets the disability rate, and authorizes treatment. Compensation takes the form of lump-sum capital for disability rates of 5–15%, or an ongoing pension for rates of 15% and above, alongside full medical-care coverage, salary continuation during temporary incapacity, prosthetics, and survivor benefits (spouse, orphans, dependents) with enhanced allowances for dependent children and those requiring third-party care. Pending amendment: Proposition de loi n° 42/2025, referred to the ARP's Defense, Security and Armed Forces Commission on May 15, 2025, would revise Article 42 to extend the regime's benefits to agents whose disability rate was administratively determined before the law's December 2013 entry into force — closing a transitional-wording gap affecting an estimated ~400 agents, mostly retirees. Per a July 1, 2026 committee report, the bill has been finalized and transmitted to the ARP's Bureau, pending a study session ahead of plenary examination; not yet enacted as of this run.
SecuritySocialPending

State of Emergency Decree — Decree No. 78-50 of January 26, 1978

Enacted: January 26, 1978 | In force: 1978 | Continuously renewed since November 2015
Establishes the legal framework for declaring and managing states of emergency. Grants expanded executive powers including restriction of movement, house arrest, administrative detention, and closure of meeting places. Tunisia declared a state of emergency in November 2015 (after the Bardo Museum and Sousse beach attacks) and has renewed it without interruption since — now its tenth consecutive year. The most recent renewal, Décret n° 2026-15 of January 30, 2026 (JORT), extended the nationwide state of emergency declared by Décret n° 2025-485 of December 29, 2025 through December 31, 2026.
SecurityConstitutional

Code de Justice Militaire — Decree No. 57-9 of January 10, 1957 (extensively amended)

Enacted: January 10, 1957 (JORT N°4, January 11, 1957) | Key amendments: Décret-loi n° 86-5 of September 16, 1986 (ratified by Loi n° 86-101 of December 9, 1986); Décret-loi n° 2011-69 of July 29, 2011; Loi n° 2024-35 of July 15, 2024
Correction (Run 134): this entry previously cited a non-existent "Law No. 86-79 of September 9, 1986" as the code's origin and asserted a "2019 amendment" restricting military-court jurisdiction over civilians. Independent, twice-corroborated research found neither claim verifiable in any Tunisian legislative database and both are now removed. The correct originating instrument is Decree No. 57-9 of January 10, 1957, subsequently amended by Décret-loi n° 86-5/1986 (ratified by Loi n° 86-101/1986), the post-revolution procedural reform Décret-loi n° 2011-69 of July 29, 2011 (created a military appeals chamber and expanded civil-party rights, but did not remove civilian jurisdiction), and most recently Loi n° 2024-35 of July 15, 2024, which narrowly amended Article 68's definition of desertion abroad in peacetime (unauthorized border crossing or absence from one's unit for 3+ days in peacetime, reduced to 1 day in wartime; also covers failure to return within 3 days after leave, mission, or training abroad ends). Military courts retain jurisdiction over civilians under Article 8 of the code, and Article 91 (criticizing/insulting the armed forces) remains actively used to prosecute journalists and activists: the 2014 Constitution's transitional provisions kept the 1957 code in force pending amendment, a November 2018 reform bill to exclude civilians from military jurisdiction stalled in the ARP and was never adopted, and civilian prosecutions have continued and increased under President Kais Saied — the National Syndicate of Tunisian Journalists (SNJT) recorded 39+ cases against journalists before military courts since May 2023, including journalist Nasreddine Halimi's March 2025 sentencing by the Kef Military Court over Facebook posts. Confirmed by Amnesty International, Human Rights Watch (World Report 2025), the ICJ, and Tunisia's November 2022 UN Universal Periodic Review submission.
SecurityCriminal

Weapons Law — Law No. 69-33 of June 12, 1969

Enacted: June 12, 1969 | Published: JORT N°23, June 13, 1969 | Implementing decree: Decree No. 70-60 of February 21, 1970
Regulates the import, trade, possession, and carrying of weapons and ammunition in Tunisia. Classifies weapons into five categories: military weapons, defensive/sporting weapons, hunting weapons, historical/collectible weapons, and air rifles. No unified "Code des Armes" exists — the framework remains this single 1969 law plus implementing decrees, with no confirmed later amendment identified.
SecurityCriminal

Law No. 93-121 of December 27, 1993National Civil Protection Office (Fire & Rescue Services) (Run 81 addition)

Enacted: December 27, 1993 | Org-chart decree: Decree No. 99-2058 of September 13, 1999 | Administrative/financial-organization decree: Decree No. 94-568 of March 15, 1994
A wholly new standalone entry — despite extensive coverage of the National Police and National Guard under the Internal Security Forces Statute above, Tunisia's separate civil-defense/fire-and-rescue institution had never been documented. Creates the Office National de la Protection Civile as an établissement public à caractère industriel et commercial (EPIC) with legal personality and financial autonomy, headquartered in Tunis and placed under the supervision of the Ministry of Interior, tasked broadly with disaster and catastrophe response (Art. 2). Confirmed via legislation-securite.tn and cross-checked secondary sourcing; the primary JORT/legislation.tn text itself could not be directly reached (HTTP 503), so this entry carries medium-high rather than primary-source-verified confidence. Note: Decree No. 99-2058 only fixes the Office's organizational chart, not general implementation — Decree No. 94-568 (administrative and financial organization) is the more substantive implementing text.
SecurityAdministrative

Law No. 2002-81 of August 3, 2002 (as amended)Private Security, Guarding & Valuables-Transport Activities (Run 159 addition)

Enacted: August 3, 2002 | Amended: Law No. 2008-14 of February 18, 2008
A wholly new standalone entry, missed by all 158 prior runs. Regulates three categories of private security activity: property monitoring/guarding ("gardiennage"), armed transport of funds and precious metals, and physical protection of persons. Companies and their legal representatives must obtain prior authorization from the Minister of Interior (Art. 4), which is personal, non-transferable, and valid for a five-year renewable term. Licensing conditions (Art. 6) require Tunisian nationality held for at least five years, enjoyment of civil and political rights, absence of convictions for theft, fraud, forgery, or terrorism-related offenses, good conduct, commercial registration, no bankruptcy history, and no concurrent professional activity. Employees (Art. 11) must meet similar nationality/conduct standards, be at least 20 years old, hold a ninth-grade education minimum, and pass physical/mental fitness requirements; agents carry a professional card whose specimen and uniform were separately fixed by Interior Ministry orders of June 11, 2003 (professional-card digitalization introduced in 2024). Article 32 criminalizes unauthorized practice or continuing operations after suspension/revocation, punishable by three years' imprisonment and a fine of TND 50,000–100,000. Law No. 2008-14 subsequently amended several provisions, including the criminal-penalty and transitional-requirement articles. (Run 160 update) Article 15 confines licensed agents strictly to their authorized "gardiennage"/protection mission and expressly bars them from crime-prevention or suspect-pursuit acts, and from any act infringing a person's freedom of movement, physical integrity, or private life (including body/bag searches or seizure of personal effects without the owner's express consent); physical force is prohibited except in self-defense. Article 16 further confines agents to their assigned premises, with street-level intervention authorized only exceptionally, against theft or property damage. Read together, these provisions are the legal basis for the position — confirmed by the Interior Ministry's licensing framework — that private-detective and private-investigation activity (surveillance, filature, personal inquiries) is not a licensable profession in Tunisia, notwithstanding the many "private detective" services advertised online to the Tunisian market.
SecurityAdministrative

"Sumood/Global Sumud Flotilla" Money-Laundering & Terrorism-Financing Prosecutions (Run 91 addition)

Arrests: March 6–7, 2026 | Remanded into custody: March 16, 2026
A wholly new standalone entry, entirely distinct from the "state security conspiracy" cluster above — at least seven Tunisian activists linked to the Sumood/Global Sumud Flotilla coordination unit (a pro-Palestinian, Gaza-bound aid-flotilla solidarity network), including Nabil Channoufi, Wael Naouar, Sana Msahli, Jawaher Channa, and Mohamed Amin Bannour, were arrested March 6–7, 2026 and remanded into custody March 16, 2026 after prolonged anti-terrorism-unit questioning. Prosecutors invoke Organic Law No. 2015-26 (Countering Terrorism and Suppression of Money Laundering, see entry above) alongside general money-laundering provisions, charging the group with "forming a money laundering conspiracy" and misuse of funds raised through an international crowdfunding campaign tied to the first Global Sumud Flotilla's September 2025 Gaza-aid mission. Tunisian and international rights groups (Amnesty International, Middle East Monitor) have called for the activists' release, framing the prosecution as a use of counter-terrorism/AML statutes against pro-Palestinian civic activism rather than a genuine financial-crime case; no trial date or verdict has been reported as of this run.
SecurityCriminal

Affaire Bilel Chaouachi — Terrorist-Organization Leadership Conviction (Run 152 addition)

First instance: December 11, 2025 | Confirmed on appeal: July 6, 2026
A wholly new standalone entry, distinct from the state-security-conspiracy cluster and the Secret Apparatus/Instalingo cases above — an ordinary (non-political) prosecution under the Anti-Terrorism Law (Organic Law No. 2015-26, see Countering Terrorism entry above) of a jihadist leader rather than an opposition figure. Bilel (also spelled Bilal) Chaouachi, alias Abou Yahya Zakaria, a leader of Ansar al-Charia and Daech/ISIS, was prosecuted for terrorist acts committed in Syria, planning terrorist operations inside Tunisia, and recruiting and facilitating young Tunisians' departure to conflict zones. He appeared in a video released after the November 24, 2015 bombing of a presidential security bus in central Tunis, threatening further attacks; he has been listed on Tunisia's national list of terrorist persons (with an associated asset freeze) since 2019. The Tunis Court of First Instance's anti-terrorism chamber sentenced him to 55 years' imprisonment on December 11, 2025, and the Tunis Court of Appeal confirmed the full sentence on July 6, 2026 — corroborated across Kapitalis, Directinfo/WebManagerCenter, webdo, and La Presse de Tunisie.
SecurityCriminal

Affaire "Créateurs de Contenu" — Mass TikTok/Instagram Prosecution (Run 155 addition)

Justice Ministry announcement: October 27, 2025 | Verdict: July 6, 2026
A wholly new standalone entry, distinct from the state-security-conspiracy, Secret Apparatus, Instalingo, and Chaouachi cases above — an ordinary (non-political) mass prosecution of social-media "content creators." Following an October 27, 2025 Justice Ministry announcement of prosecutions targeting online content "contrary to public morals," the criminal chamber of the Tunis Court of First Instance referred 18 defendants to trial and, on July 6, 2026, sentenced them to terms ranging from 1 to 27 years' imprisonment. Charges spanned production, dissemination, publication, or transmission of sexually explicit videos and images tied to TikTok and Instagram activity, alongside human trafficking, money laundering, and drug-trafficking/promotion offenses. Prosecutors relied on three statutes in combination: Organic Law No. 2015-61 of August 3, 2015 (preventing and combating trafficking in persons, see Criminal Law section), Decree-Law No. 2022-54 of September 13, 2022 (combating offenses relating to information and communication systems, see Digital Law section), and Organic Law No. 2015-26 of August 7, 2015 (countering terrorism and money laundering, see entry above) (webdo.tn, July 7, 2026).
SecurityCriminalDigital

Proposition de loi organique (unnumbered, filed by Deputy Fatma Mseddi + 9 co-sponsors, July 9, 2026)Classification of Ennahdha as a "Terrorist Organization" (Run 400 addition)

Filed with ARP: July 9, 2026 | Status: referred, no committee hearing date confirmed as of this run
A wholly new standalone entry closing a gap in the artifact's Security & Counter-terrorism coverage: a six-article draft organic law submitted to the ARP on July 9, 2026 by Deputy Fatma Mseddi together with nine co-sponsoring deputies, presented as aimed at "protecting national security, state sovereignty, and the republican regime" and preventing "the exploitation of parties and associations as fronts for terrorist organizations." The text characterizes Ennahdha as "the political and organizational continuation of the international organization of the Muslim Brotherhood" and, on that basis, proposes formally classifying it (and any association, organization, or structure with organizational, financial, or functional links to it or to the Muslim Brotherhood) as a terrorist organization. If adopted, the bill would order the immediate dissolution of all such structures; leaders whose membership or involvement is established would lose any elected mandate and be barred from standing for election or holding public office for ten years. Mseddi had signaled the initiative in March 2026 while collecting the co-sponsor signatures required to file it. As of this run, the bill is newly filed with no confirmed referral-committee hearing date, and should be distinguished from the separate, already-adjudicated "Secret Apparatus" case above (a criminal terrorist-organization prosecution under the existing Anti-Terrorism Law, Law No. 2015-26) — this bill instead seeks a prospective, generally-applicable statutory designation rather than a case-specific criminal verdict (Businessnews, L'Économiste Maghrébin, African Manager, Le Courrier de l'Atlas, Univers News, all July 9, 2026). Watch for committee referral and hearing scheduling in subsequent runs.
SecurityCriminalPending

🛂 Nationality, Immigration & Asylum

Nationality Code — Law No. 63-6 of February 28, 1963

Enacted: February 28, 1963 | In force: 1963 | Amended: 1975, 2002, 2010
Defines Tunisian nationality by birth, descent, naturalization, and reacquisition. Originally limited transmission of nationality through the paternal line; the 2010 amendment allows Tunisian mothers married to foreign nationals to transmit nationality to their children. Governs loss, renunciation, and deprivation of nationality. Naturalization requires 5 years of lawful residence. Pending reform: a bill filed at the ARP on February 27, 2026 by 36 co-sponsoring deputies (now identifiable as projet de loi n° 17/2026) would tighten naturalization by requiring proof of legal/regular entry into Tunisia plus 7 consecutive years of legal residence (up from 5), while easing rules for stateless persons born in Tunisia and creating an expedited "exceptional interest" naturalization track for high-skill foreign nationals. The ARP Rights and Freedoms Commission examined the bill at a session held May 7, 2026, which press coverage of that session also described as addressing a requirement that a stateless child's parents hold legal Tunisian residency status for the child's citizenship eligibility. Under committee review as of this update; not yet adopted.
Key provisions
  • Art. 6: Nationality by birth to a Tunisian father (jus sanguinis)
  • Art. 12: Naturalization requires 5-year lawful residence
  • 2010 amendment: children of Tunisian mother and foreign father may acquire nationality
  • Art. 30–35: Loss and deprivation of Tunisian nationality
NationalityCivil

Law No. 68-7 of March 8, 1968Entry and Residence of Foreigners

Enacted: March 8, 1968 | In force: 1968 | Amended: 1975, 2004
Governs the entry, stay, and expulsion of foreign nationals in Tunisia. Requires visa for most non-Maghreb nationals (bilateral visa exemptions apply). Establishes residence permit categories and procedures. Governs administrative detention pending deportation. Tunisia has no comprehensive asylum law; refugee status was determined by UNHCR under its mandate, but in June 2024 Tunisian authorities instructed UNHCR to suspend new registration, pre-registration, and refugee-status-determination processing (services to already-registered refugees continued); the government's stated rationale was an intent to establish a national asylum system, which had not been enacted as of Human Rights Watch's 2025 and 2026 World Report chapters on Tunisia. Update (Run 103): an Amnesty International report of July 2, 2026 quantified the suspension's effect for the first time in this artifact — UNHCR-registered refugees in Tunisia fell from 18,362 in June 2024 (when the undisclosed suspension order was given) to 7,515 by April 2026, a roughly 59% decline — and documented a government-organized "voluntary return" program for migrants operating since June 2025, alongside continued reporting of abuse against refugees and asylum-seekers left without a functioning status-determination process. (Added Run 106) See the separate Passports & Travel Documents Law entry below for the distinct 1975 statute whose border-crossing offenses (rather than this law's visa/residence-permit framework) have been used to prosecute refugee-aid humanitarian workers.
NationalityAdministrative

Passports & Travel Documents Law — Law No. 75-40 of May 14, 1975

Enacted: May 14, 1975 | In force: 1975 | Amended: Organic Law No. 2015-46 of November 23, 2015; Organic Law No. 2024-23 of March 11, 2024
Governs issuance of Tunisian passports and travel documents — ordinary passports, diplomatic/service passports, laissez-passer "B," and titres de voyage "C" and "D" for stateless persons and refugees. Articles 38, 39, and 41 criminalize facilitating, arranging, or concealing a person's irregular entry into or exit from Tunisian territory, and participating in an organization or agreement to do so — offenses distinct from the Irregular Migration Law's (2004-6) criminalization of the migrants themselves and from the 2016 Anti-Human Trafficking Law's exploitation-focused offenses. Most recently amended by Organic Law No. 2024-23 of March 11, 2024, the companion statute to that year's biometric national ID card reform (see the National Identity Card Law entry), which established the biometric passport; an earlier amendment, Organic Law No. 2015-46 of November 23, 2015, updated passport-issuance procedures. Genuinely new standalone entry (Run 106): human rights organizations have documented Articles 38/39/41 being used against humanitarian workers rather than smugglers. Tunisian authorities closed the Tunisian Council for Refugees (CTR) — a UNHCR-mandated organization providing asylum-application assistance and emergency care since 2016 — in May 2024, arresting founder Mustapha Djemali and project manager Abderrazek Krimi (plus four other staff) on charges under these articles for allegedly facilitating and harboring irregular border crossings through the CTR's refugee-assistance activities. The Tunis Court of First Instance convicted both men on November 24, 2025 (2 years, 6 months suspended; released the same day, having already served roughly 18 months in pretrial detention), and the Tunis Court of Appeal's criminal chamber upheld the conviction on May 14, 2026. Human Rights Watch (May 11, 2026) and Amnesty International characterize the prosecution as an abusive misuse of anti-smuggling provisions to criminalize refugee-aid work, consistent with this artifact's broader documentation of civil-society prosecutions (see the Associations and Judicial Organization sections). (Cross-ref, Run 213): a distinct organization prosecuted under the same articles is tracked separately at the "Tunisie Terre d'Asile" entry below.
NationalityCriminal

EU–Tunisia Memorandum of UnderstandingStrategic and Global Partnership

Signed: July 16, 2023
Non-binding political declaration between the EU and Tunisia covering five pillars: macroeconomic stability, trade and investment, green energy, people-to-people contacts, and — most emphasized in implementation — migration and mobility. The EU pledged roughly €105 million for border-control capacity, €150 million in direct budget support, and a conditional €900 million in macro-financial assistance tied to an IMF program Tunisia ultimately declined to conclude. Tunisia committed to combating smuggling networks, conducting search-and-rescue operations in its territorial waters, and accepting returns of third-country nationals. The MOU bypassed parliamentary ratification on both sides. In January 2025 the European Commission overhauled its disbursement mechanism following reports of abuses by EU-funded security forces against migrants; Tunisian officials have since pushed to renegotiate the arrangement toward greater reciprocity. Update (Run 43): marking the MOU's third anniversary, a joint letter dated July 1, 2026 from Human Rights Watch, Amnesty International, the International Commission of Jurists, the Committee to Protect Journalists, EuroMed Rights, and Avocats Sans Frontières was sent to European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen calling for a full human-rights review of the partnership and conditionality on further EU funding, citing continued reports of abuses against migrants in Tunisian territory. Forms the policy backdrop to the domestic 2023–2026 migrant-policy shift above and the EU's 2026 "safe country of origin" designation below.
NationalityAdministrative

EU Designation of Tunisia as a "Safe Country of Origin"EU Pact on Migration and Asylum

European Parliament vote: February 10, 2026 (408–184–60) | Council approval: February 23, 2026 | Rules in force: June 12, 2026
Not Tunisian domestic law, but an EU-law development directly affecting Tunisian nationals abroad: under the EU's Pact on Migration and Asylum, the European Parliament and Council designated Tunisia one of several countries presumed "safe" EU-wide, subjecting Tunisian asylum-seekers in the EU to accelerated, presumptive-rejection border-procedure examination of their claims from June 12, 2026 onward. Distinct from, but connected to, the domestic 2023–2026 migrant-policy shift and UNHCR-registration suspension documented above and the 2023 EU-Tunisia migration MOU above. Flagged as a single-source-class finding from this run pending independent corroboration of the exact vote tallies and in-force date against a European Parliament or Official Journal of the EU primary text.
NationalityUnconfirmed

Irregular Migration Law — Law No. 2004-6 of February 3, 2004

Enacted: February 3, 2004 | In force: 2004
Criminalizes irregular emigration by Tunisian nationals ("harraga") with penalties of up to 3 years imprisonment. Criticized by human rights organizations as criminalizing movement. Used to prosecute individuals attempting to cross to Europe irregularly. Tunisia also receives migrants from sub-Saharan Africa; their status is governed by the 1968 Immigration Law and UNHCR procedure. Note: No domestic asylum law as of 2026; draft asylum law discussed since 2013 but not enacted.
NationalityCriminal

Affaire Abdallah SaïdCriminalization of Migrant-Solidarity Humanitarian Work

Detained: November 12, 2024 | First-instance conviction: April 22, 2026 (Médenine Court of First Instance) | Public-prosecutor appeal: pending
Abdallah Saïd, a physician with over thirty years in public health and founding president of the Médenine-based humanitarian association "Les Enfants de la Lune" (which coordinates a network of local associations providing medical and psychological support, schooling assistance, and economic-integration programs to disabled Tunisian children, migrants, and other vulnerable groups), was placed in pre-trial detention on November 12, 2024 by the national financial-crimes investigation unit. He was initially referred to the anti-terrorism judicial unit, which found no terrorism-related basis for the case. After roughly a year and a half in provisional detention, the Médenine Court of First Instance convicted him on April 22, 2026 and sentenced him to one year's imprisonment; the public prosecutor has appealed the verdict, and Saïd remains detained. Human rights organizations, including the Committee for Justice (CFJ), characterize the case as part of the same pattern documented in the Administrative Suspensions & Judicial Dissolution Proceedings entry above and in the criminalization of migrant-solidarity work under the Irregular Migration Law framework — using provisional detention and shifting accusations (initially framed as terrorism-adjacent, ultimately prosecuted on other grounds) against humanitarian actors assisting migrants and refugees in southern Tunisia. A July 8, 2026 report by the NGO Intersection pour les droits et les libertés cited Saïd's case among 88 documented civil-society-related judicial and administrative actions recorded between May 2024 and May 2026.
CriminalNationalitySocial

Affaire "Tunisie Terre d'Asile"Criminalization of Migrant-Solidarity Humanitarian Work

Arrested: May 2024 | First-instance conviction: January 5, 2026 (Tunis Court of First Instance, criminal chamber) | Released: January 5–6, 2026
A distinct case from the Tunisian Council for Refugees (CTR) prosecution above, involving a different organization and defendants despite an identical legal theory and near-identical fact pattern. Sherifa Riahi (former director), Yadh Bousselmi, and Mohamed Joo — current or former staff of "Tunisie Terre d'Asile," the Tunisian branch of the French NGO France Terre d'Asile, which assisted asylum-seekers and migrants under a program coordinated with Tunisian authorities, local governments, and UN agencies — were placed in pre-trial detention in May 2024 alongside two former municipal officials, Mohamed Iqbal Khaled and Imen Ouardani. Initial charges of money laundering and abuse of function were dropped during proceedings, leaving prosecution solely under Passports & Travel Documents Law (No. 75-40 of 1975) Articles 38, 39, and 41 (facilitating or sheltering irregular entry, exit, or stay) and the 1968 Law on the Status of Foreigners (Law No. 68-7). After roughly 19 months (580 days) in detention, the Tunis Court of First Instance convicted all five on January 5, 2026 to two years' imprisonment with suspended execution; all were released the same or following day. Eighteen other co-defendants who remained at liberty during the proceedings were acquitted. The International Commission of Jurists, Human Rights Watch, and Amnesty International condemned the conviction as criminalizing legitimate humanitarian work, consistent with this artifact's broader documentation of civil-society and migrant-solidarity prosecutions (see the Administrative Suspensions and Affaire Abdallah Saïd entries). No appeal outcome had been reported as of this run.
CriminalNationalitySocial

Saloua Ghrissa CaseAssociation for the Promotion of the Right to Difference (ADD)

Detained: December 2024 | Provisional release: March 19, 2026 | Trial ongoing
Saloua Ghrissa, a French-Tunisian retired academic and executive director of the Association for the Promotion of the Right to Difference (ADD, founded 2011, working on social, cultural, gender, and identity diversity), was arrested in December 2024 alongside seven other ADD staff and members and held in pretrial detention for more than fifteen months on financial-crime charges centered on undeclared foreign funding — funds her defense says came from UN-affiliated bodies and NGOs operating lawfully in Tunisia. The Tunis Court of First Instance granted her provisional release on March 19, 2026 after a hearing held March 1, 2026; the underlying case against her and her co-defendants remained before the courts as of mid-2026, with no verdict reported by any outlet checked. Amnesty International and Human Rights Watch characterize the charges as unfounded and part of the same 2024–2026 wave of prosecutions of civil-society figures and organizations documented in the Administrative Suspensions & Judicial Dissolution Proceedings entry above, alongside the Affaire Abdallah Saïd and Tunisie Terre d'Asile cases and the Tunisian Council for Refugees prosecution — though, unlike those migrant/refugee-focused cases, ADD's work centers on domestic social and gender-identity advocacy rather than migrant assistance, illustrating that the foreign-funding prosecution pattern extends beyond the migration-solidarity sector specifically. Confirmed via Amnesty International, Business News (multiple 2026 reports), Nawaat, and Kapitalis, cross-checked against each other.
CriminalFinancialSocial

National Council for Tunisians Residing Abroad — Law No. 2016-68 of August 3, 2016 (reform bill pending, 2024–2026)

Enacted: August 3, 2016 | In force: 2016 | Implementing decree: Government Decree No. 2019-820 of September 10, 2019 | Reform bill: n° 2024-064, under ARP committee review since 2024 | Fully adopted at committee level: July 15, 2026 | Not yet at plenary vote
Creates the National Council for Tunisians Residing Abroad (Conseil National des Tunisiens Résidant à l'Étranger), a consultative public body with legal personality and administrative/financial autonomy, seated in Tunis and attached to the ministry responsible for Tunisians abroad. Represents Tunisia's diaspora (estimated at over 1.5 million people) before public authorities and issues an annual activity report to the President, the ARP Speaker, and the Head of Government. Government Decree No. 2019-820 (September 10, 2019) set the Council's administrative and financial organization. A reform bill (projet de loi n° 2024/064) has been under continued examination by the ARP's Foreign Affairs, International Cooperation, Tunisians Abroad, and Migration Committee since 2024, with a working session held June 4, 2026 on its text, followed by a further joint hearing on June 22, 2026 with the President of the National Council for Tunisians Abroad herself. The bill would restructure the Council's general assembly, introduce individual, direct, voluntary-basis voting (including electronic voting) for diaspora representatives on a gender-parity basis — replacing the current framework's limited effective functioning since 2016 — and reposition the Council as a horizontal coordination body unifying public policy across ministries dealing with Tunisians abroad and mobilizing expatriate expertise for national economic development. Not yet adopted as of this run. Update (Run 311): per a same-day La Presse report, the ARP's Foreign Affairs, International Cooperation, Tunisians Abroad, and Migration Committee adopted amendments to the bill's text on July 14, 2026, going beyond the hearing-stage status documented above — the amended text would add permanent members to the Council's general assembly (deputies elected from diaspora constituencies) alongside 60 diaspora representatives; this is a committee-level amendment adoption, not a completed committee report or plenary vote, and no bill-number change or JORT publication has been found. (Cross-ref added Run 109): separately from this reform bill, Article 83 of the Finance Law 2026 already enacted new tax and customs advantages specifically for this Council's constituency — see the Finance Law 2026 entry (Tax & Fiscal section). Update (Run 325): the same committee (Commission des relations extérieures, de la coopération internationale, des affaires des Tunisiens à l'étranger et de la migration, chaired by Riadh Jaïdane) fully adopted the bill's new version at committee level on July 15, 2026, per Tunisie Tribune's same-day report, independently corroborated by La Presse's July 14, 2026 article quoting Chairman Jaïdane — both describe the committee-level text as complete and confirm its central substantive change: reattaching the Council from its current ministry to the Présidence du Gouvernement directly, to improve cross-ministerial coordination on diaspora policy. Both sources trace to the same committee chairman's remarks (one primary account, two corroborating outlets, not fully independent reporting chains). Next step per both reports is a report to the ARP Bureau and possible plenary scheduling — still not a floor vote or enacted law as of this update. Update (Run 335): a same-day Tunisie Tribune report on the July 15, 2026 committee adoption ("Adoption en commission de la proposition de loi amendant le cadre juridique du Conseil national des TRE"), corroborated by La Presse de Tunisie and allAfrica, supplies additional membership detail beyond what Run 325 captured: the amended general assembly would seat elected diaspora-district deputies alongside the 60 diaspora representatives (each requiring at least five years' residency abroad), plus non-voting observer members drawn from concerned ministries; all seats remain on an unpaid, voluntary basis. No bill-number change, ARP Bureau referral, or plenary date has been found beyond the committee-level adoption already documented.
NationalityAdministrativePending

⚖️ Judicial Organization

Judicial Organization Law — Law No. 2013-13 of April 2, 2013 (as amended 2022)

Enacted: April 2, 2013 | In force: 2013 | Amended: 2022
Organizes the court system of Tunisia: Cantonal Courts (minor matters), Courts of First Instance (24, one per governorate), Courts of Appeal (3 — Tunis, Sousse, Sfax), Court of Cassation (apex civil/criminal court, Tunis), Administrative Court (apex administrative jurisdiction), and the Constitutional Court (established under 2022 Constitution). Specialized jurisdictions include the Terrorism Chamber at TFI Tunis.
Court structure
  • Cantonal Courts: minor civil matters, misdemeanor infractions
  • Courts of First Instance (24): civil, commercial, criminal chambers
  • Courts of Appeal (3 — Tunis, Sousse, Sfax): appellate jurisdiction
  • Court of Cassation (Tunis): reviews points of law only
  • Administrative Court: all public law disputes
  • Terrorism Chamber: specialized jurisdiction at TFI Tunis level
  • Constitutional Court: established under 2022 Constitution (Arts. 116–120)
AdministrativeConstitutional

Judicial Statute — Law No. 67-29 of July 14, 1967 (as amended)

Enacted: July 14, 1967 | In force: 1967 | Amended: 1997, 2013, 2022
Governs the appointment, promotion, transfer, discipline, and removal of judges. Establishes the Superior Council of the Judiciary (Conseil Supérieur de la Magistrature — CSM) as the body responsible for judicial governance. Post-2022, the CSM's composition was changed by presidential decree, significantly reducing judicial independence according to the UN Special Rapporteur on the Independence of Judges and Lawyers. Update (Run 40): tensions over judicial and legal-profession independence escalated in mid-2026. The Tunisian National Bar Association (ONAT) staged an escalating protest campaign — a May 13 Bar Council escalation vote, a symbolic May 18 red-armband action, then rallies at courts in Tunis (Bab Bnet), Bizerte, Béja, Jendouba, Le Kef, and Siliana — culminating in a nationwide lawyers' general strike on June 18, 2026 demanding reform of the profession's legal framework and stronger judicial-independence guarantees; Bâtonnier Boubaker Belthabet called it a success, but no government response had followed as of late June 2026, and on June 9, 2026 forty international legal and human-rights organizations issued a joint statement condemning an escalating campaign against lawyers, judges, and the Bar. Separately, judge Anas Hmaidi — president of the Association of Tunisian Magistrates (AMT), re-elected at its February 2026 congress despite government pressure — was sentenced to one year in prison on April 6, 2026, prompting condemnation from the International Union of Magistrates and the African Commission on Human and Peoples' Rights, both linking the prosecution to the continued absence of a permanent, independent Superior Judicial Council (see the Provisional Supreme Judicial Council entry below). Update (Run 42): the Tunis Court of Appeal's correctional chamber confirmed Hmaidi's one-year sentence on July 1, 2026 in a ruling issued by default (par défaut/in absentia), this time ordering immediate execution of the sentence — a harsher procedural posture than the first-instance judgment. TAP/Diwan FM reporting confirmed the same legal qualification (Art. 136 Penal Code, obstruction of the freedom to work) tied to his conduct at a June 13, 2022 hearing in Monastir; Hmaidi continues to dispute the legitimacy of the Provisional Supreme Council of the Judiciary body that lifted his immunity on September 20, 2022. Update (Run 43): ONAT's annual general assembly was reported scheduled for July 11, 2026, a potential venue for the Bar to set conditions for renewed dialogue with the government following the unresolved June 18, 2026 general strike; no government response to the strike's demands had been reported as of this run. Update (Run 46): confirmed and refined — the July 11, 2026 assembly is not an elective session (Belthabet was already elected bâtonnier for a 2025–2028 term in September 2025); Belthabet has framed its purpose as convening the Bar Council and general assembly to "evaluate the evolution of the dispute [with the authorities] and adopt consequent measures," i.e., a strategy review on whether to escalate or seek renewed dialogue. Separately, the public prosecutor at the Tunis Court of Appeal filed an appeal challenging the quorum/procedural validity of an earlier lawyers' extraordinary general assembly, with a hearing reported set for June 10, 2026 — outcome not independently confirmed. As of this run (July 2, 2026), the government still had not proposed any official or informal mediation, per Belthabet, though ONAT says it remains open to dialogue. Update (Run 60): on July 2, 2026, UN Special Rapporteur on the independence of judges and lawyers Margaret Satterthwaite publicly stated that Hmaidi's conviction "should have been overturned," a statement the AMT amplified in its own denunciation of the July 1 appeals ruling as unfair — the first UN Special Procedures reaction specifically naming this case, distinct from the broader CSM-composition criticism already cited above. Update (Run 91): a further, previously undocumented lawyer-harassment case closed a genuine gap — Ahmed Souab, a lawyer, former administrative judge, and prominent human rights defender, was arrested at his home on April 21, 2025 by anti-terrorism officers over public statements he made after representing defendants in the "state security conspiracy" case (see that standalone entry above), and was convicted at first instance to 5 years' imprisonment following a trial Amnesty International said lasted only seven minutes. On appeal, the Tunis Court of Appeal reduced his sentence to 10 months' firm imprisonment plus a 2-year suspended sentence and ordered his immediate release on February 23, 2026 (already served), also lifting the administrative-surveillance measure imposed at first instance — a rare, if partial, judicial correction amid the broader pattern of prosecutions against lawyers and judges documented in this entry. Update (Run 156): the July 11, 2026 ONAT general assembly's sourcing — flagged as unconfirmable on independent re-checks by Runs 119, 128, 131, 145, and 147 — is now corroborated by a further, previously uncited independent source: a June 30, 2026 Réalités Magazine article ("Avocats: Dans l'attente d'un dialogue") confirms the assembly "devrait avoir lieu samedi 11 juillet et pourrait faire connaître les conditions de reprise du dialogue," matching the strategy-review framing already documented above from Runs 43/46 rather than contradicting it. Update (Run 313): the July 11, 2026 general assembly's own outcome remains unreported by any outlet checked (French, English, or Arabic-language search), now 58+ consecutive runs since the assembly was held — the longest-standing unresolved item on this project's watchlist. One adjacent, single-source data point was found and is noted rather than asserted as resolution: Business News (July 13, 2026) reported Bâtonnier Boubaker Belthabet met UGTT secretary-general Slaheddine Selmi at ONAT headquarters "to discuss the general situation of the country and cooperation" — suggestive of the Bar continuing to build alliances rather than reaching an accommodation with the government, but not itself a reported outcome of the July 11 assembly and too thin (single outlet, no substantive detail on positions taken) to characterize further. Update (Run 423): the July 11 assembly's outcome remains unreported, now 62+ consecutive runs unresolved. A new false-positive trap for future runs: a distinct, earlier May 1, 2026 extraordinary ONAT general assembly — which voted a general strike/boycott of judicial-chamber hearings, with Bâtonnier Belthabet subsequently extending bar actions through the May 18 – June 18, 2026 window already documented above — is sometimes returned by search engines in place of the July 11 assembly; the two are separate events, and neither the May 1 assembly's own strike vote nor the June 18 general strike it produced is the still-unresolved July 11 assembly outcome tracked here. Update (Run 492): the Comité pour le Respect des Libertés et des Droits de l'Homme en Tunisie (CRLDHT) issued a statement dated July 19, 2026 (published by Kapitalis July 21) expressing "profound concern" over what it frames as a systemic campaign of judicial reprisals, triggered by the July 13, 2026 appellate ruling raising former Republic Prosecutor Béchir Akremi's sentence from 23 to 29 years (see the Chokri Belaïd Assassination Trial — Akremi/Ellouze entry for the ruling itself). CRLDHT frames Akremi's prosecution as retaliation for his judicial handling of the Belaïd and Mohamed Brahmi assassination files, and situates it within a broader pattern also encompassing the dissolution of the elected Superior Judicial Council (see the Provisional Supreme Judicial Council entry), the 2022 dismissal of 57 magistrates by presidential decree (see the Magistrate Dismissal Power entry), and pressure on lawyers and journalists — describing it collectively as an ongoing weakening of judicial independence since July 25, 2021. The statement also names two further magistrates alongside the already-documented Hmaidi case: Anas Hmaidi (AMT president, this entry above) and, previously undocumented in this artifact, Hamadi Rahmani — a sitting investigating magistrate prosecuted over six defamation complaints filed by former Court of Cassation president Taïeb Rached concerning Rahmani's social-media posts. Rahmani was questioned and granted provisional liberty by the dean of investigating judges at the Tunis Court of First Instance (reported December 2, 2024, tunisienumerique.com and African Manager); his defense (attorney Ayachi Hammami) contested his court appearance on judicial-immunity grounds, invoking an August 9, 2022 administrative-tribunal ruling that had suspended an earlier revocation measure against him, and requested referral to the Provisional Superior Council of the Judiciary as the sole body competent to rule on immunity waivers — the same body whose provisional, still-unreplaced status is documented in the CSM entry above. No further procedural outcome for the Rahmani case has been reported since December 2024; CRLDHT's July 2026 statement treats it as a still-live example of the pattern it describes rather than a closed matter.
AdministrativeConstitutional

Decree-Law No. 2022-11 of February 12, 2022Provisional Supreme Judicial Council

Enacted: February 12, 2022 | Published: JORT No. 2022-016
The specific decree-law referenced above: dissolved the elected Supreme Judicial Council (created under Organic Law No. 2016-34) and replaced it with a "Provisional Supreme Council of the Judiciary," composed predominantly of senior ex officio magistrates plus members appointed by the President from candidates submitted via the Prime Ministry. Retains three specialized sub-councils (judicial, administrative, financial). Remains the operative — still "provisional" — body as of mid-2026, with no permanent council established to replace it.
AdministrativeConstitutional

Decree-Law No. 2022-35 of June 1, 2022Magistrate Dismissal Power (Completing Decree-Law No. 2022-11)

Enacted: June 1, 2022
Grants the President of the Republic power to summarily dismiss any magistrate by simple presidential decree in cases of declared "emergency," threat to public security, or harm to "the country's superior interest," based on a report from unspecified "competent authorities." Dismissal triggers automatic criminal referral; the dismissed magistrate cannot appeal until after a final criminal judgment. Used the same day to dismiss 57 judges and prosecutors, condemned by HRW, Amnesty International, and EuroMed Rights; the Tunisian Association of Magistrates continued to denounce the dismissals as unresolved injustice as of June 2026. Update (Run 263): a professional-reconversion detail, per a July 10, 2026 Business News report: six of the magistrates dismissed under this decree-law have since completed the process of registering as practicing lawyers with the Tunisian Bar, joining the profession rather than pursuing (or after exhausting) the criminal-referral track the decree-law attaches to their dismissal — illustrating one concrete downstream career outcome for the 57 dismissed magistrates nearly four years on, alongside the still-unresolved criminal referrals and appeals tracked elsewhere in this entry.
AdministrativeConstitutionalCriminal

Organic Law No. 2013-53 of December 24, 2013Transitional Justice

Enacted: December 24, 2013 | In force: 2014
Establishes Tunisia's transitional justice framework including the Truth and Dignity Commission (IVD — Instance Vérité et Dignité). Covers: truth-seeking, reparations, institutional reform, and accountability for human rights violations committed between July 1, 1955 and December 31, 2013. IVD operated from 2014–2019; issued final report in 2019 documenting systematic torture, political persecution, and economic crimes.
AdministrativeConstitutionalCriminal

Organic Law No. 2013-43 of October 2013National Authority for the Prevention of Torture (INPT) (Run 66 addition)

Adopted by NCA: October 9, 2013 | Enacted: October 21 or 23, 2013 (sources diverge on the exact day; JORT No. 85/2013) | Members elected by ARP: March 29–30, 2016
A wholly new standalone entry — despite 65 prior research runs and the extradition entry below referencing "torture risk" as a ground for refusal, no dedicated entry existed for Tunisia's National Preventive Mechanism against torture. Implements Tunisia's OPCAT obligations (ratified June 2011) via the Instance Nationale pour la Prévention de la Torture (INPT), the first such mechanism in the MENA region: 16 members elected by Parliament from human-rights associations, magistrates, doctors, and child-protection specialists, holding legal immunity and empowered to conduct unannounced visits to any place of deprivation of liberty; authorities may refuse a visit only for narrow, written, reasoned reasons. The INPT remains active as of mid-2026, distinct from the (separately documented) Truth and Dignity Commission above, which addressed historical rather than ongoing torture: it publicly criticized severe prison overcrowding in November 2024 (population risen from ~23,000 to over 32,000–33,000 against ~17,000 capacity), published a "Guide to Detainees' Rights" in November 2025, and issued a "zero impunity" statement for the UN International Day in Support of Victims of Torture on June 27, 2026. No confirmed reports of INPT defunding or leadership removal were found for 2024–2026.
JudicialCriminalConstitutional

"State Security Conspiracy" Prosecutions and Related 2025–2026 Convictions

Arrests from: February 2023 | Instalingo first-instance: February 5, 2025 | Case 1 sentences: April 19, 2025 | Case 1 appeal: November 27–28, 2025 | Instalingo appeal: January 14, 2026 | Case 2 appeal: February 2, 2026 | "Secret Apparatus" case verdict: June 2, 2026 | Bensedrine sentenced: June 26, 2026 | Smara appeal: July 2, 2026 | "Ramadan Evening Gathering" verdict: April 14–15, 2026 | Bhiri fraudulent-documents sentence: May 7, 2026 | Abdelli sentenced: July 7, 2026
A mass prosecution of dozens of opposition politicians, lawyers, and activists on charges of conspiracy against state security, prosecuted under general Penal Code state-security provisions (Arts. 72–85, see Criminal Law section) rather than a dedicated statute — added here as a standalone entry given its significance to Tunisia's post-2021 rule-of-law trajectory, previously undocumented in this artifact despite 39 prior research runs. Case 1 (defendants including Khayam Turki, Ridha Belhaj, Ghazi Chaouachi, Jaouhar Ben Mbarek, Issam Chebbi, and Abdelhamid Jelassi) produced severe sentences on April 19, 2025, described by Human Rights Watch as a "sham trial"; on appeal (Nov 27–28, 2025), 34 of 37 sentences (5–45 years) were upheld and 3 defendants acquitted (Lazher Akremi, Hattab Ben Slama, Noureddine Boutar), with several sentences increased (Chebbi, Ben Mbarek, Chaouachi, and Belhaj raised from 18 to 20 years) while businessman Kamel Ltaief's sentence was reduced from 66 to 45 years, remaining the heaviest in the case. A related but separate Case 2, focused on Ennahda party leadership, produced an appeals ruling on February 2, 2026 sentencing roughly 20 defendants to 3–35 years; Ennahda leader Rached Ghannouchi's sentence was raised from 14 to 20 years (56 years cumulative across his cases), and co-defendants Kamel Guizani, Rafik Abdessalem, Lotfi Zitoun, and Mouadh Ghannouchi received 35 years in absentia — Amnesty International described the ruling as deepening a "rule-of-law crisis." Separately, Sihem Bensedrine — journalist and former president of the Truth and Dignity Commission (IVD, see the Transitional Justice entry above) — was sentenced to 25 years in prison (two consecutive terms of 20 and 5 years across two cases) on June 26, 2026 by the Tunis Court of First Instance, among the most severe individual sentences of a rights defender since 2021; the case concerns "using position to gain unfair advantage," fraud, and forgery allegedly connected to an arbitral reconciliation agreement the IVD concluded with businessman Slim Chiboub during her tenure, with Bensedrine, Mabrouk Korchid, and Chiboub jointly ordered to pay a fine of roughly 1.776 billion dinars. (Run 153 addition): lawyer and fellow former IVD official Khaled Krichi — arrested June 3, 2026 on the same case — was sentenced the same day (June 26, 2026) to a cumulative 10 years (two five-year terms) and named in the same joint fine, for exploiting his position to obtain undue advantages and violating procedural regulations; the Tunisian Human Rights League (LTDH) condemned both sentences as disproportionate (Businessnews, La Presse de Tunisie, Kapitalis, Human Rights Watch, July 7, 2026). Opposition politician Abir Moussi (Free Destourian Party leader, detained since October 3, 2023, when she was arrested attempting to file a legal challenge against presidential decrees on electoral redistricting) faces at least two further parallel cases beyond this conspiracy cluster. (Run 168 update): in the "bureau d'ordre" case, concerning an incident at the Presidency of the Republic, a 12-year first-instance sentence (December 2025) was reduced on appeal to 10 years by the Tunis Court of Appeal's criminal chamber on March 13, 2026 — 9 years for "aggression aimed at disrupting the functioning of the state, incitement to violence between citizens, and public disorder," plus 6 months each for unauthorized processing of personal data and obstruction of freedom to work; PDL co-defendant Meriem Sassi received 1 year on the same two lesser counts. Separately, an ISIE-initiated complaint under Decree-Law No. 2022-54 Art. 24 ("spreading false information") produced a first-instance 2-year sentence (August 5, 2024), reduced to 16 months on appeal, then annulled and remanded by the Court of Cassation; a newly-constituted Tunis Court of Appeal panel re-confirmed the 2-year sentence on December 30, 2025 (Amnesty International, Kapitalis, Business News, cross-corroborated), replacing this entry's previously vague "ruling reported around March 2026" placeholder. Amnesty International condemned both prosecutions as unjust. See the Judicial Statute entry above for the related prosecution of AMT president judge Anas Hmaidi, whose one-year sentence was confirmed on appeal (with immediate execution ordered) on July 1, 2026 — Run 42 update. Update (Run 60): a related but distinct terrorism-chamber case opened July 3, 2026: the Tunis Court of First Instance's terrorism chamber rejected pre-trial release requests (June 30, 2026) for Ennahda secretary-general Ajmi Lourimi and co-defendant Mossaâb Gharbi, prosecuted under the Anti-Terrorism Law (Law No. 2015-26, see Criminal Law section) for failing to report a terrorism-related offense; no verdict has been reported as of this run. (Run 155 update): the specialized terrorism chamber of the Tunis Court of First Instance delivered its verdict on July 4, 2026 — three-year prison sentences for both defendants, Lourimi for failing to report a terrorism-related offense and Gharbi for harboring, concealing, and facilitating the escape of a person involved in terrorism cases. Ennahdha denounced the case as fabricated (July 4, 2026), and the LTDH condemned the verdicts against Lourimi and Gharbi as disproportionate in a July 4–5, 2026 statement (Businessnews, webdo.tn, Mosaïque FM, Réalités, Kapitalis). (Run 190 correction/update): that same LTDH statement was previously read as naming Elyes Chaouachi a co-defendant in this Lourimi/Gharbi case; direct verification (Businessnews, Kapitalis, July 4–5, 2026) shows Chaouachi's July 4, 2026 sentence was instead handed down the same day in a wholly distinct anti-terrorism-chamber case, arising from a July 2024 arrest alongside a sought Ennahdha activist — the LTDH statement addressed both same-day verdicts together, not a shared docket, and this entry is corrected accordingly. Elyes Chaouachi — a France-based activist and son of Case 1 defendant Ghazi Chaouachi — received 15 years in that separate case, bringing his cumulative total across a long series of predominantly in-absentia convictions to more than 95 years and 8 months (up from over 80 years reported May 13, 2026, and 38 years reported November 25, 2025); he attributes the accumulating prosecutions to his public advocacy for freedoms and democracy and to his father's status as a conspiracy-case defendant, and the LTDH's July 4, 2026 statement separately denounced this sentence as "severe" and raising doubts about fair-trial guarantees and the presumption of innocence. Update (Run 63): a further related but distinct prosecution — former MP Sahbi Smara, charged not with joining the conspiracy but with fabricating one to incriminate political opponents ahead of the October 2024 presidential election — had his 11-year first-instance sentence reduced to approximately nine and a half years on appeal, July 2, 2026 (Kapitalis, Business News, Tunisie Numérique, Gnet News). Smara was separately sentenced in March 2026 to 6 months' imprisonment for assaulting Abir Moussi inside Parliament while both were sitting deputies, illustrating how this cluster of prosecutions has widened to include cases arising from, rather than merely alongside, the original conspiracy proceedings. Update (Run 81): Case 1 defendant Khayam Turki carries two further, entirely distinct convictions that should not be conflated with his conspiracy sentence above: a April 21, 2026 Tunis Court of First Instance ruling of 14 years for fraud, money laundering, and aggravated breach of trust, arising from a UAE company's complaint over a North African land-acquisition project (Kapitalis, Businessnews); and a July 3, 2026 first-instance conviction of 4 years on strictly tax-related offenses tied to his business-portfolio management, distinct from both prior cases (La Presse de Tunisie, Businessnews, allAfrica). Three separate Turki dockets are now on record: the conspiracy case (above), the April 2026 money-laundering case, and the July 2026 tax case. Update (Run 101): a wholly distinct, considerably larger and more severe case — the "affaire de l'appareil secret" ("Secret Apparatus" case) — reached a first-instance verdict June 2, 2026 from the specialized anti-terrorism criminal chamber of the Tunis Court of First Instance, and should not be conflated with the Case 1/Case 2 conspiracy prosecutions above: it was opened in 2022 following a complaint by lawyers for the families of assassinated leftist politicians Chokri Belaïd and Mohamed Brahmi (killed 2013), alleging that a clandestine Ennahda security structure — directly supervised, per the prosecution, by Rached Ghannouchi — orchestrated political assassinations, conducted espionage, and infiltrated state institutions; the 35 defendants were charged under the Anti-Terrorism Law (Law No. 2015-26, see Criminal Law section) with constituting a terrorist organization, rather than under the Penal Code Arts. 72–85 state-security-conspiracy provisions underlying Case 1/Case 2. Sentences: Ghannouchi received life imprisonment plus 30 additional years; alleged mastermind Mustapha Khedher received life imprisonment plus 96 additional years (the case's heaviest sentence); former Prime Minister Ali Laarayedh received 42 years; eleven further defendants received life sentences plus additional terms, and thirteen others received 10–48 years. Ennahdha denounced the sentences as "scandaleuses" and politically motivated; rights groups (DAWN) and Ghannouchi's defense contested the verdict's legitimacy (Al Jazeera, Bloomberg, La Presse de Tunisie, businessnews.com.tn, Le Courrier de l'Atlas, Kapitalis, Middle East Eye, Middle East Monitor, all June 1–6, 2026). No appeal outcome has been reported as of this run. See the separate Chokri Belaïd Assassination Trial entry (Run 239 addition) for the distinct direct prosecution of former prosecutor Béchir Akremi and Habib Ellouze over the investigative/prosecutorial handling of the Belaïd killing itself — a different docket with no defendant overlap. (Run 256 addition): a third, distinct Belaïd-case docket — the direct prosecution of the assassination's execution/recruitment/planning groups — reached a final appeal verdict on October 14, 2025 (two death sentences upheld, four life terms, lesser terms for the remainder of 24 defendants, Ennahda's leadership not implicated); see the new Chokri Belaïd Assassination Trial — Main Case entry below. Update (Run 149): a fourth, previously undocumented distinct prosecution — the "Instalingo" case — was identified and cross-checked against 148 prior runs' coverage (no match for "Instalingo" in any prior entry). Following a September 10, 2021 raid on Instalingo, a digital-media/content-production company in Kalâa Kebira (Sousse governorate), 41 defendants including Ghannouchi were charged with conspiring against state security, seeking to alter the nature of the state, incitement to violence, and money laundering — distinct from Case 1, Case 2, and the Secret Apparatus case above, both in defendant count and in its own independent sentence track. A Tunis first-instance court sentenced Ghannouchi to 22 years, an 80,000 TND fine, and a 10-year public-office ban on February 5, 2025 (co-defendants: 5–54 years); the Tunis Court of Appeal upheld the 22-year sentence and the bulk of convictions on January 14, 2026, while reducing one female defendant's sentence and ordering her release (Anadolu Agency, Middle East Monitor, North Africa Post, corroborated against Middle East Eye's broader case-cluster reporting). No further appeal outcome has been reported as of this run. Update (Run 172): a fifth, previously undocumented distinct prosecution — the "Ramadan Evening Gathering" case (also reported as the "El-Moussamara"/"discussion circle" affair) — was identified and cross-checked against 171 prior runs' coverage (no match for "Ramadan gathering," "Moussamara," or the named co-defendants in any prior entry). The charges arise from a small political gathering Ghannouchi hosted at his home in April 2023, days before his arrest, at which he discussed the country's political situation; prosecutors relied on recordings of the gathering, which the defense contends were fabricated.
