On April 14–15, 2026, the criminal chamber of the Tunis Court of First Instance sentenced Ghannouchi, Youssef Nouri, and Ahmed Mcharki to 20 years' imprisonment each on charges of "conspiracy to change the form of the state," with five further defendants — including Ghannouchi's son-in-law and former foreign minister Rafik Abdessalem, former MP Maher Zaid, and blogger Mohamed Samati — sentenced in absentia to 20 years; the defense stated Ghannouchi's lawyers were barred from his initial hearing for 48 hours and that counter-terrorism procedural rules were improperly applied to a non-terrorism charge (Jeune Afrique, Espace Manager, Sada News Agency, corroborated against a further wire-service report, all April 14–15, 2026). Tunisian press (Jeune Afrique, Espace Manager) reported Ghannouchi's cumulative fixed-term sentences across all five cases now exceed 60 years, on top of the separate life sentence in the Secret Apparatus case — a figure that should be read as press-reported rather than an official court tally, consistent with this entry's existing practice of flagging cumulative-total figures as approximate. (Run 305 addition): a sixth, distinct prosecution — over illegal foreign financing of Ennahda's 2019 electoral campaign via an undeclared BCW lobbying contract, resting on Electoral Law Art. 163 rather than state-security or terrorism charges — reached a "definitive" appeal confirmation on July 13, 2026 (3 years each for Ghannouchi and co-defendant Rafik Abdessalem, referred to in this case's press coverage by his fuller name, Rafik Abdessalem Bouchlaka); see the new standalone foreign-financing case entry below for the full multi-year procedural history. (Run 316 addition): a seventh, distinct prosecution — over Ghannouchi's October 2016 donation of Gandhi Peace and Tolerance Prize money to the Tunisian Red Crescent, treated by the prosecution as a tax offense rather than any state-security, terrorism, or campaign-financing charge — saw its 2-year first-instance sentence (November 14, 2025) increased to 3 years on appeal, July 13–14, 2026; see the new standalone Red Crescent donation case entry below. (Run 395 addition): former Justice Minister and senior Ennahda figure Noureddine Bhiri — previously undocumented by name in this entry despite Case 1's own "34 of 37 sentences" tally implying several unnamed defendants — was independently confirmed as one of the Case 1 defendants: sentenced April 19, 2025 to 43 years at first instance, reduced on appeal (November 28, 2025, the same Case 1 appeal date already documented above) to 25 years plus 5 years' administrative surveillance (Business News, La Presse de Tunisie, cross-checked against each other). Separately, and legally distinct from Case 1, Tunisian state media reported that a court sentenced Bhiri on May 7, 2026 to a further 20 years' imprisonment over allegations that, during his 2012 tenure as justice minister, he issued fraudulent passports and citizenship documents to foreign nationals wanted on terrorism-related charges — corroborated by Africanews and Anadolu Agency (both May 7, 2026). Bhiri and his defense have rejected both sets of charges as politically motivated; the pairing of a state-security conviction with a distinct fraudulent-documents/administrative conviction against the same defendant mirrors the parallel-prosecution pattern already documented above for Turki (money laundering/tax) and Ghannouchi (Instalingo, Secret Apparatus, foreign financing, Red Crescent). (Run 400 addition): an eighth, distinct prosecution — lawyer and 2014 presidential candidate Samir Abdelli (also transliterated Abdeli/Abdali) — reached a first-instance verdict on July 7, 2026 from the specialized anti-terrorism criminal chamber of the Tunis Court of First Instance: 18 years' imprisonment on money-laundering and state-security charges, with prosecutors alleging conspiracy against the state's internal and external security; his personal driver and personal secretary received 3 and 2 years respectively on related counts. The ruling is first-instance and subject to appeal, with no appeal date reported as of this run, and no confirmed defendant overlap with Case 1/Case 2, Secret Apparatus, Instalingo, or the other dockets above (Businessnews, webdo.tn, Kapitalis, Mosaïque FM, Gnet News, North Africa Post, all July 7–8, 2026). (Run 477 addition): the "one female defendant" whose sentence was reduced and who was ordered released in the Instalingo case's January 14, 2026 appeal ruling (Run 149 addition, above) is identified by name in further reporting: journalist Chadha Hadj Mbarek, held since July 20, 2023, had her original 5-year sentence reduced to 2 years on that same ruling date after the Tunis Court of Appeal dismissed the most serious charges against her (attempted change of the form of government, incitement to violence, incitement to disorder and looting), making her immediately eligible for release; she was freed the same day and honored by the SNJT on January 16, 2026 (Kapitalis, L'Économiste Maghrébin, Le Courrier de l'Atlas, Tunisie Tribune, Espace Manager, all January 14–16, 2026).
CriminalConstitutional

Affaire de l'assassinat de Chokri Belaïd — Akremi/Ellouze Direct Prosecution (Run 239 addition)

Assassination: February 6, 2013 | First-instance verdict: March 4, 2026 | Appeal hearings: June 23, then July 6, 2026 (both postponed) | Heard: July 13, 2026 | Appeal ruling: July 14, 2026 (domestically corroborated)
A wholly new standalone entry — a full-text search of 238 prior runs found no dedicated card for this docket, only an indirect mention within the "Secret Apparatus" case update (Run 101), which covers a related but legally distinct prosecution (a clandestine-organization/terrorism charge against Rached Ghannouchi and 34 co-defendants). This entry instead covers the direct criminal prosecution over the February 6, 2013 assassination of opposition leader Chokri Belaïd, tried before the specialized terrorism-chamber criminal court of the Tunis Court of First Instance. On March 4, 2026, that court convicted former Republic Prosecutor Béchir Akremi (revoked from office) to a combined 23 years across two related dossiers, chiefly for concealing documents and evidence determinant to the assassination investigation, and convicted Ennahdha figure and former MP Habib Ellouze — along with the imam of the Bab El Khadra mosque and several other co-accused — to 13 years each; all convicted parties, the Chokri Belaïd family's defense collective, and the public prosecution itself filed appeals. The appeal, before the Tunis Court of Appeal's specialized terrorism-chamber criminal division, was first calendared for June 23, 2026, postponed to July 6, 2026, and postponed again at that hearing to July 13, 2026 — the same trigger date already tracked in this artifact for the unrelated CNAM/private-biologists tiers-payant suspension; no reasons for either postponement were reported, and no outlet has yet reported the July 13 hearing's outcome (Businessnews, Webdo.tn, Mosaïque FM, Directinfo/WebManagerCenter, Espace Manager, all June 23 – July 7, 2026). Distinct from — and should not be conflated with — the "Secret Apparatus" case above, which prosecutes an alleged clandestine Ennahda security structure under Anti-Terrorism Law charges of orchestrating the Belaïd and Mohamed Brahmi assassinations among broader allegations; this entry's docket instead prosecutes the direct investigative/prosecutorial handling of the Belaïd killing itself, with no defendant overlap between the two cases. (Run 256 addition): a third, distinct docket — the direct prosecution of the assassination's execution/recruitment/planning groups (the original gunmen and their handlers, not the investigative complicity prosecuted here) — reached its own final appeal verdict on October 14, 2025, entirely separate from this Akremi/Ellouze docket's still-pending appeal; see the new Chokri Belaïd Assassination Trial — Main Case entry below. (Run 296 update): a July 14, 2026 webdo.tn report states the Tunis Court of Appeal's specialized terrorism-chamber increased sentences on appeal following the July 13 hearing: Béchir Akremi's sentence rose from 23 to 29 years, and Habib Ellouze's and co-defendant Hassan Ben Brik's each rose from 13 to 17 years, with post-release judicial supervision added for at least one defendant. As of this update, this outcome is reported by webdo.tn alone; no other outlet checked (Business News, La Presse, Mosaïque FM, Directinfo/WebManagerCenter, Espace Manager) had independently corroborated the ruling — flagged as single-source pending next-run corroboration, consistent with this artifact's established practice for time-sensitive same-day findings. (Run 298 update): a direct WebFetch of the same webdo.tn article confirmed the post-release measure — "mise sous contrôle administratif" (administrative supervision) — was ordered for all three convicts (Akremi, Ellouze, and Ben Brik), not just "at least one" as Run 296 conservatively phrased it; corrected accordingly. An African Manager headline matching the same figures ("17 ans de prison pour Béchir Akremi et Habib Ellouze") was located via search, which would be the second-outlet corroboration this entry has been awaiting since Run 296 — but the article's full text returned HTTP 403 on direct fetch and could not be independently read to confirm it actually reports the 29/17/17 breakdown rather than, say, an intermediate or partial figure, so the single-source flag is left standing rather than cleared on an unverified headline alone; resolving this (and the STEG vote tally, still not found despite the July 14 resumption) remain the top priorities for the next run. (Run 299 update): the 29/17/17-year figures and the "mise sous contrôle administratif" measure for all three convicts are now independently corroborated by a second, fully-readable source — Italpress, an Italian wire agency (July 14, 2026) — matching webdo.tn's figures exactly, resolving the corroboration in substance though it is a foreign wire rather than the second Tunisian domestic outlet this entry's flag was originally seeking; no fully-readable dedicated Tunisian-outlet article (Business News, La Presse, Mosaïque FM, Directinfo/WebManagerCenter, Espace Manager, Kapitalis, Tunisie Numérique, Nessma — the Nessma URL found returned HTTP 403) was located this run. The African Manager headline flagged at Run 298 ("17 ans de prison pour Béchir Akremi et Habib Ellouze") remains unread (still HTTP 403) and, on its face, appears internally inconsistent — it names "17 ans" in connection with Akremi, whose sentence every other source puts at 29 years — so it is now flagged as a likely garbled or imprecise headline rather than a pending corroboration source. Given the Italpress corroboration, the single-source flag is downgraded from "unresolved" to "corroborated by a non-Tunisian wire, domestic multi-source confirmation still pending" rather than cleared outright. Update (Run 301) — domestic corroboration achieved: a direct WebFetch of an allAfrica republication (fr.allafrica.com, July 14, 2026) confirms the 29/17/17-year figures and the "mise sous contrôle administratif" measure for all three convicts, and — critically — the article's byline explicitly attributes the original reporting to La Presse (Tunis), a Tunisian domestic outlet, with allAfrica acting only as republication distributor; this is the first fully-readable domestic-outlet sourcing this entry has obtained, closing the gap flagged since Run 296 and superseding the Run 298/299 African Manager lead, which remained unreadable (HTTP 403) on a further retry this run and is now treated as a dead end rather than a pending source. The flag is accordingly upgraded from "corroborated by a non-Tunisian wire, domestic multi-source confirmation still pending" to "domestically corroborated" (La Presse, via allAfrica republication, plus the earlier Italpress wire and webdo.tn's original report) — a second, independent fully-readable domestic outlet would still further strengthen this, but the original single-source concern is resolved.
CriminalPending

Affaire de l'assassinat de Chokri Belaïd — Main Case (Execution/Recruitment/Planning Groups) (Run 256 addition)

Assassination: February 6, 2013 | Alleged gunman Kamel Gadhgadhi killed by security forces: February 2014 | First-instance verdict (4 death sentences): March 27, 2024 | Final appeal verdict: October 14, 2025
A wholly new standalone entry closing a gap that had eluded all 255 prior runs: the direct prosecution of the assassination itself — the execution, recruitment, and planning groups — which is a third, distinct docket within the broader Belaïd case file, alongside (but separate from) the Akremi/Ellouze direct prosecution above and the "Secret Apparatus" case's organizational-responsibility prosecution. The terrorism-specialized chamber of the Tunis Court of First Instance sentenced four defendants to death at first instance on March 27, 2024 (alleged gunman Kamel Gadhgadhi had already been killed in a February 2014 security operation and was never tried). On appeal, the Tunis Court of Appeal's terrorism chamber delivered what multiple outlets reported as a final verdict on October 14, 2025, covering 24 defendants: it upheld death sentences by hanging for Mohamed Aouadi and Ezzedine Abdellaoui (plus 105 and 10 further years respectively on related counts), handed down life imprisonment to four further defendants, and lesser terms down to four years to the remainder — while explicitly not implicating the Ennahda movement or its leadership in the killing, a finding Ennahda publicly characterized (statement of October 15, 2025) as clearing it and its president "beyond any doubt" after "twelve years of false and malicious accusations." Belaïd's family criticized the ruling as leaving the case's separate organizational-responsibility file — covering the alleged clandestine security apparatus and its leadership, i.e. the docket now covered by the Secret Apparatus case above — unresolved, and questioned who was "blocking" that parallel investigation. Independently corroborated across TAP (Agence Tunis Afrique Presse), Al-Estiklal, and The Arab Weekly, all reporting the same October 14–15, 2025 verdict and sentences. This verdict predates this artifact's v4.0 baseline by several months yet had never been documented in any of the 255 prior runs' otherwise-thorough coverage of the Belaïd case file, apparently because research repeatedly focused on the still-pending Akremi/Ellouze and Secret Apparatus dockets rather than the (wrongly assumed, still-pending) main case, which had in fact already concluded.
CriminalConstitutional

Affaire du financement étranger de la campagne 2019 — Ghannouchi / Bouchlaka (Run 305 addition)

Origin: Cour des Comptes flags a 2014 BCW lobbying contract (~US$285K), renewed/expanded ~US$187K July–Dec 2019 | First-instance verdict: July 13, 2024 (3 years each) | Appeal confirmation: September 21, 2024 | Fresh first-instance verdict: January 26, 2026 (3 years, Ghannouchi) | Appeal "definitively" confirms: Monday, July 13, 2026
A sixth, distinct Ghannouchi prosecution track — resting on Electoral Law Art. 163's foreign-campaign-financing prohibition rather than the state-security-conspiracy or terrorism charges underlying Case 1, Case 2, the Secret Apparatus, Instalingo, or Ramadan Evening Gathering cases documented in the consolidated entry above — a wholly new standalone card, cross-checked against 304 prior runs' coverage (no match for "Bouchlaka" or "foreign financing"/"financement étranger" anywhere in this artifact prior to this run). The case traces to a Cour des Comptes finding that Ennahda's 2019 electoral campaign benefited from an undeclared foreign lobbying contract with the U.S. firm BCW (Burson Cohn & Wolfe), originally signed 2014 (~US$285,000) and renewed/expanded for roughly US$187,000 between July and December 2019. The economic/financial judicial pole of the Tunis Court of First Instance first convicted Rached Ghannouchi and his son-in-law and Ennahda external-relations official Rafik Abdessalem Bouchlaka to 3 years' imprisonment each on July 13, 2024, plus a fine reported at the time as roughly US$1.17 million; the Tunis Court of Appeal confirmed that sentence on September 21, 2024. Identity note: "Rafik Bouchlaka" in July 2026 press coverage of this case is the same individual as Rafik Abdessalem, Ghannouchi's son-in-law and former Foreign Minister, already extensively documented in the consolidated "State Security Conspiracy" entry above as a co-defendant sentenced in absentia to 35 years (Case 2, Feb. 2, 2026) and 20 years (Ramadan Evening Gathering case, April 14–15, 2026) — "Abdessalem Bouchlaka" is his fuller compound family name, per Kapitalis's own tag archive and multiple 2022–2025 Kapitalis reports explicitly identifying him as Ghannouchi's "gendre" (son-in-law); this entry does not describe a newly-discovered person, only a newly-discovered case against an already-tracked one. A fresh first-instance verdict was then handed down January 26, 2026 by the Tunis First Instance Court's 6th correctional chamber — again 3 years for Ghannouchi, with a fine reported by La Presse at a considerably lower ~45,000 TND, a figure not reconciled against the 2024 stage's ~US$1.17 million (the discrepancy could reflect a fine levied on Ennahda as a party versus Ghannouchi personally, a currency/reporting error, or an intervening partial-payment/settlement not described in any source found — flagged as unresolved pending a source that states the relationship explicitly). On July 13, 2026 the Tunis Court of Appeal's chamber specialized in financial-corruption cases "definitively confirmed" (Kapitalis's characterization) that January 2026 verdict — 3 years each for Ghannouchi and Bouchlaka, plus the dollar-value-equivalent fine. Independently corroborated across Kapitalis (July 13, 2024; September 21, 2024; July 13, 2026), La Presse (January 26, 2026), and Univers News (July 13, 2026), with the Mosaïque FM and African Manager July 13, 2026 headlines matching the same story. Open question for a future run: no source found explicitly bridges the September 2024 appeal confirmation to the "fresh" January 2026 first-instance verdict — the most likely explanation is a Cour de Cassation annulment-and-remand between those dates, but this has not been independently verified and should not be asserted as fact.
CriminalFinancialConstitutional

Affaire du don au Croissant-Rouge tunisien (Prix Gandhi) — Ghannouchi (Run 316 addition)

Underlying donation: October 2016 | First-instance verdict: November 14, 2025 (2 years) | Appeal hearings: February 7 and February 27, 2026 (postponed) | Appeal ruling: July 13–14, 2026 (3 years, increased)
A seventh, distinct Ghannouchi prosecution track — resting on a tax-related charge rather than the state-security-conspiracy, terrorism, or foreign-financing statutes underlying the six cases in the consolidated "State Security Conspiracy" entry and the standalone foreign-financing case above — a wholly new standalone card, cross-checked against 315 prior runs' coverage (no match for "Croissant-Rouge," "Red Crescent," or "Gandhi" anywhere in this artifact prior to this run). The case concerns Ghannouchi's October 2016 donation, to the Tunisian Red Crescent, of the roughly US$14,000+ prize money from the international Indira Gandhi Prize for Peace, Disarmament and Development (informally reported in Tunisian and international press as the "Gandhi Peace and Tolerance Prize") that he received that year; the Tunis Court of First Instance's criminal chamber convicted him on November 14, 2025 to 2 years' imprisonment plus a fine, on a charge his defense team describes as treating the donation as a tax-evasion offense. Ghannouchi's lawyers argued the prosecution should have been dismissed as time-barred, since the alleged offense dated to October 2016 and fell outside the statute of limitations; an appeal hearing calendared for February 7, 2026 was postponed, then postponed again from a rescheduled February 27, 2026 date. A Tunis appeals court ruled on July 13–14, 2026, increasing the sentence to 3 years plus an unspecified fine (Middle East Monitor, The New Arab, both July 14, 2026, cross-checked against each other; a Tunisian-outlet original for this specific appeal ruling — as distinct from the November 2025 first-instance sentencing, which Businessnews reported directly — was not independently located this run and should be sought in a future pass). Human rights groups cited by both outlets characterized the ruling as an abuse of law and further evidence of judicial persecution of opposition figures, consistent with this artifact's broader documentation of Ghannouchi's now seven parallel/cumulative prosecutions. Update (Run 319): a fresh search again failed to locate an independent Tunisian-outlet original for this appeal ruling specifically; a July 13, 2026 Kapitalis report ("confirmation en appel des peines prononcées contre Ghannouchi et Bouchlaka") was directly verified to concern the separate foreign-financing case's own July 13, 2026 appeal confirmation, not this Red Crescent/Gandhi Prize case — the two rulings landed within a day of each other and involve overlapping defendants, a genuine conflation risk for future runs to guard against. The Middle East Monitor/The New Arab sourcing for this entry's own appeal ruling remains the only corroboration found to date.
CriminalTaxConstitutional

Affaire Marzouki / Daïmi / Kilani — "Paris Press Conference" Case (Run 205 addition)

Verdict: June 20, 2025 (Tunis Court of First Instance, terrorism-specialized chamber)
A wholly new standalone entry, cross-checked against 204 prior runs' coverage (no match for "Marzouki," "Daïmi," or "Kilani" in any prior entry) — a genuine and significant gap, since it is the only tracked prosecution of a former Tunisian head of state. Exiled former president Moncef Marzouki (2011–2014, Tunisia's first president elected after the 2011 revolution) was sentenced in absentia on June 20, 2025 to 22 years' imprisonment on terrorism and state-security charges, arising from a Paris press conference at which he, former presidential adviser Imed Daïmi, and former National Bar Association (ONAT) president Abderrazak Kilani sharply criticized Tunisian state institutions and members of the judiciary; Daïmi and Kilani received identical 22-year in-absentia sentences, with at least two further co-defendants reportedly sentenced alongside them (The New Arab, Arab News, both corroborating the same verdict). All named defendants remain in exile; Marzouki called the ruling "surreal." Distinct from — and should not be conflated with — the "State Security Conspiracy" cluster above, which does not name any of these three defendants.
CriminalConstitutional

Affaire Habib Haouas / SOREAL — Organized Money Laundering Conviction (Run 85 addition)

Arrest warrant: November 17, 2023 | Customs reconciliation agreement: June 22, 2026 | Verdict: June 29, 2026
A wholly new standalone entry, entirely distinct from the Mabrouk/Chahed/Turki dockets above — this is an ordinary (non-political) organized-crime financial case, not part of the "state security conspiracy" cluster. Businessman Habib Haouas, former director of metal-recycling firm SOREAL Tunisie (founded 1997) and resident abroad since at least 2017, was sentenced on June 29, 2026 by the specialized financial-corruption criminal chamber of the Tunis Court of First Instance to 22 years' imprisonment (a co-defendant received 24 years) for organized-group money laundering, abusive exploitation of company mechanisms, and property damage, with customs, exchange-control, and tax-fraud offenses also charged; the underlying investigation dates to a November 17, 2023 arrest warrant from the Economic and Financial Judicial Pole. A June 22, 2026 reconciliation agreement with the Customs Directorate-General (~12 million TND) was presented to the court by defense counsel but did not alter the sentence (Businessnews, Kapitalis, La Presse de Tunisie, L'Economiste Maghrébin, all June 30–July 1, 2026).
CriminalFinancial

Affaire Makram Jlassi — Embezzlement Conviction (Run 87 addition)

Dismissed from Justice Ministry post: effective July 10, 2024 (JORT) | First-instance sentence: March 11, 2026 (5 years) | Appeal ruling: July 4, 2026 (raised to 8 years)
A wholly new standalone entry, distinct from both the "state security conspiracy" cluster and the Haouas/SOREAL case above — an ordinary embezzlement/breach-of-trust prosecution of a former Justice Ministry official. Makram Jlassi, a former mission officer (chargé de mission) in the Justice Minister's cabinet dismissed from that post effective July 10, 2024, was convicted by the Tunis Court of First Instance's criminal chamber on March 11, 2026 and sentenced to 5 years' imprisonment for embezzlement and breach of trust, following a complaint filed by the Tunisian Association for the Prevention of Road Accidents. On appeal, the Tunis Court of Appeal's criminal chamber confirmed the underlying conviction but increased the sentence to 8 years on July 4, 2026 (reported same-day by WebManagerCenter/Directinfo, webdo, African Manager, Réalités, and Kapitalis).
CriminalAdministrative

Affaire Samir Abdelli — Money Laundering & Terrorism-Financing Conviction (Run 181 addition)

Verdict: July 7, 2026 (Tunis Court of First Instance, terrorism-specialized criminal chamber)
A wholly new standalone entry — a full-text search of 180 prior runs found no match for "Abdelli." Samir Abdelli, a lawyer and 2014 presidential candidate previously named in the SwissLeaks and Panama Papers disclosures, was sentenced by the terrorism-specialized criminal chamber of the Tunis Court of First Instance to 18 years' imprisonment with no possibility of parole, after two years in pre-trial detention, on charges of money laundering, terrorism-related offenses, and conspiracy against the state's internal and external security — charged under the same general Penal Code state-security provisions and Anti-Terrorism Law (No. 2015-26) framework as the "State Security Conspiracy" cluster above, but a distinct docket with no defendant overlap identified. His driver and secretary, tried at liberty in the same case, received 3 years and 2 years respectively. No appeal had been reported as of this run (Business News, Kapitalis, Mosaïque FM, all July 7–8, 2026).
CriminalFinancial

Affaire Chawki Tabib — Former INLUCC & Bar Association President, Forgery Conviction (Run 182 addition)

Verdict: May 21, 2026 (Tunis Court of First Instance)
A wholly new standalone entry — a full-text search of 181 prior runs found no match for "Tabib." Chawki Tabib, former president of the National Bar Association (ONAT) and, from 2016–2020, former president of INLUCC (see the Declaration of Assets/INLUCC entry above), was sentenced on May 21, 2026 to a combined 10 years' imprisonment (7 years for forgery plus 3 years for possession/use of forged documents). The prosecution traces to a complaint arising from an INLUCC report issued during Tabib's own tenure that had accused former Prime Minister Elyes Fakhfakh of a conflict of interest. Independently corroborated across La Presse de Tunisie, the IAPL Monitoring Committee on Attacks on Lawyers, and CRLDHT, the latter characterizing the conviction of a former anti-corruption-authority president on forgery charges as marking a further stage in the erosion of the rule of law.
CriminalAdministrative

Affaire Ayachi Hammami — Defense Counsel Prosecuted as a Defendant in His Own Clients' Case (Run 181 addition)

Added to suspect list: May 2023 | Indicted: October 2023 | Appeal conviction: ~November 2025 (5 years) | Incarcerated: December 3, 2025 | Hunger strike: ended January 15, 2026 (43 days) | UN Human Rights Council 62nd session closed: July 8, 2026
A genuine gap despite 180 prior research runs' detailed coverage of the "State Security Conspiracy" cluster above: a Run 104 changelog note asserted this was "already substantively captured within the precision of the existing consolidated entries," but a direct full-text check found no prior mention of Hammami's conviction, detention, or hunger strike anywhere in the artifact — only his December 2025 arrest had been noted in passing in that changelog entry itself, not in a law-card. Ayachi Hammami, a prominent human-rights lawyer, represented several Case 1 defendants in the conspiracy prosecution until May 2023, when the investigating judge added him to the suspect list; he was indicted in October 2023 and, on appeal, sentenced to 5 years' imprisonment in the same mega-trial, beginning to serve the sentence at El Mornaguia prison on December 3, 2025. He conducted a 43-day hunger strike protesting his detention and restrictions on visits from his own defense counsel, ending it January 15, 2026; supporters marked 100 days of detention on March 12, 2026 by forming a national support committee. UN special-procedures experts (OHCHR) said they were "shocked" by the conviction, calling it a direct attack on the independence of the legal profession; the UN Human Rights Council's 62nd session (closed July 8, 2026) saw the UN Special Rapporteur on the independence of judges and lawyers and UN Human Rights Chief Volker Türk cite Hammami's case alongside those of AMT president Anas Hmaidi (see the Judicial Statute entry above) and Sihem Bensedrine (above) as part of a broader pattern of retaliation against Tunisia's legal and judicial community (OHCHR, Front Line Defenders, FIDH, LDH, Jeune Afrique, Kapitalis, HRW, all cross-corroborated).
CriminalConstitutional

Affaire Abdennaceur Aouini — Appeal Conviction Despite Withdrawn Complaint (Run 367 addition)

Underlying dispute: over 10 years old | First-instance ruling: non-lieu (no case to answer) | Correctional appeals chamber conviction: July 15–16, 2026 (1 year)
A wholly new standalone entry: lawyer Abdennaceur Aouini — a member of the Chokri Belaïd/Mohamed Brahmi families' legal defense committee (see the Belaïd assassination trial and main case above) — was convicted by the correctional appeals chamber of the Tunis Court of First Instance to one year's imprisonment, reversing a first-instance non-lieu. The prosecution traces to a decade-old dispute with fellow lawyer Hammadi Baccar, who had since withdrawn his original complaint; reporting checked (Business News, WebManagerCenter/Directinfo, Kapitalis, all cross-corroborated) does not specify the statutory charge under which the appellate chamber nonetheless convicted. Aouini's defense committee and rights advocates frame the ruling as retaliation for his remarks at the ONAT general assembly of May 1, 2026 and his participation in a regional lawyers' strike on May 19, 2026 — both part of the ONAT–Justice Ministry standoff already documented in the Bar Law (ONAT) entry above — rather than a genuine revival of the underlying private dispute; the Tunisian Bar and civil-society groups expressed solidarity. Distinct from the Ayachi Hammami case above (a different defense-counsel prosecution arising from the "State Security Conspiracy" cluster) — this case instead surfaces via the ONAT dispute, illustrating a second, separate channel through which lawyers engaged in that standoff have faced individual prosecution.
CriminalJudicial

Affaire Taoufik Mekacher — Unsecured Public-Bank Loans Conviction (Run 92 addition)

Related ex-bank-official first-instance sentence: February 1, 2026 (2 years) | Mekacher first-instance verdict: July 3, 2026 (6 years)
A wholly new standalone entry, distinct from the Haouas/SOREAL and Jlassi cases above — another ordinary (non-political) financial-crime prosecution from the same specialized financial-corruption criminal chamber of the Tunis Court of First Instance that has produced several of this cluster's convictions in the same window (see the Run 81 Mabrouk/Chahed and Turki updates on the Anti-Terrorism/AML and State Security Conspiracy entries above). Businessman Taoufik Mekacher — previously convicted in July 2021 to 5 years for uncovered checks and forgery, a separate matter — was sentenced on July 3, 2026 to 6 years' imprisonment for obtaining substantial loans from a public bank without sufficient guarantees, causing the institution financial harm; the court entered a non-lieu (discontinuance) for a co-defendant bank official in this same ruling. The case is part of a broader "affaire des prêts bancaires sans garantie" implicating some 31 suspects including a former minister (Samir Saïed, ordered kept in provisional detention as of a January 2025 ruling), within which a separate ex-bank-official defendant had already been convicted to 2 years on February 1, 2026 for the same underlying loan-approval practice (La Presse de Tunisie, Businessnews/Mkacher variant spelling, allAfrica, all July 3, 2026; Kapitalis and Webdo for the broader case background).
CriminalFinancial

Affaire Taïeb Rached — "Cassations Sans Renvoi" Judicial Corruption Case (Run 103 addition)

Unauthorized chambers created: 2019 | First-instance verdict: October 27–28, 2025 | Appeal verdict: May 23, 2026
A wholly new standalone entry — distinct from the Haouas/SOREAL, Jlassi, and Mekacher financial-crime cases above, and the first prosecution of a judge of this rank in Tunisian judicial history. In 2019, as First President of the Court of Cassation, Taïeb Rached created two chambers without legal basis (Nos. 35 and 36) and appointed magistrates Abderrazek Bahouri and Marouane Tlili to sit on them; those chambers issued "cassation sans renvoi" rulings (cassation without remand for retrial) that benefited businessmen Nejib Ismaïl and Fethi Jenayah, shielding them from further prosecution and causing the Tunisian state an estimated one billion TND in losses. The Tunis Court of First Instance's specialized financial-corruption chamber convicted Rached on October 27–28, 2025 to 30 years' imprisonment, with Bahouri and Tlili (both since revoked from the magistracy) sentenced to 20 years each, alongside civil damages ordered against the judges and businessman Fethi Jenayah. On appeal, the Tunis Court of Appeal's specialized financial-corruption chamber reduced all three judges' sentences to 10 years each on May 23, 2026, while confirming roughly 935 million TND in civil damages against the three judges and Jenayah; other businessman co-defendants in the case include Adel Jenayah, Kamel Taboubi, and El Wardi Nouisri. Independently corroborated across Kapitalis, L'Économiste Maghrébin, La Presse de Tunisie, Directinfo/WebManagerCenter, African Manager, and allAfrica.
CriminalJudicialFinancial

Sworn Interpreters Law — Law No. 94-80 of July 4, 1994(Run 179 addition)

Enacted: July 4, 1994 | Fee schedule: Joint Order of the Ministers of Justice and Finance, December 19, 1996 | Registration-exam rules: Ministerial Order of March 2, 2000 | Procedure manual: Ministerial Order of November 24, 1998
A wholly new standalone entry — a companion legal-profession statute to the Notaries Law (No. 94-60, above) and the Judicial Officers/Bailiffs statute, neither of which had ever been cross-referenced against this one despite both being individually documented. Organizes the profession of interprète assermenté (sworn interpreter/translator), the only category of person legally authorized to produce certified translations for use before Tunisian courts, notaries, and public administration. Establishes a roll (registre) of sworn interpreters maintained by territorial jurisdiction under the supervision of the Ministry of Justice, with admission conditioned on passing a qualifying examination whose regulations and syllabus are fixed by ministerial order (most recently the Order of March 2, 2000). A 1996 joint order of the Ministers of Justice and Finance fixed sworn interpreters' official fee schedule, and a 1998 Ministry of Justice order approved the profession's standard procedure manual. The Ministry of Justice and Tunisian consular authorities abroad (e.g., the French Consulate General in Tunis) publish official lists of registered sworn interpreters by language pair. Confirmed via ILO NATLEX's citation of the law's full title and Tunisia's Ministry of Justice site.
Judicial

Mediation Law — Law No. 2002-82 of August 3, 2002

Enacted: August 3, 2002 | In force: 2002
Establishes the legal framework for civil and commercial mediation as an alternative dispute resolution (ADR) mechanism. Mediators must be registered and trained. A mediation agreement, once signed by parties and approved by the competent court, is enforceable as a court judgment. The Startup Act (2018) encourages mediation for commercial disputes involving labeled startups. Reduces court caseloads and offers faster, confidential dispute resolution.
JudicialCivil

Notaries Law — Law No. 94-60 of May 23, 1994(Run 44 correction)

Enacted: May 23, 1994 | Predecessor: Law No. 88-27 of July 12, 1988
Run 44 correction: prior runs cited this statute as "Law No. 67-39 of June 8, 1967" — no such law could be found in any source searched (NATLEX, jurisitetunisie.com, legislation-securite.tn), and it appears to have been a fabricated citation. The verified currently-operative statute, confirmed directly from jurisitetunisie.com's full-text reproduction and cited in the preamble of the 2011 decree-law reorganizing the lawyer profession, is Law No. 94-60 of May 23, 1994 — 65 articles across 8 chapters (registration on the professional roll; powers; legal status; rights/obligations; discipline; regional chambers and national association; transitional provisions). It separated the notarial function from that of court bailiff (huissier de justice), a split not present in the probable predecessor, Law No. 88-27 of July 12, 1988. Notaries are public officers authenticating deeds, contracts, inheritance declarations, and real property transactions; notarial acts are enforceable without court order, and all real property transfers, mortgage deeds, and company-formation documents must be notarized. Supervised by regional chambers of notaries and a national professional association.
JudicialCivil

Law No. 87-79 of December 29, 1987 (as amended)Lawyers Statute (Barreau de Tunisie)

Enacted: December 29, 1987 | In force: 1988 | Amended: 1999, 2009
Governs the legal profession and bar association in Tunisia. The Bar Association (Ordre National des Avocats de Tunisie — ONAT) is the self-regulatory body for all lawyers admitted to the Tunisian bar. Sets qualification requirements (law degree + 3-year pupillage), bar admission conditions, and continuing education obligations. Regulates fee structures, professional conduct, confidentiality (avocat-client privilege), conflict of interest rules, and disciplinary procedures. Guarantees lawyers' immunity from prosecution for oral or written statements made in the exercise of their functions before courts. 2026 ONAT–Justice Ministry standoff (added Run 170): resolves the "July 11 ONAT" watchlist item this artifact had carried unconfirmed since Run 145 — it refers to this Law's own regulatory body (avocat.org.tn), not the architects' or chartered-accountants' orders. ONAT delivered written demands for legal-profession reforms to the Justice Ministry on January 23, 2026, centered on three grievances: prosecutions targeting individual lawyers, the continued absence of a permanent Supreme Judicial Council (see the Provisional Supreme Judicial Council entry above), and the composition/appointment procedure of criminal-court chambers. An exceptional ONAT general assembly held May 1, 2026 was challenged by the Tunis Court of Appeal's prosecutor general over its procedural legality (hearing held June 10, 2026); ONAT's Council responded May 13, 2026 by approving regional strikes, red armbands, and a boycott of improperly-composed criminal chambers, culminating in a national general strike and mobilization at the Tunis Palace of Justice on June 18, 2026. ONAT president Boubaker Ben Thabet maintained the actions were lawful and did not disrupt court operations, while stating openness to dialogue. (Run 249 addition): at the June 10, 2026 hearing, some 580 lawyers filed a formal acte de constitution to defend ONAT's position against the prosecutor general's challenge; the Tunis Court of First Instance postponed examination of the case to October 28, 2026 — a date business commentary (Business News) noted risks mooting the appeal's practical effect, since the actions taken at the contested May 1 assembly (strikes, boycotts) would largely have run their course well before that hearing date. ONAT's routine annual general assembly, held Saturday July 11, 2026, was expected to set out conditions for resuming dialogue with the Justice Ministry if the dispute remained unresolved by that date; no outlet had reported that assembly's outcome as of this run's cutoff — a future run should check for its resolution. Update (Run 315): still unreported by any outlet checked in French, English, or Arabic, now well over 60 consecutive runs since the assembly convened — the longest-standing unresolved item this artifact tracks. The only adjacent item found remains Bâtonnier Belthabet's July 13, 2026 meeting with UGTT secretary-general Slaheddine Selmi (already captured at Run 313), which does not itself report the assembly's outcome. Update (Run 381): a distinct, narrower thread showed movement — Justice Minister Leïla Jaffel received the newly elected president of the Tunisian Association of Young Lawyers (Association Tunisienne des Jeunes Avocats), Montassar Nefzi, on July 16, 2026 (Business News, Réalités Magazine), and announced that a new headquarters building for ISPA (Institut Supérieur de la Profession d'Avocat, the bar's professional-training institute) had been included in the Justice Ministry's draft 2027 budget, with the ministry pledging to "favorably examine" the young lawyers' other proposals. This concerns young-lawyer-specific training-institute demands, not the main ONAT–Justice Ministry standoff described above, and does not resolve or report on the still-unconfirmed July 11 general assembly outcome — offered as an adjacent dialogue signal rather than a resolution.
Key provisions
  • Art. 1–10: Bar membership; qualification requirements; pupillage (stage) period
  • Art. 11–20: Professional rights — audience before all courts; freedom of speech in court
  • Art. 21–28: Inviolability of lawyer-client communications; prohibition of office searches without ONAT involvement
  • Art. 29–36: Professional conduct rules; prohibition of conflicts of interest
  • Art. 37–44: Fee arrangements; prohibition of contingency fees as primary arrangement
  • Art. 45–52: ONAT governance — National Council, regional bars (Tunis, Sousse, Sfax, etc.)
  • Art. 53–60: Disciplinary council; sanctions ranging from warning to permanent disbarment
JudicialAdministrative

Law No. 2002-52 of June 3, 2002Legal Aid (Aide Judiciaire) (Run 44 correction)

Enacted: June 3, 2002 | Amended: Law No. 2007-27 of May 7, 2007
Run 44 correction: prior runs cited this statute as "Law No. 2002-42 of April 3, 2002" — no such law could be verified in any source searched, and it appears to have been a fabricated citation. The verified statute, confirmed via legislation-securite.tn and jurisitetunisie.com's "bureau d'aide judiciaire" page, is Law No. 2002-52 of June 3, 2002, amended by Law No. 2007-27 of May 7, 2007 (extending eligibility to criminal cassation appeals). Establishes Tunisia's legal aid system for civil and criminal matters: any natural person lacking sufficient income to cover court/enforcement costs without compromising basic necessities may apply, at any stage of proceedings; non-profit legal persons and foreigners (subject to reciprocity) also qualify. Applications are decided by a Legal Aid Bureau attached to each Court of First Instance, chaired by the Republic Prosecutor with a Finance Ministry representative and a lawyer as members; decisions are not subject to appeal, though a new application may be resubmitted. A separate, older track — Decree No. 74-882 of September 26, 1974, replaced by Organic Law No. 2011-2 of January 3, 2011 — governs legal aid specifically before the Administrative Court.
JudicialSocial

Organic Law No. 2018-9 of January 30, 2018 (Run 161 correction)Judicial Officers (Huissiers de Justice)

Enacted: January 30, 2018 | Predecessor: Law No. 95-29 of March 13, 1995 (itself replacing a 1958 governing text) | In force: 2018
Run 161 correction: prior runs cited a non-existent "Law No. 66-49 of July 2, 1966" as the governing statute; independent verification (WebSearch plus a direct fetch of the legislation-securite.tn primary-source text) found no such 1966 law and instead confirmed the actual succession: a 1958 governing text was repealed and replaced by Law No. 95-29 of March 13, 1995, which was in turn repealed and replaced by the currently operative Organic Law No. 2018-9 of January 30, 2018 — both successor statutes titled identically, "portant organisation de la profession des huissiers de justice." Regulates the profession of judicial officers (huissiers de justice) in Tunisia — public officers responsible for drafting and serving protests, notices, and summonses; executing judicial and administrative decisions; conducting inspections and judicial auctions (ventes aux enchères); and performing voluntary collections. Huissiers are appointed by the Justice Minister following a competitive exam at the Institut Supérieur de la Magistrature (judges and registered lawyers are exempted from the exam). Regulated by the National Chamber of Huissiers (Chambre Nationale des Huissiers de Justice) under a disciplinary council chaired by the national dean. Pending reform: a further modernization bill (digitized archives, revised access conditions to the profession, expanded legal protections for huissiers) is under review, supported by the professional order; a provision within it would additionally allow mutual-consent divorce to be finalized before a huissier-notaire without court involvement, which women's rights groups have criticized as weakening Personal Status Code judicial safeguards for wives. Not yet enacted.
Key provisions
  • Art. 5–9: Status and qualifications — Tunisian nationality (5+ years), legal age, law degree required
  • Art. 7: Judges and registered lawyers exempted from the competitive entry exam
  • Art. 11–12: Oath before the appellate court; registration of signature with the Justice Ministry; liability insurance required within 3 months of appointment
  • Art. 13: Core duties — service of process, execution of judicial/administrative decisions, inspections, judicial auctions, voluntary collections
  • Art. 23–38: Professional conduct — bar on other public office, commerce, or legal practice; mandatory continuing education
  • Art. 29–32: Two numbered registers (general and execution-related), inspected every 6 months, records retained 15 years
  • Art. 43–44: Fee tariff set jointly by the Justice and Finance Ministers, reviewed every 3 years; right to refuse work absent an advance payment
  • Art. 62–75: Professional discipline — warnings, suspension, or permanent removal via disciplinary council
JudicialCivilPending

Law No. 93-61 of June 23, 1993Judicial Experts (Experts Judiciaires) (as amended by Law No. 2010-33 of June 21, 2010)

Enacted: June 23, 1993 | Published: JORT No. 48, June 29, 1993 | Amended: June 21, 2010
Governs court-appointed experts (experts judiciaires) used across civil, commercial, criminal, and administrative proceedings for technical assessments ordered by a judge — registration on an official expert roll organized by specialty, qualification and integrity conditions, oath-taking, professional discipline, and fee-setting for court-ordered expert reports. The 2010 amendment revised the chapters on registration, professional obligations, and discipline. Complements the general expert-evidence provisions of the Code of Civil and Commercial Procedure and the Code of Criminal Procedure, which authorize courts to order expert reports but do not themselves regulate the experts' professional status.
JudicialCivil

Extradition & Mutual Legal Assistance — Code of Criminal Procedure, Book IV, Ch. VIII (Arts. 308–335)

Base code: Law No. 68-23 of July 24, 1968 | Extradition chapter amended by Law No. 93-113 of November 22, 1993
Tunisia has no standalone extradition or mutual-legal-assistance statute — the current cybercrime entry elsewhere in this artifact notes only in passing that the Budapest Convention "complement[s]" extradition mechanisms, without a dedicated entry for the underlying domestic framework. Extradition of foreigners is instead governed entirely by Book IV, Chapter VIII of the Code of Criminal Procedure (conditions, Arts. 308–315; procedure, Arts. 316–328, including a chambre d'accusation review and presidential decree under Art. 324; effects, Arts. 329–335), supplemented by bilateral and multilateral treaties. Tunisian nationals cannot be extradited; political offenses and torture risk are recognized grounds for refusal. Update (Run 66): beyond the 1972 France convention, dedicated bilateral extradition treaties are in force with Belgium (1990), Malta (2006), Portugal (1998), Turkey (1983), Syria (2007), and Germany (1969); a new Algeria–Tunisia judicial-cooperation and extradition convention was signed December 15, 2021 and ratified by Tunisia via Presidential Decree No. 2022-312 of April 8, 2022 (Algeria's ratification reported February 2025). No dedicated extradition treaty was confirmed with Italy, Libya, Egypt, Morocco, the UK, or the US. Multilaterally, Tunisia is party to the UN Convention against Transnational Organized Crime/Palermo Convention (ratified June 19, 2003) and its Trafficking Protocol (July 14, 2003), UNCAC (ratified September 23, 2008), and the 1998 Arab Convention on the Suppression of Terrorism (ratified via Law No. 99-10 of February 15, 1999). A 2025–2026 test case saw the Paris Court of Appeal reject Tunisia's extradition request for Halima Ben Ali, daughter of the late president, in April 2026, reportedly over inadequate fair-trial guarantees.
JudicialCriminal

Affaire Mohamed Ben Salem — Default Customs Conviction, Arrest & Release(Run 207 addition)

Default conviction: 2 years (date not independently confirmed) | Arrested: July 8, 2026 | Released after opposition filed: July 9, 2026 | Merits hearing set: July 21, 2026
A wholly new standalone entry — a full-text search of 206 prior runs found no match for "Ben Salem." Mohamed Ben Salem, a former Minister of Agriculture and Ennahda party leader, was arrested July 8, 2026 in the Cap Bon region on the strength of a prior default (in absentia) conviction to 2 years' imprisonment for customs-law offenses, handed down by the correctional chamber of the Tunis Court of Appeal. Ben Salem filed formal opposition to the default judgment, and the appellate prosecutor ordered his release on July 9, 2026 under the procedure applicable to defaulted convictions contested in this way. A merits hearing — allowing Ben Salem to mount a full defense rather than be tried in absentia — has been set for July 21, 2026 before the Tunis Court of Appeal's summer correctional chamber (Kapitalis, webdo.tn, Directinfo/WebManagerCenter, Tuniscope, all July 9, 2026). Distinct from the "State Security Conspiracy" cluster above — this case rests on customs allegations rather than state-security or speech-related charges, and Ben Salem is not named among that cluster's defendants.
CriminalAdministrative

Affaire ETAP / Champ Gazier de Nawara — Public-Funds Embezzlement Appeal Ruling(Run 257 addition)

Underlying contract: Nawara gas field concession (Tataouine) | First-instance conviction: prior to this update (date not independently confirmed) | Appeal ruling: July 11, 2026
A wholly new standalone entry, distinct from the Mabrouk/Chahed/Turki, Haouas/SOREAL, Jlassi, and Mekacher financial-corruption dockets above — this case involves the state hydrocarbons company. On July 11, 2026, the Tunis Court of Appeal's specialized criminal (financial-corruption) chamber ruled on the appeal in an embezzlement-of-public-funds case tied to state oil company ETAP's (Entreprise Tunisienne d'Activités Pétrolières — see the Hydrocarbons Code entry for ETAP's general statutory role) Nawara gas field concession contract in Tataouine. The court upheld the 2-year first-instance conviction of former ETAP CEO Mohamed Ali Khalil, while reducing the sentences of two other former officials, Abdelwaheb Khamassi and Monji Nairi, from 8 years each to 4 years each on appeal; the accompanying fines were maintained unchanged for all three defendants (directinfo.webmanagercenter.com and allAfrica, both July 11, 2026, cross-corroborated).
CriminalFinancialEnergy

Draft Organic Law on Enforcement of Court Rulings ("Ending Administrative Impunity")(Run 272 addition)

Filed with the ARP's General Legislation Committee: reported July 10, 2026 | Status: newly filed, committee review stage
A wholly new standalone entry — a 19-article member's bill filed by 10 deputies that would criminalize non-enforcement of final court rulings by public administration, imposing up to 3 years' imprisonment plus fines on officials who obstruct or fail to execute judicial decisions, and mandating annual administrative-compliance reporting to track enforcement rates. Framed by sponsors as closing a long-standing accountability gap in which Tunisian courts issue judgments against the administration — including reinstatement and compensation orders — that go unenforced for years without consequence for the officials responsible. Reported by La Presse ("Quand la justice retrouve ses clés," July 10, 2026) and reprinted by allAfrica (July 11, 2026); distinct from the general judicial-independence and court-organization framework already documented elsewhere in this section. No committee vote or plenary referral confirmed as of this run.
JudicialAdministrativePending

🌍 International Law & Key Treaties

Key International Conventions Ratified by Tunisia

Various dates
Tunisia is party to numerous international conventions forming part of domestic law upon ratification (monist approach per Art. 20 of the 2022 Constitution).
Major conventions
  • UN Charter (1956) — upon independence
  • ICCPR — ratified March 18, 1969
  • ICESCR — ratified March 18, 1969
  • CEDAW — ratified September 20, 1985 (reservations partially lifted 2011)
  • Convention on the Rights of the Child — ratified January 29, 1992
  • UN Convention Against Torture — ratified September 23, 1988
  • African Charter on Human and Peoples' Rights — ratified 1983
  • Arab Charter on Human Rights — ratified 2008
  • New York Convention on Arbitral Awards (1958) — ratified July 22, 1967
  • ILO Core Conventions — 62 conventions ratified
  • TRIPS Agreement — WTO member since March 29, 1995
  • WIPO Copyright Treaty — ratified 2012
  • Patent Cooperation Treaty (PCT) — member since March 1, 2001
  • UNCAC — ratified October 23, 2008
  • Paris Agreement on Climate Change — ratified July 3, 2017
  • UNCLOS — ratified April 24, 1985
  • EU-Tunisia Association Agreement — in force March 1, 1998
Constitutional

Withdrawal of the Article 34(6) DeclarationProtocol to the African Charter on Human and Peoples' Rights on the Establishment of an African Court

Reported: March 2026
Tunisia reportedly withdrew its declaration under Article 34(6) of the Ouagadougou Protocol establishing the African Court on Human and Peoples' Rights, which had permitted individuals and NGOs to file complaints directly with the Court without first exhausting proceedings before the African Commission. If confirmed, the withdrawal would close that direct-access route for Tunisian complainants going forward, mirroring earlier withdrawals by Rwanda, Tanzania, Benin, and Côte d'Ivoire, and would mark a rollback of one of Tunisia's post-2011 international human-rights-law commitments (Tunisia ratified the underlying African Charter in 1983 and the Protocol establishing the Court separately). Flagged as a single-source finding from this run's research pass, pending stronger corroboration (e.g., an African Court press release or JORT/Ministry of Foreign Affairs text) in a future run — consistent with this artifact's practice of noting unverified leads rather than silently omitting or overstating them.
ConstitutionalUnconfirmed

EU-Tunisia Association Agreement — Law No. 97-37 of July 1, 1997

Signed: July 17, 1995 | Ratified by Tunisia: July 1, 1997 | In force: March 1, 1998
Association agreement between Tunisia and the European Union — the first Euro-Mediterranean Partnership agreement. Established a free trade area for manufactured goods, progressively eliminating tariffs by 2008. Includes provisions on services, capital, intellectual property, and political dialogue. Supplemented by Action Plans under the European Neighbourhood Policy. DCFTA (Deep and Comprehensive Free Trade Area) negotiations launched 2016, not yet concluded as of 2026.
CommercialConstitutional

Arab Maghreb Union Treaty — 1989 (Marrakesh Treaty)

Signed: February 17, 1989 | Ratified: 1989
Treaty establishing the Arab Maghreb Union (UMA) among Tunisia, Algeria, Morocco, Libya, and Mauritania. Aims for eventual economic integration and free movement. In practice largely dormant due to Algeria-Morocco tensions. Bilateral agreements within the AMU framework remain in force, including free movement protocols for Maghreb nationals within the region.
CommercialConstitutional

Decree No. 23 of 2025Ratification of the North Western Sahara Aquifer System Consultation Mechanism

Underlying agreement signed: April 24, 2024 (Algeria, tripartite agriculture-ministers meeting) | Ratifying decree published: JORT Issue No. 4, January 11, 2025
A two-article decree: Art. 1 ratifies the tripartite agreement establishing a consultation mechanism ("mécanisme de concertation") for shared management of the North Western Sahara Aquifer System (SASS/NWSAS), a transboundary fossil groundwater resource shared by Tunisia, Algeria, and Libya and critical to southern Tunisia's water supply; Art. 2 covers publication. Algeria ratified the same agreement in parallel.
EnvironmentalConstitutional

UNCLOS Ratification — April 24, 1985

Ratified: April 24, 1985 | Convention in force: November 16, 1994
Tunisia ratified the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS). Defines maritime zones: 12-nautical-mile territorial sea, 24 nm contiguous zone, and 200 nm Exclusive Economic Zone (EEZ). Bilateral maritime delimitation agreements concluded with Italy (1971, continental shelf) and Libya (boundary established by ICJ ruling in 1982). Tunisia's EEZ in the Mediterranean is significant for fisheries and hydrocarbon exploration rights.
ConstitutionalTransport

WTO Membership and Bilateral Trade Agreements

WTO membership: March 29, 1995 | Ongoing bilateral and regional agreements
Tunisia is a WTO member and party to multiple bilateral and regional trade agreements: Pan-Arab Free Trade Area (PAFTA, 1997); EU Association Agreement (1998); Agadir Agreement with Egypt, Jordan, and Morocco (2007, implementing diagonal cumulation of origin); EFTA-Tunisia Free Trade Agreement (2005). DCFTA talks with the EU and negotiations for African Continental Free Trade Area (AfCFTA) integration remain active as of 2026.
CommercialConstitutional

Agadir Agreement — Law No. 2004-20 of February 24, 2004 (ratification) | Operational: 2007

Agreement signed: February 25, 2004 | Ratified by Tunisia: February 24, 2004 | Operational: March 2007
The Agadir Agreement on the establishment of a free trade zone between Tunisia, Morocco, Egypt, and Jordan — four countries with EU Association Agreements. Eliminates tariffs and non-tariff barriers on trade in goods between member states. A key feature is the application of diagonal cumulation of origin with the EU, allowing products manufactured from inputs originating in any member country to benefit from EU preferential tariffs. Managed by the Agadir Technical Unit (ATU) based in Amman. Tunisia's exports to Egypt and Jordan are primarily industrial goods, olive oil, and chemicals. The agreement fits within the Euro-Mediterranean Partnership framework and enhances supply chain integration between North Africa and the Middle East.
Key provisions
  • Art. 1–5: Establishment of the free trade zone; objectives
  • Art. 6–10: Tariff elimination schedule for industrial goods (immediate for most)
  • Art. 11–15: Rules of origin: Pan-Euro-Mediterranean cumulation rules apply
  • Art. 16–20: Agricultural goods: partial liberalization with tariff-rate quotas for sensitive items
  • Art. 21–25: Trade facilitation: simplified customs procedures, single document
  • Art. 26–30: Dispute resolution mechanism; role of ATU
  • Art. 31–35: Safeguard clauses; anti-dumping provisions
CommercialConstitutional

AfCFTA Ratification — Law No. 2021-19 of June 7, 2021

Agreement adopted: March 21, 2018 | Tunisia ratified: June 7, 2021 | Trading began: January 1, 2021 (for earlier ratifiers)
Tunisia ratified the Agreement Establishing the African Continental Free Trade Area (AfCFTA) in June 2021, becoming one of 44 countries to ratify by that date (out of 55 AU member states). The AfCFTA creates the world's largest free trade area by number of participating countries, covering 1.3 billion people and a GDP of approximately $3.4 trillion. Phase I covers trade in goods (tariff reduction) and trade in services. Phase II covers investment, intellectual property, competition, and e-commerce. Tunisia hopes to use the AfCFTA to diversify its export markets beyond Europe (currently ~70% of trade), targeting sub-Saharan Africa's consumer markets, particularly for processed foods, pharmaceuticals, textiles, and ICT services.
Key provisions
  • Tariff reduction: 90% of tariff lines phased to zero over 5–10 years (LDC treatment: 15 years)
  • Sensitive goods: 7% of tariff lines; longer liberalization schedules
  • Excluded goods: 3% (limited exclusions for security or development reasons)
  • Rules of origin: negotiated per sector; generally 25–35% local value addition
  • AfCFTA Secretariat in Accra, Ghana manages dispute resolution and implementation
  • Services schedules: Phase II negotiations ongoing; Tunisia targeting professional services and ICT
CommercialConstitutional

Organic Law (Projet de loi organique n° 07/2026) — Ratified by ARP April 7, 2026Hosting Agreement for the African Centre of Excellence for Inclusive Markets (AIMEC)

Underlying agreement signed: July 14, 2022 (sidelines of the 41st AU Executive Council session, Lusaka) | Ratified by ARP plenary: April 7, 2026 (92 for, 3 against, 3 abstentions) | AIMEC officially launched in Tunis: October 14, 2025
Organic law ratifying the 2022 headquarters agreement between Tunisia and the African Union Commission establishing the seat of the African Centre of Excellence for Inclusive Markets (AIMEC) in Tunis. AIMEC is an AU Commission institution supporting the development of inclusive, integrated markets across Africa — fostering the participation of vulnerable groups in production and consumption, advancing Sustainable Development Goals, and promoting innovation in economic policy. The law establishes the legal framework governing the Centre's activities in Tunisia and guarantees its administrative independence. During ARP committee review, Trade Minister Samir Abid was questioned over a perceived deficit of Tunisian diplomatic representation within the Centre's governance. Following ratification, Youth and Sports Minister Sadok Mourali received AIMEC's executive director, Dr. Youssef Ahmed Kardfani, on April 10, 2026, to discuss deepening cooperation on youth economic inclusion. (Run 77 addition — a genuine gap in the international-treaties watchlist that 76 prior runs had missed entirely, cross-verified across African Manager, WebManagerCenter, Business News, and La Presse de Tunisie.)
ConstitutionalCommercial

ILO Convention C193 — Decent Work in the Digital Platform Economy (Run 99 addition)

Adopted: 114th International Labour Conference, Geneva, June 28, 2026 (406–8–36) | Tunisia's vote: in favor | Status: not yet ratified domestically
Not yet Tunisian domestic law — a newly-adopted ILO convention Tunisia voted to support, distinct from ratification. The 114th International Labour Conference adopted Convention No. 193 extending core labor protections (occupational safety and health, fair compensation, protection from violence and harassment, data-privacy safeguards, algorithmic-transparency/review rights, and social-security access) to digital-platform workers, described by the ILO as a "decisive step" ensuring technological innovation respects worker rights. Tunisia's Minister of Employment and Vocational Training, Issam Lahmar, announced at the same conference that Tunisia is developing a governance-framework study with the ILO covering an estimated 150,000–250,000 platform workers across roughly 60 platforms in 15 sectors — a considerably broader figure than the domestic UGTT study's narrower 60,000-worker estimate for delivery/care/moto-transport platforms specifically (see the companion Labor Law section entry). No domestic ratification instrument, implementing bill, or ARP text has been filed as of this run; watch for a ratification bill and the ILO governance-framework study's completion. (leconomistemaghrebin.com, letemps.news, both June 28–29, 2026.)
LaborConstitutionalPending

🚢 Transport Law

Maritime Commercial Code — Law No. 62-13 of April 24, 1962(as amended) (Run 44 correction)

Enacted: April 24, 1962 | In force: July 1, 1962 | Amended: 1972, 1990, 1998, 2004-3
Run 44 correction: prior runs cited "Law No. 62-36 of March 27, 1962" — no such law could be verified, and it appears to have been a fabricated citation. NATLEX (ILO's legislative database) directly confirms the actual founding instrument as Law No. 62-13 of April 24, 1962, "portant promulgation du Code de commerce maritime," entering into force July 1, 1962; corroborated by droit-afrique.com/FAOLEX and the Ministry of Transport's own consolidated text. Comprehensive statute governing merchant shipping, maritime contracts, and maritime liability: registration/flagging of vessels, maritime liens and mortgages (ranked above ordinary privileges), carriage of goods incorporating the 1924 Brussels Convention (Hague Rules) — carrier liability capped at 400 dinars per package since a 1990 decree (up from 100). Law No. 2004-3 (January 20, 2004) replaced the ship-ownership/registration articles. Maritime ports managed by OMMP (Office de la Marine Marchande et des Ports).
TransportCommercial

Merchant Marine Organization Law — Law No. 95-33 of April 14, 1995

Enacted: April 14, 1995 | In force: 1995
Governs professions in the Tunisian merchant marine: maritime officers, engineers, and seafarers. Establishes training, certification, and competency requirements for seafarers (implementing the STCW Convention — Standards of Training, Certification and Watchkeeping). Sets working conditions, wages, and social protection for crew members. Regulates the Tunisian ship register and conditions for flagging vessels. Enforced by the OMMP and the Merchant Navy General Secretariat (created by Decree 2019-144).
TransportLabor

Maritime Ports Code — Law No. 2009-48 of July 8, 2009

Enacted: July 8, 2009 | In force: 2009 | Replaced: Law No. 99-25 (commercial ports) and Law No. 2002-47 (fishing ports)
Unifies the legal regime for Tunisia's commercial and fishing ports into a single code, repealing the previously separate 1999 commercial-ports law and 2002 fishing-ports law. Governs port administration, concessions, safety, and access; administered by OMMP (Office de la Marine Marchande et des Ports), the same authority referenced under the Maritime Commercial Code above, of which this ports-specific code had not previously had its own standalone entry. (Run 158 addition): the Skhira commercial port (Sfax governorate) illustrates a long-running gap in this code's practical application to shared oil-terminal infrastructure: the concession originally granted to the Société de Transport des Hydrocarbures par Pipelines au Sahara (TRAPSA) expired roughly 18 years ago, leaving OMMP (the code's designated port authority) and TRAPSA (the terminal's historical operator) without a current convention governing their respective responsibilities, maritime/land boundary delimitation, or public-domain status. In response to a deputy's (Saber Masmoudi) written parliamentary question, the Ministry of Transport announced on July 6, 2026 urgent measures to finalize the OMMP–TRAPSA operating convention, delimit the port's boundaries, and draft the implementing text on public-domain delimitation, alongside integrating Skhira into the 2040 national ports master plan (due for approval in 2027) — the latest in a string of unresolved regularization attempts (a 2020 memorandum of understanding, a 2021 convention announcement, 2023 development studies, and a 2025 TRAPSA regularization request) (Businessnews, Tunisie Tribune, Le Temps News, African Manager, Managers.tn, La Presse de Tunisie, all July 6, 2026).
TransportAdministrative

Civil Aviation Code — Law No. 99-58 of June 29, 1999

Enacted: June 29, 1999 | In force: 1999 | Implementing law: 98-110 (OACA)
Governs civil aviation in Tunisia: aircraft registration, airworthiness certification, pilot licensing, air navigation rules, airport operations, and air carrier liability. The Office of Civil Aviation and Airports (OACA — Office de l'Aviation Civile et des Aéroports), created by Law No. 98-110 of December 28, 1998, manages 7 international airports (Tunis-Carthage, Djerba-Zarzis, Sfax-Thyna, Monastir-Habib Bourguiba, Tozeur-Nefta, Tabarka, Gafsa-Ksar). Tunisia is an ICAO member. National carrier: Tunisair (state-owned). Domestic aviation partially opened to private carriers. Pending drone (UAV) regulation (added Run 130): civilian drone use remains prohibited outside case-by-case military-authorized exceptions; Transport Minister Khaled Chili confirmed (reported November 12, 2025) that the Directorate General of Civil Aviation, coordinating with the Ministry of Defense, has completed a draft regulatory text — via a dedicated inter-ministerial commission — governing civil and security aspects of drone use, now circulated to stakeholders for comment; no decree number or publication date has been confirmed. A narrower carve-out was separately enacted first: Finance Law 2026 Art. 135 (see that entry) authorizes farmers specifically to import and use agricultural drones, distinct from this still-pending general framework.
Key provisions
  • Aircraft registration: Tunisian nationality mark TS-
  • OACA: national aviation authority; safety, security, airspace management
  • Air carrier liability: aligned with Montreal Convention (ratified)
  • Airport concessions: governed by OACA and Ministry of Transport
  • Private carrier licensing: open skies principles for international routes (EU Horizontal Agreement 2021)
TransportCommercial

Draft Law on Air Transport Sector Modernization (pending)

Announced to ARP commission: July 13, 2026 | Status: inter-ministerial coordination, not yet submitted to Council of Ministers as of July 18, 2026
A new Transport Ministry-drafted bill to modernize Tunisia's civil aviation legal framework (updating the Civil Aviation Code, Law No. 99-58, above), presented to the ARP's Strategic Planning, Sustainable Development, Transport, Infrastructure and Territorial Planning Commission (chaired by Saber Jelassi) on July 13, 2026. Stated aims: align Tunisia with international civil aviation standards, liberalize traffic rights, and attract foreign direct investment in the sector. As of this run the text remains in the "arbitration and coordination" phase between concerned ministries, ahead of an announced but not-yet-occurred transmission to the Council of Ministers — no bill number, article count, or ARP filing exists yet. The same July 13, 2026 commission session separately examined two distinct organic bills ratifying bilateral air-service agreements with Oman and Kuwait (aimed at direct flight routes), and deputies used the session to criticize service quality at Tunis-Carthage airport as "far below the legitimate expectations of users," calling for an emergency recovery plan — raised as context for, not yet formal provisions of, the modernization bill. Watch for Council of Ministers submission and a bill number before treating this as more than an announced intention.
TransportPending

Road Traffic Code — Law No. 99-71 of July 26, 1999 (as amended) (Run 50 correction)

Enacted: July 26, 1999 | Adopted by Chamber of Deputies: July 6, 1999 | Published: JORT No. 61, July 30, 1999 | In force: 6 months after publication | Amended: 2001-101, 2003, 2014, 2019
Run 50 correction: prior runs cited an enactment date of "August 23, 1999" — this could not be verified, and two independent sources (jurisitetunisie.com's promulgation-law page and a matching targeted web search) instead confirm July 26, 1999 as the date of Law No. 99-71, adopted by the Chamber of Deputies on July 6, 1999 and published in JORT No. 61 of July 30, 1999; treat July 26 as the correct date absent a primary JORT text showing otherwise. Governs road traffic, vehicle registration, driver licensing, traffic offenses, and penalties in Tunisia. Sets speed limits (50 km/h urban, 90 km/h interurban, 110 km/h highways), blood alcohol limits (0.3‰ — stricter than EU), seat belt and helmet requirements, and mobile phone restrictions. Amended by Law No. 2001-101 of October 22, 2001, among later amendments; the 2014 amendment introduced a points-based driving license system and increased penalties for serious traffic offenses, and the 2019 amendment strengthened sanctions for drunk driving and speeding. Road safety authority under the Ministry of Transport. Pending reform: a "violence routière" (road violence) reform package — automatic driving-license withdrawal for speeding 10 km/h or more over the limit, expanded radar/camera enforcement, and a zero-tolerance regime — was approved as a draft bill by the Council of Ministers on July 28, 2025 (not, as sometimes reported, an already-enacted law of that date) and forwarded to the ARP; as of the most recent confirmed reporting (December 2025) it remained under ARP examination, with no JORT promulgation number identified as of June 2026. Update (Run 136): a July 6, 2026 Business News fact-check explicitly debunked a circulating rumor that a general reform of this Code had entered into force on July 1, 2026, confirming the operative text remains unchanged Law No. 99-71 as amended above — corroborating, rather than altering, this entry's existing "pending, not yet enacted" framing of the violence-routière package. Update (Run 164): driving-instructor licensing and driving-school (auto-école) approval operate at decree level under this Code rather than via a separate statute — Décret gouvernemental n° 2021-510 of June 18, 2021 (JORT No. 2021-059) sets driving-license categories and the required theoretical/practical training curriculum delivered through ATTT-approved establishments, and a September 1, 2025 procedural tightening made a signed training contract between candidate and driving school mandatory before sitting the theoretical exam; confirms this is a decree/administrative-approval matter, not a standalone legislative gap.
Transport

Land Transport Law — Law No. 2004-33 of April 19, 2004

Enacted: April 19, 2004 | In force: 2004
Regulates land transport of passengers and goods in Tunisia. Governs the licensing of transport operators (buses, taxis, trucks, coaches). Establishes the Land Transport Observatory. Sets vehicle technical inspection requirements and roadworthiness standards. Regulates freight transport contracts (lettre de voiture). Governs special transport (hazardous goods, heavy loads, exceptional convoys). National carrier TRANSTU operates urban buses and metro in Tunis; regional bus networks operated by SRTG companies. (Added Run 196, resolved Run 197): taxi, louage (shared intercity taxi), and rural-transport operators called a general strike for July 13, 2026, over the continued non-implementation of provisions tied to this Law and Decree No. 2023-581 of September 7, 2023 (JORT No. 2023-104) — now confirmed via 9anoun.tn (JORT mirror) to concern the "organisation du transport public routier non régulier des personnes" (organization of non-regular/irregular public road transport of persons, i.e. taxis, louages, and rural transport), repealing and replacing the prior Decree No. 2202-2007 (as amended by Decree No. 828-2016) on the same subject; African Manager's July 3, 2026 strike coverage independently corroborates the subject matter, citing operators' demand for "finalisation des textes relatifs à la loi n°33 de 2004 et au décret n°581 de 2023."
TransportCommercial

Railway Law — Law No. 69-31 of May 9, 1969, Law No. 98-74 of August 19, 1998 & Law No. 98-90 of November 2, 1998

Enacted: May 9, 1969; August 19, 1998; November 2, 1998 | In force: 1969 (statute), 1998 (network & operator framework)
Governs Tunisia's national railway network and its state operator, SNCFT (Société Nationale des Chemins de Fer Tunisiens — itself dating to a 1956 nationalization of the colonial-era railway administration), across three successive laws distinct from the general Land Transport Law (No. 2004-33) above, which covers road-based transport (buses, taxis, trucks) rather than rail. Law No. 69-31 approved SNCFT's original corporate/public-establishment statute. Law No. 98-74 ("relative aux chemins de fer") is the general railway-system law, covering the network and its operation, including safety regulation — Article 42 mandates periodic medical, safety-knowledge, and practical-competency exams for train drivers and other safety-critical railway agents. Law No. 98-90 updated SNCFT's own statute to align with the 1998 general railway framework. Supplemented by implementing decrees on SNCFT personnel regulations (Decree No. 2000-1230), public-service-obligation state compensation (Decree No. 2001-2621), organizational structure (Decree No. 2002-422), tax advantages (Decree No. 98-2243), and administrative/financial organization (Decree No. 2004-1056). (Added Run 163) A wholly new standalone entry closing a gap that survived at least two prior rail-transport gap-scans (Runs 136 and 140), both of which had assumed rail was covered by the Land Transport Law without verifying that law's text actually addresses railways — it does not. Verified via SNCFT's own official documents page (sncft.com.tn) and cross-corroborated by Tunisia's official legislation.tn portal, which cites Law No. 98-74 Art. 42 in a 2004 implementing arrêté. JORT issue numbers for the three laws could not be independently confirmed this run and remain unverified pending a future primary-source check.
TransportCommercial

⚡ Energy & Natural Resources

Hydrocarbons Code — Law No. 99-93 of August 17, 1999 (amended 2002, 2004, 2008, 2017)

Enacted: August 17, 1999 | In force: 1999 | Last amended: Law 2017-41 of May 30, 2017
Primary legal framework for oil and gas exploration and production in Tunisia. Governs concession agreements, production sharing contracts, and service contracts. State oil company ETAP (Entreprise Tunisienne d'Activités Pétrolières) holds mandatory minimum participation.
