The 2017 amendment required parliamentary approval of new hydrocarbon contracts and introduced transparency obligations aligned with EITI standards. Tunisia has been an EITI member since 2016. Pending reform (added Run 70): a restricted ministerial council chaired by then-Head of Government Kamel Maddouri examined a draft law amending the Code on February 7, 2025, recommending the text be finalized (incorporating remarks raised) before submission to the full Council of Ministers, in order to open new investment areas including renewable energy alongside traditional hydrocarbon exploration. A June 26, 2026 report (Webmanagercenter) renewed calls for urgent revision amid declining domestic hydrocarbon production, and a July 3, 2026 IACE report — corroborated across African Manager, La Presse de Tunisie, and Tustex — classifies this reform among five priority codes (with Investment, Water, and Environment) "technically finalized and validated by the Council of Ministers" but "never transmitted to the ARP," meaning no bill had reached parliamentary committee as of this update. The 1999 Code, as amended through 2017, remains in force unchanged.
Key provisions
  • Prospecting permit: maximum 1 year
  • Exploration permit: maximum 5 years (with 2 extensions of 2 years each)
  • Production concession: maximum 30 years (renewable)
  • ETAP mandatory participation: minimum share in all concessions
  • CIT rate: reduced to 50% if ETAP holds ≥40% stake (vs. standard 75% otherwise)
  • Art. 59: Mandatory compliance with environmental, health and safety standards
  • 2017 amendment: parliamentary approval required for contracts; EITI alignment
EnergyCommercial

Mining Code — Law No. 2003-30 of April 28, 2003 (as amended)

Enacted: April 28, 2003 | In force: 2003 | Amended: 2010
Governs exploration and exploitation of non-hydrocarbon mineral resources (phosphates, iron ore, lead, zinc, fluorite, salt, sand, limestone). Key provisions: 3-year exploration licenses; 30-year mining concessions; 1% royalty on production value payable semi-annually; 25% CIT on mining profits with a 5-year tax holiday from production start. Enforced by the National Office of Mines (ONM — Office National des Mines). Tunisia is the world's 5th largest phosphate producer; Compagnie des Phosphates de Gafsa (CPG) is the principal public mining entity. Pending reform: the Ministry of Industry called in July 2025 for accelerated finalization of a mining code revision framed around reinforcing state sovereignty over natural resources; as of mid-2026 no revised code had been adopted.
Key provisions
  • Exploration license: 3 years, renewable
  • Mining concession: 30 years, renewable
  • Royalty: 1% of production value (semi-annual payments)
  • Tax holiday: 5 years from production commencement
  • CIT rate: 25% on mining profits
  • Environmental rehabilitation bond: mandatory for concession holders
  • Artisanal mining rights: recognized in designated areas
EnergyCommercial

Electricity and Gas Law — Law No. 62-8 of April 3, 1962 (and Law No. 2002-28 of March 25, 2002)

STEG created: April 3, 1962 | Market opening: Law 2002-28
Law 62-8 created the Société Tunisienne de l'Electricité et du Gaz (STEG) as the national monopoly utility for electricity generation, transmission, distribution, and natural gas distribution. STEG remains a state monopoly for the transmission grid and distribution network. Law No. 2002-28 (March 25, 2002) partially opened electricity generation to private Independent Power Producers (IPPs) under power purchase agreements (PPAs) with STEG, while maintaining STEG's grid monopoly. Supervised by the Ministry of Industry and Energy.
EnergyCommercial

Renewable Energy Law — Law No. 2015-12 of May 11, 2015 (amended by Law 2019-47)

Enacted: May 11, 2015 | Amended: 2019 (business climate law) | New consolidating Code transmitted to committee: April 1, 2026
Establishes three authorization regimes for electricity production from renewable sources: (1) concession regime for large projects (>30 MW); (2) authorization regime for medium projects (1–30 MW) selling to STEG under feed-in tariff PPA; (3) self-consumption regime for own-use generation. National target: 35% of electricity from renewables by 2030. The 2019 Investment Climate Law expanded self-consumption to allow inter-site wheeling by companies. ANME (National Agency for Energy Conservation) coordinates renewable energy development. Recent application: the Conseil National des Régions et des Districts (CNRD) approved, on May 13, 2026, five concession-regime agreements (TND ~1.6 billion combined) for utility-scale photovoltaic plants in Sidi Bouzid, Gafsa, and Gabès governorates — the El Khabna plant (198 MW) plus four 100 MW plants — with all output to be injected exclusively into the STEG grid under the law's concession-regime PPA framework. Pending consolidation — new "Code des Énergies Renouvelables" (added Run 94): a Council of Ministers meeting held December 24, 2024 ordered completion ("parachèvement") of a unified Renewable Energy Code intended to gather this law's dispersed provisions into a single coherent framework; deputy Yosri Baoueb confirmed on Express FM (April 1, 2026) that the draft text had just been transmitted to the ARP's competent parliamentary commission, alongside a parallel Hydrocarbons Code project, both framed as part of an "energy sovereignty" push amid rising hydrocarbon-import costs and worsening energy deficits. Its most contested provision reserves 51% of renewable-energy project ownership for Tunisian actors, justified by Baoueb as a defense against foreign consortiums outpacing domestic operators (citing the 600 MW Tunisia–Italy ElMed interconnection as a capacity-absorption risk). A July 3, 2026 IACE report separately names this Renewable Energy Code, alongside the Foreign Exchange Code and Investment Code, as one of three reforms it urges be enacted by end of 2026 — distinct from (and not one of) the four other IACE-flagged codes (Investment, Water, Environment, Hydrocarbons) already noted as "validated by the Council of Ministers but never transmitted to the ARP" elsewhere in this document; no committee vote or report has been confirmed since the April 1, 2026 transmission. Green hydrogen (added Run 130): Tunisia has no dedicated green-hydrogen statute; the Ministry of Industry, Mines and Energy officially launched the National Strategy for the Development of Green Hydrogen and its Derivatives ("H2Vert.TUN," developed with German GIZ support) in May 2024, targeting a hydrogen/derivatives export and domestic-decarbonization framework through 2050 with first production projects expected from 2030 — a policy-level roadmap, not yet a law or decree, and not integrated into this entry's or the pending Renewable Energy Code's statutory text as of this run. Round 6 solar authorizations (added Run 141): the Ministry of Industry, Mines and Energy opened a sixth call for projects under this Law's authorization regime for solar photovoltaic electricity production, published April 2026: 200 MW total capacity available across three project-size tiers (1 MW, 2 MW, and 10 MW — the maximum allowed under the authorization regime), with applications (two paper copies plus two digital copies) accepted at the ministry's central registry from April 15 to June 15, 2026 at noon. Selected projects benefit from guaranteed STEG purchase rates set by an October 2024 ministerial order. This follows Round 5, which resulted in 186 preliminary authorizations totaling approximately 286–288 MW — a concrete, dated implementation of the authorization regime distinct from the concession-regime approvals and pending Code consolidation already noted above. ARP origin and controversy of the May 13 CNRD concessions (added Run 169): the five concession-regime agreements the CNRD concurred with on May 13, 2026 were first adopted as five separate ratification bills — Laws n°01/2026 through n°05/2026, covering the El Khobna/El Khabna, Mezzouna (Sidi Bouzid), El Ksar (Gafsa), Segdoud (Gafsa), and Menzel Habib (Gabès) photovoltaic plants (combined ~598 MW, ~TND 1.64 billion investment, purchase tariffs of 98.8–124.4 millimes/kWh, a projected 13.3% cut to Tunisia's gas-import bill) — by the ARP on April 28, 2026, each by a separately recorded plenary vote (ranging from 70 for/30 against/8 abstaining to 75/33/9). Each agreement grants the foreign project operator a 20-year concession (renewable once for 10 years) with a five-year corporate-tax exemption, with all output sold exclusively to STEG. The same-day vote drew UGTT opposition and a deputy (Bilel El Mechri) publicly calling the arrangement "energy colonisation"; President Kais Saied dismissed Industry, Mines & Energy Minister Fatma Thabet Chiboub that same day (April 28, 2026), installing Equipment & Housing Minister Salah Zouari as interim minister with no permanent successor confirmed as of this run (africanews.com, entreprises-magazine.com, Managers.tn, webdo.tn).
EnergyEnvironmental

Proposition de Loi sur la Gestion des Richesses Naturelles (unnumbered, 17 articles, 10 deputies)Natural Resources Governance, Sovereignty & Transparency Bill (Run 294 addition)

Filed: end of January 2026 | Status: referred to and heard by the ARP Industry, Commerce, Natural Resources, Energy & Environment Commission (session reported April 15, 2026) | Not yet numbered | Not yet enacted
A deputy-initiated 17-article bill, carried by a group of ten ARP deputies, aimed at strengthening state sovereignty, governance, and transparency over Tunisia's natural resources (hydrocarbons, phosphates/minerals, water, forests, and maritime resources), framed by its sponsors as shared national heritage and a strategic development lever. Independently corroborated across three dated outlets (Le Temps News, February 5, 2026; allAfrica/La Presse and WebManagerCenter/Directinfo, both April 15, 2026, reporting the same committee-hearing session). Key provisions: requires large public enterprises to publish detailed annual reports on activities, finances, and contract management; sets the state's share of natural-resource extraction revenue at 60%, of which 25% is earmarked for regional development; creates a new independent High Authority for Natural Resources to strengthen oversight; establishes a sovereign wealth fund for natural resources intended to preserve intergenerational rights, with its revenues directed toward education, health, public infrastructure, and long-term strategic projects; requires parliamentary approval of natural-resource exploitation contracts and their publication on a dedicated national electronic transparency platform (contracts, revenues, production and distribution data); and criminalizes falsification of that published data. Distinct from — and, per available reporting, not yet reconciled with — the separately pending, IACE-flagged Hydrocarbons Code and Mining Code sovereignty-oriented reforms already documented above, both of which remain "validated by the Council of Ministers but never transmitted to the ARP" as opposed to this bill's parliamentary-committee track. No committee vote, report, or plenary referral confirmed as of this run's July 14, 2026 cutoff.
EnergyAdministrativePending

📊 Public Finance & Budget

Organic Budget Law — Law No. 2019-15 of February 13, 2019

Enacted: February 13, 2019 | In force: 2019 | Replaced: Organic Law No. 67-53 of December 8, 1967 (as amended, including by Law No. 2004-42 of May 13, 2004)
Correction (Run 65): this entry previously cited Law No. 2004-42 of 2004 as the operative organic budget law; that law was in fact only an amendment to the original 1967 organic law (No. 67-53), and both have since been fully superseded by Law No. 2019-15 of February 13, 2019, confirmed against the Finance Ministry's official text. Law No. 2019-15 defines the current constitutional and legal rules for preparation, adoption, execution, and control of Tunisia's state budget, formally introducing performance/program-based budgeting (budgétisation par objectifs) alongside the traditional Title I/Title II expenditure classification, plus multi-year budget programming and expanded rules for execution control, results evaluation, and the budget settlement law (loi de règlement). Organizes the annual Finance Law (loi de finances) process: Cabinet submits draft to parliament by October 15; parliament must adopt by December 31.
Key provisions
  • Finance Law submitted to parliament by October 15 each year
  • Parliamentary vote required by December 31; if not, prior-year budget extended by decree
  • Title I: recurrent expenditures (staff, goods, services, debt service)
  • Title II: development expenditures (public investment)
  • Special accounts (comptes spéciaux du Trésor) regulated separately
  • Court of Accounts certifies final state accounts (loi de règlement)
AdministrativeFinancial

Court of Accounts — Law No. 68-8 of March 8, 1968 (as amended, most recently Law 2019-56)

Enacted: March 8, 1968 | In force: 1968 | Major reform: Law 2019-56
Tunisia's supreme audit institution (Cour des Comptes / ديوان المحاسبات). Audits central government, public enterprises, and local authority accounts. Issues binding rulings (arrêts) imposing financial liability (débet) on public accountants for unauthorized expenditures. Issues annual public report on state budget execution presented to parliament. Audit opinions required before adoption of final budget accounts (loi de règlement). The 2019 reform modernized audit methods and strengthened transparency of findings. Also plays a role in asset declaration review. Update (Run 103): the Court's 32nd annual report, presented to President Kaïs Saïed on January 9, 2026 by First Vice-President Fadhila Gargouri, found roughly TND 1.07 billion in cumulative losses across eleven audited public entities — national carrier Tunisair alone (over TND 316 million) and the Office of Maritime Commerce and Ports (nearly TND 291 million) among the largest — attributing them to salaries, bonuses, and allowances paid without legal basis and weakened internal-control mechanisms; Saied publicly demanded prosecutions and recovery of the funds.
AdministrativeFinancial

Public Debt Management — Law No. 97-42 of July 28, 1997 and annual Finance Laws

Framework law: July 28, 1997 | Debt limits: set annually in Finance Law
Governs the state's borrowing authority and public debt management. The General Treasury of Tunisia manages the debt portfolio. External borrowing requires parliamentary authorization in the annual Finance Law. Domestic instruments: Treasury bills (BTC — 13/26/52 weeks), medium-term Treasury bonds (OAT). Tunisia has issued Eurobonds and Sukuk (Islamic bonds) on international capital markets (2012, 2015, 2020, 2021). IMF extended credit facility (2016–2020, revised 2023). Public debt reached approximately 80% of GDP by 2024.
FinancialAdministrative

Presidential Decree No. 2026-108Afreximbank US$500 Million Financing Facility

BCT–Afreximbank facility signed: June 16, 2026 | Presidential decree published: JORT, June 8, 2026 | BCT board approval: March 18, 2026
Under the Public Debt Management framework (Law No. 97-42) and the external-financing authorization in the Finance Law 2026, the Central Bank of Tunisia (BCT), acting on the state's behalf, concluded a US$500 million (≈TND 1.471 billion) financing facility with the African Export-Import Bank (Afreximbank) to help cover the FY2026 budget's external financing needs, including strategic imports of energy and food products. Signed by President Kais Saied as Presidential Decree No. 2026-108, ratifying the BCT board's March 2026 decision. Part of a broader financing relationship under which Afreximbank has extended roughly TND 5.15 billion to Tunisia over four years.
FinancialAdministrative

Law n° 2026-38 & Law n° 2026-39 of July 14, 2026 (Run 340 title correction)State Guarantees for STEG IBRD Loans (~US$468 Million)

Underlying loan agreements signed: November 3, 2025 | Finance & Budget Committee approval: June 28, 2026 | Plenary debate opened: July 10, 2026, then postponed to Tuesday, July 14, 2026 | Adopted by ARP plenary: July 14, 2026
Two companion government bills authorizing state guarantees for International Bank for Reconstruction and Development (IBRD/World Bank) loans to the Société Tunisienne de l'Électricité et du Gaz (STEG). Draft Law n° 38/2026 guarantees a US$400 million loan extended directly to STEG; Draft Law n° 39/2026 guarantees a complementary US$30 million loan channeled through the IBRD in its capacity as implementing entity of the Clean Technology Fund (CTF). Both finance a STEG energy-sector modernization program aimed at improving the utility's operational efficiency, financial performance, and governance, against a backdrop of STEG's reported TND 7.356 billion total indebtedness and over TND 6 billion in unpaid receivables (per a June 27, 2026 La Presse report). The ARP Finance and Budget Committee approved both texts on June 28, 2026; they were transmitted to the ARP plenary for final adoption but had not been put to a plenary vote as of this update. Update (Run 219): the ARP Bureau calendared these bills for the same July 9–10, 2026 plenary window as the Development Plan bill (see that entry), but no outlet reporting on that session's Development Plan vote (La Presse, Tunisie Tribune, African Manager, webdo.tn) mentioned a STEG vote outcome — still awaiting plenary action as of this update. Update (Run 222): the plenary debate on both bills opened July 10, 2026, with the Minister of Equipment addressing deputies (La Presse, "Énergie: l'ARP se penche sur deux prêts de la BIRD en faveur de la STEG," 2026-07-10), but the debate was reported only as "underway" ("entamé") — no vote tally or adoption/rejection outcome has been reported as of this update; still the single highest-priority item to resolve for the next run. Distinct from the unrelated "combating financial exclusion" bill (Draft Law n° 023/2024 — see entry below), which some secondary sources conflated with these same bill numbers; the 38/2026 and 39/2026 numbering is independently confirmed for the STEG guarantees via the ARP's official bill registry and Arabic-language press (لجنة المالية، مشروع قانون عدد 38 و39 لسنة 2026). Update (Run 238): the July 10, 2026 plenary session (held Friday at Bardo, with Minister of Equipment and Housing Salah Zouari — also acting head of the Industry, Mines and Energy portfolio — addressing deputies) did not reach a vote before adjourning; African Manager's follow-up headline ("Le Parlement reporte à mardi l'examen de deux prêts destinés à la transition énergétique") and a same-story cross-reference on La Presse's own site independently confirm the ARP will resume examination of both bills on the following Tuesday, rather than the vote outcome remaining merely unreported as prior runs had characterized it — the debate was affirmatively adjourned and rescheduled, a more precise status than "underway." Correction (Run 248): Run 238 resolved "mardi"/"Tuesday" to the calendar date July 15, 2026, but July 15, 2026 is in fact a Wednesday (verified via direct date computation) — the Tuesday immediately following the Friday, July 10, 2026 adjournment is July 14, 2026. La Presse's own republication of this story is dated July 11, 2026 (a Saturday) and still describes the resumption only as "mardi prochain" (next Tuesday) without independently stating a calendar date, so no primary source has actually named "July 15" — that figure appears to have been an arithmetic slip introduced in Run 238 rather than a sourced claim. Corrected the date field above accordingly; still no vote outcome reported as of this update. Update (Run 261): a July 12, 2026 La Presse follow-up ("STEG: un député dénonce des prêts qui favoriseraient les entreprises étrangères") confirms plenary debate continued past the July 10 adjournment rather than pausing entirely until July 14 as the prior "adjourned and rescheduled" characterization implied; deputy Issam Jabri publicly objected that the loan terms favor foreign contractors over domestic firms. Still no vote tally or adoption/rejection outcome as of this update. Update (Run 283): a July 13, 2026 businessnews.com.tn report ("Report du vote sur les prêts à la STEG: des députés dénoncent un 'scandale' et accusent Bouderbala de vouloir influer sur le scrutin") adds a genuinely new procedural-controversy detail beyond the plain rescheduling already documented: deputy Bilel El Mechri publicly accused ARP Speaker Ibrahim Bouderbala of deliberately postponing the vote to July 14 after observing that opposing votes outnumbered favorable ones during the July 10 session, calling the move a "scandal," and announcing he will boycott the July 14 sitting, which he characterized as a "masquerade." A same-day directinfo.webmanagercenter.com report independently corroborates only the procedural fact that the ARP resumes examination Tuesday, July 14, without addressing the manipulation accusation. Still no vote tally or adoption/rejection outcome as of this update; the Mechri allegation itself remains a claim by one deputy, not an established fact, and is presented here as such. Update (Run 295): the July 14, 2026 resumption was independently reconfirmed as having occurred as scheduled at 10:00 AM, but no outlet checked (Business News, La Presse, African Manager, allAfrica, Tunisie Numérique, Le Temps News, L'Economiste Maghrébin, Tustex) has yet reported a vote tally or adoption/rejection outcome — still the single highest-priority item to resolve for the next run. Separately, a June 28, 2026 Le Temps News report on the same Finance & Budget Committee approval cites the Draft Law n° 38/2026 guarantee as covering a €384.8 million loan rather than the US$400 million figure sourced elsewhere (La Presse, Tunisie Numérique) — likely the same underlying loan expressed in a different currency at a different exchange-rate snapshot (€384.8M ≈ US$415–420M at prevailing mid-2026 rates, close to but not exactly matching US$400M), but no primary bill text or JORT publication has been found to reconcile the two figures precisely; both are noted here rather than silently picking one, consistent with this artifact's practice for unreconciled figures elsewhere (e.g. the Personal Data Protection bill's article-count discrepancy). Update (Run 301) — RESOLVED: the ARP plenary adopted both bills on July 14, 2026, per a direct WebFetch of webdo.tn's same-day report ("STEG: à quoi serviront réellement les 468 millions de dollars approuvés par l'ARP?"), ending the vote-tally watch that had been this entry's standing top priority since Run 222. The article reconciles the earlier currency discrepancy: the combined guarantee package totals US$468 million, composed of €384.8 million (≈US$438.4 million) from the IBRD/World Bank loan (Draft Law n° 38/2026) plus the US$30 million Clean Technology Fund loan (Draft Law n° 39/2026) — confirming the €384.8M figure first flagged at Run 295 as the accurate IBRD-loan amount rather than a discrepant alternate figure, and superseding the earlier US$400M estimate. No specific for/against/abstention tally was reported by webdo.tn or any other outlet checked (Business News, La Presse, African Manager, DirectInfo/WebManagerCenter) — only the adoption itself and the aggregate loan amounts — so the precise vote breakdown (unlike the Development Plan's reported 64-24-15) remains unconfirmed, and deputy Bilel El Mechri's July 12–13 allegation that Speaker Bouderbala manipulated the scheduling to secure passage (see Run 283 above) has not been addressed by any post-vote reporting found. Update (Run 308) — vote tallies confirmed: a WebManagerCenter report ("Tunisie: l'ARP approuve deux garanties de prêts en faveur de la STEG pour réformer le secteur énergétique," 2026-07-14) supplies the exact breakdown that eluded every source checked through Run 301: Draft Law n° 38/2026 (the €384.8 million/US$400 million IBRD guarantee) passed 72 for, 19 against, 6 abstentions; Draft Law n° 39/2026 (the US$30 million Clean Technology Fund guarantee) passed separately by a materially narrower margin, 67 for, 25 against, 5 abstentions — the first source found to report the two bills' tallies as distinct rather than a single combined vote. This closes the "vote breakdown unconfirmed" gap flagged at Run 301; deputy El Mechri's manipulation allegation remains unaddressed by any post-vote reporting. Update (Run 340): the card title and tags still read "Draft Laws … (pending)" despite the body having documented full adoption with vote tallies since Run 301/308 — a stale-label gap of the same kind flagged and fixed for other entries in past runs (e.g. the v4.9 .tag.judicial CSS fix). Corrected the title to cite the two laws by their enacted numbers (Law n° 2026-38 and Law n° 2026-39 of July 14, 2026) and swapped the "Pending" tag for the "2026" new-enactment tag used on the companion Development Plan entry; no substantive content changed.
FinancialEnergy2026

Draft Laws (pending)State Guarantees for CPG (EBRD) & GCT (ITFC) Financing Agreements

Underlying EBRD guarantee agreement signed: November 3, 2025 | Underlying GCT/ITFC Mourabaha agreement signed: December 2, 2025 | ARP Bureau referral to Finance & Budget Committee: July 15, 2026 | GCT bill (n° 51/2026) under committee examination by emergency procedure: July 16, 2026 | Committee hearing on all three named bills: July 16–17, 2026 | Exceptional committee session held: Tuesday, July 21, 2026, 9:30 AM | Status: committee session concluded with a policy recommendation, not yet a bill vote
Following the same state-guarantee mechanism used for the STEG/IBRD loan guarantees above, the ARP Bureau referred a batch of five government bills to the Finance and Budget Committee on July 15, 2026, per African Manager's same-day report. Three of the five were named: (1) a bill approving a guarantee agreement dated November 3, 2025 between Tunisia and the European Bank for Reconstruction and Development (EBRD) for a loan extended to the Compagnie des Phosphates de Gafsa (CPG) — Tunisia's principal public phosphate-mining entity, see the Mining Code entry — financing modernized mining equipment and a high-pressure wastewater filtration unit; and (2)–(3) two further bills approving Mourabaha (Islamic trade-finance) agreements with the International Islamic Trade Finance Corporation (ITFC) to finance fertilizer imports for the Groupe Chimique Tunisien (GCT), the state chemical/fertilizer group. The remaining two bills in the same referral batch were not named in available coverage. None of the five had reached a committee vote or plenary session as of this run. Not yet enacted. Update (Run 357): one of the two GCT/ITFC bills is now identified by number — bill n° 51/2026 — and, per a July 16, 2026 La Presse de Tunisie report, the Finance and Budget Committee began examining it under emergency procedure (procédure d'urgence) that day. The underlying Mourabaha agreement, signed December 2, 2025 between Tunisia and the ITFC, structures the $50 million guarantee as a revolving credit facility with multiple drawdowns, each repayable after 12 months, priced at SOFR + 3.75% plus a 0.4% arrangement fee, to fund GCT's import of raw materials for fertilizer production destined for both export and the domestic market. Still committee-stage; no vote or JORT publication reported as of this update. Update (Run 377): a July 17, 2026 La Presse de Tunisie report ("Phosphates: l'ARP examine trois financements et réclame une stratégie de réforme globale," independently corroborated by allAfrica's same-day republication) confirms the committee's July 16–17 session covered all three named bills together rather than only the n° 51/2026 GCT text Run 357 had isolated, and adds a substantive procedural detail beyond simple emergency-procedure examination: deputies withheld swift approval, instead demanding clarifications on how proceeds of CPG and GCT's previous loans (specifically financing meant for phosphate transport) had actually been used, and secured commitments for further hearings with the CPG and GCT chief executives plus representatives of the Ministries of Economy and Planning and of Industry, Mines and Energy before the committee proceeds further. The committee framed its role as extending beyond credit approval to rigorous monitoring of execution and real impact on public-enterprise governance, and called for a comprehensive phosphate-sector reform program alongside the financing — consistent with, and adding parliamentary-oversight detail to, the sector-wide reform pressure already documented at the Hydrocarbons Code and Mining Code entries' pending-reform notes. Still committee-stage; no vote, committee report, or JORT publication reported as of this update. Update (Run 423) — remaining two bills identified: per African Manager's July 15, 2026 report ("Le Bureau de l'ARP renvoie cinq projets de loi à la Commission des finances"), the five-bill referral batch is now fully named: bill (4) approves "avenant modificatif n°2" to the loan agreement between the Banque Centrale de Tunisie, acting on the state's behalf, and Afreximbank (the African Export-Import Bank — see the related Afreximbank US$500 Million Financing Facility entry above), amending the terms of part of that state-budget financing; and bill (5) approves the clôture du budget de l'État for fiscal year 2023 (formal closure of the FY2023 state budget accounts), an ordinary budget-execution formality distinct in kind from the CPG/GCT/Afreximbank financing-guarantee bills. Unlike bills (1)–(3), the Bureau's referral of bills (4)–(5) was not reported under the emergency (urgence) procedure. This closes the "two bills unidentified" caveat that had stood since Run 357/377; no vote or committee report has been reported for any of the five bills as of this update. Update (Run 434) — numbering completed, session confirmed, financing figure reconciled: a July 20, 2026 La Presse de Tunisie report ("ARP: examen de trois projets de loi pour financer la CPG et le Groupe chimique tunisien," independently corroborated the same day by allAfrica) confirms the Finance and Budget Committee will hold an exceptional session Tuesday, July 21, 2026 at 9:30 AM, with representatives of the Ministry of Industry, Mines and Energy and the Ministry of Economy and Planning in attendance, to examine all three named bills together. This closes the bill-numbering gap left open since Run 357: the CPG/EBRD guarantee is bill n° 49/2026, and the two GCT/ITFC Mourabaha guarantees are bills n° 50/2026 and n° 51/2026 (previously only n° 51/2026 had been individually numbered). The Presidency of the Republic itself requested the accelerated (urgence) examination procedure for all three, and the ARP Bureau has recommended consolidating bills n° 50/2026 and n° 51/2026 into a single committee report to speed plenary adoption. The article also reconciles the GCT financing figure: rather than a single $50 million facility (bill n° 51/2026 alone, per Run 357), the two GCT/ITFC Mourabaha agreements together constitute a renewable credit facility of up to $70 million — the earlier $50 million figure is not contradicted so much as superseded by this combined-facility total, which was not available until both GCT bills were identified. Still committee-stage as of this update; no vote or committee report has been reported for bills 49–51/2026. Update (Run 449) — exceptional session concluded, no bill vote yet: the Tuesday, July 21, 2026, 9:30 AM session confirmed at Run 434 was held as scheduled; per an African Manager report published the same day ("L'ARP exige le suivi des financements de la CPG et du GCT"), deputies used the session to press CPG and GCT representatives on the sector's underlying finances rather than proceeding straight to a vote on bills 49–51/2026, again criticizing the absence of a clear strategic vision and reform program for the phosphate sector (echoing the Run 377 findings) despite the CPG/GCT leadership's previously-documented 9.4-million-tonne 2035 production target and TND 2.7 billion investment plan. The committee closed the session by recommending exceptional, urgent measures to stabilize CPG and GCT's finances — enabling them to honor supplier commitments and restore confidence among banks and financing partners — alongside a "realistic and adequate" sector-revival strategy, rather than by voting the three guarantee bills themselves. No vote tally, committee report, or JORT publication for bills 49–51/2026 has been reported as of this update; the strategic-vision/reform-program demand remains the standing precondition the committee has now reiterated twice (Run 377 and Run 449) before it will advance the financing bills.
FinancialAdministrativePending

Law No. 2026-16 of July 20, 2026, Approving the 2026–2030 National Development Plan

Filed under Constitution Arts. 75 & 77 | ARP standing-committee examination: June 29 – July 2, 2026 (concluded) | Plenary session: July 9–10, 2026 | Adopted by ARP plenary: July 10, 2026 (64 for, 24 against, 15 abstentions) | Adopted by CNRD plenary: July 17, 2026 (56 for, 6 against, 5 abstentions) | Promulgated by President Kaïs Saïed: July 20, 2026 | Law No. 2026-16 of July 20, 2026, published JORT July 21, 2026 — confirmed, in force
The economic and social planning framework law for 2026–2030, formally deposited at the ARP as draft law n°42/2026 on June 17, 2026 following its adoption by the Council of Ministers. Structured as a single-article bill — the Assembly votes yes/no/abstain on the plan as a whole rather than amending individual provisions, per committee rapporteur Saber Jlassi — it was examined jointly by all Assembly of People's Representatives standing committees beginning June 29–30, 2026. Committee discussion noted the prior five-year plan's execution rate stood at under 40%, a benchmark cited by critical deputies alongside the new plan's more ambitious targets. Targets raising the average annual growth rate to 4.2% over the period (versus 2.4% in the prior five-year period), reaching 5.1% by 2030, and raising investment from roughly 15.5% to 20% of GDP via a public-investment project portfolio of approximately TND 102 billion. A June 30, 2026 joint hearing with Minister of Economy and Planning Samir Abdelhafidh preceded committee deliberations, followed by a second joint ARP audition of the Minister on July 1, 2026 to gather further committee input (Tunisian media noted the plan's limited treatment of the media/audiovisual sector as a specific point raised in that session, and a July 1, 2026 report noted several deputies voicing doubts about the feasibility of the plan's growth and investment targets, with the Finance Committee separately flagging concerns over the underlying economic and financial assumptions). Separately on July 1, 2026, the National Union of Tunisian Journalists (SNJT) publicly criticized the plan by name for omitting a dedicated media-sector strategy, calling for the assembly to formally recognize media as an economic and cultural sector, incorporate press-freedom legislation, and include the sector in the plan's monitoring mechanisms. The plan's headline targets include lowering the poverty rate to under 15%, a portfolio of roughly 21,100 public-investment projects funded at approximately TND 102 billion, and raising the renewable-energy share of electricity production to 35% by 2030; committee coverage also noted that of 40,748 development proposals submitted by local councils, 34,715 were retained in the plan's regional components. Once adopted, it is expected to serve as the legal reference framework underpinning the national AI strategy and the NDC 3.0 climate financing plan already noted elsewhere in this document. Update (Run 71): ARP President Ibrahim Bouderbala announced July 2, 2026 that the joint standing-committee examination had formally concluded — independently reported by both Le Temps News and African Manager (both July 3, 2026) — with a consolidated synthesis report now being drafted for submission to the ARP Bureau, which will schedule the plenary session before the text proceeds to the Conseil National des Régions et des Districts under Art. 77; no plenary date has yet been announced. Bouderbala used the same occasion to publicly call for accelerating adoption of the separately-tracked Foreign Exchange, Water, Hydrocarbons, and Investment codes (see those entries). Not yet enacted — the most active legislative process in Tunisia as of this update. Update (Run 141): Finance Commission member Issam Chouchane publicly criticized the plan on July 6, 2026 (Business News), arguing the document reads as a project list rather than a genuine strategic vision and lacks explicit objectives in several sectors he considers priorities, including food security, agriculture, phosphate, tourism, and new technologies/artificial intelligence; he also questioned the feasibility of the plan's average-growth target given recent economic performance, and reiterated that deputies hold only a consultative, up-or-down role on the single-article bill (echoing an earlier June 26, 2026 critique from the same deputy on the plan's financial feasibility). Update (Run 174): on Monday, July 6, 2026, the ARP Bureau formally referred the bill to a fresh round of joint examination by all permanent standing committees — chaired by the Assembly president or a vice-president — rather than sending it directly to plenary; a consolidated synthesis report from that joint round will go back to the Bureau, which will then schedule the plenary session (RTCI, July 6–7, 2026). No plenary date has yet been announced as of this update. Update (Run 185): the ARP Bureau met the morning of July 7, 2026 and formally scheduled the plenary session on the bill for Thursday, July 9, 2026, with the possibility of extending into Friday, July 10, 2026 if debate runs long (RTCI, July 7, 2026, 14:54, corroborated by a La Presse de Tunisie report of a 10:30 AM Thursday sitting) — the first concrete plenary date announced since the bill's June 17, 2026 filing. No vote outcome had been reported as of this update; still not yet enacted. Update (Run 202): the plenary session opened as scheduled at 10:30 AM on July 9, 2026 and floor debate ran the full day with no vote outcome reported by its close, consistent with the Bureau's built-in contingency to extend proceedings into July 10. Two deputies delivered the most substantive floor criticism of the bill documented to date. Deputy Mohamed Ali argued deputies were being asked to grant "un chèque en blanc pour cinq ans" (a blank check for five years) rather than vote on an ordinary law, since Parliament had not participated in drafting the plan and the single-article, up/down structure leaves no room for genuine amendment; he cited public debt of roughly TND 147 billion (over 80% of GDP) against a still-low national savings rate and a persistently deficient budget, faulted the plan for listing a large number of projects without a clear implementation priority ranking or demonstrated administrative capacity, and closed by calling on deputies to vote against the bill to defend "le droit du peuple à un développement réel, pas un développement sur papier" (Business News, July 9, 2026). Separately, ARP Bureau vice-president Nabil Hamdi — while confirming the Bureau's own procedural decision to send the bill to this plenary — criticized its substance, saying the plan in its current form "reflète davantage la volonté de l'administration que celle du peuple" (reflects the will of the administration more than that of the people), faulting the project portfolio as insufficiently detailed for the standing committees and citing what he called a "systematic exclusion" of Parliament from the plan's drafting phase (Radio Express FM, July 9, 2026). No JORT publication or confirmed floor vote had followed by this run's cutoff; still not yet enacted. Update (Run 218): the ARP plenary concluded debate on July 10, 2026 — Minister of Economy and Planning Samir Abdelhafidh replied to deputies' interventions before the vote (Tunisie Tribune, African Manager, same-day) — and adopted the bill in its entirety by a recorded vote of 64 for, 24 against, and 15 abstentions (La Presse de Tunisie, July 10, 2026), resolving the single most-tracked pending item across this changelog's recent runs. This vote outcome was, as of this update, reported only by La Presse among the outlets checked (African Manager and Tunisie Tribune's own July 10 pieces describe the session as still in progress ahead of the vote, likely filed before it concluded); flagged as single-source pending next-run corroboration, consistent with this artifact's established practice for time-sensitive same-day findings. The bill still requires presidential promulgation and JORT publication before entering into force — neither had been identified as of this update. Update (Run 219): the 64-24-15 tally is now independently corroborated by a second outlet, webdo.tn (July 10, 2026, 12:02 PM), reporting identical figures — no longer single-sourced. Presidential promulgation and JORT publication remain outstanding as of this update; the companion STEG loan-guarantee bills (n°38/39-2026, see below) remain undecided despite reportedly sharing the same July 9–10 plenary window. Update (Run 232): a procedural detail not previously captured: per Constitution Art. 77's bicameral design, the plan bill does not proceed straight from ARP adoption to presidential promulgation — it must also pass a second-chamber vote at the Conseil National des Régions et des Districts (CNRD), with disagreements between the two chambers referred to a 10-member joint committee and, if still unresolved, final say reverting to the CNRD (Tunisie Numérique, quoting deputy Jmaï Zouidi). This clarifies why promulgation/JORT publication remain outstanding well past the July 10 ARP vote: a CNRD vote is a still-outstanding procedural step, not merely an administrative delay in signature. No CNRD vote date had been reported as of this update. Update (Run 236): the CNRD's examination has now expanded beyond the first district (Bizerte, Béja, Jendouba, Le Kef) to a second district as well, per an African Manager report of CNRD president Imed Derbali's hearing with Economy Minister Samir Abdelhafidh on regional-disparity priorities — specific figures cited included a 0.51 territorial-development index for Bizerte and a 7.3% poverty rate for Jendouba. Still no CNRD floor vote, presidential promulgation, or JORT publication confirmed as of this update. Update (Run 266): the CNRD, under president Imed Derbali, examined the plan's regional/territorial components on or around July 12, 2026, per La Presse de Tunisie ("La Tunisie acte le virage de la planification par la base," July 12, 2026) and a corroborating African Manager report on the same session calling for reduced territorial disparities — confirming the CNRD stage is actively underway post-ARP-vote and consistent with the sequence documented at Run 232/236 above. No CNRD floor vote, presidential promulgation, or JORT publication had been reported as of this update. Update (Run 315): no change — no CNRD floor vote, promulgation, or JORT publication has been reported since Run 266's confirmation of the district-by-district regional-component review. Update (Run 319): the CNRD's "commission des plans de développement et des grands projets" (Development Plans and Major Projects Committee), chaired by Mohamed Lakou, unanimously adopted its final report on the plan on Tuesday, July 14, 2026 at CNRD headquarters in Bardo (Business News, "Les 5 infos de la journée," July 14, 2026; corroborated by African Manager same-day coverage) — a committee-level milestone that moves the file past the district-by-district regional review documented since Run 266/315, but is still one procedural step short of a CNRD plenary floor vote; no floor-vote date, presidential promulgation, or JORT publication had been reported as of this update. Update (Run 349): the CNRD announced July 16, 2026 that it will hold the plenary session to examine the bill on Friday, July 17, 2026, starting 10:00 AM (businessnews.com.tn, July 16, 2026) — the first confirmed CNRD floor-vote date since the July 14 committee-report milestone. No outcome had been reported as of this update, since the session is scheduled for the day after this run. Update (Run 374): the CNRD plenary session was held as scheduled on the evening of Friday, July 17, 2026, and adopted the bill in its entirety by a recorded vote of 56 for, 6 against, and 5 abstentions, per La Presse de Tunisie's same-day report, cross-checked against Business News' July 16 preview coverage and a Tunisie Numérique piece on the run-up — resolving the single standing procedural gap this entry has tracked since Run 232. With both the ARP (July 10) and the CNRD (July 17) having now approved the bill under Constitution Art. 77's bicameral design, the only remaining steps before the plan takes legal effect are presidential promulgation and JORT publication, neither of which had been identified as of this update. A same-run sweep for JORT publications and ARP floor votes dated July 16–17, 2026 found nothing else rising to the level of a new primary-legislation entry, beyond the already-tracked CPG/GCT phosphates-financing bills (see above), which remained at committee stage. Confirmed Run 428: a July 19, 2026 La Presse de Tunisie op-ed ("Plan 2026-2030: dans l'attente de la concrétisation") independently reconfirms the plan is still awaiting concrete implementation, with no mention of a presidential decree or JORT publication — corroborating, from a source distinct from the L'Économiste Maghrébin piece already cited at Run 425/427, that promulgation remains outstanding. Confirmed Run 429: a July 19, 2026 L'Économiste Maghrébin piece ("Ça y est, le Plan de développement 2026-2030 définitivement adopté") was independently checked via direct fetch and, consistent with Run 428's finding from a different outlet, confirms only the CNRD's July 17 adoption as "definitive" from a parliamentary-procedure standpoint — it does not mention presidential promulgation or JORT publication, so the legal-effect status is unchanged: adopted by both chambers, not yet in force. Update (Run 430): a July 20, 2026 Webmanagercenter piece ("La Tunisie engage une réforme en profondeur de la fonction publique") independently fetched and reviewed adds substantive, previously-uncaptured detail on the plan's civil-service/public-administration reform component — distinct from the growth, investment, and poverty targets already documented above — including a full revision of civil-service legislation, predictive HR-management systems, a public-sector occupations/job-description guide, new individual performance-evaluation systems, "eliminating all forms of precarious employment through regularizing the situation of substitutes, contractors and construction workers," a restructured National School of Administration (blended in-person/distance training), and a digital platform for administrative-structure data management. The article does not mention presidential promulgation or JORT publication, so the legal-effect status remains unchanged: adopted by both chambers, not yet in force. Update (Run 431): a fresh direct fetch of the same July 19, 2026 L'Économiste Maghrébin piece cited at Run 429 ("Ça y est, le Plan de développement 2026-2030 définitivement adopté") surfaced a fuller set of quantified targets around the CNRD's July 17 vote than this entry had previously captured — distinct from the growth/investment/poverty/renewable-energy figures already documented above: a progressive reduction of the budget deficit to near 3% of GDP, a public-debt ceiling of 80% of GDP (the plan's own target, distinct from Deputy Mohamed Ali's July 9 floor-debate criticism of the current ~80%-of-GDP debt level cited above), raising Tunisia's Human Development Index into the "very high" tier, cutting primary energy consumption 30% (alongside the already-documented 35%-of-mix renewable-energy target), and narrowing the trade deficit toward 9.9% by 2030. Added as an in-place enrichment; none of this bears on the still-outstanding promulgation/JORT-publication question. A same-run re-check of the two other standing watchlist items with open leads found no change: the Mseddi/Ennahdha terrorist-designation bill — reconfirmed filed July 9, 2026 (6 articles) via Business News, L'Économiste Maghrébin, and African Manager's original coverage, still no confirmed committee hearing date; and the ONAT general-assembly outcome — a fresh search surfaced only unrelated corporate "assemblée générale" results (French/European listed companies), no Tunisian Bar Association outcome, consistent with every run since Run 119. Update (Run 446): presidential promulgation — the single most persistently-tracked open item on this artifact's watchlist, unresolved across roughly 227 runs since Run 218/219's July 10, 2026 ARP-vote finding — is now confirmed. President Kaïs Saïed received Prime Minister Sarra Zaâfrani Zenzri, Minister of Economy and Planning Samir Abdelhafidh, and the Central Bank Governor at Carthage Palace on Monday, July 20, 2026, specifically to discuss implementation of what both outlets covering the meeting independently describe, in near-identical terms, as the law approving the plan having already been promulgated: Réalités Magazine ("s'est félicité...de la promulgation de la loi portant approbation du Plan de développement 2026-2030") and webdo ("la loi de ratification a été promulguée"), two independently-bylined reports of the same meeting, satisfying this artifact's two-independent-source rule. At the same meeting Saïed called for converting Tunisia's accumulated debt into concrete investment to help fund public-service reconstruction, while affirming Tunisia has met all its financial obligations on time. No specific JORT issue number, exact promulgation date, or presidential decree number was independently located despite a dedicated search of JORT-listing sites (jort.tn's own homepage listing showed only "Annonces Légales" issues N°084-086 with no visible law-text index; 9anoun.tn's public archive did not appear to be indexed past April 2026 at the time of this pass) — this citation gap is flagged as the sole remaining follow-up rather than a reason to withhold recognition of the law's enactment, consistent with how this artifact has treated other promulgated-but-not-yet-JORT-cited items in the past. The status line above is updated accordingly; this entry remains in the Public Finance section rather than being relocated, consistent with this artifact's practice of tracking status changes in-place via dated updates rather than moving cards between sections. Update (Run 447): a third independent Tunisian outlet, Business News (July 21, 2026, "Kaïs Saïed appelle à convertir la dette tunisienne..."), independently refers to "la loi récemment promulguée portant approbation du Plan de développement 2026-2030" in coverage of the same July 20 Carthage Palace meeting, corroborating Réalités Magazine and webdo beyond the minimum two-source rule. A dedicated follow-up fetch of jort.tn, jort.tn/lois/, and 9anoun.tn again found no JORT issue number, law number, or exact promulgation date (africanmanager.com's parallel coverage returned HTTP 403) — the citation gap remains the entry's sole open item. Update (Run 448): a fourth independent outlet, La Presse de Tunisie (July 21, 2026), covering the same July 20 Carthage Palace meeting, was identified as also referring to the plan-approval law in already-promulgated terms, alongside Réalités Magazine, webdo, and Business News. A renewed dedicated search of jort.tn and 9anoun.tn again returned no JORT issue/decree number — the citation gap remains the entry's sole open item, now unresolved across 230 runs since Run 218/219. Update (Run 451): a fifth independent outlet, L'Économiste Maghrébin (July 21, 2026, "Kaïs Saïed: 'la Tunisie a honoré ses engagements' malgré une dette 'sans bénéfice pour le peuple'"), independently references "la loi promulguée relative à l'approbation du Plan de développement 2026-2030" in its own coverage of the same July 20 Carthage Palace meeting, alongside Réalités Magazine, webdo, Business News, and La Presse de Tunisie. Renewed site-restricted searches of jort.tn and 9anoun.tn again returned no JORT issue number, law number, or exact promulgation date — the citation gap remains the entry's sole open item, now unresolved across 233 runs since Run 218/219. Update (Run 486): a promising but still single-sourced lead surfaced this run: four independently-phrased WebSearch queries each returned a consistent, specific citation — Law No. 2026-16 of July 20, 2026, a single-article law ("le plan de développement 2026-2030, annexé à la présente loi, est approuvé"), published in the JORT of July 21, 2026 — all four times traced back to the same single outlet, Business News' July 22, 2026 piece ("Le Plan de développement 2026-2030 publié au Journal officiel"). Repeated direct WebFetch attempts on that article (four total, across this run) each returned HTTP 503 rather than article content, so the citation could not be confirmed by reading the primary text directly; parallel searches restricted to La Presse, WebManagerCenter, and African Manager, plus a direct query for the exact string "2026-16," found no second independent outlet corroborating the specific law number, and renewed jort.tn/9anoun.tn checks still did not surface it independently. Given this artifact's two-independent-source rule for legal citations (and its prior experience rejecting a fabricated aggregator citation for the Data Protection bill), this specific number is deliberately not treated as confirmed yet — flagged here as a concrete, worth-checking lead for the next run (re-attempt the direct fetch; check whether Business News' article text is reachable via a different route) rather than either asserted or discarded. The other six standing-watchlist items were also re-checked this run with no change: LTDH (still adjourned to July 29, 2026, not yet due), ONAT general assembly outcome (still unreported by any outlet, now 31 consecutive runs unresolved), Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution (still unreported beyond the June 1, 2026 hearing date located this run — itself a further postponement from the May 11, 2026 hearing already documented, but still no ruling), Mseddi/Ennahdha bill (still no confirmed committee referral beyond its July 9, 2026 filing), the telework/platform-economy bills (still "nearly finalized" per Minister Lahmar's July 20, 2026 remarks, not yet formally filed at the ARP), and the Foreign Exchange Code (still ~90%-complete committee hearings, BCT non-response, no floor vote). Confirmed Run 488: the Law No. 2026-16 citation flagged as an unconfirmed single-source lead at Run 486/487 is now independently corroborated by a second outlet — L'Économiste Maghrébin ("Plan de développement 2026-2030 : la loi d'approbation publiée au JORT," July 23, 2026), directly fetched and confirmed to independently state the same three facts as Business News' July 22 piece: Law No. 2026-16 of July 20, 2026, single-article ("est approuvé le plan de développement 2026-2030, annexé à la présente loi"), adopted by both the ARP and the CNRD, promulgated by President Kaïs Saïed, and published in the JORT of July 21, 2026. This satisfies this artifact's two-independent-source rule after 32 consecutive runs (Run 218/219 onward) without a corroborated citation — the entry's title and status line above have been updated accordingly, and it is now treated as a confirmed, in-force law rather than a pending bill. This run's fresh sweep of the other six standing-watchlist items found no change: LTDH (independently reconfirmed by five outlets — African Manager, Tunisie Tribune, Directinfo/Webmanagercenter, Réalités Magazine, and businessnews.com.tn — as still adjourned to July 29, 2026, not yet due); ONAT general assembly outcome (still unreported by any outlet, now 33 consecutive runs unresolved); Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution (still unreported beyond the July 6, 2026 hearing; a search for the Mosbah appeal confirmation and Al Khatt timeline found both already captured at equal or greater precision than newly available sources); Mseddi/Ennahdha bill (still no confirmed committee referral beyond its July 9, 2026 filing); telework/platform-economy bills (Minister Issam Lahmar reconfirmed July 21, 2026 they remain "nearly finalized," not yet formally filed at the ARP); and the Foreign Exchange Code (still ~90%-complete committee hearings, BCT non-response, no floor vote located).
FinancialConstitutional2026

🌾 Agriculture & Fisheries

Fisheries Code — Law No. 94-13 of January 31, 1994 (as amended 2002, 2014)

Enacted: January 31, 1994 | In force: 1994 | Amended: 2002, 2014
Comprehensive statute governing fishing activities in Tunisian territorial waters and the EEZ. Commercial fishing vessels require licenses from the Ministry of Agriculture. Sets fishing quotas, prohibited fishing methods (dynamite, poison, trawling within 3 nm of coast), protected marine species, and seasonal closures. Establishes the National Fisheries Office (ONP — Office National des Pêches) for market organization and port management. Tuna and swordfish fishing regulated under ICCAT commitments. Aquaculture (fish farming) licensing governed by supplementary 2006 decree.
AgricultureEnvironmental

Plant Protection Law — Law No. 92-72 of August 3, 1992; Veterinary Law — Law No. 2005-35 of May 25, 2005

Plant Protection: August 3, 1992 | Veterinary: May 25, 2005
(v4.178 correction, Run 178): The plant-protection half of this entry previously cited "Law No. 99-24 of February 23, 1999" and an administering "INAP — National Institute for Plant Protection." Neither checks out: Law No. 99-24 is in fact dated March 9, 1999 and governs an unrelated subject — veterinary sanitary control at import and export — not plant health (confirmed via two independent legal-database sources), and no institute named "INAP" could be independently corroborated as Tunisia's plant-protection body (the confirmed body is the Direction Générale de la Santé Végétale et du Contrôle des Intrants Agricoles, under the Ministry of Agriculture). The correct citation for Tunisia's phytosanitary/plant-protection framework is Law No. 92-72 of August 3, 1992 (recasting prior phytosanitary legislation into a unified regime), independently confirmed via FAOLEX's full text and a second corroborating source. It prohibits import, possession, cultivation, and transit of quarantine pests absent authorization and conditions plant/plant-product imports on a phytosanitary certificate per the International Plant Protection Convention model (implementing detail set by ministerial order, e.g. a February 19, 2016 decree on import requirements and inspection procedures), with narrow exemptions for processed agricultural goods. The separate Law No. 99-24 of March 9, 1999 (veterinary sanitary control at import/export) remains a genuine, distinct Tunisian statute not otherwise documented in this artifact. The Veterinary Law (2005-35) citation is unchanged from prior runs and was not re-verified this pass; it regulates animal health surveillance, veterinary practice licensing, slaughterhouse standards, and control of notifiable animal diseases. Both plant- and animal-health regimes implement Tunisia's WTO Agreement on Sanitary and Phytosanitary Measures (SPS Agreement) obligations for agricultural trade.
AgricultureHealth

Veterinary Pharmacy Law — Law No. 78-23 of March 8, 1978 (as amended by Law No. 2000-40 of April 5, 2000)Réglementation de la Pharmacie Vétérinaire (Run 174 addition)

Enacted: March 8, 1978 | Amended: April 5, 2000 | Implementing decree: Décret n° 79-831 of September 28, 1979
A previously undocumented, narrowly distinct statute from the Veterinary Law entry above: while Law No. 2005-35 governs animal health surveillance and veterinary practice licensing, this earlier 1978 law (as substantially amended in 2000) specifically regulates veterinary medicines — manufacturing-facility authorization, market-authorization (visa) applications and renewals for veterinary pharmaceutical products, and pharmacovigilance, administered by the Ministry of Public Health's Direction de la Pharmacie et du Médicament (DPM). Décret n° 79-831 of September 28, 1979 sets good-manufacturing-practice and quality-control standards, plus packaging/labeling rules, for veterinary-medicine production. This narrow medicines-licensing gap survived at least four prior targeted "veterinary" gap-scans (Runs 82, 90, 113, 116) that confirmed only the broader animal-health/practice statute, without distinguishing this separate pharmaceutical-regulation track — parallel to how Run 147 found the companion-animal-welfare gap after similar prior scans of adjacent terrain.
AgricultureHealth

Agricultural Development Law — Law No. 71-72 of December 31, 1971 and Law No. 2000-30 of March 20, 2000

Development Areas: 1971 | Irrigated Perimeters: March 20, 2000
The 1971 law empowers the Ministry of Agriculture to designate agricultural development areas with state support for irrigation and land improvement. The 2000 law governs management of public irrigated perimeters (périmètres irrigués publics — PIPs): water pricing through GDA user associations (Groupements de Développement Agricole), infrastructure maintenance, and cost recovery. Emergency water restrictions (2023 drought) were issued under the Water Code's provisions with inter-ministerial coordination.
AgricultureLand

Organic Agriculture Law — Law No. 99-30 of April 5, 1999

Enacted: April 5, 1999 | In force: 1999 | Amended: 2009
Establishes the legal framework for organic farming, certification, labeling, and inspection in Tunisia. Organic products must be certified by an accredited certification body and labeled with the national organic mark. Controls use of synthetic pesticides, fertilizers, and GMOs on certified organic land. Administered by the Ministry of Agriculture and the Office for Organic Farming Development (BIO). Tunisia exports significant volumes of certified organic olive oil and dates to EU markets.
AgricultureCommercial

Law No. 67-41 of June 24, 1967 (as amended)Agricultural Cooperatives

Enacted: June 24, 1967 | In force: 1967 | Amended: 1982, 2001, 2020
Establishes the legal framework for agricultural cooperatives (coopératives agricoles) in Tunisia. Agricultural cooperatives are governed by a hybrid regime combining principles from general cooperative law and specific agricultural provisions. Types include: production cooperatives, service cooperatives (input supply, marketing), and credit cooperatives (Caisses de Crédit Agricole Mutuels). Registration with the Ministry of Agriculture and the Office of Agricultural Credit Cooperatives (ODCO) is required. Cooperatives benefit from tax exemptions on profits and reduced registration fees. The Tunisian Cooperative Union (UTAP — Union Tunisienne de l'Agriculture et de la Pêche) represents cooperatives at national level. The 2020 amendment strengthened governance requirements and introduced digital voting for cooperative assemblies.
Key provisions
  • Art. 1–10: Definition of agricultural cooperative; democratic governance (one member, one vote)
  • Art. 11–20: Types: production, marketing, supply, credit cooperatives
  • Art. 21–28: Registration requirements; statutes; minimum capital
  • Art. 29–36: Distribution of surplus to members proportional to participation
  • Art. 37–44: Tax advantages: exemption from corporate tax; reduced stamp duties
  • Art. 45–52: Dissolution and liquidation procedures
AgricultureCommercial

Law No. 67-4 of January 19, 1967 (Run 382 addition)General Statute of Cooperation (Statut Général de la Coopération)

Enacted: January 19, 1967 | In force: 1967
The base, cross-sectoral statute establishing the legal form and general principles of the cooperative society (société coopérative) in Tunisia — democratic one-member-one-vote governance, member-proportional surplus distribution, and a registration/dissolution regime — beneath which sector-specific texts operate, most prominently the Agricultural Cooperatives Law (No. 67-41, above) as well as separate consumer, housing, craft/industrial, and fishing cooperative regimes. Sourced via ILO NATLEX's Tunisia legislative database entry ("Loi n° 67-4 portant statut général de la coopération") and independently corroborated by the Coops4Dev/International Cooperative Alliance's 2020 legal-framework analysis of Tunisia, which describes Tunisian cooperative law as split across six category-specific texts sitting on this general 1967 base and — consistent with this artifact's practice of flagging rather than resolving genuine primary-source gaps — itself calls the resulting framework "overly complex," recommending eventual consolidation into a single modern code. This closes a gap first flagged as a low-confidence "genuinely ambiguous" open item at Run 129 and left unresolved across the roughly 250 runs since: the general statute's own text (as opposed to the agricultural-sector implementation already documented) had not previously been independently sourced. The full article-by-article text was not directly retrievable this run (NATLEX's detail page returned HTTP 403 and a related PDF was unparseable), so no line-item provisions are cited here pending a future run's direct-text confirmation; the law's existence, number, and general subject are corroborated by two independent sources.
AgricultureCommercial

Law No. 2001-94 of August 7, 2001Pastoral and Communal Lands Code

Enacted: August 7, 2001 | In force: 2001
Regulates the use and management of collective pastoral lands (terres collectives de parcours) and communal grazing areas in Tunisia, particularly relevant in the arid and semi-arid regions of central and southern Tunisia. These lands — covering approximately 5 million hectares — are traditionally managed by tribal communities. The code establishes a management framework balancing customary pastoral rights with sustainable land management obligations. Creates Pastoral Development Associations (associations de développement pastoral) for each defined rangeland unit. Prohibits unauthorized subdivision, sale, or permanent agricultural conversion of collective pastoral lands without Ministry of Agriculture approval. Provides mechanisms for resolving boundary disputes between pastoral communities.
Key provisions
  • Art. 1–8: Definition of collective pastoral lands; state ownership with usufruct rights to communities
  • Art. 9–16: Pastoral Development Associations (PDA) — formation, governance, mandate
  • Art. 17–24: Seasonal grazing circuits; carrying capacity limits per zone
  • Art. 25–30: Prohibition of unauthorized permanent crop installation; penalties
  • Art. 31–36: Water point management in pastoral zones
  • Art. 37–42: Dispute resolution between communities; role of Ministry of Agriculture
AgricultureLand

Law No. 2019-25 of February 26, 2019Food and Animal Feed Safety

Enacted: February 26, 2019 | In force: 2019
(v4.178 correction, Run 178): This entry previously cited "Law No. 2009-11 of February 11, 2009" as the food-safety framework law and "ANCSEP" as the body it created — both incorrect. Law No. 2009-11 (March 2, 2009) is in fact Tunisia's building fire/explosion/panic-safety code, an entirely unrelated statute (confirmed via legislation-securite.tn and citet.nat.tn), and ANCSEP (Agence Nationale de Contrôle Sanitaire et Environnemental des Produits) was created earlier, by Decree No. 99-779 of April 5, 1999, as a general sanitary/environmental product-control coordinator — not a food-safety-specific body created by this law. The actual food-safety framework statute is Law No. 2019-25 of February 26, 2019, relative à la sécurité sanitaire des denrées alimentaires et des aliments pour animaux, which establishes a unified legal regime for foodstuff and animal-feed safety grounded in three principles: risk analysis based on scientific evidence, precaution, and transparency (public consultation and consumer information). It creates the National Authority for Food Safety (Instance Nationale de la Sécurité Sanitaire des Produits Alimentaires — INSSPA), which brings together representatives from the health, agriculture, and commerce ministries and functions as Tunisia's sole risk-management and risk-notification authority for food products — distinct from and complementing ANCSEP's broader coordination role. Mandates food-chain traceability from producer to consumer. Confirmed via FAOLEX's full text of the law and INSSPA/ANCSEP institutional-comparison sources; specific article numbers are not asserted here pending direct verification against the official JORT text, consistent with this project's practice of omitting unverifiable citations.
AgricultureHealthCommercial

Law No. 2026-5 of March 11, 2026Regularization of Distressed Agricultural Debts

Adopted by ARP: March 10, 2026 | Promulgated: March 11, 2026 | Provisions apply through: December 31, 2026
Requires public and private banks to reschedule the full principal and initial contractual interest of classified/distressed agricultural debts (BCT class 4 and above, held before September 30, 2025) owed by farmers and agricultural companies, over up to 7 years including a 1-year grace period. Mandates automatic removal of the resulting negative banking classification once a rescheduling agreement is approved, restoring debtors' access to new financing. The Central Bank of Tunisia (BCT) was required to issue implementing conditions within 15 days of publication. Excludes debts connected to unresolved corruption or money-laundering proceedings. Enacted to address accumulated financial distress in the agricultural sector that had left many farmers and agri-businesses unable to settle liabilities or obtain new credit.
AgricultureFinancial

Proposition de loi n° 74/2024 (pending)Animal Protection and Domestic-Carnivore Regulation

Filed: July 23, 2024 (Deputy Chokri Bahri) | Examined: ARP Agriculture, Food Security & Maritime Fishing Commission, May 5, 2026 | Status: not yet enacted as of July 2026
A member's bill that would modernize Tunisia's animal-protection framework — described by its authors as dating to 19th-century legislative instruments never adapted to current social, economic, and environmental conditions — and specifically regulate the detention and circulation of domestic carnivores (dogs and cats) amid a persistent stray-dog and rabies-control problem in Tunisian cities. Proposes shifting municipal stray-animal management away from mass culling campaigns toward prevention, registration, and sterilization programs, while strengthening penalties for cruelty and negligence. At the May 5, 2026 Agriculture Commission session, deputies broadly welcomed the initiative as aligning Tunisia with international animal-welfare standards but raised concerns about the technical and financial capacity of municipalities to run effective sterilization programs at scale. Remains at the committee-examination stage; no committee vote or plenary referral confirmed as of this run. (Added Run 147 — a wholly new standalone entry outside the pending-bill watchlist, closing a gap in the Agriculture & Fisheries section that prior runs' "veterinary/animal health" gap-scans had not distinguished from this separate animal-welfare/companion-animal track.) Update (Run 176): Review of the bill accelerated after a video showing a performance-troupe member dancing on the belly of a horse lying on the ground during a wedding ceremony in Sahline (Monastir governorate) went viral around July 6–7, 2026; the animal-protection association Arrahma announced it was pursuing a formal criminal complaint against the identified troupe (a second, initially accused troupe was cleared after verification) and separately called on Parliament to expedite the reform. Business News (July 7, 2026) reported the ARP was accelerating examination of the animal-welfare framework, and press coverage detailed proposed tiered penalties not previously captured here: 15 days'–1 month imprisonment plus a TND 500 fine for general mistreatment, rising to 1–10 years plus a TND 2,000 fine where abuse causes permanent disability, and 5 years where it causes death, with enhanced sentences for repeat offenders. Corroborated across Business News, Univers News, and allAfrica (citing Arrahma's president Jamila Ammar); no committee vote or plenary referral confirmed as of this run. This is the same Proposition de loi n° 74/2024 tracked above, not a separate bill, despite one headline's reference to "deux propositions de loi." Correction & update (Run 203): that Run 176 dismissal appears to have been premature. A separate Business News article with the same "deux propositions de loi" headline (July 7, 2026, sourcing deputy Mohamed Zied El Maher's same-day Diwan FM interview) was independently re-checked and, across three separately-worded searches, consistently identified Proposition de loi n° 66/2024 — also filed July 23, 2024 "by a group of deputies," also before the Agriculture Commission, which "has already heard the project initiators and several sector stakeholders" — as the bill carrying the reinforced financial/criminal sanctions for mistreatment, capture, or killing of an animal (i.e., the tiered-penalty provisions Run 176 attributed to n° 74/2024 above may in fact belong to this companion bill), while n° 74/2024 is described in this account as the complementary prevention/awareness text providing for animal-welfare and environmental-protection content in school curricula. This conflicts with the July 26, 2024 Kapitalis filing announcement (sourced directly via WebFetch), which names only Deputy Chokri Bahri and a single unnumbered bill combining both sanction-strengthening and "culture of respect" language — the article that appears to underlie this artifact's existing n° 74/2024 attribution above. Given this unresolved conflict between a 2024 primary filing source (one bill, one sponsor, mixed content) and 2026 committee-status coverage (two numbered bills, split by content) — and a failed direct WebFetch of the July 7, 2026 Business News article itself (HTTP 503) that prevented full-text confirmation — this update stops short of re-splitting the entry or reattributing content, and instead flags Proposition de loi n° 66/2024 as a probable second, separately-numbered animal-protection bill before the same Agriculture Commission, to be confirmed or resolved by a future run with direct access to the ARP's bill registry or the source article's full text. Update (Run 204): a direct WebFetch of the same July 7, 2026 Business News article again failed (HTTP 503, matching Run 203's failure and confirming this is a persistent access issue rather than a transient one), so full-text confirmation still could not be obtained; however, two independently-worded WebSearch queries run this pass both returned AI-generated summaries of that article converging on the same account: Proposition de loi n° 66/2024 (filed July 23, 2024 "by a group of deputies") is the bill carrying the reinforced sanctions — a summary this run renders as 1–3 years' imprisonment plus a fine of up to TND 2,000 where abuse causes permanent injury, rising to 5 years where it causes death (qualified as a "crime" rather than a lesser offense) — while n° 74/2024 remains the complementary prevention/awareness bill (school-curriculum content on animal welfare and environmental protection). The same summaries quote deputy Mohamed Zied El Maher explaining the delay since 2024: government observations prompted reworking of the text's legal definitions and implementation modalities, and the Agriculture Commission has already heard both the bills' initiators and sector stakeholders. Note the sanction figures in this account (1–3 years/TND 2,000/permanent injury; 5 years/death) do not exactly match the tiered penalties Run 176 sourced to the same underlying reporting (15 days'–1 month/TND 500/general mistreatment; 1–10 years/TND 2,000/permanent disability; 5 years/death) — since both derive from search-engine summarization rather than a directly fetched primary text, this discrepancy is flagged rather than silently resolved in either direction. On balance this run treats the bill-number split (n° 66/2024 = sanctions, n° 74/2024 = prevention) as now reasonably well-corroborated across three independent search passes (Runs 203 and 204), even though the exact sanction figures remain unconfirmed pending a successful direct fetch or ARP registry check. Update (Run 423): a Business News article dated May 6, 2026 (the day after the already-cited May 5, 2026 Agriculture Commission session) describes the bill under review that day as a single comprehensive structuring text of 12 titles and 111 articles, built around a "one health" (santé unique, i.e. human/animal/environmental health interdependence) framework, that would also mandate minimum living-condition standards for kept animals and introduce electronic identification/microchipping for domestic carnivores, alongside the sterilization-vs-culling debate already documented above. This 12-title/111-article structure does not obviously map onto either the "n° 66/2024" or "n° 74/2024" bill numbers already flagged as unresolved above, and may describe a consolidated version of the text presented to that same session rather than a third bill — flagged here as an additional, not-yet-reconciled data point rather than a resolution of the existing numbering ambiguity.
AgriculturePending

🏛️ Tourism & Cultural Heritage

Tourism Organization Law — Law No. 2012-49 of July 2, 2012 (Code du Tourisme)

Enacted: July 2, 2012 | In force: 2012 | Replaced: Decree-Laws No. 73-3 & 73-4 of October 3, 1973 (ratified by Laws No. 73-58 & 73-59 of November 19, 1973) and their implementing classification Decree No. 73-510 of October 30, 1973
Governs all tourism activities in Tunisia: classification and licensing of hotels (1–5 stars), travel agencies, tour operators, holiday villages, and tourist guides. Hotels classified by the Ministry of Tourism with annual inspection. The National Tourism Office (ONTT — Office National du Tourisme Tunisien) promotes tourism internationally. Investment incentives for hotel renovation under the Investment Law 2016. Tourism zone master plans govern coastal development. Replaced the sector's original 1973 framework — Decree-Law 73-3 (ratified by Law 73-58) controlled tourist-establishment management/operation, Decree-Law 73-4 (ratified by Law 73-59) controlled their construction, and Decree No. 73-510 set hotel classification standards (itself amended in 1983 and 1986, later updated by Decree No. 2007-457 of March 6, 2007, prior to this 2012 Code's full recodification). 2025: Draft Law No. 93/2025 proposed 30% social tourism quota, generating hotel federation opposition.
