(Run 174 addition): a second, previously undocumented reform bill was examined alongside the social-tourism quota at the same ARP Tourism Committee session on March 11, 2026 — a proposal to replace the tourist-guide licensing regime, unchanged since the 1970s, with stricter qualification requirements (specialized training, Tunisian history/culture knowledge, foreign-language proficiency); as of that session, the committee had not yet decided whether to amend the existing framework or draft an entirely new one, and said it would consult further stakeholders before finalizing the text (La Presse, Managers.tn, both March 2026). No later committee action or vote has been found as of this run.
TourismCommercial

Law No. 2008-33 of May 13, 2008Shared-Time Tourist Accommodation (Hébergement Touristique à Temps Partagé)

Enacted: May 13, 2008 | Published: JORT No. 40, May 16, 2008 | Implementing texts: Decree No. 2009-1935 of June 15, 2009; Ministerial Order of June 16, 2009
Regulates Tunisia's "timeshare" tourist-accommodation sector, distinct from the general Tourism Organization Law above. Amends an earlier 1997 framework and requires operators to obtain prior authorization before offering shared-time accommodation rights. Characterizes the right acquired by a purchaser as a droit de jouissance (usufruct-type real right) rather than co-ownership (multipropriété) as sometimes marketed — a deliberate divergence from the ownership model used in some other jurisdictions. Decree No. 2009-1935 (June 15, 2009) sets the conditions for obtaining the required prior operating authorization; a companion Ministerial Order of the same month approved a mandatory standard contract requiring at least 13 provisions (parties' identities, subject matter, duration, price, statutory reflection/cooling-off period, management arrangements, termination modalities, and dispute resolution). JurisiteTunisie's consumer guidance separately warns prospective buyers about timeshare-marketing scams predating and motivating the law. Never previously documented in 132 prior research runs.
TourismCivilCommercial

Cultural Heritage Protection Law — Law No. 94-35 of February 24, 1994

Enacted: February 24, 1994 | In force: 1994 | Amended: 2013
Classifies and protects Tunisia's movable and immovable cultural heritage: archaeological sites, historic monuments (medinas, mosques, ribats, Roman amphitheaters), traditional crafts, and museum collections. Unauthorized excavation, construction near classified sites, and export of antiquities are criminal offenses. The National Heritage Institute (INP — Institut National du Patrimoine) manages classified sites. Tunisia has 8 UNESCO World Heritage Sites (Carthage, Medina of Tunis, El Jem Amphitheatre, Dougga, Ichkeul National Park, Kerkouane, Kairouan, Sousse Medina). Implements the 1972 UNESCO World Heritage Convention (ratified 1975).
Key provisions
  • Classification of monuments, sites, and historic districts by ministerial order
  • Protection zones (périmètres de sauvegarde) around classified sites
  • Prohibition on unauthorized excavation — criminal penalties
  • Export of classified antiquities prohibited; unlawful export criminalized
  • INP manages museums and archaeological parks
  • International cooperation with UNESCO, ICOMOS, and bilateral partners
TourismEnvironmental

Crafts Law — Law No. 2009-25 of May 23, 2009 (Loi relative à l'artisanat)

Enacted: May 23, 2009 | In force: 2009
Defines and protects traditional Tunisian crafts (artisanat). Establishes the National Office of Tunisian Crafts (ONAT — Office National de l'Artisanat Tunisien). Creates the "artisan" professional status with access to specific microfinancing (BTS loans). Protects geographical craft designations (e.g., "Tapis de Kairouan," "Poterie de Nabeul," "Chéchia de Tunis"). Quality certification and export promotion administered by ONAT. The crafts sector employs over 300,000 artisans and is a significant contributor to tourism revenue.
TourismCommercial

Cinema and Cultural Industries — Law No. 93-40 of April 26, 1993 and Ministerial Orders

Cinema framework: April 26, 1993 | Ongoing regulatory updates | Reform bill: Proposition de loi n° 2024-03, committee review ongoing as of June 2026
Governs Tunisian cinema production, distribution, and exhibition. Establishes the Centre National du Cinéma et de l'Image (CNCI) which administers production subsidies, co-production agreements, and film classification. Tunisia maintains an active film industry with international co-productions. The broader cultural sector is governed by the Ministry of Cultural Affairs, which funds the national theater (TNT), opera (OCT), symphonic orchestra, and public libraries network. Cultural associations are registered under Decree 88 of 2011. Pending reform: Proposition de loi n° 2024-03, a dedicated statute to organize, structure, and develop the cinema industry, has been under review by the ARP's Tourism, Culture, Services, and Handicrafts Committee. Correction: the June 24, 2026 session was described in committee reporting as "continuing the study" (mouwasalat dirasa) of the bill rather than a completed article-by-article finalization as previously tracked; no committee report has been confirmed as finalized. Not yet referred to plenary as of this run. Update (Run 39): independent of this pending reform, the sector's existing subsidy mechanism was reinforced through ordinary budget legislation — Article 61 of the Finance Law 2026 (Law No. 2025-17, see Tax & Fiscal section) earmarks approximately TND 100 million per year for the CNCI's production-support fund, a concrete funding increase that took effect January 1, 2026 ahead of, and separate from, the still-pending organizational reform bill above. Update (Run 137): secondary reporting on the June 24, 2026 committee session (already cited above) additionally describes the bill as introducing an age-based film-classification system in place of the CNCI's current prior-censorship-style review, and states it would consolidate or replace older, narrower cinema-sector texts alongside the CNCI's own founding framework. This classification detail is plausible and consistent with the bill's stated purpose, but the specific predecessor-statute numbers given in that reporting could not be independently reconciled with this entry's verified Law No. 93-40/CNCI citation, so they are deliberately omitted pending a primary ARP/JORT text — treat as an open sourcing question for a future run rather than a correction. Update (Run 171): a further, distinct fiscal measure for the sector — Proposition de loi n° 103, adopted by ARP floor vote on December 3, 2025 (57 for, 34 against, 8 abstentions) — sets a single 7% tax rate on equipment for cinematographic, photographic, and audiovisual production by legally-declared enterprises, plus a full (0%) customs-duty exemption on imports of such equipment; corroborated via two independent sources (La Presse, Dec. 3, 2025; WebManagerCenter/Directinfo, Dec. 3, 2025). The Finance Minister flagged the measure with reservations during the floor debate, noting the sector already receives fiscal advantages, and described the article as regulatory text requiring separate issuance rather than an amendment folded directly into the Finance Law 2026 — no JORT publication confirming its issuance has been identified as of this run, so its current in-force status should be treated as unresolved rather than assumed enacted.
TourismPress

Law No. 2026-8 of May 13, 2026Status of the Artist and Artistic Professions

Adopted by the ARP and published in JORT: May 13, 2026 | In force: 2026
New law defining and regulating the legal status of artists and artistic professions, adopted after more than a decade of debate and lobbying by cultural-sector unions — entirely absent from this artifact despite 39 prior research runs and the existing cinema-industry entry above covering the adjacent film-subsidy framework. Establishes formal recognition and professional-card registration for artists across disciplines, and notably extends recognition to digital-arts practitioners and questions of AI-assisted creative work — one of the only Tunisian statutes to date touching generative-AI authorship issues, albeit narrowly, within the cultural sector rather than as general AI regulation (compare the still-pending Personal Data Protection & AI bill in the Digital & ICT section, which remains the primary vehicle under consideration for broader AI governance). Update (Run 423): the ARP plenary adopted the bill 76 for, 1 against, 0 abstentions on May 12, 2026, one day ahead of the May 13, 2026 JORT publication date already cited above; the enacted text runs to 45 articles and, beyond the professional-card mechanism, sets a minimum-quota requirement for Tunisian artistic content on national broadcasters, a detail not previously captured here.
AdministrativeLaborTourism

Law No. 88-95 of August 2, 1988 (as amended)National Archives, Libraries, and Documentary Heritage

Enacted: August 2, 1988 | In force: 1989 | Amended: 2002, 2018
Establishes the legal framework for the Tunisian national archives, public libraries, and documentary heritage preservation. Creates the National Archives of Tunisia (Archives Nationales de Tunisie — ANT) as the authority responsible for collecting, preserving, and providing access to historical state archives. Mandates transfer of public documents from ministries and agencies to the ANT after statutory retention periods (typically 30 years for classified documents). Establishes the National Library of Tunisia (Bibliothèque Nationale de Tunisie — BNT), receiving legal deposit copies of all publications printed in Tunisia (2 copies of each work). Governs public library network (bibliothèques de lecture publique) in each governorate. The 2018 amendment introduced digital archiving standards and authorized digitization programs for rare manuscripts, especially the Zeitouna manuscript collection (one of the largest Islamic manuscript collections in North Africa).
Key provisions
  • Art. 1–8: National Archives mandate; ANT organizational structure under Ministry of Culture
  • Art. 9–16: Document retention schedules; mandatory transfer to ANT after retention period
  • Art. 17–22: Public access to archives: 30-year rule for ordinary documents; 60-year rule for sensitive documents; 100-year rule for personal data
  • Art. 23–28: Legal deposit obligation for all Tunisian publishers (books, periodicals, maps, audiovisual)
  • Art. 29–34: National Library structure; special collections (Arabo-Islamic manuscripts)
  • Art. 35–40: Public library network; municipal libraries under regional cultural authorities
  • 2018 amendment: Digital preservation strategy; mass digitization of fragile and rare items
TourismEducation

Tourism Investment Code — Law No. 90-21 of March 19, 1990Code des Investissements Touristiques (Run 172 addition)

Enacted: March 19, 1990 | In force: 1990 | Partially superseded: Investment Law No. 2016-71 (see Investment & Business section)
A wholly new standalone entry — a genuinely uncovered sector-specific investment statute, distinct from the general Tourism Organization Law (2012-49, licensing/classification) and the general Investment Law (2016-71, see Investment & Business section). While the 2016 law repealed the general 1993/1994 incentive codes it replaced, several tourism-specific articles of the 1990 code — Arts. 3, 5, 6, 7, and 8, covering state land allocation to hotel/tourist-residence projects, sector-specific project-approval procedures, and tourism-project financing conditions — remain explicitly in force alongside it, since the 2016 code did not expressly repeal them. One of the tracked pending bills in this section's Tourism Organization Law entry (Draft Law No. 93/2025's social-tourism quota) operates within this same sector-specific-incentive space rather than superseding it.
TourismFinancial

🏆 Sport & Youth

Sport Law — Organic Law No. 95-11 of February 6, 1995(as amended) (Run 44 correction)

Original: February 6, 1995 | Amended: reportedly 2004 and 2006 (grew from 24 to 28 articles)
Run 44 correction: prior runs cited an amendment by "Law No. 2014-17 of April 14, 2014" — no such law could be verified in any source searched, and it appears to have been a fabricated citation; drop it rather than repeat it. Multiple independent secondary sources (a university thesis on Tunisian sports associations, kapitalis.com) instead describe the law as "modified and supplemented on 3 occasions, once in 2004 and a second time in 2006," growing from 24 to 28 articles — the exact JORT numbers for these amendments could not be confirmed from available sources and should be verified against a primary text before being cited as fact. Governs the organization of sport in Tunisia: national sports federations (a federation groups specialized associations within one or more disciplines), sports clubs, ministerial model-statutes and dissolution power over mismanaged federation boards, minimum 20% of public subsidies earmarked for youth training, and the amateur/non-amateur (professional) practice framework. Complemented by Law No. 94-104 of August 3, 1994 (physical-education and sports-activity organization: national sports council, Comité Olympique Tunisien, school/university and disabled-sports federations), which itself replaced Law No. 84-63 of 1984. Tunisia adheres to the World Anti-Doping Code; the Tunisian Anti-Doping Agency (LTDLA) is the national anti-doping authority.
SportAdministrative

Youth Policy — Law No. 2002-51 of May 13, 2002 and National Youth Strategy 2022

Framework law: May 13, 2002 | National Youth Strategy: 2022
Framework governing youth policy, youth centers (maisons de jeunes — over 300 nationwide), summer camp programs, and youth civic engagement initiatives. The Ministry of Youth and Sports coordinates implementation. Tunisia has a young population (median age ~31); youth unemployment remains a structural challenge (30%+ rate for 15–24). The 2022 National Youth Strategy targets youth entrepreneurship, digital skills, combating irregular emigration ("harraga"), and volunteer service. The Startup Act (2018) is the principal legislative tool for youth entrepreneurship.
SportSocial

Anti-Doping Regulations — Law No. 2015-50 of December 21, 2015

Enacted: December 21, 2015 | In force: 2016
Establishes Tunisia's national anti-doping legal framework, implementing the World Anti-Doping Code (WADA). Creates the Tunisian Anti-Doping Control Agency (LTDLA — Laboratoire Tunisien de Contrôle du Dopage et des Analyses). Mandates testing programs for national and international competitions, out-of-competition testing, and therapeutic use exemptions (TUEs). Sets sanctions aligned with WADA rules: 2-year ban for first offense, 4 years for aggravated violation. Implements UNESCO International Convention against Doping in Sport (ratified 2006).
SportCriminal

Draft Organic Law on Sports Structures (pending)

Approved in principle: Council of Ministers, November 29, 2025 | Under review: ARP Education, Vocational Training, Scientific Research, Youth & Sports Commission (since March 5, 2026) | Status: not yet enacted as of July 2, 2026
A government-drafted organic law, reported at 211 articles across 7 chapters, that would repeal and replace the Sport Law (Organic Law No. 1995-11 of February 6, 1995, above). Presented to the Council of Ministers on November 29, 2025 by Youth and Sports Minister Sadok Mourali, under Prime Minister Sarra Zaafrani Zenzri, after revision based on inter-ministerial and comparative-law input. Stated objectives: democratizing governance of sports structures, guaranteeing transparency, modernizing sports-club management, and improving financing mechanisms and the sustainability of public sports services. Reported provisions include Art. 13 (limiting federation/club executive-bureau officers to three terms) and Art. 120 (authorizing conversion of sports clubs into professional sports companies). Under examination by the ARP's Education, Vocational Training, Scientific Research, Youth and Sports Commission since a March 5, 2026 session, with expert hearings on sports law and sports arbitration continuing into spring 2026. At a May 15, 2026 hearing, sports-law experts (Fethi Mouldi, Maher Snoussi) publicly warned the draft grants the supervising ministry excessive powers — including appointing members of clubs' ethics/governance boards and an approval right over international representation — that could conflict with international sports-body autonomy requirements (risking non-recognition of Tunisian federations) despite the bill's stated "independence" objective; concerns were also raised about compliance burdens on small amateur clubs. Update (Run 30): following further expert testimony (including the Tunisian Olympic Committee, the Paralympic Committee, and a sports-law association), the commission has moved past the hearing stage into drafting its committee report — a modest procedural step beyond the May 15 hearing checkpoint — though no committee vote or plenary date has been confirmed. Update (Run 343): a July 16, 2026 African Manager report confirms the commission is still in active examination, more than a year after the November 2025 Council of Ministers presentation, and adds two provisions not previously documented here: an anti-cumul-of-office rule restricting individuals from simultaneously holding leadership positions across multiple sports federations or clubs, and a decision to strip the bill of its disciplinary and dispute-resolution provisions entirely, on the rationale that internal discipline and dispute resolution are matters for the federations' own statutes rather than state organic law. No committee vote or plenary date has been confirmed. Not yet enacted as of this run. Update (Run 344): a fuller read of the same July 16, 2026 African Manager coverage clarifies the anti-cumul-of-office rule is not absolute: it carves out an explicit exception permitting an individual to simultaneously hold a sports-federation or club executive-bureau seat alongside a leadership position at the Tunisian Olympic Committee or Tunisian Paralympic Committee, reflecting those two bodies' distinct national-umbrella role rather than ordinary federation/club governance. No committee vote or plenary date confirmed. Update (Run 346): a July 16, 2026 African Manager report on the same commission session confirms the examination has moved on to candidacy-condition provisions for federation/club leadership roles — a "legal capacity" (capacité juridique) requirement, a minimum baccalaureate-level education requirement, a minimum management-experience threshold, and female-representation quota rules — corroborating and dating the single-source, undated Espace Manager lead that Run 345 had deliberately withheld for lack of verification. No committee vote or plenary date confirmed; not yet enacted as of this run. Update (Run 353): a July 16, 2026 committee session (chaired by Abderrazek Aouidet, with vice-president Mounir Kammouni and rapporteur Néjib Akremi) resolved one of the candidacy-condition sub-debates flagged at Run 346: on whether legally-resident foreigners could stand for sports-club and federation directing-committee seats, the majority position limits them to ordinary member status only, excluding them from the president or vice-president roles; the existing bar on holding the presidency of, or belonging to, more than one sports structure simultaneously was reaffirmed, alongside the Olympic/Paralympic Committee cumul exception already documented at Run 344. No committee vote or plenary date confirmed; not yet enacted as of this run.
SportPending

Proposition de loi n° 83/2025 (pending)Combating Violence in and around Stadiums

Filed: July 2025 | Under review: Education Commission (ARP) | Status: not yet enacted as of July 1, 2026
A parliamentary bill creating a dedicated criminal framework for violence at sporting events, filling a gap left by the Sport Law (No. 1995-11) and the general Penal Code. Covers acts of violence in stadiums, sports facilities, and surrounding gathering areas nationwide. Proposes: 6 months' to 5 years' imprisonment for acts of violence at sporting events; a minimum 5,000 TND fine for throwing projectiles or using flares/pyrotechnics; stadium bans of at least 5 years (life for repeat offenders); and 5 to 10 years' imprisonment for carrying or using weapons or dangerous objects at sports events. Would create a national committee to monitor stadium violence, with representatives from the Ministry of Youth and Sports, the Ministry of the Interior, the Tunisian Football Federation, civil-society associations, and supporters' groups. Under review by the ARP's Education Commission as of mid-2026; not yet enacted.
SportCriminalPending

🧭 Areas for Future Expansion

This artifact has been maintained across 486 research runs since late June 2026, each cross-checking Tunisian legal news, JORT publications, and ARP/CNRD proceedings against the existing content before adding anything, to avoid duplication. By this point the overwhelming majority of enacted Tunisian legislation across the 30 sections above is documented, and most runs now function as verification passes on a small “standing watchlist” of not-yet-resolved bills rather than discovery of new coverage gaps — detailed run-by-run history of that process is preserved in the versioned changelog entries below (this paragraph was itself trimmed at Run 369 after growing to roughly 38,000 characters of largely superseded run-by-run narrative through Run 311; nothing in it was unique — every finding it described is preserved, generally with more precision, either in a law-card entry's own inline “Update (Run N)” annotations or in the changelog list below). As of Run 446 (July 21, 2026), the single most time-sensitive open item tracked since Run 218 has been resolved: the 2026–2030 National Development Plan, adopted by both the ARP plenary (July 10, 2026, 64–24–15) and the CNRD (July 17, 2026, 56–6–5), was confirmed promulgated by President Kaïs Saïed as of July 20, 2026 (two independent sources — Réalités Magazine and webdo — reporting the same July 20 Carthage Palace meeting); the exact JORT issue/decree-number citation remains outstanding and is now the item's sole open follow-up. Two other items remain open, re-checked again through Run 446: the LTDH's appeal against its administrative suspension, adjourned by the Tunis Court of First Instance to July 29, 2026 (not yet due); and Deputy Fatma Mseddi's July 9, 2026 draft organic law to classify Ennahdha as a "terrorist organization," still not referred to a confirmed committee hearing date. The once-outstanding "two still-unnamed bills" caveat on the CPG/GCT loan-guarantees five-bill referral batch was in fact resolved back at Run 423 (this paragraph had simply gone unsynced with that update since) — all five bills are now numbered (CPG/EBRD: n° 49/2026; GCT/ITFC Mourabaha: n° 50/2026 and n° 51/2026; plus an Afreximbank loan-amendment bill and an FY2023 budget-closure bill), and the Finance and Budget Committee held an exceptional session on bills 49–51/2026 on July 21, 2026, closing with a call for a phosphate-sector reform strategy rather than a vote — see the standalone entry for the current, still-pending status (Run 453 correction). The pending-bill list below tracks the remaining watchlist items (Foreign Exchange Code, Personal Data Protection/AI, Audiovisual Communication, Investment Code, and others); consult each item's own most recent update for current status rather than assuming staleness.
  • The new Foreign Exchange Code (Draft Law 115/2025, 91 articles) — committee chair Maher Ktari has called continued Central Bank of Tunisia consultation "essential" before final adoption (per a June 23, 2026 report), with the BCT still not having formally responded as of that date; a July 1, 2026 report on the separate Development Plan debate found deputies still publicly urging "acceleration" of this Code's adoption, so Ktari's pre-recess target should be treated as unconfirmed rather than on track. The related exchange-violations amnesty bill (No. 58/2025 — see standalone entry) remains with the Finance Committee with reporting suggesting the two tracks are being harmonized; watch for whether they merge into a single text or advance separately.
  • (Updated Run 375) The 2026–2030 National Development Plan bill — see the standalone entry: adopted by both the ARP plenary (July 10, 2026, 64–24–15) and the CNRD (July 17, 2026, 56–6–5); presidential promulgation and JORT publication remain the only outstanding steps as of this run's cutoff — watch for both before treating the Plan as fully in force.
  • The pending Personal Data Protection & AI organic bill and the Associations Decree reform — both remain under active ARP Rights & Freedoms Commission review with no confirmed outcome to the lapsed June 24, 2026 report deadline; as of a June 4, 2026 update, the commission was still awaiting written ministry opinions on data protection, and on associations was awaiting government feedback with civil-society hearings still unscheduled — status essentially stalled since Run 38. Note: the Data Protection bill's article count is reported inconsistently across primary sources (132 vs. 123 articles) and remains unresolved — do not "correct" one figure to the other without a primary JORT/ARP text. Separately (Run 39 addition), see the civil-society-suspensions entry below for Amnesty International/HRW reporting on ~25 associations administratively suspended since mid-2025 under the still-unreformed 2011 decree.
  • The Draft Organic Law on Audiovisual Communication (99 articles, filed February 25, 2025, to replace HAICA) — no plenary movement confirmed; the May 20, 2026 jurisdictional dispute between the Rights & Freedoms Commission and the General Legislation Committee over which body hears the bill remains the last confirmed status, with no independent confirmation that the announced June 2026 hearings occurred.
  • The Investment Code reform bill — approved in principle by the Restricted Ministerial Council on December 31, 2025; the ARP Strategic Planning Commission (chaired by Saber Jelassi) remained at the article-by-article voting stage as of a May 31, 2026 report, with no committee vote or referral to plenary confirmed since. (Added Run 70) A July 3, 2026 IACE report separately classifies this reform as "validated by the Council of Ministers but never transmitted to the ARP" — possibly describing the government's own track as distinct from the committee-stage MP-initiated proposition n°26/2024 noted in the standalone entry; not yet reconciled.
  • A reported but largely unconfirmed new Code de l'eau (Water Code) replacement — its seven-chapter structure is now sourced to Minister Ezzeddine Ben Cheikh's February 25, 2025 Conseil ministériel restreint presentation (traced Run 51), but no draft has been filed with the ARP and no publicly available draft text exists as of this run; the 1975 Code (Law No. 75-16) remains the operative statute. (Sharpened Run 70) now corroborated across three July 3, 2026 sources as "validated by the Council of Ministers but never transmitted to the ARP" — an executive-stage bottleneck, not a drafting one.
  • (Added Run 70) The Hydrocarbons Code amendment (see standalone Law No. 99-93 entry) — examined by a restricted ministerial council February 7, 2025, renewed as an "urgent" priority by a June 26, 2026 press report amid declining production, and confirmed by the July 3, 2026 IACE report as "validated by the Council of Ministers but never transmitted to the ARP," alongside the Investment, Water, and Environment codes above.
  • The gambling/online-betting reform bills remain under General Legislation Committee review since a January 29, 2026 referral, with no confirmed timeline; the Tax Adviser Organic Law (proposition de loi organique n° 2023-013) showed incremental forward movement — a June 3–4, 2026 session was reported as the commission's "last examination session" on disputed articles — but no confirmed article-by-article vote conclusion or committee-report transmission has followed as of this run, so it remains formally not finalized.
  • The Proposition de loi n° 42/2024 (social-media sales cahier des charges, consumer-protection chapter, 3-day right of withdrawal) already documented under Digital/Commercial Law — a June 24, 2026 secondary source described the working group's text as reaching a "near-final" shape, but no committee vote or report is confirmed, so the cahier-des-charges-vs-electronic-declaration debate remains formally open; beyond it, no comprehensive general e-commerce statute has been identified.
  • CNAM / national health-insurance reform — the January 2026 tripartite payment-guarantee agreement and the July 1, 2026 pharmacist payment-commitment addressed successive liquidity flashpoints (see standalone entry) amid a ~TND 1.95 billion 2025 debt to public hospitals, but a structural reform bill (splitting social-service management from health-insurance management) has still not reached the ARP. (Superseded Run 309 — corrects a stale bullet left unsynced with the standalone entry's Run 255 update since Run 46) The private biologists' union's planned July 13, 2026 tiers-payant suspension was itself called off on July 10, 2026 after the union met the Minister of Social Affairs and CNAM, which committed to settling its outstanding debt to private laboratories within an agreed timeframe; billing has continued uninterrupted at private labs since, as with the July 1 pharmacist fix, a negotiated stopgap rather than the underlying structural-financing reform.
  • The Combating Financial Exclusion bill (Draft Law No. 023/2024, its number corrected in Run 43 — see standalone entry) and the related but separate "banque postale" bill (Proposition de loi n° 82/2025, now its own standalone entry) both remain temporized/under review as of June 28, 2026, confirmed unchanged; the Proposition de loi n° 83/2025 (stadium violence) and the nationality-code revision bill (n° 17/2026, 36 co-sponsors, filed by Deputy Syrine Mrabet, examined by committee May 7 and June 4, 2026) both remain under committee review with no vote scheduled and no progress reported since Run 38.
  • (Updated Run 311) The National Council for Tunisians Residing Abroad reform bill (n° 2024/064 — see Nationality section) — the ARP Foreign Affairs Committee moved from hearings to adopting amendments on July 14, 2026 (adding permanent diaspora-constituency deputy members plus 60 diaspora representatives to the Council's general assembly), still short of a completed committee report or plenary vote; and the cinema-industry bill (Proposition de loi n° 2024-03 — see Tourism/Culture section) — a June 24, 2026 session was described as "continuing the study" rather than a completed article-by-article finalization (corrected in Run 39 from an earlier overstatement), and still awaits its committee report. Both should be watched for referral to plenary.
  • The Unified Electoral Code proposal (261 articles/three parts, transmitted to committee May 7, 2026) — no movement beyond the May 19, 2026 hearing of the bill's 29 initiating deputies and the unresolved 5,000-dinar candidacy-deposit/education-level debate; note this is a distinct bill from the narrower September 27, 2024 ISIE dispute-jurisdiction amendment already enacted (see Electoral Law entry).
  • The Draft Organic Law on Sports Structures (see Sport & Youth section) — approved in principle by the Council of Ministers November 29, 2025, under ARP Education/Sports Commission review since March 5, 2026, continuing past the hearing stage into committee-report drafting (per the standalone entry's Run 30 update); no report completion or plenary vote confirmed since Run 38.
  • (Added Run 45) The Penal Code theft/robbery penalty amendment (Arts. 261/262/264 — see standalone Criminal Law entry) — in General Legislation Committee hearings since April 2026, opposed by the Syndicat des Magistrats Tunisiens as disproportionate; watch for a committee vote or report.
  • (Added Run 400) The Ennahdha "terrorist organization" designation bill (see standalone Security & Counter-terrorism entry) — a six-article draft organic law filed July 9, 2026 by Deputy Fatma Mseddi and nine co-sponsors; not yet referred to a confirmed committee hearing date as of this run. Watch for committee referral and, separately, whether the eighth conspiracy-cluster prosecution (Samir Abdelli, see standalone Judicial/Criminal entry) is appealed.
  • (Added Run 45) The Drug Law (No. 92-52) reform bill (see Narcotics entry) — a proposal balancing tougher trafficking enforcement with a therapeutic approach to consumption, in committee since April 2025 with STADD input as of May 2026; no vote confirmed. (Flagged Run 46) the bill's article count is reported inconsistently across sources (17 vs. 40 articles) — unresolved, do not pick one figure without a primary ARP/JORT text.
  • (Added Run 45) The construction-regularization bill updating Law No. 2015-11 (see Urban Planning Code entry) — approved in amended form by the ARP Strategic Planning/Urban Planning Committee on June 23, 2026; its registry number is still unconfirmed as of Run 46 and should be captured once reported.
  • (Added Run 46) The ONAT (Bar Association) — government standoff (see Judicial Statute entry) — the July 11, 2026 general assembly is confirmed as a strategy-review session, not an election; a separate prosecutorial challenge to the quorum/validity of an earlier lawyers' extraordinary general assembly was reported with a hearing set for June 10, 2026, outcome unconfirmed. Watch for the assembly's outcome and any government response to the June 18, 2026 general strike's demands.
  • (Added Run 94) The "Code des Énergies Renouvelables" (see Renewable Energy Law entry, Energy section) — a Council of Ministers meeting December 24, 2024 ordered its completion; transmitted to the ARP's competent committee April 1, 2026, containing a contested 51%-Tunisian-ownership rule for renewable-energy projects; named by a July 3, 2026 IACE report as one of three reforms (with the Foreign Exchange and Investment codes) it urges enacting by end of 2026. No committee vote or report confirmed since transmission.
  • (Added Run 99) Digital platform ("gig-economy") worker regulation (see standalone Labor Law entry) — no bill or draft text has been filed with the ARP; watch for a legislative response to UGTT's June 29, 2026 demands and the joint ILO governance-framework study announced by Employment Minister Issam Lahmar. Separately, watch for a Tunisian ratification bill for newly-adopted ILO Convention No. 193 (see standalone International Law & Treaties entry) — Tunisia voted in favor at the June 28, 2026 International Labour Conference but has not yet filed domestic ratification legislation.
  • (Confirmed Run 114) Runs 110 through 114 have each re-checked the full watchlist within hours of one another (same calendar day) and found no movement on any item — this is expected given the short intervals, not a sign the watchlist is stale. The four dated triggers (July 6, 8, 11, 13, 2026) remain the points at which real status changes are most likely to appear; re-running the identical same-day sweep between now and the first of those dates has low marginal value. The next run should prioritize checking whether July 6 (Foreign Exchange Code committee meeting) has actually passed before repeating this sweep.
  • (Added Run 115) The Proposition de loi n° 30/2026 (see standalone Social Security & Protection entry) — ending the cumul-emploi-retraite ban and granting retiree transport/tourism tariff reductions — remains under ARP Health/Social Affairs Commission review (last confirmed session May 25, 2026) with an unresolved Article 5 debate over whether full statutory repeal is needed; watch for a committee vote or report, and for whether the bill's own tariff percentages (reported inconsistently as 30%/40% for transport and 40%/"40–50%" for tourism) are settled in a later text.
  • (Added Run 117) The July 6, 2026 Foreign Exchange Code committee-meeting trigger date has now arrived, but no source confirms whether a session actually occurred or what its outcome was; the last hard data point remains chair Maher Ktari's ~90%-complete hearing count as of June 17, 2026 with an internally-targeted (unconfirmed) article-by-article vote window of June 20–30, 2026. The next run should search specifically for post-July-6 reporting on this committee — not just re-run the standard sweep — since this is the first of the four dated triggers to actually pass.
  • (Added Run 118) A new, fifth dated trigger to watch: ARP Finance Commission president Maher Ktari's June 9, 2026 statement that a supplementary/"rectificative" finance law for 2026 is now "inevitable" in September–October 2026 (see the Finance Law 2026 entry's pending-amendment note), driven by the gap between the enacted law's USD 63.3/barrel oil-price assumption and actual prices near USD 110/barrel. No amending bill has been filed yet; watch for the scheduled July 2026 Finance Ministry meeting on the discrepancy and for any move from "inevitable" rhetoric to an actual ARP-filed text.
  • (Confirmed Run 119) The July 6, 2026 Foreign Exchange Code trigger has now technically passed with no reported vote or session outcome from any source — the commission was still working the file as of a June 23, 2026 status report. Independent economist Ridha Chkoundali corroborates Ktari's oil-shock financing-gap figures on the supplementary finance law item. The July 11 ONAT general assembly could not be independently re-corroborated this run (only May 2026 events surfaced in the sweep) — not a retraction of the existing Run 46 sourcing, but flag for fresh confirmation alongside its outcome next run.
  • (Added Run 119) Consumer-protection, telecom, and public-private-partnership regulation — a rotating gap-scan found no active 2026 reform bill in any of these three areas beyond the existing PPP Law No. 2015-49 and e-commerce entries; flagged as a standing candidate for a future dedicated gap-scan rather than padded with unverifiable content now.
  • (Added Run 120) The Proposition de loi n° 42/2025 amending Article 42 of the Internal Security Force Work-Accident Compensation Law (see standalone Security & Counter-terrorism entry, Law No. 2013-50) — finalized by the Defense, Security and Armed Forces Commission per a July 1, 2026 report and transmitted to the ARP Bureau; watch for the study session and plenary scheduling that should follow.
  • (Confirmed Run 121) The July 6, 2026 Foreign Exchange Code trigger has now technically passed a second time with still no reported committee vote, session outcome, or plenary referral from any source since the ~90%-hearings-complete, June 17–23, 2026 status quo. The Personal Data Protection bill's 123-vs-132 article-count conflict was re-investigated and remains genuinely unresolved at the primary-source level (not merely under-researched) — future runs should not "resolve" it toward either figure without a primary JORT/ARP text, since the same handful of secondary press accounts recur regardless of how many independent searches are run.
  • (Confirmed Run 122) The July 6, 2026 Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unconfirmed a third time — no outlet has yet reported whether the session occurred. A specific claim that the Constitutional Court bill (Organic Law Proposal No. 100/2025) had a March 27, 2026 Bar Association hearing was tested again via direct source fetch and independent search and again could not be corroborated, reproducing Run 62's identical negative result on the same claim — future runs encountering this specific "March 27" detail should treat it as an already twice-tested, uncorroborated lead rather than re-investigating from scratch. A "29-article ISIE reform bill (July 2025, 14 deputies)" lead was traced to a garbled restatement of the already-documented 261-article Unified Electoral Code proposal, not a distinct text — a new false-positive pattern for future runs to watch for.
  • (Confirmed Run 123) The July 6, 2026 Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unconfirmed a fourth time — same-day fresh searches surfaced no report of the Monday session's occurrence or result beyond Ktari's own June 17, 2026 statements. (Added Run 123) An unresolved tension between two accounts of Tunisia's access-to-information/data-protection institutions was surfaced and deliberately left flagged rather than silently reconciled: a December 1, 2025 report describes INAI as merely "operating without a president and deputy" as of an executive reform announcement, which conflicts with the multiply-sourced (ARTICLE19, RSF, Jeune Afrique, SNJT) finding already in this artifact that INAI was fully dissolved and its offices closed in August 2025 — the August 2025 closure is treated as authoritative pending any source that reconciles the two; the same report's INPDP finding (governing council mandate lapsed since April 16, 2024) was independently corroborated and added to the Personal Data Protection entry without this conflict.
  • (Confirmed Run 125) The July 6, 2026 Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unconfirmed a fifth time — the newest available reporting is still the June 23, 2026 status update. A check for an announced plenary-session date for the 2026–2030 Development Plan bill (committee review concluded July 2, per Run 71) found none yet scheduled. A false-positive duplicate-id alarm ("renewables-law" appearing twice under a naive grep) was traced to a changelog prose reference rather than a second card — future runs re-running the duplicate-id check should filter matches inside <code>-quoted changelog text.
  • (Confirmed Run 126) The July 6, 2026 Foreign Exchange Code committee session — the trigger date itself — has now arrived with still no source reporting whether it occurred or its outcome; treat as unresolved until a post-July-6 report surfaces. A "Projet de loi n° 2024/036 relatif à la cybersécurité" (single-sourced, dated December 2024, provisions overlapping the enacted Decree-Law No. 2023-17 cybersecurity framework) was investigated and deliberately not added — its relationship to the existing framework (superseded draft vs. distinct parallel organic-law track) is unresolved at the primary-source level; future runs should treat this bill number as an already-investigated open question rather than a fresh lead. The July 11 ONAT general assembly and July 13 CNAM/biologists' tiers-payant suspension are now the nearest-term triggers (5–7 days out) and should be the first check for the next run.
  • (Confirmed Run 128) The July 6, 2026 Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unconfirmed a third time since the trigger date arrived — no outlet has yet reported whether Monday's session occurred, beyond a July 3, 2026 IACE commentary re-urging its promulgation before end-2026 (framing, not status). The July 11 ONAT general assembly could not be freshly re-corroborated this run either (a second consecutive miss, after Run 119) — treat this as a standing gap to close the moment any July-dated ONAT source appears, not a sign the underlying Run 46 sourcing was wrong. July 8 and July 13 remain confirmed on track.
  • (Confirmed Run 129) The July 6, 2026 Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unconfirmed a fourth time since the trigger date arrived — same-day reporting lag, not evidence either way; last hard data point is still Ktari's ~90%-hearings-complete status of June 17, 2026. A ten-area structural gap-scan chosen specifically to cover ground no prior run's gap-scan list had named (cinema/audiovisual code, national archives law, heritage/antiquities code, waste-management code, cooperatives law, weapons law, casino/gambling law, veterinary pharmacy law, organ-donation law, mental-health law) found nine of the ten already covered by existing entries and one (a unified modern cooperatives statute) genuinely ambiguous at the primary-source level — flagged here as a standing low-confidence item rather than asserted as a gap, since only scattered Code des Obligations et des Contrats-era provisions could be located. July 8, 11, and 13 remain the next concrete triggers, in that order.
  • (Confirmed Run 130) The July 6, 2026 Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unconfirmed a fifth time since the trigger date arrived — same-day reporting lag persists. A ten-area gap-scan (space/satellite law, cryptocurrency, drone/UAV regulation, genetic-testing/bioethics, surrogacy/ART, franchise law, PPP law, renewable-hydrogen strategy, cybersecurity incident-reporting, consumer-credit/microcredit) found eight of the ten already covered or non-existent as Tunisian law, and closed two genuine refinement gaps: a pending civil drone (UAV) regulatory decree (Civil Aviation Ministry/Ministry of Defense, draft complete, stakeholder review as of November 2025 — see Civil Aviation Code entry) distinct from the already-enacted agricultural-drone carve-out (Finance Law 2026 Art. 135, 60–29–14 vote, December 4, 2025 — now cited precisely in that entry), and the Ministry of Industry's May 2024 "H2Vert.TUN" green-hydrogen national strategy (policy roadmap only, no law — added to the Renewable Energy Law entry). Note for future runs: do not re-add genetic-testing, surrogacy, franchise, PPP, or consumer-credit as "gaps" — each was independently re-confirmed already covered by an existing entry this run (Bioethics Law 2015-31; Franchise Law 2009-69; PPP Law 2015-49; Consumer Credit/Household Over-Indebtedness entry) despite fresh research surfacing them as candidates.
  • (Confirmed Run 131) The July 6, 2026 Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unconfirmed a sixth time; the July 11 ONAT general assembly could not be freshly corroborated a third time (after Runs 119 and 128) — treat as a standing sourcing gap to close the moment any July-dated ONAT report appears, not a retraction of the Run 46 finding. This run's WebSearch tool itself was degraded (roughly half of all queries returned "unavailable," on top of Tavily's continued HTTP 402 outage) — future runs hitting the same degradation should retry failed queries rather than treat empty results as confirmation of "no news." A check of domestic/household workers ("travailleuses domestiques") confirmed this remains a genuine, undocumented-because-nonexistent gap in Tunisian statute (general Labor Code coverage only, verbal contracts permitted, no dedicated law or pending bill) rather than a research omission — do not add a fabricated standalone entry for this topic absent an actual bill or statute to cite.
  • (Confirmed Run 132) The July 6, 2026 Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome — the trigger date itself, arriving for the second time (the first being Run 126/128's calendar day) — remains unreported by any outlet as of this run's same-day cutoff; same-day reporting lag, not evidence either way. Tavily remained dead (HTTP 402) this run too. A fresh sweep re-confirmed the Mabrouk/Chahed/Ben Gharbia, Turki, Mekacher, and Haouas July 2026 rulings already fully captured at equal or greater precision (Run 81/85/92 updates) — none re-added. One genuinely new item entered the specialized financial-corruption chamber's docket: businessman Maher Chaâbane and a dozen-plus co-defendants had their trial postponed to September 24, 2026 (WebManagerCenter/Directinfo, July 6, 2026) — no verdict yet, so (consistent with this project's practice of only creating standalone entries once a verdict or enacted text exists) it is flagged here rather than added as a card; watch for the September hearing. A single-sourced, internally conflicting claim that Constitutional Court organic law Proposal No. 100/2025 had lost half its ten signatories was investigated and left unresolved — it conflicts with the committee-hearing continuation already documented (Run 76's Feb 18, 2026 hearing) and no primary ARP source could confirm a withdrawal; treat as unconfirmed, not as a status change. The July 11 and 13, 2026 trigger dates remain the nearest concrete checkpoints for the next run.
  • (Confirmed Run 133) The July 6, 2026 Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported a third consecutive pass since the trigger date arrived; all seven other watchlist items (ONAT assembly, CNAM/biologists suspension, Development Plan bill, Investment/Water/Hydrocarbons/Environment codes' ARP-transmission status, Chaâbane trial, Constitutional Court Proposal 100/2025) were independently re-confirmed unchanged. Closed a genuine gap outside the watchlist: a wholly new standalone Shared-Time Tourist Accommodation Law (No. 2008-33 of May 13, 2008) entry, missed by all 132 prior runs. Also newly tracked: a ten-deputy sovereign natural-resources fund bill (filed ~January 2026, examined by parliamentary commission April 15, 2026) remains at committee stage, not yet enacted — watch for a committee vote or plenary referral. A July 6, 2026 CGA insurance-regulator "2026–2030 roadmap" press item describing a future "progressive obligation" for agricultural insurance was investigated and correctly excluded as a policy announcement rather than enacted law.
  • (Confirmed Run 134) The July 6, 2026 Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported an eighth consecutive trigger-date pass; the Maher Chaâbane trial's September 24, 2026 postponement was independently re-confirmed as formally decided on July 6, 2026 itself, matching Run 132's expectation exactly. Corrected a factual error found via cross-agent verification rather than a coverage gap: the Military Justice Code entry's "Law No. 86-79 of 1986" citation and its "2019 civilian-jurisdiction restriction" claim were both unverifiable and are now replaced with the code's real origin (Decree No. 57-9 of January 10, 1957) and amendment chain, including a real, previously-missed 2024 amendment (Loi n° 2024-35). Note for future runs: this is the second time a prior run's "already covered, no gap" verdict on a topic (Run 133's military-justice gap-scan) concealed a citation error rather than confirming accuracy — when a gap-scan candidate turns out to already have an entry, spot-checking that entry's specific citations against a fresh independent search is worth the extra step, not just confirming the entry's existence. July 11 and 13, 2026 remain the nearest concrete triggers for the next run.
  • (Confirmed Run 135) The July 6, 2026 Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported a ninth consecutive trigger-date pass. New false-positive trap for future runs: a "Conseil de gouvernement"-adopted June 25, 2026 reform of "Law 08-12" governing the medical profession, sourced to medias24.com/lavieeco.com/leseco.ma, is Moroccan legislation, not Tunisian — Morocco's weekly cabinet body and its own Ordre National des Médecins statute — a new country-mislabeling trap parallel to the existing Algeria/Saïd Sayoud party-financing trap (Run 84/74); treat any "Law 08-12"/"Conseil de gouvernement" medical-profession lead as this same trap rather than re-investigating. A ten-area gap-scan (posted-workers agreements, medical- and pharmacy-profession statutes, e-waste law, data-localization rules, student-loan financing law, VTC/ride-hailing regulation, temporary-work-agency regulation, consumer-arbitration/ombudsman law, SMIG/SMAG mechanism) found all ten already covered or confirmed to have no distinct Tunisian statute. July 11 and 13, 2026 remain the nearest concrete triggers for the next run.
  • (Confirmed Run 136) The July 6, 2026 Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported a tenth consecutive trigger-date pass; the Chaâbane trial's September 24, 2026 postponement, the July 13 CNAM/biologists suspension, and the July 11 ONAT assembly (still uncorroborated with a July-dated source) were each independently re-checked with no status change. An eleven-area gap-scan (disability rights, child protection, blood transfusion, tobacco control, food safety, rail transport, postal services, housing, condominium/co-ownership, civil protection, refugee/asylum) found all eleven already covered by existing entries. Tavily remained down (HTTP 402) this run; WebSearch was used instead.
  • (Confirmed Run 137) The July 6, 2026 Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported an eleventh consecutive trigger-date pass — same-day reporting lag, not evidence either way. The July 11 ONAT assembly gained a single-source (Réalités Magazine) date reconfirmation but no new detail. The Cinema and Cultural Industries entry's pending-reform note gained a cautiously-worded age-classification detail while a conflicting predecessor-statute-number claim was deliberately withheld pending primary-source resolution — flag this specific "Law 19/1960" / "Decree-Law 86/2011" citation as an open question for a future run rather than re-investigating from scratch. New false-positive trap: web searches for "lanceurs d'alerte" and "Code de l'environnement" heavily surface French (not Tunisian) legislative content — filter by country before treating any hit as a Tunisian development, the same discipline already applied to the Algeria/Saïd Sayoud and Morocco/Law-08-12 traps. July 11 and 13, 2026 remain the nearest concrete triggers for the next run.
  • (Confirmed Run 138) The July 6, 2026 Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported a twelfth consecutive trigger-date pass, roughly an hour after Run 137's own same-day check — genuinely too short an interval for new Tunisian outlet reporting to appear, not evidence of stalling. The only fresh, verifiable, dated item this run's sweep surfaced was the July 1/6, 2026 Ministry of Higher Education response on physician diploma-equivalence (see the Medical Professions Organization entry's Run 138 update) — an implementing-regulation clarification, not primary legislation. July 11 (ONAT) and July 13 (CNAM/biologists) remain the nearest concrete triggers; future runs arriving within an hour or two of the prior run's cutoff should prioritize checking those two dated triggers directly over re-running the full same-day watchlist sweep, which has shown near-zero marginal yield across a dozen consecutive same-day passes.
  • (Confirmed Run 139) The July 6, 2026 Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported a thirteenth consecutive trigger-date pass; the July 11 and 13, 2026 triggers themselves had still not arrived as of this run's same-day cutoff, so no update to either is expected until a run lands after those dates. This run's genuine contribution was outside the pending-bill watchlist entirely: the Insurance Code entry's Fonds de Garantie des Victimes des Accidents de la Circulation — the traffic-accident-victim compensation fund underlying the still-pending cap-raising bill already tracked there — had never been documented itself, nor its January 1, 2025 restructuring into a special Treasury account under Finance Law 2025 Art. 18 (repealing Insurance Code Arts. 172/174–176). Future runs should not re-flag this fund as a gap; it is now fully cited. The next run landing before July 11 should likewise expect a same-day, no-movement watchlist result and prioritize a fresh structural gap-scan (in an area not yet covered by the dozen-plus prior scans) over re-running the identical pending-bill sweep.
  • (Confirmed Run 140) The July 6, 2026 Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported a fourteenth consecutive trigger-date pass; July 11 and 13, 2026 still had not arrived as of this run's cutoff. With pending-bill sweeps and structural gap-scans now showing near-zero marginal yield across roughly twenty combined passes, this run instead spot-checked citation accuracy on five older entries never individually re-verified since addition — four (National Standardization System Law 2009-38, Public Enterprises & Privatization Law 89-9, Debt-Recovery Companies Law 98-4, Passports & Travel Documents Law 75-40) confirmed exact matches, but the Commercial Register Law entry was found to cite a since-repealed statute (Law No. 95-44 of 1995) rather than its actual current successor (Law No. 2018-52 of October 29, 2018, creating the RNE) — now corrected. Future runs should prioritize (1) checking July 11 and July 13 for actual outcomes the moment either date passes, over re-running the exhausted same-day sweep, and (2) continuing a rolling citation spot-check of early-run (Run 1–70) additions, since this "real but superseded citation" error class appears distinct from — and likely more common than — the outright-fabrication class Run 134 found, and no systematic pass for it has been run project-wide.
  • (Confirmed Run 141) The July 6, 2026 Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported a fifteenth consecutive trigger-date pass; July 11 and 13, 2026 still had not arrived as of this run's cutoff (only days away now). A second five-entry citation spot-check (Narcotics Law 92-52, Anti-Human Trafficking Law 2016-61, Prison/Penitentiary Law 2001-52, Racial Discrimination Law 2018-50, Private International Law 98-97) found all five accurate and currently in force — no repeat of Run 140's superseded-citation finding this round. This run's genuine yield came from three enforcement/implementation-level developments under already-documented statutes: the Competition and Prices Law entry gained the Conseil de la Concurrence's December 31, 2025 fines (TND 4.2M combined, reported January 2026); the Renewable Energy Law entry gained the Round 6 solar-authorization call (200 MW, April 15 – June 15, 2026 application window); and the 2026–2030 National Development Plan entry — untouched since Run 71 — gained deputy Issam Chouchane's July 6, 2026 public criticism. A secondary source's claim that the Personal Data Protection/AI bill enters into force July 11, 2026 could not be corroborated against any primary text and was deliberately withheld pending confirmation. Future runs should continue the rolling early-run citation spot-check (roughly 15 of ~120 Run 1–70 additions now individually re-verified across Runs 140–141) and prioritize checking July 11/13 for actual outcomes the moment either date passes.
  • (Confirmed Run 146) The Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported a twenty-third consecutive trigger-date pass, and July 11/13, 2026 still had not arrived as of this run's cutoff. Rather than re-running the exhausted same-day sweep, this run traced two external leads to ground: a search-engine summary conflating a stale 2024 Tunisian Council of Ministers session with unrelated French (elysee.fr/info.gouv.fr) government content (a new variant of the stale-date/cross-country conflation trap), and an adams.africa legal blog's inaccurate claim that the pending copyright/Marrakesh-Treaty bill (No. 047/2024) was "approved" May 12, 2026 — actually the unrelated Status of the Artist Law's adoption date. Both were correctly withheld. A citation spot-check corrected the SICAR Law entry, which had cited only its 1995 amendment as if it were the foundational statute rather than the true base law (88-92 of 1988). Future runs should continue the rolling citation spot-check of early-run additions and prioritize checking July 11/13 for actual outcomes the moment either date passes.
  • (Confirmed Run 147) This run's designated branch held no PR history and was checked out at a stale v2.6-era baseline (July 2026-dated commits topping out at 6 entries/v2.6) while origin/main had independently progressed through 146 merged runs to v4.146 (273 entries); confirmed via git log origin/main and an empty search_pull_requests result for the branch, and reset directly to current origin/main before drafting — the same recurring stale-checkout failure mode documented throughout this changelog. A fresh sweep re-confirmed the standing watchlist: the Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported a twenty-fourth consecutive trigger-date pass (Ktari's ~90%-hearings-complete, BCT-non-responsive status as of mid-June 2026 is still the last hard data point); the Personal Data Protection/AI bill's "enforcement began July 11, 2026" claim resurfaced from the same dpo-consulting.com/regulations.ai marketing sources and was again independently rejected as uncorroborated (primary Tunisian outlets — businessnews.com.tn, WMC, africanmanager — still show the bill in ARP Rights & Freedoms Commission hearings as of May 2026, with no plenary vote); and the July 11, 2026 "ONAT general assembly" item's Run 145 sourcing concern (unconfirmable against any of the three plausible ONAT bodies) could not be resolved either way this run and remains flagged as an open sourcing question. July 11 and 13, 2026 had still not arrived as of this run's cutoff. This run's genuine contribution was a new standalone entry entirely outside the pending-bill watchlist: Proposition de loi n° 74/2024 (see the new Animal Protection and Domestic-Carnivore Regulation entry, Agriculture & Fisheries section) — filed by Deputy Chokri Bahri on July 23, 2024 and examined by the ARP Agriculture, Food Security & Maritime Fishing Commission on May 5, 2026, independently corroborated via Kapitalis (July 26, 2024 filing report) and two businessnews.com.tn articles (both May 6, 2026, on the committee session). Prior runs' "veterinary/animal health" gap-scans (Runs 82, 113, 116) had confirmed only the general Veterinary Law (No. 2005-35) as covering animal health, without distinguishing this separate companion-animal-welfare/stray-dog legislative track — a genuine gap that survived at least three prior targeted scans of adjacent terrain. One new standalone entry added (274 total); no existing entries required revision. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 274 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, and the header stat, footer version string, and page title all synchronized to v4.147/274. Future runs should continue the rolling citation spot-check of early-run additions and prioritize checking July 11/13 for actual outcomes — and the ONAT sourcing question — the moment either date passes.
  • (Confirmed Run 148) The Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported a twenty-fifth consecutive trigger-date pass since July 6, 2026; the July 8 (graduate-recruitment protest), July 11 (ONAT assembly), and July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant suspension) trigger dates had each still not arrived as of this run's same-day cutoff — all three are now imminent and should be the first check for the next run landing after any of them. Three fresh-search candidate leads (a "conseils locaux et régionaux" law-adoption headline, a public-sector "recrutements exceptionnels" signature headline, and the July 5 plastic-bag bill) were each traced to already-documented material rather than new developments — see the v4.148 changelog note above for the specific stale-date/already-covered resolution of each. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required correction (274 total remains accurate).
  • (Confirmed Run 149) This run again hit the stale-branch failure mode (see the v4.149 changelog note above) and, after resetting to origin/main, closed one genuine gap missed by all 148 prior runs: the "Instalingo" case, a fourth distinct conspiracy-cluster prosecution (41 defendants, Feb. 5, 2025 first-instance verdict, Jan. 14, 2026 appeal upholding Ghannouchi's 22-year sentence), added as a Run 149 extension to the consolidated "State Security Conspiracy" Prosecutions entry. The July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 trigger dates remain imminent and are the first check for the next run landing after any of them.
  • (Confirmed Run 150) The Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported a twenty-sixth consecutive trigger-date pass since July 6, 2026 — no outlet has confirmed whether that session occurred or its result; Ktari's ~90%-hearings-complete, BCT-non-responsive status of mid-June 2026 is still the last hard data point. A direct check of jort.tn found no July 2026 law/decree publications, only routine "Annonces Légales" numbering (issues 076–078). The July 8 (graduate-recruitment protest), July 11 (ONAT assembly), and July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant suspension) trigger dates were all reconfirmed as still scheduled but not yet arrived as of this same-day cutoff — these remain the first check for the next run landing after any of them. Two already-flagged false-positive traps resurfaced and were again correctly withheld: the "conseils locaux et régionaux" law headline (still its actual Feb. 27, 2025 vote/March 13, 2025 JORT publication, a database-crawl-timestamp trap) and the dpo-consulting.com "Personal Data Protection enforcement begins July 11, 2026" marketing claim (still uncorroborated by any primary source). No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required correction (274 total remains accurate).
  • (Confirmed Run 151) The Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported a twenty-seventh consecutive trigger-date pass since July 6, 2026. The July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 trigger dates were all reconfirmed as still scheduled but not yet arrived as of this same-day cutoff. A fresh sweep also surfaced a discrepancy worth flagging for reconciliation rather than silent resolution: the Investment Code's ARP committee-track status ("article-by-article voting phase," per the standalone entry) and the Run 70 IACE finding that the same reform is "validated by the Council of Ministers but never transmitted to the ARP" continue to read as describing two distinct tracks (executive-drafted vs. MP-initiated proposition n°26/2024) rather than a contradiction — no primary source yet reconciles them, so both are left standing as previously documented. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required correction (274 total remains accurate).
  • (Added Run 152) The Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported a twenty-eighth consecutive trigger-date pass; the July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 trigger dates still had not arrived as of this run's cutoff (roughly one hour after Run 151's). This run's genuine contribution was outside the pending-bill watchlist: a new standalone Affaire Bilel Chaouachi entry (Security & Counter-terrorism section) documenting a jihadist leader's 55-year terrorism sentence, confirmed on appeal July 6, 2026 — a fourth distinct Anti-Terrorism Law (Organic Law 2015-26) prosecution track alongside the state-security-conspiracy, Secret Apparatus, and Instalingo cases already documented. One new standalone entry added (275 total).
  • (Confirmed Run 153) The Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported a twenty-ninth consecutive trigger-date pass; the July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 trigger dates still had not arrived as of this run's cutoff. This run enriched two existing rights-defender entries (Bensedrine/Krichi in the State Security Conspiracy Prosecutions entry; Mosbah and five Mnemty co-defendants in the Racial Discrimination Law entry) via a July 7, 2026 Human Rights Watch report — see the v4.153 changelog note above for full detail. New false-positive trap for future runs: a "July 8" mass Ennahda/opposition sentencing (21 of 24 defendants, 12–35 years) surfaced by a fresh search is the already-documented July 8, 2025 Case 2 first-instance verdict with its year dropped by a search-engine summary, not a new 2026 event — treat any bare "July 8" reference to this case as this same trap rather than re-investigating. No new standalone entries were added (275 total remains accurate).
  • (Confirmed Run 154) This run again hit the stale-branch failure mode (an initial drafting pass was mistakenly started against the v2.6-era fork before being caught and discarded — see the v4.154 changelog note above). After resetting to origin/main, the Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome was reconfirmed unreported a thirtieth consecutive trigger-date pass — the only July 6, 2026 hit found was a deputy's floor remarks urging faster passage, not a reported committee vote. No new JORT publication, watchlist-item movement, or court ruling newer than Run 153's July 7, 2026 Human Rights Watch report was found. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required correction (275 total remains accurate). The July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 trigger dates remain the highest-priority items for the next run to check the moment each passes.
  • (Added Run 155) The Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported a thirty-first consecutive trigger-date pass; the July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 trigger dates still had not arrived as of this run's cutoff. This run's genuine contributions: the previously-pending Ajmi Lourimi/Mossaâb Gharbi terrorism-chamber case (State Security Conspiracy Prosecutions entry) was updated with its July 4, 2026 verdict (3 years each), and a new standalone Affaire "Créateurs de Contenu" entry (Security & Counter-terrorism section) documents an 18-defendant, 1–27-year mass sentencing of TikTok/Instagram content creators on July 6, 2026. One new standalone entry added (276 total).
  • (Confirmed Run 156) The Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported a thirty-second consecutive trigger-date pass; the July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 trigger dates still had not arrived as of this run's cutoff. This run closed a standing sourcing question rather than a substantive gap: the July 11 ONAT general assembly — independently unconfirmable to Runs 119, 128, 131, 145, and 147 — is now corroborated by a June 30, 2026 Réalités Magazine article (see the Judicial Statute entry's Run 156 update). A "graduate-recruitment bill approved" headline and a "zero paper" digitalization lead were both traced to already-documented material (Law No. 2025-18 and the Electronic Procedures entry's Run 111 update, respectively). No new standalone entries were added (276 total remains accurate).
  • (Confirmed Run 158) The Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported (Run 157 did not re-check this item; this run's fresh search found no post-July-6, 2026 committee session outcome either, extending the no-movement streak Run 156 last confirmed at its thirty-second consecutive pass); the July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 trigger dates still had not arrived as of this run's cutoff. This run closed a narrow implementation-level gap instead: the Maritime Ports Code entry gained the Skhira commercial port's 18-year-old OMMP/TRAPSA concession impasse and the Transport Ministry's July 6, 2026 regularization measures, previously undocumented across 157 prior runs. No new standalone entries were added (276 total remains accurate).
  • (Added Run 159) The Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported; the July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 trigger dates still had not arrived as of this run's cutoff, and a dedicated research pass found no watchlist-item status change beyond what Run 158 already captured. This run's genuine yield was two structural gaps entirely outside the pending-bill watchlist, both verified against primary/near-primary sources: the Private Security, Guarding & Valuables-Transport Activities Law (No. 2002-81/2002) and the Telemedicine framework (Presidential Decree No. 2022-318/2022, supplemented by June 2024 ministerial orders) — both areas of everyday regulated activity this project had never documented despite 158 prior runs' gap-scans. A candidate French decree (2024-164) was correctly excluded after direct-source verification revealed it governs French, not Tunisian, teleconsultation companies. Future runs should continue prioritizing the July 8/11/13 trigger dates the moment each passes, and consider further "everyday regulated activity" gap-scans (e.g., private detective/investigation licensing, funeral/burial services regulation, taxi-meter/fare regulation) as a productive vein alongside the pending-bill watchlist, since both new entries this run came from that category rather than from politics/legislation news.
  • (Confirmed/enriched Run 160) The Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported; the July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 trigger dates still had not arrived as of this run's cutoff. Of Run 159's three suggested next-vein leads, two (funeral-services regulation, taxi-meter tariffs) surfaced no citable dedicated Tunisian statute — both are administrative/decree-level, not standalone codes — but the third, private-detective/investigation licensing, resolved into a genuine enrichment rather than a dead end: Article 15 (and the related Article 16) of the Private Security Law (No. 2002-81, added Run 159) was fetched directly from legislation-securite.tn and added to that entry as the specific legal basis, previously undocumented, for Tunisia's prohibition on private-investigation activity. No new standalone entries were added (278 total remains accurate); one existing entry (Private Security Law) enriched. Future runs should continue prioritizing the July 8/11/13 trigger dates the moment each passes.
  • (Corrected Run 161) The Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported; the July 8, 11, and 13, 2026 trigger dates still had not arrived as of this run's cutoff. A thirteen-topic "everyday regulated activity" gap-scan closed no new standalone gaps — twelve of thirteen topics checked were already covered by existing entries or exist only at decree/administrative level (credit-rating agencies confirmed as a genuine legislative void, not a citable gap; elderly care homes traced to the already-existing Elderly Persons — Rights and Protection entry, Law No. 1994-2). This run's genuine yield came from a citation spot-check instead: the Judicial Officers (Huissiers de Justice) entry's long-standing "Law No. 66-49 of 1966" citation was found, via direct primary-source verification, to reference a law that does not exist — corrected to the real governing statute, Organic Law No. 2018-9 of January 30, 2018 (successor to Law No. 95-29 of 1995). Future runs should treat "Law No. 66-49" as a closed, resolved error rather than re-investigating, and should continue both the rolling early-run citation spot-check and prioritizing the July 8/11/13 trigger dates the moment each passes.
  • (Confirmed Run 162) The July 8, 2026 trigger date has now arrived, but same-day indexing lag left the graduate-recruitment protest's outcome (and whether implementing decrees for Law No. 2025-18 were published) unconfirmed — the next run should check this first. The Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported; July 11 (ONAT) and July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant) had not yet arrived as of this run's cutoff. A ten-topic economic/regulatory gap-scan (Environmental Code, Water Code, Data Protection Law, Competition Law, Telecommunications Code, e-invoicing, Investment Code, renewable-energy self-production, Hydrocarbons Code, Constitutional Court organic law) found all ten already covered by existing entries or correctly excluded as still-unenacted drafts — no genuine gaps. A viral false claim of a new Road Traffic Code entering into force July 1, 2026 was investigated and correctly excluded. No new standalone entries were added (278 total remains accurate). Future runs should prioritize same-day confirmation of the July 8 decree-publication status, then July 11 and July 13.
  • (Added Run 163) The July 8, 2026 graduate-recruitment protest/Law No. 2025-18 decree-publication outcome remains unconfirmed by same-day reporting; July 11 (ONAT) and July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant) still had not arrived as of this run's cutoff. This run's genuine yield came from naming specific "everyday regulated activity" statutes rather than assuming coverage by a broader entry — a technique that closed two gaps missed by prior scans: a new standalone Sales Techniques and Commercial Advertising Law (No. 98-40/1998) entry (Commercial Law) and a new standalone Railway Law entry (Transport) covering SNCFT's three founding/governing statutes (Laws No. 69-31/1969, 98-74/1998, 98-90/1998) — the latter specifically survived the Run 136/140 "rail transport" gap-scans, which had apparently assumed the general Land Transport Law (2004-33) covers rail without checking that its text is road-only. Note for future runs: the railway-law entry's underlying JORT issue numbers could not be independently confirmed this run (only titles/dates, via SNCFT's own site and legislation.tn) — a citation spot-check target for a future run. Two new standalone entries added (280 total). Future runs should prioritize the July 8 decree-publication status, then July 11 and July 13, and continue naming specific narrow statutes (e.g., driving-school licensing decree-vs-statute status, cinema/audiovisual production incentives, port-worker/docker labor statute) rather than re-running broad thematic gap-scans, which have shown near-zero marginal yield across 30+ combined passes.
  • (Confirmed Run 167) The July 8, 2026 graduate-recruitment protest was confirmed as having actually occurred today near Place de la Kasbah in Tunis (webdo.tn), with Law No. 2025-18's implementing decrees and digital application platform still unpublished — now reflected directly in that entry's own text. The Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported (a June 11, 2026 article suggesting movement toward a plenary session was checked and found superseded by the already-documented June 17, 2026 status). July 11 (ONAT) and July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant) remain unchanged and not yet arrived. Two narrow leads (insurance-broker/actuary licensing, halal-certification authority) were confirmed already adequately covered by the existing Insurance Code entry and the National Standardization System Law entry, respectively — not standalone gaps. No new standalone entries were added (280 total remains accurate). Future runs should prioritize July 11 and July 13 the moment each passes, and continue naming specific narrow statutes (e.g., driving-school licensing, cinema/audiovisual production incentives, port-worker/docker labor statute) as a gap-finding technique.
  • (Added Run 169) The Renewable Energy Law entry's May 13, 2026 CNRD note gained its missing ARP origin: the five photovoltaic concession bills (Laws n°01/2026–05/2026) were first adopted by the ARP on April 28, 2026 with individually recorded votes, the same day President Saied dismissed Energy Minister Fatma Thabet Chiboub amid UGTT/opposition "energy colonisation" criticism of the 20(+10)-year foreign-operator concessions — cross-verified via direct WebFetch of africanews.com and entreprises-magazine.com rather than trusted from a WebSearch synthesis alone. A related WebSearch synthesis claiming a "July 6, 2026" vote on the Local/Regional/District Councils organic law was checked via direct source fetch and found to be a date artifact of search summarization, not a real event — the underlying Organic Law No. 2025-4 (March 12, 2025) was already accurately documented since Run 13; future runs encountering this "July 6" date for that specific law should treat it as an already-debunked search-synthesis artifact. The Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported; July 11 (ONAT) and July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant) remain the nearest-term triggers, still not yet arrived as of this run's cutoff.
  • (Resolved Run 170) The "July 11 ONAT" trigger, unconfirmed since Run 145 flagged it as possibly misattributed among three candidate professional orders, is now resolved: ONAT is the Ordre National des Avocats de Tunisie (Bar Association), confirmed via avocat.org.tn.
