The Lawyers Statute entry — previously silent on any 2026 development — was enriched with the underlying ONAT–Justice Ministry standoff (January 23, 2026 reform demands; the disputed May 1, 2026 exceptional general assembly; the June 18, 2026 national general strike; the July 11, 2026 annual assembly expected to set dialogue terms). The Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported; July 11's assembly outcome and July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant) remain the nearest-term triggers, still not yet arrived as of this run's cutoff. A five-topic check (midwifery, customs brokers, driving-school licensing, hunting, Development Plan bill plenary status) found no new standalone gaps — all are either already covered or exist only at ministerial-order/administrative level. No new standalone entries were added (280 total remains accurate).
  • (Confirmed/enriched Run 171) This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-h8259v) again hit the stale-checkout failure mode documented throughout this changelog: it held a self-contained v1.0–v2.11 lineage forked from the project's very first commit, 249 commits behind origin/main's independent 170-run v4.170 history. Two candidate "gaps" drafted before catching this (Law No. 2025-9 labor-subcontracting reform; Decree-Law 2023-17/ANCS cybersecurity) were discarded unmerged upon discovering both were already fully documented in main (see the Employment Reform Law and National Cybersecurity Framework entries) — a useful confirmation that this changelog's "near-zero marginal yield" warning extends even to gaps that look real from a stale baseline. After resetting to origin/main, the July 8, 2026 graduate-recruitment/Law 2025-18 decree-publication status was rechecked and remains unpublished as of the latest identified reporting (Le Temps News, June 22, 2026) — no change from Run 170. July 11 (ONAT) and July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant) still had not arrived as of this run's cutoff. This run's genuine yield was a narrow enrichment: the Cinema and Cultural Industries entry gained a distinct, previously undocumented fiscal measure — Proposition de loi n° 103, an ARP floor vote on December 3, 2025 (57–34–8) setting a 7% tax rate on cinema/audiovisual production equipment and a full customs-duty exemption on its import — corroborated via two independent sources (La Presse, WebManagerCenter/Directinfo, both Dec. 3, 2025); its actual in-force/JORT-publication status is flagged as unresolved per the Finance Minister's own floor-debate characterization of it as separate regulatory text. A driving-school/auto-école lead and a docker/port-worker-labor-statute lead were both checked and found either already covered (Run 164) or not resolving into a citable standalone statute. No new standalone entries were added (280 total remains accurate). Future runs should prioritize July 11/13 outcomes the moment each passes, and should verify their designated branch against origin/main (not local commit history alone) before drafting any changes.
  • (Added/corrected Run 172) This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-1xaalz) again hit the stale-checkout failure mode: it held a self-contained v1.0–v2.10 lineage (13 commits, its own changelog topping out at "170" entries) forked from the project's first commit, entirely unaware of origin/main's independent 171-run v4.171 history (280 entries) until git fetch origin main/git ls-remote --heads origin surfaced it — a fresh commit had already been pushed against the stale base before the discrepancy was caught; the branch was reset to origin/main and that commit's candidate findings were re-verified from scratch rather than reused. Of six candidates drafted against the stale base, four were confirmed already present in main with equal or greater precision and discarded: the Sports Structures Law (already Organic Law No. 95-11, corrected in Run 44), the CNAM health-insurance law (already the extensively-maintained CNAM entry, actively tracking the July 13 tiers-payant trigger), the Agadir Agreement (already the detailed Agadir Agreement entry), and GAFTA (already covered as "PAFTA, 1997" within the WTO Membership and Bilateral Trade Agreements entry — same agreement, different common acronym). One candidate — an initial search result appearing to show a Tunisian court verdict dated exactly July 8, 2026 — was investigated and found to be the search tool conflating the already-documented July 8, 2025 Case 1 first-instance verdict with the June 2026 Secret Apparatus sentencing; discarded as a false positive, consistent with this changelog's established practice of rejecting date-conflation artifacts. That same investigation surfaced a genuine sixth Ghannouchi prosecution — the "Ramadan Evening Gathering" case (April 14–15, 2026, 20 years, five further defendants in absentia) — added as a Run 172 update within the "State Security Conspiracy" Prosecutions entry after cross-checking against all 171 prior runs' coverage (no match found). Two further genuine items were closed: a new standalone Tourism Investment Code entry (Law No. 90-21 of 1990), a sector-specific statute distinct from both the general Investment Law and the Tourism Organization Law and never previously documented; and a correction to the Deposit Guarantee Fund entry, which had cited the fund's superseded 2006 predecessor (Law No. 2006-19, "FGTD," TND 20,000 cap) — independent verification against the fund's own official site (fgdb.gov.tn) confirmed the scheme was re-created under Art. 149 of the 2016 Banking Law as the FGDB, with a TND 60,000 cap and 20-working-day payout. One new standalone entry added (281 total); two existing entries substantially revised (one corrected, one extended). July 11 (ONAT) and July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant) still had not arrived as of this run's cutoff.
  • (Enriched Run 173) Before drafting, this run confirmed its designated branch (claude/nice-edison-b0aab2) again held only a stale v2.0–v2.10 lineage (13 commits) while origin/main had independently progressed through 172 merged runs to v4.172 (281 entries) — the branch was reset directly to origin/main before any research began, avoiding the recurring failure mode documented throughout this changelog. A research pass rechecked the standing watchlist (Foreign Exchange Code committee status, the July 11 ONAT and July 13 CNAM/biologists tiers-payant triggers) and found no change: none of the three dates had arrived or produced a reported outcome as of this run's cutoff, and the Code des Changes remains at Ktari's mid-June ~90%-hearings-complete, BCT-non-responsive status with no confirmed committee vote or plenary date. Cross-checking CPJ's May 2026 "Beyond Decree 54" report (already cited in the Decree-Law 2022-54 entry) against its full source text surfaced a genuine, previously undocumented gap: the escalation of the civil-society crackdown from mere administrative suspension (already tracked in the Administrative Suspensions entry) to actual judicial dissolution proceedings. Al Khatt — the association publishing the investigative outlet Inkyfada (an ICIJ partner) since 2014 — was suspended for 30 days in October 2025, lost its court challenge to that suspension, and was then targeted by a Presidency-filed dissolution request (announced April 21–23, 2026); the Tunis Court of First Instance opened joint dissolution proceedings against Al Khatt and the anti-racism association Mnemty (whose president Saadia Mosbah is separately being prosecuted on unrelated charges, see the Racial Discrimination Law entry) on May 11, 2026, postponing the session first to June 1 and then to July 6, 2026 — corroborated across RSF, ICIJ, businessnews.com.tn, webdo.tn, and Inkyfada's own reporting, with no outcome confirmed by any source as of this run's cutoff. Both the Administrative Suspensions entry (retitled to reflect the dissolution escalation) and the Decree-Law 2022-54 entry were cross-referenced accordingly. No new standalone entry was added (281 total remains accurate, since this enriches two existing cards); two existing entries substantially revised. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 281 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.173/Run 173. July 11 (ONAT) and July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant) remain the highest-priority items for the next run, alongside the still-unresolved LTDH appeal (postponed to July 15) and the Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution case's still-pending July 6 outcome.
  • (v4.174 update, Run 174) Before drafting, this run confirmed its designated branch (claude/nice-edison-8pzix2) again held only a stale, self-contained v1.0–v2.10 lineage entirely unaware of origin/main's independent 173-run history (281 entries, v4.173) — reset directly to current origin/main before any research began, per this changelog's established practice. A dedicated research agent ran a structured gap-scan across twelve regulated-activity/profession candidates (money-changers, public weighbridges, beekeeping, veterinary pharmacy, dockworker labor, private tutoring, car rental, tour-guide licensing, hunting/wildlife, dam safety, PPP implementing decrees, data-center/cloud licensing, consumer arbitration) plus a fresh July 6–8, 2026 news sweep. Ten of the twelve candidates were confirmed already covered at equal or greater precision by existing entries, decree/circular-level (not a legislative gap), or a genuine regulatory absence not yet reduced to enacted text (consumer ADR) — none re-added. Two survived independent verification: (1) a new standalone Veterinary Pharmacy Law entry (Law No. 78-23 of 1978, as amended by Law No. 2000-40 of 2000) — a narrow but genuine gap distinct from the existing Veterinary Law (2005-35) entry, since 78-23 governs veterinary-medicine manufacturing/market-authorization rather than animal-health surveillance or practice licensing, and had survived four prior "veterinary" gap-scans (Runs 82, 90, 113, 116); (2) a pending tour-guide-licensing reform bill examined by the ARP Tourism Committee on March 11, 2026 alongside the already-tracked social-tourism-quota bill, added as an enrichment to the existing Tourism Organization Law entry rather than a new card. A third finding — the ARP Bureau's Monday, July 6, 2026 referral of the 2026–2030 National Development Plan bill to a fresh joint-committee examination round rather than directly to plenary — was added as an enrichment to that entry (confirmed via direct fetch of the primary RTCI report). A claimed "Constitutional Court Proposal 100/2025" gap and a claimed "Personal Data Protection full enforcement July 11, 2026" claim were both investigated and found already accurately captured (the former in the existing Constitutional Court entry with more precision than the new lead; the latter re-confirmed as the same recurring marketing-blog false positive rejected in the Run 147 note above). The July 6, 2026 Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution-hearing outcome remains unreported by any source as of this run's cutoff — a genuine "not yet indexed" result rather than a resolved item, consistent with the two-days-since-hearing timing. A recurring "July 8" date-conflation trap (the already-documented July 8, 2025 Case 2 verdict, entry 220) was re-encountered and correctly not re-added. One new standalone entry added (282 total); three existing entries enriched (Tourism Organization Law, 2026–2030 National Development Plan, and — via the research agent's confirmation — no change needed to the Constitutional Court entry). A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 282 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.174/Run 174. July 11 (ONAT), July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant), July 15 (LTDH appeal), and the still-pending Al Khatt/Mnemty outcome remain the highest-priority items for the next run.
  • (Confirmed Run 175) Before drafting, this run confirmed its designated branch (claude/nice-edison-kx88s2) again held only a stale, never-merged v2.0–v2.10-era fork (unaware of origin/main's 174-run history, 282 entries, v4.174) — confirmed via git fetch origin main and git log --oneline origin/main before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main per established practice. A dedicated research agent re-checked the standing highest-priority watchlist — the July 6, 2026 Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution-hearing outcome, the Foreign Exchange Code (Draft Law 115/2025) committee status, the 2026–2030 Development Plan bill, the Personal Data Protection & AI organic bill, the Associations Decree reform, the July 11 ONAT assembly, the July 13 CNAM/biologists tiers-payant suspension, and the July 15 LTDH appeal — and found no change to any item: the Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution outcome remains unreported by any source, and the three forward trigger dates (July 11, 13, 15) had still not arrived as of this run's cutoff. Four candidate leads were investigated and confirmed already fully captured rather than gaps: Saadia Mosbah/Mnemty co-defendants' June 23, 2026 appeal ruling (already documented in detail since Run 153, Racial Discrimination Law entry); the July 1, 2026 CNAM–pharmacist "engagement de paiement" resolution (already documented since Run 30, CNAM entry); the July 11 ONAT trigger (already resolved at Run 170 as the Bar Association, Lawyers Statute entry); and a recirculating rumor of a new Road Traffic Code entering into force July 1, 2026 (already investigated and debunked at Run 162). One newly-encountered false-positive trap was identified and correctly rejected rather than added: a search-engine synthesis suggesting a "July 8, 2026" mass sentencing of 21 defendants including Rached Ghannouchi did not match the text of the HRW source article it was purportedly drawn from on direct fetch — an apparent hallucination/conflation with an earlier (2025) proceeding, distinct from the already-documented "State Security Conspiracy" prosecutions entry; flagged here so a future run encountering the same claim does not re-investigate it as if new. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required revision (282 total remains accurate). A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 282 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 1338/1338 balanced <div> tags, 282/282 balanced <h3> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.175/Run 175. July 11 (ONAT), July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant), July 15 (LTDH appeal), and the still-pending Al Khatt/Mnemty outcome remain the highest-priority items for the next run.
  • (Enriched Run 176) This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-sk21m2) again held only a stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.11 lineage (13 commits, its own changelog topping out at "171" entries) forked from the project's very first commit, entirely unaware of origin/main's independent 175-run history (282 entries, v4.175); a fresh commit had already been drafted and pushed against the stale base (adding an LTDH-suspension note and a standalone animal-welfare pending-reform card) before git fetch origin main and git log --oneline origin/main surfaced the divergence. Both candidates were checked against the real current artifact rather than reused: the LTDH suspension was found already covered in far greater depth by the existing Administrative Suspensions & Judicial Dissolution Proceedings entry (which additionally tracks ASF Tunisia, ATFD, FTDES, Al Khatt, and Mnemty), and the animal-welfare bill was found already documented as the existing Proposition de loi n° 74/2024 entry — both discarded as duplicates rather than re-added, and the branch was reset directly to origin/main before restarting. A re-check of the standing watchlist (Al Khatt/Mnemty July 6 hearing outcome, Foreign Exchange Code committee status) found no movement on either since Run 175. This run's genuine yield was a same-day news update to the animal-welfare entry: a video showing a performance-troupe member dancing on the belly of a horse lying on the ground during a wedding ceremony in Sahline (Monastir governorate) went viral around July 6–7, 2026, prompting the animal-protection association Arrahma to pursue a formal criminal complaint against the identified troupe and Parliament to accelerate review of the pending bill; Business News (July 7, 2026) reported the acceleration, and press coverage (Business News, Univers News, allAfrica) supplied the bill's previously-uncaptured tiered penalty structure (15 days–1 month/TND 500 for general mistreatment, 1–10 years/TND 2,000 for abuse causing permanent disability, 5 years for abuse causing death). No new standalone entry was added (282 total remains accurate, since this enriches one existing card); one existing entry enriched. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 282 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.176/Run 176. July 11 (ONAT), July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant), July 15 (LTDH appeal), and the still-pending Al Khatt/Mnemty outcome remain the highest-priority items for the next run.
  • (Confirmed Run 177) This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-ssaj54) again held only a stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.10 lineage (12 commits, its own changelog topping out at a "v2.10" label with no matching pull request found via list_pull_requests), entirely unaware of origin/main's independent 176-run history (282 entries, v4.176) — confirmed via git fetch origin main and git log --oneline origin/main before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main per established practice. Tavily again returned HTTP 402 "account disabled" (consistent with every run since roughly Run 68), so this run relied on WebSearch alone. A fresh sweep re-checked the full standing watchlist — the Foreign Exchange Code (Draft Law 115/2025) committee vote, the 2026–2030 Development Plan bill, the Personal Data Protection & AI organic bill, the Associations Decree reform, the July 11 ONAT general assembly, the July 13 CNAM/biologists tiers-payant suspension, the July 15 LTDH appeal, and the still-unreported July 6, 2026 Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution-hearing outcome — and found no movement on any item; a direct check for new July 2026 JORT publications likewise surfaced nothing beyond what prior runs already captured. This run also re-encountered the recurring "July 8" mass Ennahda/opposition sentencing false-positive trap (21 of 24 defendants, 12–35 years, purportedly including Rached Ghannouchi) first flagged at Run 153 and re-confirmed at Run 175; rather than relying on search-summary text alone, this run fetched the actual source articles directly (HRW's July 7, 2026 "Tunisia: Harsh Sentences for Rights Defenders" and July 8, 2026 "Sounding the Alarm Over Tunisia's Crackdown") and confirmed neither mentions Ghannouchi or any 21-defendant ruling — both instead discuss the already-documented Bensedrine/Krichi/Mosbah rights-defender sentencings — reinforcing with primary-source verification that this remains the same year-dropped conflation with the July 8, 2025 Case 2 verdict, not a new 2026 event. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required substantive revision (282 total remains accurate) — a pure confirmation run. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 282 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 282/282 balanced <h3> tags, 1338/1338 balanced <div> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.177/Run 177. July 11 (ONAT), July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant), July 15 (LTDH appeal), and the still-pending Al Khatt/Mnemty outcome remain the highest-priority items for the next run.
  • (v4.178 update, Run 178): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-vlq0nj) again held only a stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.11 lineage (its own changelog topping out at a "v2.11" label, freshly drafted and opened as PR #201 before a merge-conflict error on submission caught the divergence from origin/main's independent 177-run history, 282 entries, v4.177) — the same recurring failure mode documented throughout this changelog; the branch was reset directly to current origin/main before any further edits, per established practice. The stale draft's own candidate findings (a food-safety entry, a plant-protection entry, and an Abir Moussi case entry) were individually cross-checked against the real current artifact rather than reused: the Abir Moussi case was found already covered, at greater precision, within the consolidated "State Security Conspiracy" Prosecutions entry (Run 168's detailed "bureau d'ordre" and ISIE-case update). The food-safety and plant-protection leads, however, led to a genuine discovery: both topics already had standalone entries (Food Safety, Plant Health/Veterinary), but cross-checking the stale draft's independently-sourced law numbers against theirs surfaced two real, previously-unnoticed citation errors. (1) The Food Safety entry's citation of "Law No. 2009-11 of February 11, 2009" and "ANCSEP" as its creating authority was wrong on both counts: Law No. 2009-11 (actually dated March 2, 2009) is Tunisia's building fire/explosion/panic-safety code, unrelated to food safety (confirmed via legislation-securite.tn and citet.nat.tn), and ANCSEP was created separately by Decree No. 99-779 of 1999 as a general product-safety coordinator. The entry is corrected to cite Law No. 2019-25 of February 26, 2019, which creates the actual food-safety-specific authority, INSSPA, confirmed via FAOLEX's full text and independent INSSPA/ANCSEP institutional-comparison sources. (2) The Plant Health entry's citation of "Law No. 99-24 of February 23, 1999" for plant protection was also wrong: Law No. 99-24 is in fact dated March 9, 1999 and governs veterinary sanitary control at import/export, an unrelated subject, corroborated across two independent legal databases; the entry is corrected to cite Law No. 92-72 of August 3, 1992, Tunisia's actual phytosanitary/plant-protection recast statute (FAOLEX-confirmed), and its unverifiable "INAP" institutional claim is replaced with the independently-confirmed Direction Générale de la Santé Végétale et du Contrôle des Intrants Agricoles. Both corrections retain the artifact's practice of not asserting article-level citations that cannot be independently verified. A fresh check of the standing watchlist (Foreign Exchange Code, July 11 ONAT, July 13 CNAM/biologists, July 15 LTDH appeal, Al Khatt/Mnemty outcome) found no movement on any item, and this run's own attempt at a broader gap-scan (tobacco, gambling, whistleblower, urban-transport candidates) found each already covered by prior runs. No new standalone entries were added (282 total remains accurate, since both genuine findings are corrections to existing cards); two existing entries (Food Safety, Plant Health) corrected for citation accuracy. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 282 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 282/282 balanced <h3> tags, 1338/1338 balanced <div> tags, and all three version markers synchronized at v4.178/Run 178. July 11 (ONAT), July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant), July 15 (LTDH appeal), and the still-pending Al Khatt/Mnemty outcome remain the highest-priority items for the next run.
  • (v4.179 update, Run 179): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-olmm1d) again held no commits of its own beyond a stale fork, and was reset directly to current origin/main (v4.178, 282 entries, 178 merged runs) before drafting, per established practice. A dedicated research agent re-checked the full standing watchlist: the Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome remains unreported; the July 11 (ONAT), July 13 (CNAM/biologists), and July 15 (LTDH appeal) trigger dates had still not arrived as of this run's cutoff; and the July 6, 2026 Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution-hearing outcome remains genuinely unreported by any outlet checked (businessnews, webdo, Kapitalis, Inkyfada, RSF, ICIJ, Amnesty) — a real information gap rather than a resolved item, flagged for the next run to re-check rather than assumed to be a further postponement. The 2026–2030 Development Plan bill's July 2–3 committee-conclusion status was reconfirmed as already accurately captured by the existing entry (including its Run 174 "referred to fresh joint-examination round" update), so no change was made there despite an initial research lead suggesting a discrepancy. A ten-topic "everyday regulated activity" gap-scan (consumer protection, firearms, prison administration, an ombudsman-equivalent institution, forestry, mining, civil aviation, postal services, notaries, and sworn translators/interpreters) found eight of ten already covered by existing entries at the correct citation (cross-checked directly against the live document rather than trusting the agent's summary — e.g., a candidate "Consumer Protection Law" citation matched the existing entry verbatim, and a candidate "Notaries Law" citation matched the existing entry, both already correct since Run 44). Two survived as genuine gaps, independently verified via legislation-securite.tn, AOMF, and ILO NATLEX before inclusion: (1) a new standalone Administrative Mediator (Médiateur Administratif) entry (Decree No. 92-2143 of 1992; Law No. 93-51 of May 3, 1993) — Tunisia's ombudsman-equivalent institution for administrative grievances, never previously documented despite the closely related Administrative Court entry existing since early runs; (2) a new standalone Sworn Interpreters Law entry (Law No. 94-80 of July 4, 1994) — a companion legal-profession statute to the Notaries and Bailiffs statutes, both already documented, that had never itself been added. Two new standalone entries added (284 total). A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 284 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 284/284 balanced <h3> tags, 1346/1346 balanced <div> tags, and all three version markers synchronized at v4.179/Run 179. July 11 (ONAT), July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant), July 15 (LTDH appeal), and the still-pending Al Khatt/Mnemty outcome remain the highest-priority items for the next run.

A fresh research pass re-checked the full standing watchlist — the Foreign Exchange Code committee status, the July 11 (ONAT), July 13 (CNAM/biologists), and July 15 (LTDH appeal) trigger dates, and the July 6, 2026 Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution-hearing outcome — and found no movement on any item: none of the three forward dates had arrived as of this run's cutoff (July 8, 2026, ~21:00 UTC), the Exchange Code remains at its ~90%-hearings-complete, BCT-non-responsive status with a July meeting with the Finance Minister still only "envisaged," and the Al Khatt/Mnemty outcome remains genuinely unreported by any outlet checked. Two HRW pieces dated July 7 and July 8, 2026 ("Tunisia: Harsh Sentences for Rights Defenders" and "Sounding the Alarm Over Tunisia's Crackdown") were fetched directly and confirmed to recap only the already-documented June 2026 Bensedrine and Mosbah/Mnemty sentences, with no new July 2026 legal development and no Ghannouchi mass-sentencing event — reinforcing the same year-dropped July 8, 2025-vs-2026 conflation trap flagged at Runs 153/175/177. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required substantive revision (284 total remains accurate) — a pure confirmation run. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 284 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 284/284 balanced <h3> tags, 1346/1346 balanced <div> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.180/Run 180.   • (v4.182 update, Run 182): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-94lzz6) again held only a stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.12 lineage forked from the project's first commit (fourteen commits, its own changelog topping out at a freshly-drafted "v2.12" label) while origin/main had independently progressed through 181 merged runs to v4.181 (286 entries) — confirmed via git fetch origin main and git log --oneline origin/main ^HEAD (269 commits ahead) after a first commit had already been pushed against the stale base; that commit was not reused, and the branch was reset directly to origin/main before redoing the research from an accurate baseline, per established practice. The stale draft's three candidate case entries (Samir Abdelli, Chawki Tabib, Rached Khiari) were individually cross-checked against the real current artifact rather than blindly re-added: Abdelli was found already present, at equal precision, as the existing Affaire Samir Abdelli entry (added Run 181) — not re-added. Khiari's case was found genuinely under-documented: a Run 155 changelog note had asserted it was "already fully captured," but a direct full-text search for "Khiari" found no such content anywhere in the artifact body, only in that changelog note itself — the existing Telecommunications Code entry documented only the Zied El Heni Article 86 prosecution; added as a new Run 182 update to that same entry (his 8-month first-instance sentence reduced to 6 months on appeal, April 10, 2026, plus CFJ's health concerns), rather than a new standalone card, matching the entry's existing convention of grouping Article 86 prosecutions together. One candidate survived as a genuine, previously-undocumented standalone gap: the Affaire Chawki Tabib case — the former ONAT (Bar Association) president and, notably, former 2016–2020 president of INLUCC (the anti-corruption body already documented in the Declaration of Assets/INLUCC entry), sentenced May 21, 2026 to 10 years for forgery in a case tied to an INLUCC-era report against former PM Elyes Fakhfakh — a full-text search of 181 prior runs found no match for "Tabib"; corroborated across La Presse de Tunisie, the IAPL Monitoring Committee on Attacks on Lawyers, and CRLDHT, and cross-referenced both directions with the existing INLUCC entry. One new standalone entry added (287 total, up from 286); one existing entry (Telecommunications Code) enriched. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 287 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, balanced <div>/<section> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.182/Run 182. The Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome and the July 11 (ONAT) and July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant) trigger dates remain the highest-priority watchlist items for the next run.
  • (v4.181 update, Run 181): This run's designated branch again held no commits of its own beyond a stale, never-merged v2.11-era fork built without first checking origin/main — the branch was reset directly to origin/main (v4.180, 284 entries, 180 merged runs) before drafting, per established practice; the stale draft's own candidate findings (three new case entries and a plastics-bill/land-release/Family-Code note) were individually cross-checked against the real current artifact rather than reused. Two survived as genuine gaps; the rest did not. Cross-checking found the stale draft's Makram Jlassi and single-use-plastics-bill candidates already present in main at equal or greater precision (Jlassi since Run 87; the plastics bill since Runs 109/132, already citing the same 25-deputy sponsorship, bag-material standards, and imprisonment tier the stale draft had independently re-derived), and its proposed "Family Code/joint-custody" pending-reform entry was found to duplicate, without adding anything the existing 2018 Presidential Commission Report/Inheritance Equality entry doesn't already cover more precisely — none of the three were re-added. Two genuine, independently-verified gaps closed: (1) a wholly new standalone Affaire Samir Abdelli entry (Judicial Organization section) — a lawyer and 2014 presidential candidate previously named in the SwissLeaks/Panama Papers disclosures, sentenced July 7, 2026 by the Tunis terrorism-specialized criminal chamber to 18 years for money laundering, terrorism financing, and state-security conspiracy (driver and secretary separately sentenced to 3 and 2 years), a case with zero prior mentions across 180 runs — corroborated across Business News, Kapitalis, and Mosaïque FM; (2) a wholly new standalone Affaire Ayachi Hammami entry, a genuine gap despite a Run 104 changelog note asserting his case was "already substantively captured" — direct full-text verification found no prior law-card documented his conviction, El Mornaguia incarceration (December 3, 2025), 43-day hunger strike (ended January 15, 2026), or the UN Human Rights Council's 62nd-session citation (closed July 8, 2026) of his case by the UN Special Rapporteur on judicial independence and High Commissioner Volker Türk — only the fact of his 2025 arrest had been name-dropped in prior changelog prose, never in a card; corroborated across OHCHR, Front Line Defenders, FIDH, LDH, and Jeune Afrique, and cross-referenced from the existing State Security Conspiracy Prosecutions entry, since Hammami was defense counsel to several of that case's defendants before being added as a defendant himself. One further refinement outside the pending-bill watchlist: the Sociétés Communautaires (Decree-Law 2022-15/2025-3) entry gained a July 6, 2026 concrete-application note — the state's release of roughly 17,000 hectares of state-owned agricultural land to newly-formed community enterprises under this framework's existing land-leasing mechanism (La Presse, single-sourced pending further corroboration). No change was made to the standing watchlist (Foreign Exchange Code, July 11 ONAT, July 13 CNAM/biologists, July 15 LTDH appeal, Al Khatt/Mnemty outcome), all reconfirmed unchanged as of this run's cutoff. Two new standalone entries added (286 total, up from 284); one existing entry (Sociétés Communautaires) enriched. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 286 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, all internal anchor links resolving, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.181/Run 181.
  • (v4.183 update, Run 183): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-ad0npp) again held only a stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.11 lineage forked from the project's first commit (its own changelog topping out at a freshly-drafted "v2.12" label documenting a Penal Code Art. 96/98 note and a new animal-welfare pending-reform card) while origin/main had independently progressed through 182 merged runs to v4.182 (287 entries) — confirmed via git fetch origin main and git log --oneline e0a880f..origin/main (271 commits ahead) after a first commit had already been pushed against the stale base; that commit was not reused, and the branch was reset directly to origin/main before redoing the research from an accurate baseline, per established practice. Both of the stale draft's candidate findings were individually cross-checked against the real current artifact rather than blindly re-added, and both were found already present at equal or greater precision: the Penal Code amendment as the existing Law No. 2025-14 of July 28, 2025 standalone entry, and the animal-welfare bill as the existing Proposition de loi n° 74/2024 entry — neither was re-added. A fresh research pass then re-checked the full standing watchlist: the Foreign Exchange Code committee's July 6, 2026 session outcome remains unconfirmed by any outlet as of this run's cutoff, though a June 11, 2026 Tuniscope/Tunisie Numérique report (not previously cited) added texture to the already-documented committee posture — Finance Committee president Maher Ktari was quoted arguing it is "not possible to block a proposed law due to the absence of interaction from the Central Bank," with article-by-article voting then targeted for June 20–30, 2026 — but no source confirms that vote actually occurred, so the entry's existing "not yet enacted" status stands unchanged. The Al Khatt/Mnemty judicial dissolution hearing, postponed to July 6, 2026 per the existing entry, likewise has no reported outcome from any source checked (Amnesty, ICIJ, RSF, Inkyfada, businessnews.com.tn, webdo.tn, directinfo.webmanagercenter.com) — still genuinely unreported, not a research gap. The ONAT general assembly (July 11) and CNAM/biologists tiers-payant suspension (July 13) trigger dates had not yet arrived as of this run's July 9, 2026 cutoff. No newly enacted JORT law, floor vote, or court ruling dated to July 8–9, 2026 was identified in a targeted sweep (JORT publications, Council of Ministers decisions, ARP committee activity). No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required substantive revision — a pure confirmation run (287 total remains accurate). A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 287 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, balanced <div>/<section>/<h3> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.183/Run 183. The Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome and the July 11 (ONAT) and July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant) trigger dates remain the highest-priority watchlist items for the next run.
  • (v4.184 update, Run 184): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-2rsat5) again held only a stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.11 lineage forked from the project's first commit (its own changelog topping out at a freshly-drafted "v2.12" label documenting Abdallah Saïd-unrelated Hammami/Abdelli entries and a local-councils false-positive check) while origin/main had independently progressed through 183 merged runs to v4.183 (287 entries); a first commit and PR (#207) had already been opened against the stale base before git fetch origin main and git log --oneline origin/main surfaced the divergence — the PR was closed without merging (it would have regressed main from 287 entries to 172) and the branch was reset directly to origin/main before redoing the research from an accurate baseline, per established practice. Both of the stale draft's candidate case entries (Samir Abdelli, Ayachi Hammami) were cross-checked against the real current artifact and found already present, at equal or greater precision, as the existing Affaire Samir Abdelli and Affaire Ayachi Hammami entries (both added Run 181) — neither was re-added. A fresh research pass re-checked the standing watchlist (Foreign Exchange Code, Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution outcome, ONAT July 11, CNAM/biologists July 13) and found no movement on any item, consistent with Run 183's same-day findings. One genuine, previously-undocumented gap was found and closed via a July 8, 2026 Kapitalis report on an Intersection pour les droits et les libertés civil-society survey: a new standalone Affaire Abdallah Saïd entry — founding president of the Médenine humanitarian association "Les Enfants de la Lune," detained November 12, 2024 by the financial-crimes investigation unit (after the anti-terrorism unit itself found no terrorism basis), convicted April 22, 2026 to one year's imprisonment for activities supporting migrant, refugee, and disabled children, with the public prosecutor's appeal still pending — corroborated across Committee for Justice, Business News, Kapitalis, and Réalités Magazine, and cross-referenced from the existing Administrative Suspensions and Irregular Migration Law entries; a full-text search of 183 prior runs found no match for "Saïd" or "Enfants de la Lune." The same survey's other named organizations (ATFD, Mourakiboun, I Watch) were checked and found already covered by the existing civil-society-suspensions entry since Run 39. One new standalone entry added (288 total, up from 287). A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 288 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates (one false-positive duplicate flagged by a naive id-grep was traced to changelog prose referencing an id inside a <code> tag, not a real duplicate element), 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 288/288 balanced <h3> tags, 1362/1362 balanced <div> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.184/Run 184. The Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome and the July 11 (ONAT) and July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant) trigger dates remain the highest-priority watchlist items for the next run.

Eight showed no change; one genuine, dated enrichment was found and verified before inclusion: the 2026–2030 National Development Plan entry's Run 174 text explicitly stated "no plenary date has yet been announced" — a fresh RTCI report (July 7, 2026, 14:54), corroborated by a La Presse de Tunisie report of a 10:30 AM sitting, shows the ARP Bureau met the morning of July 7 and formally scheduled the plenary session for Thursday, July 9, 2026 (today), possibly extending to Friday, July 10 if debate runs long — the first concrete plenary date since the bill's June 17, 2026 filing; no vote outcome had been reported as of this update. The already-flagged Personal Data Protection "full enforcement July 11, 2026" marketing-site claim resurfaced and was again correctly withheld as uncorroborated by any primary source (consistent with Runs 72/76/174). No new standalone entries were added (288 total remains accurate); one existing entry was enriched. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 288 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 288/288 balanced <h3> tags, 1362/1362 balanced <div> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.185/Run 185. The Foreign Exchange Code committee outcome, today's Development Plan plenary vote result, and the July 11 (ONAT), July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant), and July 15 (LTDH appeal) trigger dates remain the highest-priority watchlist items for the next run.

Cross-checking the stale draft's four candidate findings against the real current artifact found all four already present, in every case with materially more precision: the civil-society suspension wave (LTDH, ASF Tunisia, ATFD, FTDES, Aswat Nissa, OMCT-Tunisia, I Watch, Nawaat) as the existing Administrative Suspensions & Judicial Dissolution Proceedings entry, which additionally tracks the Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution case the stale draft had missed entirely; the Bilel Chaouachi terrorism case as the existing standalone entry added at Run 152; the Development Plan 2026-2030 plenary-session detail (bill n°42/2026, July 9 10:30am opening, possible extension to July 10) as the existing entry's Run 185 update, worded almost identically; and the Tunisian Council for Refugees/Terre d'Asile humanitarian-worker prosecutions (Djemali, Krimi) as the existing Passports & Travel Documents Law entry added at Run 106 — none were re-added. A fresh research pass then re-checked the standing watchlist and found no movement: no Foreign Exchange Code floor vote or JORT publication, no Development Plan vote outcome (plenary session opened as scheduled but remained in deputies'-interventions stage as of this run's cutoff, per a fresh RTCI/La Presse check), and the July 11 (ONAT), July 13 (CNAM/biologists), and July 15 (LTDH appeal) trigger dates had not yet arrived. One genuine, previously-uncaptured enrichment survived: the Intersection association for rights and freedoms' civil-society monitoring report (published via Kapitalis, July 8, 2026 — the same underlying source as Run 184's Abdallah Saïd addition, but whose aggregate statistics had not themselves been extracted into the artifact) supplies the first documented overall scale figures for the pattern — 88 cases over its May 2024–May 2026 window, 47 organizational restriction/prosecution situations, 41 individual judicial proceedings, 8 pre-trial detentions, 34 releases, 7 first-instance convictions, 22 one-month suspension orders, and four named recurring mechanisms — added as an update to the existing Administrative Suspensions entry rather than a new card. No new standalone entries were added (288 total remains accurate); one existing entry enriched. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 288 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 288/288 balanced <h3> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.186/Run 186.   • (v4.187 update, Run 187): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-jdbj5x) again held only a stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.11 lineage (14 commits topping out at a "v2.11" label, 170 entries) unaware of origin/main's independent 186-run history (288 entries, v4.186) — confirmed via git fetch origin main and git log --oneline before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly to origin/main per established practice; no stale content was reused. A fresh research pass re-checked the standing watchlist (Foreign Exchange Code, Development Plan plenary vote, Personal Data Protection organic law, ONAT/CNAM/LTDH trigger dates) via WebSearch and found no movement: the Development Plan 2026-2030 plenary session opened as scheduled at 10:30 AM on July 9, 2026 (bill n°42/2026) with no vote outcome yet reported (RTCI, Business News, La Presse de Tunisie, all July 8–9, 2026, consistent with the existing entry's Run 185/186 text), and the July 11 (ONAT), July 13 (CNAM/biologists), and July 15 (LTDH appeal) trigger dates had not yet arrived. A candidate lead surfaced by search — a "July 8" report of a Tunis court sentencing 21 Ennahda-linked defendants including a "14-year" in-absentia sentence for Rached Ghannouchi — was investigated and rejected as stale-date/search-index conflation: the underlying event is the already-documented July 8, 2025 first-instance "Conspiracy 2" verdict, whose sentence was subsequently raised to 20 years on the February 2, 2026 appeal already captured in the State Security Conspiracy entry — not a new 2026 ruling, and correctly not added, consistent with the cross-country/stale-date conflation trap flagged in the Runs 74/77/97/122/144/146 notes. One genuine, dated enrichment survived verification: the Constitutional Court entry's Run 76 text ended at a February 18, 2026 hearing with "consultations would continue"; a fresh search found the General Legislation Committee relaunched the file on March 27, 2026 with a hearing of the Ordre National des Avocats de Tunisie (ONAT), whose delegation objected that the bill's all-magistrate composition would isolate the Court from outside legal expertise, while the bâtonnier linked the Court's creation to the still-unformed Conseil Supérieur de la Magistrature — corroborated across two independent WebSearch passes returning matching detail from businessnews.com.tn, though a fully distinct second outlet's independent report of this specific hearing could not be located this run and the entry flags that limitation explicitly. No new standalone entries were added (288 total remains accurate, since this run's genuine finding enriches an existing card rather than extending the count); one existing entry (Constitutional Court) enriched. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 288 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 288/288 balanced <h3> tags, 1362/1362 balanced <div> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.187/Run 187. The entry is corrected to reflect two separate cases, and enriched with previously-uncaptured detail: Chaouachi, a France-based activist and son of Case 1 defendant Ghazi Chaouachi, has accumulated a long series of predominantly in-absentia convictions now totaling more than 95 years and 8 months (up from over 80 years as of May 13, 2026, and 38 years as of November 25, 2025) — a serial-conviction pattern this artifact had never previously tracked as a running total despite 189 prior runs' coverage of the broader conspiracy-case cluster. No new standalone entry was added (288 total remains accurate, since this run's genuine finding corrects and enriches an existing card); one existing entry (State Security Conspiracy Prosecutions) revised for accuracy and currency. The viral "new Road Traffic Code effective July 1, 2026" rumor, already debunked and documented at Run 190, was reconfirmed false and not re-added. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 288 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 288/288 balanced <h3> tags, 1352/1352 balanced <div> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.196/Run 196.   • (v4.197 update, Run 197): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-ia5o1s) again held only a stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.11 lineage unaware of origin/main's independent 196-run history (288 entries, v4.196) — confirmed via git fetch origin main and git log --oneline origin/main before any drafting began; the branch was reset directly to origin/main per established practice and no stale content was reused. A dedicated research agent re-checked all four standing watchlist items via WebSearch and found no resolution on the three unresolved ones: the Foreign Exchange Code (Draft Law 115/2025) committee remains at its ~90%-hearings-complete, BCT-non-responsive status with no floor vote (lapresse.tn, tuniscope.com, businessnews.com.tn, africanmanager.com); the Development Plan 2026-2030 plenary (bill n°42/2026), confirmed underway since July 9, 10:30 AM with Economy Minister Samir Abdelhafidh present and explicitly slated to possibly extend into July 10, had no reported vote outcome as of this run's cutoff (businessnews.com.tn, RTCI, Le Temps); and the Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution proceedings' July 6 hearing outcome remains genuinely unreported by any source, matching the existing entry's own "no outcome confirmed" text verbatim. The July 11 (ONAT), July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant), and July 15 (LTDH appeal) trigger dates were reconfirmed as not yet arrived and unchanged. One genuine enrichment closed a Run 196 open item: the Land Transport Law entry's flagged-but-unresolved Decree No. 2023-581 is now confirmed, via 9anoun.tn's JORT mirror (Decree of September 7, 2023, JORT No. 2023-104) independently cross-checked against African Manager's July 3, 2026 strike coverage, to concern the "organisation du transport public routier non régulier des personnes" — the licensing framework for taxis, louages, and rural transport, repealing the prior Decree No. 2202-2007 (as amended by Decree No. 828-2016). A candidate lead — a Le Desk (Morocco) report framing the ONAT lawyers' dispute as having "become regional mobilization" — was investigated and correctly excluded as insufficiently corroborated (a single non-Tunisian source, unmatched by any Tunisian outlet's framing). No new standalone entry was added (288 total remains accurate, since the genuine finding enriches an existing card); one existing entry (Land Transport Law) enriched. Two genuine, independently-sourced developments survived cross-checking against the live document and were added: (1) a July 9, 2026 update to the Political Parties entry documenting deputy Fatma Mseddi's newly-filed 6-article proposition de loi seeking to classify Ennahdha as a terrorist organization — a fresh filing distinct from this decree-law's own party-dissolution mechanism, with no prior mention across 200 runs (L'Économiste Maghrébin, Business News); (2) a July 9, 2026 update to the Law No. 2025-18 graduate-recruitment entry noting 52 ARP deputies' formal petition to the Head of Government demanding the still-unpublished implementing decrees (La Presse). A village-justice.com opinion piece on the pending associations-reform organic law and a businessnews.com.tn fact-check debunking a false "new Highway Code entered into force July 1, 2026" rumor were both independently checked against the live document and found to add nothing new: the associations reform is already tracked in depth at Decree-Law No. 2011-88 and the pending organic-law entry, and no Highway Code claim exists anywhere in this artifact to correct. One genuine, dated development survived cross-checking and was added: a Run 202 update to the Development Plan 2026-2030 entry documenting the two most substantive floor criticisms of the bill to date — deputy Mohamed Ali's "chèque en blanc pour cinq ans" (blank-check) speech citing TND 147 billion in public debt (over 80% of GDP) and calling on deputies to vote against the bill, and ARP Bureau vice-president Nabil Hamdi's floor criticism that the plan "reflète davantage la volonté de l'administration que celle du peuple" and systematically excluded Parliament from its drafting (Business News and Radio Express FM, both July 9, 2026) — neither previously captured in the entry itself (Run 201's changelog note had referenced these deputies' criticism only in passing, without the entry being updated). No new standalone entries were added (288 total remains accurate, since the finding enriches an existing card); one existing entry enriched. One genuine finding survived cross-checking and was added, with appropriate hedging given a source conflict: a July 7, 2026 Business News article ("deux propositions de loi"), independently re-confirmed across three separately-worded searches, names Proposition de loi n° 66/2024 — filed the same day (July 23, 2024) as the already-tracked n° 74/2024 and before the same Agriculture Commission — as a probable second, separately-numbered animal-protection bill carrying the reinforced sanctions provisions, contradicting Run 176's dismissal of that headline as referring to a single bill. This conflicts with a July 26, 2024 Kapitalis filing announcement (fetched directly) naming only Deputy Chokri Bahri and one unnumbered bill with mixed sanction/awareness content, and a direct WebFetch of the July 7, 2026 article itself failed (HTTP 503), so the animal-welfare entry was updated to flag the probable second bill and the unresolved sourcing conflict rather than re-splitting the card or reattributing its existing content. No new standalone entries were added (288 total remains accurate, since the finding enriches an existing card); one existing entry enriched. In its place, two independently-worded WebSearch queries this run both returned AI-summarized excerpts of that article converging on Run 203's "probable" attribution — Proposition de loi n° 66/2024 as the sanctions bill, n° 74/2024 as the prevention/awareness bill — with added detail (a specific 1–3-year/5-year sanction tier and deputy Mohamed Zied El Maher's quoted explanation for the delay), which was added to the entry with an explicit flag that the sanction figures conflict with Run 176's differently-sourced tiers and remain unconfirmed by primary text.   • (v4.205 update, Run 205): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-v7xrfl) again held only a stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.11 lineage (170 entries, a self-contained changelog running to "v2.12") unaware of origin/main's independent 204-run history (288 entries, v4.204) — a first draft round had already been committed on the stale fork (adding the same Marzouki/Daïmi/Kilani finding below, plus a Constitutional Court enrichment) before the mismatch was caught via git ls-remote origin/git fetch origin main/git log --oneline origin/main, consistent with the identical failure mode documented in nearly every prior run note; the branch was reset directly to origin/main and the stale commit discarded rather than merged. Per established practice, both stale-draft candidates were individually cross-checked against the real current artifact before re-adding anything: the proposed Constitutional Court enrichment (a March 27, 2026 General Legislation Commission hearing of the National Bar Association, plus the bill's 61-article/9-member structure) was found already fully documented, in materially greater depth, at the Constitutional Court entry since Run 187 — correctly discarded as a duplicate. The Marzouki/Daïmi/Kilani finding survived cross-checking (no match for any of the three names anywhere in origin/main) and was independently re-verified via two further sources (The New Arab, Arab News) before being added as a wholly new standalone entry in the Judicial Organization section: a June 20, 2025 in-absentia terrorism conviction of exiled former president Moncef Marzouki, alongside former presidential adviser Imed Daïmi and former ONAT president Abderrazak Kilani (22 years each), arising from a critical Paris press conference — a genuine gap given it is the only tracked prosecution of a former Tunisian head of state, despite falling squarely within the artifact's 2022–2026 research window. A brief follow-up WebSearch sweep for JORT publications and ARP floor votes dated July 8–9, 2026 found nothing beyond the already-documented Development Plan 2026-2030 plenary session (still without a reported vote outcome) and Finance Law 2026; the Foreign Exchange Code, Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution, and July 11/13/15, 2026 trigger dates remain unchanged from Run 204. One new standalone entry added (289 total); no existing entries required revision.   • (Confirmed Run 206): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-udx7lm) again held only a stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.11 lineage (fourteen commits topping out at a "v2.11" label) with no pull request of its own — confirmed via git fetch origin main, git log --oneline origin/main, and mcp__github__list_pull_requests (empty) before any drafting began — and was reset directly to current origin/main (v4.205, 289 entries, 205 merged runs) per established practice; no stale content existed to cross-check beyond the original fork point. A dedicated research sweep re-checked the full standing watchlist. The 2026–2030 Development Plan plenary (opened July 9, 10:30am, per Run 187/189/190) was independently reconfirmed still in session with deputies raising financing-source and monitoring-mechanism concerns (businessnews.com.tn, africanmanager.com, rtci.tn, all July 8–9) — no vote tally or adoption outcome found by any outlet as of this run's cutoff; a distinct July 10, 2026 plenary item (bill n°39/2026, IBRD/STEG clean-energy loan-guarantee conventions) was identified and correctly not conflated with the Plan vote. The Foreign Exchange Code committee (~90% hearings complete, BCT still non-responsive per chair Maher Ktari) and the Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution-hearing outcome (postponed to July 6, still genuinely unreported by any outlet checked) both remain unchanged since Run 205. The July 11 (ONAT), July 13 (CNAM/biologists tiers-payant), and July 15 (LTDH appeal) trigger dates were each independently reconfirmed still on track and had not yet arrived as of this run's cutoff. A single-sourced Kapitalis claim of a "July 5, 2026" Lourimi (3 years)/Chaouachi (15 years) verdict was checked and found to be the same verdict already documented in the State Security Conspiracy Prosecutions entry (Run 155/190 updates) at July 4, 2026 — a one-day source variance, not a new event; discarded as a duplicate. The Personal Data Protection entry's existing text (committee sessions through June 25, 2026, the 132-vs-123-article resolution) was reconfirmed already current and was not altered. A ten-area structural gap-scan (veterinary profession, architects, private security/gardiennage, Mining Code, Civil Aviation Code, maritime/ports, Insurance Code, cybersecurity agency ANCS, midwifery, real-estate agents/brokers) found eight already covered by existing entries at correct citation. Midwifery was re-examined consistent with Run 170's finding that it exists only at general paramedical-framework level (Law No. 92-74 of August 3, 1992) with no dedicated professional statute or Order — still not a standalone gap, though a live 2026 advocacy campaign for one was noted (lapresse.tn, May 2026). A candidate Real Estate Agents/Brokers citation ("Law 81-55"/"Law 2001-66") could not be corroborated on jurisitetunisie.com or any primary database and was correctly withheld — flagging a new false-positive trap for future runs: generic francophone "agent immobilier"-style professional-licensing searches are prone to pulling French/Belgian/Gabonese law by shared terminology, and should be anchored with "Tunisie"/"JORT" and cross-checked against jurisitetunisie.com or legislation.tn rather than generic legal-SEO sites. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required substantive revision (289 total remains accurate) — a pure confirmation run. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 289 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 289/289 balanced <h3> tags, 1366/1366 balanced <div> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.206/Run 206. The Development Plan plenary vote result remains the single most likely item to resolve before the next run, ahead of the July 11, 13, and 15, 2026 trigger dates and the still-unreported Al Khatt/Mnemty outcome.
  • (v4.207 update, Run 207): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-u08rvk) again held only a stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.11 lineage (its own changelog independently topping out at a freshly-drafted "v2.12" label, 172 entries, adding the same Ben Salem and Chaouachi findings documented below before any cross-check) with no pull request of its own — confirmed via git fetch origin main, git log --oneline origin/main, and git log origin/main..HEAD before pushing anything, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main (v4.206, 289 entries, 206 merged runs) per established practice; the stale draft's Chaouachi finding and Development Plan plenary-session note were both independently confirmed already present in main — the former in the "State Security Conspiracy" Prosecutions entry since the Run 190 correction (95y8m cumulative total, correctly attributed to a distinct anti-terrorism-chamber case rather than the Lourimi/Gharbi docket), the latter in the standing watchlist since Run 187/189/190/206 — both discarded as duplicates rather than re-added. Only one candidate from the stale draft survived cross-checking as a genuine, previously-undocumented gap: the Mohamed Ben Salem customs-conviction, arrest, and release (arrested July 8, released July 9, merits hearing set July 21, 2026) — a full-text search of all 206 prior runs found no match for "Ben Salem" — independently re-verified against four sources (Kapitalis, webdo.tn, Directinfo/WebManagerCenter, Tuniscope, all July 9, 2026) before being added as a new standalone Judicial Organization entry. Two further candidates from the stale draft were investigated and correctly declined: (1) two STEG/World Bank (IBRD) loan-guarantee ratification bills (n°38/2026, n°39/2026) headed to the July 10, 2026 plenary session — already documented in this run's own predecessor note (Run 206) and judged, consistent with that note, too routine for a standalone entry; (2) a trendsnafrica.com claim that the Personal Data Protection reform reaches "full enforcement beginning July 11, 2026" — re-investigated and re-confirmed unreliable per the Run 97 finding, contradicted by every primary source, which places the bill still in ARP Rights and Freedoms Committee review with no vote held. One new standalone entry added (290 total); no existing entries required revision. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 290 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 290/290 balanced <h3> tags, 1370/1370 balanced <div> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.207/Run 207. The Development Plan plenary vote result remains the single most likely item to resolve before the next run, ahead of the July 11, 13, and 15, 2026 trigger dates and the still-unreported Al Khatt/Mnemty outcome.
  • (v4.208 update, Run 208): This run's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-88r6yb) again held only a stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.11 lineage (thirteen commits topping out at a "v2.11" label, 170 entries) unaware of origin/main's independent 207-run history (290 entries, v4.207) — confirmed via git fetch origin main and git log --oneline origin/main before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main per established practice; no stale content existed worth cross-checking. Two dedicated research passes re-checked the standing watchlist (Foreign Exchange Code, Development Plan bill n°42/2026, Personal Data Protection/AI bill, Associations Decree reform, Audiovisual Communication bill, Consumer Credit bill, Sports Structures bill, Animal Protection bill, theft-penalty bill, Games of Chance reform, construction-regularization bill, STEG/IBRD guarantee bills, exchange-violations amnesty bill) plus a scan for new JORT-published legislation and rule-of-law developments in the July 8–10, 2026 window. The Development Plan plenary (opened July 9, with a built-in contingency to extend into July 10) remained without a confirmed vote outcome as of this run's cutoff despite a dedicated verification pass — reconfirmed still the single highest-priority open item. The recent Mosbah, Bensedrine, Hmaidi, and Lourimi/Gharbi rulings, the Fatma Mseddi Ennahdha "terrorist organization" filing, and the reconstituted Criminal Reconciliation Commission (Decree No. 131/2026) were all independently re-surfaced and confirmed already fully documented at their respective entries (Runs 40/91/155/190/201/46). One genuine, independently-verified enrichment survived cross-checking: La Presse de Tunisie (June 19, 2026) reports the General Legislation Committee, chaired by MP Faouzi Daâs, found the pending theft/aggravated-robbery Penal Code amendment (Arts. 261, 262 & 264) so substantially altered during hearings that it asked the bill's initiators to file a new proposal rather than continue amending this one — effectively restarting that bill's legislative process; added to the existing Harsher Theft & Aggravated-Robbery Penalties entry. No new standalone entries were added (290 total remains accurate, since this finding enriches an existing card); one existing entry revised for currency. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 290 unique law-card ids, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 290/290 balanced <h3> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.208/Run 208. The Development Plan plenary vote result remains the single most likely item to resolve before the next run, ahead of the July 11, 13, and 15, 2026 trigger dates and the still-unreported Al Khatt/Mnemty outcome.

A dedicated research sweep re-checked the Development Plan 2026-2030 plenary first: multiple French- and Arabic-language sources (webmanagercenter.com, businessnews.com.tn, La Presse, African Manager, RTCI, Tunisie Numérique, Kapitalis, Mosaïque FM, Le Maghreb) confirm the session opened July 9, 10:30am under Speaker Ibrahim Bouderbala and remained sharply contested (pro-plan deputies calling it a foundational roadmap; critics objecting parliament received a "closed text" with no drafting role) — but no outlet reports a vote tally, pass, or rejection as of this run's cutoff, so it remains unresolved a second consecutive run. The related STEG/IBRD loan-guarantee ratification bills (n°38/39, referred to the same July 10 plenary by the ARP Bureau) likewise show no reported vote outcome. The Foreign Exchange Code (Ktari still awaiting BCT consultation) and Personal Data Protection/AI bill (still in Rights and Freedoms Committee) were reconfirmed unchanged; a regulations.ai claim that the data-protection law reaches "full enforcement beginning July 11, 2026" was re-encountered and correctly rejected as the same unreliable content-marketing pattern flagged since Run 72/97. No new JORT-published legislation was found in the July 9–10, 2026 window. A cross-check of judicial matters surfaced during this sweep (Khayam Turki's July 3 tax conviction, the Lourimi/Gharbi July 3 verdict, Bilel Chaouachi's July 6 appeal, Samir Abdelli's July 7 money-laundering/terrorism conviction, and the July 7 mass sentencing of eighteen TikTok/Instagram content creators) confirmed all five already fully documented at their respective entries — none added as new. One false positive was caught and discarded: an elbalad.news "development plan" article referenced Egypt's 2026/2027 plan, not Tunisia's. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required revision (290 total remains accurate) — a pure confirmation run. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 290 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 290/290 balanced <h3> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.209/Run 209. A dedicated WebSearch/WebFetch sweep re-checked the full standing watchlist a third consecutive run. The 2026–2030 Development Plan plenary and the related STEG/IBRD loan-guarantee bills (n°38/39 of 2026 — independently re-confirmed as a $400M direct BIRD guarantee plus a $30M Clean Technology Fund co-guarantee, both stemming from a November 3, 2025 financing agreement) again showed no reported vote tally, adoption, or rejection across six outlets checked (Business News, La Presse, African Manager, RTCI, Tunisie Numérique, allAfrica). The Foreign Exchange Code was reconfirmed at its ~90%-hearings-complete status (African Manager, Tuniscope), with chair Maher Ktari's floated option of a committee vote without the Banque Centrale still not exercised as of this run's cutoff. The Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution proceedings' July 6 hearing outcome remains unreported by any source four days on. Two candidate leads were investigated and correctly excluded rather than added: (1) a cluster of foreign crypto-industry blog posts (lightspark.com, coinfomania.com) claiming an ARP committee is "considering a draft bill to decriminalize cryptocurrency possession" — no primary Tunisian source (JORT, ARP registry, or mainstream Tunisian press) corroborates any such bill, consistent with this artifact's established practice of declining single-source, non-primary claims; Tunisia's underlying prohibition (BCT foreign-exchange circular, criminalizing unauthorized crypto transactions, up to 5 years' imprisonment) remains accurately reflected in the existing Digital Law & ICT section with no pending-reform claim added. (2) A July 6, 2026 Business News fact-check ("Non, aucun nouveau Code de la route n'est entré en vigueur en Tunisie le 1er juillet 2026") debunking a social-media rumor of a new Traffic Code taking effect July 1 was independently re-surfaced and confirmed to describe a false claim this artifact never carried — no action needed. A re-derivation check of the Status of the Artist and Artistic Professions Law (independently re-confirmed as Law No. 2026-8 of May 13, 2026, adopted 76–1–0) found it already captured at the existing entry since Run 40, with greater precision than this run's independent search. No new JORT-published legislation was found for the July 9–10, 2026 window. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required revision (290 total remains accurate) — a pure confirmation run. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 290 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 290/290 balanced <h3> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.211/Run 211. The 2026–2030 Development Plan plenary (bill n°42/2026, in session since 10:30 AM July 9 under its built-in contingency to extend into July 10) again showed no reported vote tally, adoption, or rejection across the outlets checked (Business News, La Presse, RTCI, allAfrica, Tunisie Numérique). (2) A July 9, 2026 webdo.tn article on the JORT publication of the labor-contracts/anti-subcontracting law was independently re-surfaced and confirmed to describe the same May 2025 reform already comprehensively documented at the existing Law No. 2025-9 entry — no new provision or later JORT publication was identified. A re-derivation check of deputy Fatma Mseddi's Ennahdha "terrorist organization" bill (formally filed July 9, 2026) and the Ghannouchi "Secret Apparatus"/Instalingo case sentencing detail (including co-defendant Mustapha Khedher's life-plus-96-years sentence) both independently re-confirmed already captured at the Political Parties and State Security Conspiracy Prosecutions entries respectively, in each case with equal or greater precision than this run's own research. No new JORT-published legislation was found for the July 9–10, 2026 window. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required revision (290 total remains accurate) — a pure confirmation run. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 290 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 290/290 balanced <h3> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.212/Run 212. Tavily search access remained disabled (HTTP 402, "account currently disabled") for this run as well, so research relied on WebSearch/WebFetch. A dedicated sweep re-checked the full standing watchlist: the 2026–2030 Development Plan plenary and the STEG/IBRD loan-guarantee bills (n°38/39) again showed no reported vote tally, adoption, or rejection across the outlets checked (Business News, La Presse, RTCI, allAfrica, Tunisie Numérique, Radio Express FM); the Foreign Exchange Code (Draft Law 115/2025) and the Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution proceedings (July 6 hearing outcome still unreported) both remain unchanged. The BCT's US$312 million Arab Monetary Fund balance-of-payments facility (signed July 7, announced July 8) was re-surfaced and reconfirmed already captured at the Central Bank Law entry since Run 191. One genuine, previously-undocumented gap was found and closed: the "Tunisie Terre d'Asile" case — five defendants (Sherifa Riahi, Yadh Bousselmi, Mohamed Joo, Mohamed Iqbal Khaled, Imen Ouardani) convicted January 5, 2026 by the Tunis Court of First Instance to two years' suspended imprisonment under Passports & Travel Documents Law Articles 38/39/41 and the 1968 Foreigners Law, after roughly 19 months' pre-trial detention, with 18 co-defendants acquitted. Prior runs (see the Run 186 note) had conflated this case with the already-documented Tunisian Council for Refugees (CTR)/Djemali prosecution at the Passports & Travel Documents Law entry; independent verification (FIDH, ICJ, Amnesty International, Business News, Kapitalis, L'Economiste Maghrébin) confirmed "Tunisie Terre d'Asile" (the Tunisian branch of France Terre d'Asile) is a distinct organization from CTR, with different defendants and a different verdict date (January 5, 2026 vs. November 24, 2025) — a genuine gap, not a duplicate. Added as a new standalone entry cross-referenced from the CTR entry, the Administrative Suspensions entry, and the Affaire Abdallah Saïd entry. One new standalone entry added (291 total, up from 290). A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 291 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates (the one apparent "renewables-law" id collision found by a naive grep, as in Run 208, traces to changelog prose referencing the id inside a <code> tag, not a real duplicate element), 30/30 balanced <section> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.213/Run 213. The Development Plan plenary vote result remains the single most likely item to resolve before the next run, ahead of the July 11 (ONAT), July 13 (CNAM/biologists), and July 15 (LTDH) trigger dates and the still-unreported Al Khatt/Mnemty outcome.
