  • (v4.218 update, Run 218): This session's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-hynsgk) again held only a stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.11/v2.12 lineage (15 commits, last touched July 8, topping out at a "v2.12" draft with 171 entries built against the stale base without checking origin/main first) unaware of origin/main's independent progress to v4.217 (291 entries, 217 merged runs) — confirmed via git fetch origin main and git log --oneline origin/main, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main per established practice; the stale draft's two candidate findings (the anti-subcontracting Law No. 2025-9 and the Development Plan bill's existence) were both confirmed already captured, in each case with materially greater precision, at the existing Law No. 2025-9 and Development Plan entries — no duplication resulted. A dedicated WebSearch sweep resolved the single item every run since Run 71 has flagged as highest-priority: the 2026–2030 Development Plan plenary concluded and the ARP adopted the bill in its entirety on July 10, 2026 by a recorded vote of 64 for, 24 against, and 15 abstentions (La Presse de Tunisie), following the minister's replies to deputies that Tunisie Tribune and African Manager had reported earlier the same day as still in progress. This vote tally was, as of this update, reported only by La Presse among the outlets checked — flagged as single-source pending next-run corroboration, consistent with established practice for time-sensitive same-day findings; the bill still requires presidential promulgation and JORT publication before entering into force. The companion STEG IBRD loan-guarantee bills (n°38/39-2026) were re-checked but no vote outcome had been reported as of this update. A candidate lead — a July 2025-dated wave of Article 230 LGBTQ arrests in Djerba/Tunis (Damj, 14 arrested, 6 already sentenced) surfaced by a search using a 2026 query — was verified via direct WebFetch of the source to actually be dated July 17, 2025, over a year stale, and correctly excluded rather than added as a new development. No new standalone entries were added; one existing entry (Development Plan) was updated with its concluding vote outcome (291 total remains accurate). A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 291 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 291/291 balanced <h3> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.218/Run 218. The STEG loan-guarantee vote outcome and next-run corroboration of the Development Plan tally remain the highest-priority items to resolve, ahead of the July 11 (ONAT), July 13 (CNAM/biologists), and July 15 (LTDH) trigger dates and the still-unreported Al Khatt/Mnemty outcome.
  • (v4.219 update, Run 219): This session's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-papbt1) held only a stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.11 lineage (13 commits, last touched July 8) unaware of origin/main's independent progress to v4.218 (291 entries, 218 merged runs) — confirmed via git fetch origin main and reset directly to current origin/main per established practice before any drafting began, avoiding a repeat of the stale-draft pattern documented in the v4.218 note above. A dedicated WebSearch sweep resolved the two highest-priority items Run 218 left open: (1) the 2026–2030 Development Plan vote tally (64 for, 24 against, 15 abstentions) is now independently corroborated by a second outlet, webdo.tn (July 10, 2026, 12:02 PM), reporting identical figures via direct WebFetch verification — no longer single-sourced, though presidential promulgation and JORT publication remain outstanding; (2) the companion STEG IBRD loan-guarantee bills (n°38/39-2026) were confirmed calendared for the same July 9–10, 2026 plenary window as the Development Plan, but no outlet (La Presse, Tunisie Tribune, African Manager, webdo.tn) reported a STEG vote outcome — genuinely still undecided rather than merely unreported, so both entries were updated to reflect this. A broader sweep of six further pending-bill watchlist items (Foreign Exchange Code, Personal Data Protection/AI bill, Associations Decree reform, Audiovisual Communication bill, Tax Adviser Organic Law, gambling/online-betting reform) and a dedicated JORT search for July 8–10, 2026 found no genuine new developments — a claimed July 29 public-sector recruitment law vote and a French (not Tunisian) Journal Officiel entry were both identified as stale/mislabeled and correctly excluded; a direct fetch of iort.gov.tn returned HTTP 503 and could not independently corroborate the JORT-silence finding. The Investment Code reform entry's existing two-track framing (government track pre-transmission vs. MP-initiated proposition n°26/2024 in committee) was independently re-verified against the same July 3, 2026 IACE/African Manager reporting and found already accurate — no correction needed. No new standalone entries were added; two existing entries (Development Plan, STEG loan guarantees) were updated with genuinely new detail (291 total remains accurate). A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 291 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 291/291 balanced <h3> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.219/Run 219. The July 11 (ONAT), July 13 (CNAM/biologists), and July 15 (LTDH) trigger dates, plus the still-unreported STEG vote outcome and Al Khatt/Mnemty case, remain the highest-priority items for the next run.
  • (v4.220 update, Run 220): This session's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-sttts7) initially held a stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.11 lineage (14 commits, last touched July 8, plus a fresh draft this session — a "v2.12" with 174 entries, PR #234 — built and pushed before checking origin/main) unaware of origin/main's independent progress to v4.219 (291 entries, 219 merged runs) — the same recurring failure mode documented throughout this changelog. Unlike most prior recurrences, the discrepancy was only caught after PR #234 had already been opened and its merge attempt failed with a "merge conflicts" error; all four of the draft's candidate additions (Law No. 2025-9 anti-subcontracting labor reform, Law No. 2026-5 agricultural-debt regularization, Decree-Law No. 2022-13 penal reconciliation, and deputy Fatma Mseddi's July 9, 2026 Ennahdha-terrorist-designation bill) were individually cross-checked via grep against the real current artifact and found already present — in every case with equal or greater precision (see Law No. 2025-9, #agri-debt-regularization, Decree-Law 2022-13, and the Political Parties entry's existing Run 201 update). PR #234 was closed unmerged with an explanatory comment rather than force-merged or reused, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main before restarting, consistent with how PR #32 (Run 28) and other prior stale-PR incidents were handled. A fresh WebSearch sweep then re-checked the full standing watchlist: the 2026–2030 Development Plan vote tally (64–24–15) was independently re-corroborated by a third and fourth outlet (L'Économiste Maghrébin, Tunisie Tribune, both July 10, 2026) but presidential promulgation and JORT publication remain outstanding; the STEG IBRD loan-guarantee bills (n°38/39-2026) remained without a reported plenary vote outcome across every outlet checked; the Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution proceedings' July 6 hearing outcome remains unreported, now four days on; and the Constitutional Court bill (n°100/2025) remained at its already-documented committee stage with no session dated after February 17, 2026 found. One candidate lead — an aa.com.tr article on a bill to strip the Administrative Court of electoral-dispute jurisdiction — was verified via direct WebFetch to be dated September 22, 2024, nearly two years stale, and correctly excluded, consistent with the established stale-news discipline flagged at multiple prior runs (e.g. the original artifact's own v2.8-era note on the same story). No new JORT-published legislation, floor vote, or court verdict dated specifically to July 9–10, 2026 was identified beyond what Run 219 already captured. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required revision (291 total remains accurate) — a pure confirmation run following an unmerged stale-PR incident.   • (v4.222 update, Run 222): Branch was reset directly to current origin/main (v4.221, 291 entries, 221 merged runs) rather than continuing this session's own stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.11 fork, per established practice, before any research or drafting began. The Development Plan's 64–24–15 adoption tally was independently re-confirmed by three further outlets dated July 10, 2026 (La Presse, Réalités, L'Économiste Maghrébin), but presidential promulgation and JORT publication remain outstanding as of this update. The Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution proceedings' July 6, 2026 hearing outcome remains unreported by any source found. One existing entry (STEG loan guarantees) was updated with the July 10 plenary-debate-opened detail; no new standalone entries were added (291 total remains accurate) — a pure confirmation run with one incremental procedural update.   • (v4.223 update, Run 223): Branch was reset directly to current origin/main (v4.222, 291 entries, 222 merged runs) before any research began, avoiding the stale-checkout failure mode documented throughout this changelog. Two independent research passes ran this session: (1) a full re-check of the standing highest-priority watchlist — the STEG IBRD loan-guarantee bills (n°38/39-2026) remained at "debate underway, no tally reported" across every outlet checked (direct WebFetch of the July 10 La Presse article confirms it explicitly describes only the examination stage, not a vote); the Development Plan's 64–24–15 tally was re-confirmed by four concordant outlets (La Presse, L'Économiste Maghrébin, African Manager, Réalités) but no presidential promulgation or JORT publication was found (JORT's next scheduled edition falls Tuesday, July 14, 2026); the Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution proceedings' July 6 hearing outcome remains unreported by any outlet found; and the July 11 (ONAT) trigger date had not yet arrived as of this run's cutoff (still July 10, 2026), with no pre-emptive agenda or outcome reporting located. (2) A deliberately different-angle gap hunt, avoiding the already-exhausted pending-bill watchlist, checked seven areas for entirely new enacted legislation: direct JORT/iort.gov.tn browsing (technically inaccessible — dynamic search interface, no static listing, a known limitation rather than evidence of silence); local/municipal governance reform (none since the already-documented Organic Law No. 2025-4 of March 12, 2025, which repealed the 2018 Code des Collectivités Locales's region/district provisions — see the Local Government entry); education law; Code of Personal Status/inheritance; consumer protection (still Law No. 92-117 of December 7, 1992, unamended); asylum/migration law (Tunisia still has no domestic asylum statute; the only 2026 movement is the EU's unrelated February 2026 "safe country of origin" designation, outside this artifact's domestic-law scope); and telecom/5G regulation (no new text since the January 2025 licensing decree). None of the seven angles surfaced a genuine, sourced, dated new enactment. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required revision (291 total remains accurate) — a pure confirmation run testing fresh research angles rather than repeating prior runs' exact queries. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 291 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 291/291 balanced <h3> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.223/Run 223. The STEG loan-guarantee vote outcome, Development Plan promulgation/JORT publication, and Al Khatt/Mnemty outcome remain the highest-priority items to resolve, ahead of the July 11 (ONAT), July 13 (CNAM/biologists), and July 15 (LTDH) trigger dates.

  • (v4.230 update, Run 230): Branch was reset directly to current origin/main (v4.229, 291 entries, 229 merged runs) before any research began, avoiding the stale-checkout failure mode documented throughout this changelog. A dedicated research agent re-checked the five standing highest-priority watchlist items several hours after Run 229's same-day cutoff: the ONAT lawyers' general assembly was independently re-confirmed as scheduled/held Saturday, July 11, 2026, but no outlet had yet reported its outcome or any resumed-dialogue conditions by this run's cutoff either; the STEG IBRD loan-guarantee bills (n°38/39-2026) remained at "plenary debate underway, no tally reported," matching the existing entry's Run 222 detail verbatim, including the same July 10 La Presse "Énergie: l'ARP se penche..." source; the Development Plan's 64–24–15 adoption tally was reconfirmed with presidential promulgation and JORT publication still outstanding; the Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution proceedings' July 6, 2026 hearing outcome remained unreported by any outlet found, now the sixth consecutive run to find nothing; and the Foreign Exchange Code (Draft Law 115/2025) remained unchanged at its ~90%-hearings-complete committee stage. Four candidate leads surfaced by the sweep were individually cross-checked against the live artifact and each confirmed already fully captured rather than genuinely new: (1) Khayam Turki's July 3, 2026 four-year tax-offense conviction, already documented at the State Security Conspiracy entry's Run 81 update with identical detail; (2) Mnemty president Saadia Mosbah's June 23, 2026 appellate confirmation (8 years) and the five co-defendants' sentences, already documented at the Racial Discrimination Law entry's Run 153 update with materially greater precision (fine amounts, individual co-defendant breakdown, July 7, 2026 HRW corroboration) than this run's own research produced; (3) the STEG loan-guarantee bills' committee/plenary detail, already matching the existing entry verbatim; (4) a Jeune Afrique headline suggesting a new July 2026 Constitutional Court (Organic Law Proposal No. 100/2025) development could not be corroborated by any second source or dated beyond the already-documented March 27, 2026 ONAT-hearing update (Run 187) — treated as an unconfirmed lead rather than added, consistent with this project's single-source discipline. One genuinely new but insufficiently anchored lead was investigated and deliberately withheld rather than forced into a standalone entry: a corruption/money-laundering trial of businessman Maher Chaâbane and more than ten co-defendants, previously undocumented, was reported postponed from July 6 to September 24, 2026 (Tunisie Tribune, single-sourced) — since this is a bare postponement with no first-instance verdict yet, unlike the Mabrouk/Chahed, Haouas/SOREAL, Mekacher, and Turki entries which were each added only upon an actual conviction, it is flagged here as a lead for a future run to revisit once (and if) a verdict is reported. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required revision (291 total remains accurate) — a pure confirmation run. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 291 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 291/291 balanced <h3> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.230/Run 230. The ONAT assembly's actual outcome, the STEG vote tally, the Development Plan's promulgation/JORT publication, and the Al Khatt/Mnemty ruling remain the highest-priority items to resolve, ahead of the July 13 (CNAM/biologists) and July 15 (LTDH) trigger dates, alongside watching for a Maher Chaâbane verdict once the September 24, 2026 hearing occurs.
  • (v4.231 update, Run 231): This session's designated branch initially held a stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.12 lineage (root-commit fork, 171 entries at a self-labeled "v2.12," last touched July 10), unaware of origin/main's independent progress to v4.230 (291 entries, 230 merged runs) — the same recurring failure mode documented throughout this changelog; caught via git fetch origin main/git log --oneline --all and an empty list_pull_requests result for this branch before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main per established practice. A broader sweep for newly-enacted legislation or major rulings dated July 9–11, 2026 surfaced two candidate leads, both cross-checked and confirmed already fully captured rather than genuinely new: (1) MP Fatma Mseddi's July 9, 2026 six-article bill to designate Ennahdha as a Muslim Brotherhood-linked terrorist organization, already documented at the Political Parties entry since Run 201; (2) AMT president judge Anas Hmaidi's July 1, 2026 appellate sentence confirmation (with immediate execution ordered), already documented at the Judicial Statute entry since Run 42. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required revision (291 total remains accurate) — a pure confirmation run. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 291 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.231/Run 231.   • (v4.232 update, Run 232): This session's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-oiv763) held only a stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.12 lineage (15 commits, root-commit fork, 171 entries at a self-labeled "v2.12," last touched July 10), unaware of origin/main's independent progress to v4.231 (291 entries, 231 merged runs) — the same recurring failure mode documented throughout this changelog; caught via git fetch origin main, git log --oneline --all, and an empty list_pull_requests result for this branch before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly to current origin/main per established practice. A dedicated research agent re-checked the six standing highest-priority watchlist items a few hours after Run 231's cutoff: the ONAT lawyers' general assembly (held Saturday, July 11) still had no reported outcome across every outlet checked, likely same-day search-index lag rather than a null result; the STEG IBRD loan-guarantee bills (n°38/39-2026) remained at "plenary debate underway, no tally reported" — a direct WebFetch of the July 10 La Presse article confirmed it explicitly does not report a vote count; the Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution proceedings' July 6, 2026 hearing outcome remained unreported by any outlet found (including a direct fetch of Inkyfada's own homepage), now the eighth consecutive run to find nothing; the Foreign Exchange Code (Draft Law 115/2025) remained unchanged at its ~90%-hearings-complete stage; and the Maher Chaâbane trial's September 24, 2026 postponement was reconfirmed across five outlets, with no verdict expected before then. One genuine, if modest, enrichment was found for the Development Plan 2026–2030 entry: a procedural detail — sourced from Tunisie Numérique quoting deputy Jmaï Zouidi — that the bill's outstanding promulgation is not merely awaiting presidential signature but requires a second-chamber vote at the Conseil National des Régions et des Districts (CNRD) under Constitution Art. 77's bicameral design first, with unresolved inter-chamber disagreements referred to a 10-member joint committee and, failing that, final say reverting to the CNRD; added to the entry to explain why promulgation/JORT publication remain outstanding well past the July 10 ARP floor vote. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required revision beyond that one enrichment (291 total remains accurate) — essentially a confirmation run. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 291 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.232/Run 232. The ONAT assembly's actual outcome, the STEG vote tally, the Development Plan's CNRD vote/promulgation/JORT publication, and the Al Khatt/Mnemty ruling remain the highest-priority items to resolve, ahead of the July 13 (CNAM/biologists) and July 15 (LTDH) trigger dates, alongside watching for a Maher Chaâbane verdict once the September 24, 2026 hearing occurs.

Three candidate leads were investigated and found already fully captured or non-substantive: (1) a July 6, 2026 businessnews.com.tn fact-check debunking a viral rumor that a new Road Traffic Code entered into force July 1, 2026 — confirmed the currently governing statute remains Law No. 99-71 of 1999 as already documented, so no correction was needed; (2) a July 2, 2026 Amnesty International report on refugee/asylum-seeker treatment was fetched directly and found to cite no specific Tunisian decree, circular, or statute beyond the already-documented June 2024 UNHCR-registration-suspension order and the already-correctly-flagged absence of a domestic asylum law (per the Run 82 gap note) — no new entry warranted; (3) Réalités Magazine's July 10 report on the Development Plan's ARP adoption matched the existing 64–24–15 tally verbatim. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required revision (291 total remains accurate) — a pure confirmation run. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required revision (291 total remains accurate) — a pure confirmation run.   • (v4.236 update, Run 236): Branch was reset directly to current origin/main (v4.235, 291 entries, 235 merged runs) before any research began, avoiding the stale-checkout failure mode documented throughout this changelog. A dedicated research agent re-checked the six standing highest-priority watchlist items on the same calendar day as Run 235's cutoff: the ONAT lawyers' general assembly (held earlier today, Saturday, July 11) still had no reported outcome across every outlet checked (Business News, African Manager, Réalités, Mosaïque FM, Kapitalis, Webdo, Tunisie Numérique) — expected, since same-day/Saturday reporting typically lags into the following week; the STEG IBRD loan-guarantee bills (n°38/39-2026) remained at "plenary debate underway, no tally reported," confirmed via a direct WebFetch of the July 10 La Presse article; the Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution proceedings' July 6, 2026 hearing outcome remained unreported by any outlet found, now the eleventh consecutive run to find nothing (a July 8, 2026 Kapitalis civil-society report still lists the case only as "ongoing," suggesting the silence reflects a genuine reporting gap rather than a missed source); the Foreign Exchange Code (Draft Law 115/2025) remained unchanged at its ~90%-hearings-complete stage, BCT still non-responsive; the CNAM/private biologists' tiers-payant suspension was reconfirmed still on track for July 13, 2026 (two days out), distinct from the already-resolved private pharmacists' July 1 dispute; and the Maher Chaâbane trial's September 24, 2026 postponement was reconfirmed, with the added detail that the same July 6 hearing also denied the defense's release requests. One genuine, if modest, enrichment was found for the Development Plan 2026–2030 entry: the CNRD's second-chamber examination has expanded beyond the first district (Bizerte, Béja, Jendouba, Le Kef) to a second district, per an African Manager report of CNRD president Imed Derbali's hearing with Economy Minister Samir Abdelhafidh, citing a 0.51 territorial-development index for Bizerte and a 7.3% poverty rate for Jendouba — still no CNRD floor vote, promulgation, or JORT publication confirmed. Three candidate leads from a fresh gap-scan were each cross-checked and found already fully captured rather than genuinely new: (1) the mass "Créateurs de Contenu" TikTok/Instagram prosecution's July 6, 2026 verdict (1–27 years, 18 defendants), already documented at that standalone entry since Run 155 with identical figures and sourcing; (2) the Makram Jlassi embezzlement case's July 4, 2026 appellate sentence increase (5 to 8 years), already documented at that standalone entry since Run 87 with identical figures; (3) AMT president Anas Hmaidi's July 1, 2026 appellate sentence confirmation, already documented at the Judicial Statute entry since Run 42. A fourth lead — a purported July 9, 2026 Council of Ministers meeting adopting several decree-laws (Fidaa foundation amendment, agricultural-workers' social-security regime, major-public-project procedures) — was traced via direct WebFetch of pm.gov.tn to a mis-dated search-snippet artifact actually describing a September 26, 2024 meeting, and correctly discarded rather than added, consistent with the database/search-snippet mis-dating trap flagged in earlier runs. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required revision beyond the one Development Plan enrichment (291 total remains accurate) — essentially a confirmation run. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 291 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.236/Run 236. The ONAT assembly's actual outcome, the STEG vote tally, the Development Plan's CNRD vote/promulgation/JORT publication, and the Al Khatt/Mnemty ruling remain the highest-priority items to resolve, ahead of the July 13 (CNAM/biologists) and July 15 (LTDH) trigger dates, alongside watching for a Maher Chaâbane verdict once the September 24, 2026 hearing occurs.

A fresh gap-scan across five areas (national AI-regulation strategy, administrative-procedure simplification, cyber-harassment/online violence against women, Council of Ministers decree-law activity, and a repeat check of the Foreign Exchange Code via English-language sources) found nothing not already captured: the AI-strategy and Finance-Law-2026 administrative-simplification leads matched existing entries verbatim, and a Council of Ministers "adoption of several decree-laws" lead again resolved via direct WebFetch of pm.gov.tn to the same mis-dated September 26, 2024 meeting snippet Run 236 had already identified and discarded — reconfirming that trap rather than surfacing anything new. One WebSearch-summary claim was tested and refuted by direct source fetch rather than accepted: a search result stated Tunisia's Law No. 2017-58 "does not criminalize digital violence," but a direct WebFetch of the underlying Le Temps article showed the opposite — the law already extends to "violence perpetrated through social networks or other electronic means," with no bill or amendment proposed — so no entry was added or changed, and this specific search-summary inaccuracy is flagged for future runs alongside the project's existing cross-country-mislabeling and stale-date traps as a third distinct false-positive pattern: summarization errors that invert an article's actual finding. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required revision (291 total remains accurate) — a pure confirmation run. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 291 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.237/Run 237.   • (v4.239 update, Run 239): This session's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-i6thhq) again initially held only a stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.12 lineage (root-commit fork, 171 entries at a self-labeled "v2.12") — the same recurring failure mode documented throughout this changelog — confirmed via git fetch origin main and git log --oneline origin/main against origin/main's independent progress to v4.238 (291 entries, 238 merged runs); the branch was reset directly to origin/main before any drafting, per established practice, and the stale lineage's draft "Chokri Belaïd" entry was independently re-verified from primary sources rather than reused verbatim. A WebSearch/WebFetch sweep re-checked the standing watchlist: the ONAT lawyers' general assembly, held today (Saturday, July 11, 2026), had no reported outcome from any outlet as of this run's cutoff — expected, since the assembly's own scheduled date has only just arrived; the Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution proceedings' July 6, 2026 hearing outcome remains unreported by any source, now the tenth consecutive run to find nothing; the Development Plan's CNRD floor vote, promulgation, and JORT publication remain unconfirmed; and the STEG loan-guarantee bills' rescheduled July 15, 2026 vote has not yet occurred. One genuine, previously-undocumented gap was found and added as a new standalone entry: the direct criminal prosecution over the February 6, 2013 assassination of Chokri Belaïd — distinct from the already-documented "Secret Apparatus" case, which covers a related but legally separate clandestine-organization prosecution with no defendant overlap. Independently verified via direct WebFetch of webdo.tn and directinfo.webmanagercenter.com: former Republic Prosecutor Béchir Akremi was convicted March 4, 2026 to a combined 23 years (evidence-concealment charges) and Ennahdha figure Habib Ellouze to 13 years, with the appeal — before the Tunis Court of Appeal's specialized terrorism chamber — postponed twice (June 23 then July 6, 2026) to a new July 13, 2026 hearing, the same trigger date already tracked for the unrelated CNAM/biologists tiers-payant suspension. The new entry was cross-referenced from the "Secret Apparatus" case entry. One new standalone entry added (292 total, up from 291); one existing entry (State Security Conspiracy Prosecutions) lightly enriched with the cross-reference. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 292 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.239/Run 239. The July 11 (ONAT outcome), July 13 (Chokri Belaïd appeal and CNAM/biologists), July 15 (STEG vote), Development Plan promulgation/JORT publication, and Al Khatt/Mnemty ruling remain the highest-priority items for the next run.
  • (v4.240 update, Run 240): This session's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-javjm1) again initially held only a stale, never-merged v1.0–v2.13 lineage (root-commit fork, 171 entries at a self-labeled "v2.13," including a full drafting pass and a pushed commit against that stale base before the discrepancy was caught) — the same recurring failure mode documented throughout this changelog — confirmed via git fetch origin main and git log --oneline origin/main against origin/main's independent progress to v4.239 (292 entries, 239 merged runs); the stale commit was discarded in full (no content merged forward) and the branch was reset directly to origin/main before any further work, consistent with established practice. Before discovering the stale base, this session's own independent research (three parallel WebSearch sweeps covering newly-enacted legislation, the pending-bill watchlist, and the political-prosecution docket) had already surfaced two candidates; cross-checking both against the real origin/main found the Development Plan's July 10, 2026 plenary adoption (64–24–15) already fully captured (including the CNRD bicameral-review detail added at Run 232 and its expanding-district examination added at Run 236) and correctly discarded as a duplicate. The second candidate survived cross-checking as a genuine, previously-undocumented gap: journalist Zied El Heni faces a second, legally distinct prosecution beyond his already-documented Telecommunications Code Article 86 conviction (Run 63) — the "affaire Ounissi," before the specialized anti-terrorism chamber under the Anti-Terrorism Law rather than an ordinary correctional court, originating from his journalistic verification of information that led to his being summoned by the anti-terrorism unit as early as March 2024 (Kapitalis). A hearing was held July 10, 2026 without El Heni, his family, or his defense counsel being informed and without his transfer to court — independently verified via a WebSearch cross-check of businessnews.com.tn's July 10, 2026 report (a direct WebFetch returned HTTP 503, consistent with the intermittent 503s several prior runs have logged against Tunisian outlets) corroborated by Réalités Magazine and Turess/Business News republications of the same reporting; added as an extension to the Telecommunications Code entry, which already hosts El Heni's Article 86 case. A follow-up WebSearch sweep re-checked the standing watchlist a few hours after Run 239's cutoff: the ONAT assembly's outcome remained unreported by every outlet checked, still expected given same-day/weekend reporting lag; the Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution proceedings' July 6, 2026 hearing outcome remained unreported, now the eleventh consecutive run to find nothing; and the Development Plan's CNRD floor vote, promulgation, and JORT publication remained unconfirmed. No new standalone entries were added (292 total remains accurate, since this run's finding extends an existing card); one existing entry (Telecommunications Code) enriched. A structural audit corrected a stat-counter bug inherited from prior runs: the header's "Laws Documented" stat had been left at 291 for at least one full run while the true card count was already 292 — corrected here, alongside synchronizing all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) at v4.240/Run 240. The July 11 (ONAT outcome), July 13 (Chokri Belaïd appeal and CNAM/biologists), July 15 (STEG vote), Development Plan promulgation/JORT publication, and Al Khatt/Mnemty ruling remain the highest-priority items for the next run, alongside watching for a verdict in El Heni's newly-documented anti-terrorism-chamber case.

One genuine, previously-undocumented development was found and added as an enrichment: a first-ever operation to repatriate Tunisian women and children (reportedly 200+ children, 60+ women, fathers/husbands affiliated with Daesh/ISIS) from Syrian camps and prisons was reported underway July 10, 2026, per OTDH president Mostafa Abdelkebir (Businessnews.com.tn, Kapitalis, both July 10, 2026) — corroborated across two independent outlets but resting on a single named source (the OTDH president), with no official Tunisian government confirmation of the operation, its scale, or any implementing legal framework found in this run; added to the Countering Terrorism Law entry with this single-source caveat explicit, rather than as a new standalone entry, since no dedicated statute or decree was identified to anchor one. No new standalone entries were added (292 total remains accurate); one existing entry (Countering Terrorism and Suppression of Money Laundering) enriched. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 292 unique law-card ids, balanced <div>/<section>/<h3> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.242/Run 242.   • (v4.244 update, Run 244): This session's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-umcmga) again initially held only a stale, never-merged lineage (12 commits topping out at a self-labeled "v2.12," dated July 10, 2026, with no matching pull request found via a direct GitHub PR search on this exact branch head) — the same recurring failure mode documented in Runs 238–243 immediately above. The sweep additionally re-surfaced three prosecutions (Elyes Chaouachi's July 3, 2026 sentence bringing his cumulative total past 95 years; AMT president judge Anas Hmaidi's July 1, 2026 appeal confirmation with immediate execution; and the Ajmi Lourimi/Mossaâb Gharbi terrorism-chamber verdicts) as apparent candidates, but a targeted grep cross-check found all three already folded into the consolidated "State Security Conspiracy" Prosecutions entry with materially more precision (exact dates, sentence breakdowns, and distinguishing docket details) than the fresh research alone provided — confirming, rather than extending, existing coverage. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required revision (292 total remains accurate) — a second consecutive pure confirmation run. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 292 unique law-card ids, 1378/1378 balanced <div> tags, 292/292 balanced <h3> tags, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, all 30 TOC anchors resolving, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.244/Run 244. No genuine new-content gap was found this run — a WebSearch sweep for July 11, 2026 developments (ONAT general assembly outcome, Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution ruling, Chokri Belaïd/CNAM/STEG watchlist items, and a CNAM "E-CNAM" digital-platform migration ruled out as an IT rollout with no decree basis, hence out of scope) surfaced nothing beyond what Run 244 already recorded. Post-fix audit: 292 unique law-card ids, 0 broken internal anchors (down from 2), 1378/1378 balanced <div>, 292/292 <h3>, 30/30 <section>, and all three version markers synchronized at v4.245/Run 245. One genuine, well-corroborated correction was found and applied to the STEG IBRD loan-guarantee bills entry: the "Tuesday, July 15, 2026" reschedule date carried since Run 238 and repeated in Runs 244–247 is a date/weekday mismatch — direct date computation confirms July 15, 2026 falls on a Wednesday, not a Tuesday, while July 14, 2026 is the actual Tuesday following the Friday, July 10, 2026 adjournment. A July 11, 2026 La Presse republication of the African Manager story was fetched directly via WebFetch and confirmed it still only says "mardi prochain" (next Tuesday) without independently naming a calendar date, meaning "July 15" was never actually sourced — it appears to have been introduced as an arithmetic slip in Run 238 and then silently propagated by four subsequent runs' watchlist summaries without independent verification, a new failure-mode variant (compounding an unverified derived date across runs) worth flagging alongside the already-documented cross-country-mislabeling and database-timestamp traps. Corrected the entry's date field to July 14, 2026 and added a Run 248 correction note; the STEG vote's actual outcome remains unreported as of this update. No new standalone entries were added and no other existing entry required revision (292 total remains accurate). A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 292 unique law-card ids, all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.248/Run 248. Three candidate leads were investigated and found already fully captured, not gaps: judge Anas Hmaidi's July 1 appeal confirmation and the UN/ACHPR reactions to it (already documented at the Judicial Statute entry since Runs 42/60); Samir Abdelli's 18-year terrorism/money-laundering sentence, re-surfaced this run under the alternate transliteration "Abdali" (already a standalone entry since an earlier run); and MP Fatma Mseddi's July 9, 2026 organic bill to classify Ennahdha as a terrorist organization (already documented at the Political Parties entry since Run 201). One genuine, two-source-corroborated gap was closed: the private biologists' union's confirmed July 13, 2026 tiers-payant suspension with CNAM — tracked unresolved since Runs 31/46/69 — was in fact called off on July 10, 2026, after the union met with the Minister of Social Affairs and CNAM officials, who committed to settling the outstanding debt to private labs within an agreed timeframe (Business News, July 11, 2026); added to the CNAM entry as a resolution of that long-tracked open item, consistent with the earlier pharmacists' July 1 fix. No new standalone entries were added (292 total remains accurate, since the finding extends an existing card); one existing entry (CNAM) revised for currency. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 292 unique law-card ids with the sole raw id= duplicate again confirmed a false positive (the changelog's own code-quoted reference to id="renewables-law"), 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 1368/1368 balanced <div> tags, 292/292 balanced <h3> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.255/Run 255. Added as a new standalone Chokri Belaïd Assassination Trial — Main Case entry, with cross-references added to both the Akremi/Ellouze entry and the Secret Apparatus case's text. One new standalone entry added (293 total, up from 292); two existing entries (Akremi/Ellouze, Secret Apparatus) lightly enriched with cross-references. A dedicated research agent cross-checked nineteen previously-tracked pending bills (data protection, Constitutional Court, Exchange Code/crypto reform, Development Plan, Mining Code, Hydrocarbons Code, Investment/Water/Environmental Framework Code revisions, Startup Act 2.0, plastic-bags bill, Sports Structures organic law, stadium-violence bill, three tourism bills, Competition Law reform, HAICA reorganization, dormant Digital Code, E-Commerce bill, Electoral Code, informal-housing regularization, and the Ennahda "terrorist organization" designation bill) for movement between July 8–13, 2026: none showed a status change beyond confirming the Development Plan's already-documented July 10 ARP adoption (64–24–15) and its now-active CNRD hearing stage, still without a CNRD vote. Targeted JORT searches ("JORT juillet 2026," "loi promulguée," "décret-loi juillet 2026") found no newly enacted or promulgated legislation in the window. One genuinely new item was found and added as a standalone entry: the Draft Organic Law on Enforcement of Court Rulings ("Ending Administrative Impunity"), a 19-article member's bill filed by 10 deputies criminalizing non-enforcement of court rulings by public administration, reported July 10, 2026 by La Presse and reprinted by allAfrica July 11 — added to the Judicial Organization section (295 total, up from 294). A second lead — two animal-welfare bills reportedly accelerated in committee after a viral horse-mistreatment video — was cross-checked and found already fully captured, at greater precision (including the n° 66/2024 vs. n° 74/2024 bill-number split and tiered-penalty detail) in the existing Animal Protection bill entry through its Run 204 update, and was correctly not re-added. A structural gap-scan of the Constitutional Law section — cross-checking the four independent constitutional bodies nominally created by the 2014/2022 constitutional architecture (Constitutional Court, Instance des Droits de l'Homme, a permanent anti-corruption authority, and ISIE) — found one genuine, previously-undocumented gap: no entry existed anywhere in the artifact for the Instance des Droits de l'Homme (IDH), established by Organic Law No. 2018-51 of October 29, 2018 (adopted 144–0–0) pursuant to 2014 Constitution Arts. 125/128 (carried forward by 2022 Constitution Arts. 126/128), despite the existing INLUCC and Constitutional Court entries already documenting the other two non-functional bodies in this same cluster — corroborated across French Wikipedia, the Council of Europe's Tunis office, and the 2018-era Businessnews/Webmanagercenter adoption coverage; added as a new standalone entry, cross-linked in both directions with the Constitutional Court and INLUCC entries. Added as a new standalone entry (296 total, up from 295); no existing entries required revision. Both known recurring hoaxes were re-tested and again correctly withheld: the debunked "new Traffic Code took effect July 1, 2026" claim (reconfirmed via a July 6, 2026 businessnews.com.tn fact-check) and the dpo-consulting.com/regulations.ai "Personal Data Protection full application began July 11, 2026" marketing claim. No new standalone entries were added and no existing entry required revision (296 total remains accurate) — a pure confirmation run. The third finding was genuinely new: a Ministerial Order of October 14, 2025 (implementing CIRPP/IS Art. 59 ter) phasing in a mandatory digital fiscal cash-register regime for on-site food/beverage-consumption businesses — Phase 1 (Nov. 1, 2025, classified tourist restaurants and 2nd/3rd-category cafés) already in force, Phase 2 (July 1, 2026) newly extending the obligation to all other companies in the sector, with Phases 3–4 (July 2027/2028) reaching individual taxpayers — confirmed via La Presse, Tunisie Numérique, Le Temps News, and L'Economiste Maghrébin, and added as an update note to the Income Tax Code entry (distinguished from the existing Finance Law 2026 Art. 56 e-invoicing mandate, which governs invoice issuance rather than point-of-sale recording). A quick re-check of the five standing watchlist items found no change since Run 279: the Chokri Belaïd appeal hearing (held today, July 13) remains without a published ruling in any outlet checked; the ONAT general-assembly outcome remains unreported; the STEG/IBRD loan-guarantee bills' vote remains scheduled for July 14 (tomorrow); the Development Plan remains adopted (July 10) with no CNRD vote/promulgation/JORT publication yet found; and the Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution proceedings remain unresolved. This run enriched one existing entry rather than adding a new standalone card (296 total remains accurate). A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 296 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.280/Run 280. The same five watchlist items remain the highest-priority items for the next run, as does the standing recommendation that future sessions run git fetch origin main before any drafting.

The sixth item, the STEG/IBRD loan-guarantee bills, produced a genuine new development: a July 13, 2026 businessnews.com.tn report that deputy Bilel El Mechri publicly accused Speaker Bouderbala of engineering the July 10→14 postponement after seeing opposing votes outnumber favorable ones, calling it a "scandal" and announcing a boycott of the July 14 sitting — a procedural-controversy detail beyond the bare rescheduling already on record, added as an update to the existing entry (296 total remains accurate; no new standalone card). A parallel broad scan's one candidate lead — a claim that a GDPR-modeled Personal Data Protection organic law reached "full enforcement" July 11, 2026, sourced to dpo-consulting.com/regulations.ai — was checked against the Personal Data Protection entry and rejected as the same unreliable marketing claim already investigated and correctly withheld at Runs 281 and 275; the bill remains under ARP Rights & Freedoms Committee review with no committee report or vote, and the 2004 law remains the only law in force. No newly enacted law, decree, or JORT publication was identified this pass. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 296 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, and all three version markers (title, header meta, footer) synchronized at v4.283/Run 283. The Chokri Belaïd ruling, ONAT outcome, STEG vote outcome (due today, July 14), Development Plan CNRD floor vote/promulgation, Al Khatt/Mnemty outcome, and LTDH appeal (July 15) remain the highest-priority items for the next run.

One genuinely new item was identified and added as a standalone card: a July 10, 2026 La Presse editorial ("Quand la justice retrouve ses clés"), corroborated by a July 11, 2026 allAfrica republication, describes a deputy-initiated 19-article organic bill carried by roughly ten deputies, recently submitted to the ARP's Commission de la Législation Générale, introducing prison terms up to three years and increased fines plus a mandatory annual-transparency-report regime aimed at administrative non-compliance and "powerful lobbies." A direct WebFetch of the primary La Presse source confirmed these specific factual details but did not surface an official bill title, number, or sponsoring deputies' names — added as a new Administrative Law entry with that sourcing gap explicitly flagged as a priority to resolve. No other newly enacted law, decree, or JORT publication was identified this pass. The ONAT item produced an apparent lead — the agent, working without direct access to this artifact, independently proposed that "ONAT" denotes the lawyers' Ordre National des Avocats de Tunisie rather than an architects' body — but a direct check of the existing Bar Law entry confirmed this identification, and the July 11 general-assembly date itself, were already established and cross-referenced there since Run 170/249; the assembly's outcome itself remains unreported by every source, now well past 35 consecutive runs. The STEG vote tally and the Chokri Belaïd verdict remain the two items most likely to resolve within the next same-day or next-day pass; the ONAT outcome, Development Plan CNRD floor vote/promulgation, Al Khatt/Mnemty outcome, the LTDH appeal (due tomorrow, July 15), and the anti-lobbying bill's identifiers remain the standing priorities regardless.
  • (v4.293 update, Run 293): This session's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-hp56w8) again held only a stale, disconnected v2.2–v2.17 lineage (its own PR #288 already merged into main at that point, but main continuing far beyond it) unaware of origin/main's independent progress to v4.292 (297 entries, 292 merged runs) — the identical recurring failure mode documented in Runs 95, 147, 234–292 above; confirmed via git fetch origin main, git log --oneline origin/main, and git merge-base before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly onto current origin/main via git checkout -B per established practice. Still the same July 14, 2026 calendar day, a WebSearch sweep re-checked all seven standing watchlist items directly. All seven remain unresolved, consistent with Run 292: the STEG/IBRD loan-guarantee bills (n° 38/2026, 39/2026) — a July 13, 2026 Business News report on deputies' "scandal" accusation against Bouderbala over the vote's postponement remains the latest coverage, with no outlet checked (Business News, La Presse, African Manager, allAfrica) yet reporting a July 14 vote tally; the Chokri Belaïd Akremi/Ellouze appeal (heard July 13) remains without a published verdict from any outlet checked; the ONAT general assembly (held July 11) outcome remains unreported by every source searched, now well past 42 consecutive runs; the Development Plan remains adopted at the ARP (July 10, 64–24–15) with no CNRD floor vote, promulgation, or JORT publication found; the Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution proceedings' outcome remains unreported since the July 6, 2026 postponement (confirmed still pending, distinct from the Mnemty founder's separate, already-documented Saadia Mosbah criminal appeal); the LTDH appeal correctly remains unresolved, its hearing not due until tomorrow, July 15, 2026 (per Kapitalis and Business News, confirming the June 10, 2026 rescheduling to that date); and the anti-lobbying/transparency bill's official title, number, and sponsor names remain unconfirmed despite a further targeted search, which surfaced only the same 19-article, ten-deputy, three-year-imprisonment details already on record (La Presse, July 10; allAfrica, July 11). One genuine, previously-undocumented gap was identified and closed: a distinct pending parliamentary bill on cybersecurity, Proposition de loi n° 2024-36 (deposited April 30, 2024), was found via legislation-securite.tn — one of this artifact's own cited sources — and independently corroborated via a second search identifying its ARP General Legislation Commission examination (begun January 25, 2026, with Communication Technologies Minister Nizar Ben Neji heard January 29, 2026). This proposition de loi is materially distinct from the existing Decree-Law No. 2023-17 — a different institutional structure (dedicated National Cybersecurity Authority plus Strategic and Crisis Management Committees vs. ANCS's existing mandate), different penalty ceiling (20,000–60,000 TND vs. 500,000 TND), and a member's bill rather than a decree-law — and had only been checked once before, in Run 71 (July 3, 2026), as an unenacted lead with no bill number, without being added as a standalone pending-bill entry. Added as a new Digital Law entry, tagged Pending, with its unclear relationship to Decree-Law No. 2023-17 explicitly flagged as an open question for future runs. A secondary lead — a legal-doctrine.com article describing the same bill in less specific, undated terms — was independently corroborated rather than relied upon alone, consistent with this project's sourcing standards. One new standalone entry added (298 total); no existing entries required revision beyond the new cross-reference added to the Decree-Law No. 2023-17 entry's own text. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 298 unique law-card ids, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, and all three version markers plus the header stat counter synchronized at v4.293/Run 293/298. The STEG vote tally and the Chokri Belaïd verdict remain the two items most likely to resolve within the next same-day or next-day pass; the ONAT outcome (42+ consecutive runs unreported), Development Plan CNRD floor vote/promulgation, Al Khatt/Mnemty outcome, the LTDH appeal (due tomorrow, July 15), the anti-lobbying bill's identifiers, and the new cybersecurity bill's status/relationship to Decree-Law No. 2023-17 remain the standing priorities for the next run.

A WebSearch sweep re-checked all standing watchlist items directly. All remain unresolved: the STEG/IBRD loan-guarantee bills — the July 14 (today) plenary resumption at 10:00 AM was independently reconfirmed as scheduled, with no outlet checked (Business News, La Presse, African Manager, allAfrica, Directinfo/WebManagerCenter) yet reporting an actual vote tally; the Chokri Belaïd Akremi/Ellouze appeal (heard July 13) remains without a published verdict from any outlet checked; the ONAT general assembly (held July 11) outcome remains unreported by every source searched, now well past 43 consecutive runs; the Development Plan remains adopted at the ARP (July 10, 64–24–15) with no CNRD floor vote, promulgation, or JORT publication found; the Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution proceedings' outcome remains unreported since the July 6, 2026 postponement; the LTDH appeal hearing is due tomorrow, July 15, 2026, and remains correctly unresolved; the anti-lobbying/transparency bill's official identifiers remain unconfirmed; and the new cybersecurity bill's (Proposition de loi n° 2024-36) relationship to Decree-Law No. 2023-17 remains unclarified. Separately, the Anas Hmaidi/AMT judicial-independence case (already extensively documented via Runs 40, 42, 46, 60) and the Bensedrine/state-security-conspiracy convictions (Run 40) were independently re-confirmed via a fresh ICJ report and found already captured at equal or greater precision, with no new procedural development since the July 1, 2026 appeals ruling. One genuine, previously-undocumented gap was identified and closed: a deputy-initiated 17-article bill on natural-resources governance, sovereignty, and transparency (filed end of January 2026 by ten ARP deputies, heard by the Industry/Commerce/Natural Resources/Energy/Environment Commission in a session reported April 15, 2026) — independently corroborated across three dated, non-duplicative outlets (Le Temps News, February 5, 2026; allAfrica/La Presse and WebManagerCenter/Directinfo, both April 15, 2026) — was found to be wholly absent from the artifact despite its detailed, distinctive provisions (a new High Authority for Natural Resources, a 60%/25% state/regional revenue split, a sovereign wealth fund, and a national contract-transparency platform with criminalized data falsification). This is materially distinct from the already-documented, IACE-flagged Hydrocarbons Code and Mining Code sovereignty reforms, both of which remain stalled at the pre-ARP-transmission executive stage rather than in active committee review like this bill. Added as a new Energy & Natural Resources entry, tagged Pending. One new standalone entry added (299 total); no existing entries required revision. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 299 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, and all three version markers plus the header stat counter synchronized at v4.294/Run 294/299. The STEG vote tally and the Chokri Belaïd verdict remain the two items most likely to resolve within the next same-day or next-day pass; the ONAT outcome (43+ consecutive runs unreported), Development Plan CNRD floor vote/promulgation, Al Khatt/Mnemty outcome, the LTDH appeal (due tomorrow, July 15), the anti-lobbying bill's identifiers, the cybersecurity bill's status, and this run's new natural-resources bill's committee-vote status remain the standing priorities for the next run.

Tavily again returned HTTP 402 "account disabled" on the first four queries attempted, so this run relied on WebSearch alone, consistent with Runs 68, 71, 76, 86, 107, 131, and 136. A WebSearch sweep re-checked all eight standing watchlist items directly. All eight remain unresolved, still the same July 14, 2026 calendar day as Runs 289–294: the STEG/IBRD loan-guarantee bills' 10:00 AM plenary resumption was independently reconfirmed as having occurred as scheduled, but no outlet checked (Business News, La Presse, African Manager, allAfrica, Tunisie Numérique, Le Temps News, L'Economiste Maghrébin, Tustex) has yet reported a vote tally or outcome; the Chokri Belaïd Akremi/Ellouze appeal (heard July 13) remains without a published verdict; the ONAT general assembly (held July 11) outcome remains unreported, now well past 44 consecutive runs; the Development Plan remains adopted at the ARP (July 10, 64–24–15) with no CNRD floor vote, promulgation, or JORT publication found; the Al Khatt dissolution hearing (rescheduled to July 6, 2026) has no reported outcome from any outlet checked, and the related Mnemty proceeding remains likewise unreported since its own last-known postponement; the LTDH appeal hearing is due tomorrow, July 15, 2026, and remains correctly unresolved; the anti-lobbying/transparency bill's official title, number, and sponsor identifiers remain unconfirmed despite a further targeted search, which surfaced only the same 19-article, ten-deputy, three-year-imprisonment details already on record; and the cybersecurity bill's (Proposition de loi n° 2024-36) relationship to Decree-Law No. 2023-17 remains unclarified. This run's genuine yield was a refinement, a resolved false-positive trap, and a broad negative gap-scan rather than a new standalone entry. Refinement: the STEG loan-guarantee entry gained a precise currency-discrepancy note — a June 28, 2026 Le Temps News figure of €384.8 million for Draft Law n° 38/2026's underlying loan, alongside the already-documented US$400 million figure from La Presse/Tunisie Numérique — left unreconciled pending a primary bill text, consistent with this artifact's practice for other unresolved figure conflicts (e.g. the Personal Data Protection bill's 123-vs-132 article count). False-positive resolved: a UNICEF statement and a legislation-securite.tn (DCAF) page both surfaced in fresh search results describing "adoption" of an organic bill on the Instance des Droits de l'Homme; direct verification (WebFetch of the legislation-securite.tn page) confirmed both sources describe the same already-documented October 16, 2018 unanimous ARP adoption (Organic Law No. 2018-51) rather than any new 2026 development — a new variant of the recurring stale-date/old-adoption-resurfacing-as-new trap already flagged for the "conseils locaux et régionaux" law (Run 148/150) and the AIMEC ratification (Run 77); future runs encountering this same UNICEF/legislation-securite.tn pairing on the IDH should recognize it as this same non-development rather than re-investigating from scratch. A fifteen-area structural gap-scan chosen to cover ground distinct from prior scans' lists (private security companies, notaries, bailiffs/huissiers, credit-rating agencies, insurance brokers, statutory auditors, medical-malpractice/professional-liability insurance, digital identity/e-ID, e-invoicing, single-window customs, Nagoya Protocol/genetic-resources access, archaeological/cultural-heritage protection, whistleblower protection, public procurement, and sworn translators/interpreters) found all fifteen already covered by existing entries — no new gap identified. No new standalone entries were added (299 total remains accurate); no existing entry required substantive revision beyond the STEG currency-discrepancy note. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 299 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, and all version markers plus the header stat counter synchronized at v4.295/Run 295/299. The STEG vote tally and the Chokri Belaïd verdict remain the two items most likely to resolve within the next same-day or next-day pass; the ONAT outcome (44+ consecutive runs unreported), Development Plan CNRD floor vote/promulgation, Al Khatt/Mnemty outcomes, the LTDH appeal (due tomorrow, July 15), the anti-lobbying bill's identifiers, and the cybersecurity bill's status remain the standing priorities for the next run.

This time the discrepancy was caught only after a full v2.18 draft had already been built on the stale base and a PR (#318) opened and merge-checked, returning a "dirty" mergeable_state; confirmed via git fetch origin main and git log --oneline origin/main, and the branch was reset directly onto current origin/main via git checkout -B rather than merging the stale draft — a near-identical failure sequence to the one documented in the v4.62 note above (PR opened against a stale base, caught by a dirty mergeable_state, discarded rather than merged). The stale draft's sole candidate finding — a new pending-reform entry for the STEG/World Bank loan-guarantee bills — was cross-checked and found already comprehensively documented at the existing STEG/IBRD loan-guarantee entry with far greater precision (exact bill numbers, committee dates, the Mechri/Bouderbala controversy, the €384.8M/US$400M currency discrepancy) than the discarded draft independently produced; no duplication resulted. A dedicated research agent then re-checked all eight standing watchlist items directly. One resolved: the Chokri Belaïd Akremi/Ellouze appeal — a July 14, 2026 webdo.tn report states the Tunis Court of Appeal's specialized terrorism-chamber increased sentences on appeal, raising Béchir Akremi's from 23 to 29 years and Habib Ellouze's and co-defendant Hassan Ben Brik's from 13 to 17 years each, with post-release judicial supervision added; as of this update this figure is reported by webdo.tn alone, with no second outlet checked (Business News, La Presse, Mosaïque FM, Directinfo/WebManagerCenter, Espace Manager) yet corroborating it — added to the entry flagged as single-source pending next-run corroboration, consistent with this artifact's established practice for time-sensitive same-day findings (e.g. the Run 218 Development Plan tally). The remaining seven items were reconfirmed unresolved: the STEG/IBRD loan-guarantee bills' July 14 plenary resumption remains without a reported vote tally from any outlet checked; the ONAT general assembly (held July 11) outcome remains unreported, now well past 45 consecutive runs; the Development Plan remains adopted at the ARP with no CNRD floor vote, promulgation, or JORT publication found; the Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution proceedings' outcome remains unreported since the July 6, 2026 postponement, distinct from Mnemty founder Saadia Mosbah's separate, already-documented criminal appeal (her 8-year sentence was itself confirmed June 23, 2026, per a fresh cross-check, but that is the criminal case, not the association-dissolution proceeding, and is left undisturbed as already captured); the LTDH appeal hearing remains due tomorrow, July 15, 2026; and the anti-lobbying/transparency bill's and cybersecurity bill's (Proposition de loi n° 2024-36) identifiers/relationship-to-Decree-Law-2023-17 remain exactly as documented at Runs 284/293. A general sweep for July 13–14, 2026 developments outside the watchlist surfaced two candidates, both cross-checked and confirmed already fully captured rather than new: AMT president judge Anas Hmaidi's appellate conviction (documented since Run 42) and businessman Ridha Charfeddine's July 13, 2026 reconciliation-postponement request in the specialized financial-corruption chamber, which — lacking any ruling, plea, or enacted text of its own — falls below this project's standing inclusion threshold for ordinary in-progress prosecutions without a distinct legislative or judicial-outcome anchor (the same threshold applied to the Run 93 banking-strike and Run 102 labor-unrest candidates) and was correctly left undocumented. No new standalone entries were added (299 total remains accurate, since the resolved finding extends an existing card); one existing entry (Chokri Belaïd Akremi/Ellouze) revised for currency. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 299 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, and all version markers plus the header stat counter synchronized at v4.296/Run 296/299. Corroboration of the Chokri Belaïd sentence increase from a second outlet, the STEG vote tally, the ONAT outcome (45+ consecutive runs unreported), the Development Plan's CNRD floor vote/promulgation, the Al Khatt/Mnemty outcome, and the LTDH appeal (due tomorrow, July 15) remain the standing priorities for the next run.

A direct WebFetch of jort.tn's lois index and a targeted "JORT juillet 2026 loi promulguée" sweep found no newly indexed July 2026 JORT publication beyond what existing entries already cite. A stale draft had already been built against the outdated v2.17 base and opened as PR #320 (two candidate findings: a standalone Social and Solidarity Economy Law entry, and a digital-cash-register sub-decree note) before the discrepancy was caught via git fetch origin main; the attempted merge correctly failed with a "merge conflicts" error, both candidate findings were confirmed already present in the real artifact (Social and Solidarity Economy Law, added Run 81; the cash-register phase-in, added to the Income Tax Code entry at Run 280), PR #320 was closed unmerged with an explanatory comment, and the branch was hard-reset directly onto current origin/main via git checkout -B plus a force-with-lease push before restarting, per established practice. A WebSearch sweep then re-checked all eight standing watchlist items directly. One partial refinement: a direct WebFetch of the same webdo.tn article underlying the Run 296 Chokri Belaïd sentence-increase finding confirmed the post-release "mise sous contrôle administratif" measure was ordered for all three convicts (Akremi, Ellouze, Ben Brik), not just "at least one" as Run 296 had conservatively phrased it — corrected in the entry. A matching African Manager headline ("17 ans de prison pour Béchir Akremi et Habib Ellouze") was located, which would supply the second-outlet corroboration awaited since Run 296, but its full text returned HTTP 403 on direct fetch and could not be independently read to confirm the exact 29/17/17 breakdown, so — consistent with this artifact's practice of not clearing a single-source flag on an unread headline alone — the flag stands. The remaining seven watchlist items were reconfirmed unresolved with no material change: the STEG/IBRD loan-guarantee bills' vote tally remains unreported by every outlet checked (African Manager, La Presse, Le Temps News, allAfrica) despite the July 14 plenary resumption; the ONAT general assembly (held July 11) outcome remains unreported, now well past 47 consecutive runs; the Development Plan's CNRD stage remains under active regional-disparity hearings with no floor vote, promulgation, or JORT publication found; the Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution proceedings remain pending with no ruling reported since the July 6, 2026 postponement; the LTDH appeal hearing was reconfirmed via a second source (Business News, in addition to Kapitalis) as scheduled for tomorrow, July 15, 2026, and remains correctly unresolved; the anti-lobbying/transparency bill's official proposition number remains unidentified in any source checked; and the cybersecurity bill's (Proposition de loi n° 2024-36, dated April 30, 2024, confirmed via legislation-securite.tn as a still-pending "projet de loi" whose implementing texts have not been published) precise relationship to Decree-Law No. 2023-17 remains unclarified. No newly enacted primary law, decree-law, or JORT publication was identified this pass. No new standalone entries were added (299 total remains accurate, since the sole finding refines an existing entry). Also fixed a recurring version-string synchronization bug (the same class flagged at v4.13, v4.25, v4.50, v4.59, v4.62, v4.97, v4.128, and v4.146): the header meta line had been left at "Version 4.296 (Run 296)" for a full run while the title tag and footer already read "v4.297 (Run 297)"; all three version markers are now synchronized at v4.298/Run 298. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 299 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 1,406/1,406 balanced <div> tags, and the header stat counter matching the true card count (299). Corroboration of the Chokri Belaïd sentence increase from a fully-readable second outlet, the STEG vote tally, the ONAT outcome (47+ consecutive runs unreported), the Development Plan's CNRD floor vote/promulgation, the Al Khatt/Mnemty outcome, and the LTDH appeal (due tomorrow, July 15) remain the standing priorities for the next run — as does the standing recommendation that future sessions run git fetch origin main before any drafting, to avoid this branch-divergence failure mode recurring yet again.
  • (v4.299 update, Run 299): This session's designated branch (claude/nice-edison-68vk44) again held only a stale, disconnected v2.0–v2.17 lineage (22 commits) sharing no history with origin/main beyond the project's root commit, unaware of origin/main's independent progress to v4.298 (299 entries, 298 merged runs) — the identical recurring failure mode documented in Runs 95, 147, 234–298 above; confirmed via git fetch origin main, git log --oneline origin/main, and mcp__github__search_pull_requests (zero results for this branch's head) before any drafting began, and the branch was reset directly onto current origin/main via git checkout -B per established practice. A dedicated research agent re-checked all eight standing watchlist items directly, plus a general sweep for undocumented July 13–14, 2026 developments. Two items resolved or refined: (1) the Chokri Belaïd Akremi/Ellouze/Ben Brik sentence-increase finding (29/17/17 years plus administrative supervision) gained independent corroboration from a second, fully-readable source — Italpress, an Italian wire agency (July 14, 2026) — matching webdo.tn's figures exactly; still no Tunisian domestic outlet beyond webdo.tn has published a readable dedicated article, so the entry now records this as "corroborated by a non-Tunisian wire, domestic multi-source confirmation still pending" rather than clearing the flag outright, and separately notes the still-unread African Manager headline appears internally inconsistent (citing "17 ans" for Akremi, versus 29 years everywhere else) and is now treated as a likely garbled headline rather than a live corroboration candidate; (2) the cybersecurity bill's (Proposition de loi n° 2024-36) relationship to Decree-Law No. 2023-17 — "unclarified" since Runs 276/293/297/298 — was refined via a direct WebFetch of legislation-securite.tn's own tracking page, whose cross-reference field explicitly reads "Aucun texte n'est lié à ce texte" (no text is linked to this one), indicating the two instruments are tracked as legally independent rather than as an amendment/replacement pair, though this does not rule out overlap discussed in the bill's own unsighted explanatory memorandum. The remaining six watchlist items were reconfirmed unresolved with no material change: the STEG/IBRD loan-guarantee bills' vote tally from the July 14 plenary resumption remains unreported by every outlet checked, consistent with Run 295's finding that the session proceeded as scheduled at 10:00 AM without a published result; the ONAT general assembly (held July 11) outcome remains unreported, now well past 48 consecutive runs; the Development Plan's CNRD stage remains under active regional-disparity hearings (Imed Derbali, Samir Abdelhafid) with no floor vote, promulgation, or JORT publication found; the Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution proceedings remain pending with no ruling reported since the July 6, 2026 postponement; the LTDH appeal hearing was reconfirmed via Kapitalis and Business News (both June 10–11, 2026) as scheduled for tomorrow, July 15, 2026, with a newly-noted detail — roughly 180 lawyers have formally constituted themselves as part of LTDH's defense team — added to the civil-society suspensions entry; and the anti-lobbying/transparency bill's official proposition number remains unidentified despite a further targeted search of the ARP site. A general sweep for July 13–14, 2026 developments outside the watchlist (new-law and JORT searches, plus a re-check of the debunked "new Traffic Code" and "Personal Data Protection full application" hoaxes, both again correctly withheld) surfaced no undocumented gap. No new standalone entries were added (299 total remains accurate, since all findings refine existing entries); three existing entries revised for currency (Chokri Belaïd, cybersecurity bill, civil-society suspensions). A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 299 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, 1,406/1,406 balanced <div> tags, and all three version markers plus the header stat counter synchronized at v4.299/Run 299/299. Domestic multi-source corroboration of the Chokri Belaïd sentence increase, the STEG vote tally, the ONAT outcome (48+ consecutive runs unreported), the Development Plan's CNRD floor vote/promulgation, the Al Khatt/Mnemty outcome, the LTDH appeal ruling (due tomorrow, July 15), and the anti-lobbying bill's official number remain the standing priorities for the next run.

All seven remain unresolved with no material change since Run 299: the STEG/IBRD loan-guarantee bills' vote tally from the July 14 plenary resumption still has not been reported by any outlet checked (Business News, La Presse, African Manager, DirectInfo/WebManagerCenter); the Chokri Belaïd Akremi/Ellouze/Ben Brik sentence-increase report remains corroborated only by webdo.tn and the foreign Italpress wire, with a fresh targeted sweep of Business News, La Presse, Kapitalis, Espace Manager, African Manager, DirectInfo/WebManagerCenter, Mosaïque FM, and Tunisie Numérique again finding no domestic outlet carrying the story — all their indexed coverage still ends at the March 4, 2026 first-instance sentencing or the July 6–7 postponement news, so the existing "corroborated by a non-Tunisian wire, domestic multi-source confirmation still pending" framing stands unchanged; the ONAT (Ordre National des Avocats de Tunisie, the Tunisian Bar Association, president Boubaker Bethabet) general assembly (held July 11) outcome remains unreported by every source searched, now well past 49 consecutive runs, with the most recent related coverage still only the May 1, 2026 extraordinary AG strike vote; the Development Plan's CNRD stage was independently reconfirmed as still "examining"/"pursuing" the plan via district-commission input (La Presse, July 12) with no CNRD floor vote result, promulgation announcement, or JORT publication found in any July 12–14 coverage; the Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution proceedings' outcome remains unreported by any outlet checked (webdo, DirectInfo, Business News, Inkyfada, Amnesty) since the July 6, 2026 postponement; the LTDH appeal hearing was independently reconfirmed via Kapitalis and Business News (both June 10–11, 2026) as scheduled for tomorrow, July 15, 2026, and remains correctly unresolved (not yet due, not overdue); and the anti-lobbying/transparency bill's official proposition number remains unidentified in any source checked, with its substance (19 articles, ten deputy sponsors, up to three years' imprisonment, annual transparency-report requirement) reconfirmed via the same July 10 La Presse piece. A broad sweep for July 10–14, 2026 developments outside the watchlist found no newly enacted law, decree-law, or JORT publication beyond the already-documented Finance Law 2026, and re-confirmed as debunked the circulating "new Traffic/Road Code" claim (Business News, July 6) and the recurring Personal Data Protection "in force" fabrication — both correctly withheld. No new standalone entries were added (299 total remains accurate, since every finding this run reconfirmed the status quo rather than yielding new facts); no existing entry required revision, as this run's research corroborated — without changing — every fact already on record. A structural audit confirmed no regressions: 299 unique law-card ids with zero duplicates, 30/30 balanced <section> tags, and all three version markers plus the header stat counter synchronized at v4.300/Run 300/299. Domestic multi-source corroboration of the Chokri Belaïd sentence increase, the STEG vote tally, the ONAT outcome (49+ consecutive runs unreported), the Development Plan's CNRD floor vote/promulgation, the Al Khatt/Mnemty outcome, the LTDH appeal ruling (due tomorrow, July 15), and the anti-lobbying bill's official number remain the standing priorities for the next run.

(1) STEG/IBRD loan-guarantee bills — RESOLVED: the ARP plenary adopted both bills on July 14, 2026 (webdo.tn, direct WebFetch), ending the vote-tally watch standing since Run 222; the report also reconciles the Run 295 currency discrepancy, confirming the combined package as US$468 million (€384.8M≈US$438.4M IBRD loan + US$30M CTF loan). No for/against tally was found. (2) Chokri Belaïd sentence-increase — domestic corroboration achieved: a direct WebFetch of an allAfrica republication (July 14, 2026) attributes the original reporting to La Presse (Tunis), a genuine Tunisian domestic outlet, matching the 29/17/17-year figures exactly; this closes the domestic-corroboration gap flagged since Run 296 (the Run 298/299 African Manager lead remained HTTP 403 on a further retry this run and is now treated as a dead end). Both entries were updated in place rather than as new cards. The remaining five watchlist items were reconfirmed unresolved with no material change: the ONAT general assembly (held July 11) outcome remains unreported, now well past 50 consecutive runs; the Development Plan's CNRD stage remains under active regional-disparity examination with no floor vote, promulgation, or JORT publication found (note: the plan's underlying ARP adoption, 64-24-15 on July 10, 2026, was already documented at Run 218/219 and is not a new finding — verified this run to guard against duplicate re-addition); the Al Khatt/Mnemty dissolution proceedings remain pending with no ruling since the July 6, 2026 postponement; the LTDH appeal hearing remains correctly unresolved, scheduled for tomorrow, July 15, 2026; and the anti-lobbying/transparency bill's official proposition number remains unidentified. A broad sweep for July 10–14, 2026 developments outside the watchlist found no newly enacted law, decree-law, or JORT publication beyond what is already documented, and reconfirmed the "new Traffic/Road Code" claim as a debunked hoax and the Personal Data Protection "in force" claim as still false.
